SERVANT.
I was ready, madam.
HOUSEKEEPER.
You were ready to do nothing.
SERVANT.
I was ready to begin doing something.
HOUSEKEEPER.
That is what you always say.
SERVANT.
And one day it shall be true.

LEON.
You have twisted my meaning completely.

FELIX.
Then perhaps it was bent to begin with.

LEON.
You never miss a chance.

FELIX.
To improve another man's argument? Rarely.

LEON.
Improve it?

FELIX.
Certainly. I take ordinary nonsense and give it polish.

LEON.
I thought the nonsense was yours.

FELIX.
Only in part. I borrow freely.

MARCUS.
That much is evident.

FELIX.
There speaks a man who contributes little and judges much.

MARCUS.
A comfortable arrangement.

LEON.
Your wit is restless today.

FELIX.
It has had no proper exercise. If I do not let it run about, it becomes destructive.

LEON.
More destructive than usual?

FELIX.
I hope not.

Enter HOUSEKEEPER and SERVANT.

LEON.
Here comes company.

FELIX.
Aye, and with purpose. Casual visitors do not march so directly.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Thomas!

SERVANT.
Madam?

HOUSEKEEPER.
My basket. Have you lost it again?

SERVANT.
No, madam.

FELIX.
A remarkable achievement. Let it be recorded.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Good day, gentlemen.

FELIX.
A very good day, if appearances may be trusted.

HOUSEKEEPER.
And if they may not?

FELIX.
Then we are all deceived together.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You have an answer for everything.

FELIX.
Not at all. Only for things I do not understand.

LEON.
Which explains the quantity.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Can someone tell me where I might find Master Leon?

LEON.
You have found him already, though perhaps not improved by the discovery.

HOUSEKEEPER.
That remains to be seen.

FELIX.
A cautious judgment. I approve.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Sir, I would have a private word.

MARCUS.
Now there is a phrase that always awakens curiosity.

FELIX.
Or anxiety.

LEON.
Usually in those not invited to hear it.

FELIX.
A cruel custom.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Will you leave us, then?

FELIX.
Since I am plainly unwanted, I shall preserve what remains of my dignity and go willingly.

MARCUS.
You cannot preserve what was never there.

FELIX.
A painful farewell gift.

Come, Marcus. Let us find food before philosophy becomes necessary.

MARCUS.
Lead on.

FELIX.
Madam, farewell. Thomas, keep watch over the basket. It appears to depend upon you.

Exeunt FELIX and MARCUS.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Who is that fellow?

LEON.
A good friend, though not always at convenient times.

HOUSEKEEPER.
He speaks enough for three men.

LEON.
And listens for half of one.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Had he continued much longer, I should have put him in his place.

SERVANT.
I would have assisted.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You?

SERVANT.
Certainly.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You never assist until the matter is over.

SERVANT.
That is unfortunate timing, not lack of courage.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Keep telling yourself so.

SERVANT.
I do, madam. It is a great comfort.

How far is his court distant from this shore? FIRST FISHERMAN. Marry sir, half a day’s journey: and I’ll tell you, he hath a fair daughter, and tomorrow is her birth-day; and there are princes and knights come from all parts of the world to joust and tourney for her love. PERICLES. Were my fortunes equal to my desires, I could wish to make one there. FIRST FISHERMAN. O, sir, things must be as they may; and what a man cannot get, he may lawfully deal for—his wife’s soul. Re-enter Second and Third Fishermen, drawing up a net. SECOND FISHERMAN. Help, master, help! here’s a fish hangs in the net, like a poor man’s right in the law; ’twill hardly come out. Ha! bots on’t, ’tis come at last, and ’tis turned to a rusty armour. PERICLES. An armour, friends! I pray you, let me see it. Thanks, Fortune, yet, that, after all my crosses, Thou givest me somewhat to repair myself, And though it was mine own, part of my heritage, Which my dead father did bequeath to me, With this strict charge, even as he left his life. ‘Keep it, my Pericles; it hath been a shield ’Twixt me and death;’—and pointed to this brace;— ‘For that it saved me, keep it; in like necessity— The which the gods protect thee from!—may defend thee.’ It kept where I kept, I so dearly loved it; Till the rough seas, that spares not any man, Took it in rage, though calm’d have given’t again: I thank thee for’t: my shipwreck now’s no ill, Since I have here my father gave in his will. FIRST FISHERMAN. What mean you sir? PERICLES. To beg of you, kind friends, this coat of worth, For it was sometime target to a king; I know it by this mark. He loved me dearly, And for his sake I wish the having of it; And that you’d guide me to your sovereign court, Where with it I may appear a gentleman; And if that ever my low fortune’s better, I’ll pay your bounties; till then rest your debtor. FIRST FISHERMAN. Why, wilt thou tourney for the lady? PERICLES. I’ll show the virtue I have borne in arms. FIRST FISHERMAN. Why, d’ye take it, and the gods give thee good on’t! SECOND FISHERMAN. Ay, but hark you, my friend; ’twas we that made up this garment through the rough seams of the waters: there are certain condolements, certain vails. I hope, sir, if you thrive, you’ll remember from whence you had them. PERICLES. Believe’t I will. By your furtherance I am clothed in steel; And spite of all the rapture of the sea, This jewel holds his building on my arm: Unto thy value I will mount myself Upon a courser, whose delightful steps Shall make the gazer joy to see him tread. Only, my friend, I yet am unprovided Of a pair of bases. SECOND FISHERMAN. We’ll sure provide: thou shalt have my best gown to make thee a pair; and I’ll bring thee to the court myself. PERICLES. Then honour be but a goal to my will, This day I’ll rise, or else add ill to ill.  [_Exeunt._]    SCENE II. The same. A public way, or platform leading to the lists. A pavilion by the side of it for the reception of the King, Princess, Lords, etc. Enter Simonides, Thaisa, Lords and Attendants. SIMONIDES. Are the knights ready to begin the triumph? FIRST LORD. They are, my liege; And stay your coming to present themselves. SIMONIDES. Return them, we are ready; and our daughter, In honour of whose birth these triumphs are, Sits here, like beauty’s child, whom Nature gat For men to see, and seeing wonder at. [_Exit a Lord._]  THAISA. It pleaseth you, my royal father, to express My commendations great, whose merit’s less. SIMONIDES. It’s fit it should be so; for princes are A model, which heaven makes like to itself: As jewels lose their glory if neglected, So princes their renowns if not respected. ’Tis now your honour, daughter, to entertain The labour of each knight in his device. THAISA. Which, to preserve mine honour, I’ll perform. The first Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa. SIMONIDES. Who is the first that doth prefer himself? THAISA. A knight of Sparta, my renowned father; And the device he bears upon his shield Is a black Ethiope reaching at the sun: The word, _Lux tua vita mihi._  SIMONIDES.

A herald sounds within.
HERALD. Make way there! The noble challengers approach the field.
Enter a Knight and his Squire.
KNIGHT. Good sir, announce me to the king.
HERALD. Your name and country?
KNIGHT. I am a lord of Macedon, come from distant lands to prove my worth before the princess.
HERALD. Stand forth, then.
Enter King Leontes, Princess Helena, Lords, and Attendants.
LEONTES. What knight is this that seeks our favour?
KNIGHT. My gracious lord, though fortune hath not smiled on me of late, yet courage remains. Hearing of your daughter's beauty and virtue, I have ventured hither to contend among these worthy men.
HELENA. Sir knight, your courtesy becomes you well. What device bear you on your shield?
SQUIRE. A silver falcon mounting through the clouds toward a star; beneath is written, Spes super astra.
LEONTES. A noble emblem. It speaks of one whose hopes rise higher than his condition.
KNIGHT. My lord, a man may be poor in treasure and yet rich in honour. If I prevail, let merit be my witness; if I fail, I shall still esteem the journey well spent.
LEONTES. You answer wisely. Many boast before the contest begins; few speak so modestly.
FIRST LORD. The other challengers are assembled and await your command.
LEONTES. Then let the trumpets sound. This day shall prove what metal lies beneath bright armour.
Trumpets flourish.
HELENA. Father, I confess these spectacles delight me less for victory than for the virtues they reveal. True worth is not always known until it is tried.
LEONTES. Well said, daughter. The furnace discovers the gold, and trial the man.
HERALD. Let the lists be opened!
The Knights prepare themselves.
KNIGHT. Whatever fortune grants, I thank the heavens for this occasion. A brave attempt is itself a kind of triumph.
LEONTES. Go then, and may honour attend the deserving.
Exeunt toward the tournament.

A herald is heard offstage.
HERALD. Room there! The challengers come to the field.
A Knight enters with his Squire.
KNIGHT. Friend, let the king know I am arrived.
HERALD. State your name and country.
KNIGHT. A nobleman of Macedon. I have crossed many seas to seek honour in this court and to offer my service before the princess.
HERALD. Wait here and be presented.
Enter King Leontes, Princess Helena, with Lords and attendants.
LEONTES. Who comes before us?
KNIGHT. My liege, fortune has dealt unevenly with me, yet she has not robbed me of courage. News of your daughter's grace drew me here, and I ask leave to compete among the other knights.
HELENA. You speak with good manners, sir. Tell us, what sign is carried on your shield?
SQUIRE. A silver falcon rising through the clouds toward a single star. The motto reads, Spes super astra.
LEONTES. An ambitious image. It suggests a man who aims beyond the limits of his station.
KNIGHT. Perhaps so, my lord. Wealth is uncertain, but honour belongs to any man willing to earn it. Should I succeed, let my deeds speak; should I fail, I shall regret nothing.
LEONTES. A measured answer. Confidence sits better when it is not dressed as pride.
FIRST LORD. Sire, the remaining competitors are ready.
LEONTES. Then there is no cause to delay. Let the trial begin.
Trumpets sound.
HELENA. I often think these contests reveal more than strength. A person's nature is seen most clearly when something important is at stake.
LEONTES. There is wisdom in that. Gold is tested by fire, and character by challenge.
HERALD. Open the lists!
The knights move toward the field.
KNIGHT. Whatever happens today, I count myself fortunate to have stood among such company. Even the attempt carries its own reward.
LEONTES. Go forward, then. May honour find its rightful owner.
They exit toward the tournament grounds.

MACDUFF. Is the King stirring, worthy thane? MACBETH. Not yet. MACDUFF. He did command me to call timely on him. I have almost slipp’d the hour. MACBETH. I’ll bring you to him. MACDUFF. I know this is a joyful trouble to you; But yet ’tis one. MACBETH. The labour we delight in physics pain. This is the door. MACDUFF. I’ll make so bold to call. For ’tis my limited service. [_Exit Macduff._]  LENNOX. Goes the King hence today? MACBETH. He does. He did appoint so. LENNOX. The night has been unruly: where we lay, Our chimneys were blown down and, as they say, Lamentings heard i’ th’ air, strange screams of death, And prophesying, with accents terrible, Of dire combustion and confus’d events, New hatch’d to the woeful time. The obscure bird Clamour’d the live-long night. Some say the earth Was feverous, and did shake. MACBETH. ’Twas a rough night. LENNOX. My young remembrance cannot parallel A fellow to it. Enter Macduff. MACDUFF. O horror, horror, horror! Tongue nor heart cannot conceive nor name thee! MACBETH, LENNOX. What’s the matter? MACDUFF. Confusion now hath made his masterpiece! Most sacrilegious murder hath broke ope The Lord’s anointed temple, and stole thence The life o’ th’ building. MACBETH. What is’t you say? the life? LENNOX. Mean you his majesty? MACDUFF. Approach the chamber, and destroy your sight With a new Gorgon. Do not bid me speak. See, and then speak yourselves. [_Exeunt Macbeth and Lennox._]  Awake, awake!— Ring the alarum bell.—Murder and treason! Banquo and Donalbain! Malcolm! awake! Shake off this downy sleep, death’s counterfeit, And look on death itself! Up, up, and see The great doom’s image. Malcolm! Banquo! As from your graves rise up, and walk like sprites To countenance this horror! [_Alarum-bell rings._]  Enter Lady Macbeth. LADY MACBETH. What’s the business, That such a hideous trumpet calls to parley The sleepers of the house? Speak, speak! MACDUFF. O gentle lady, ’Tis not for you to hear what I can speak: The repetition, in a woman’s ear, Would murder as it fell. Enter Banquo. O Banquo, Banquo! Our royal master’s murder’d! LADY MACBETH. Woe, alas! What, in our house? BANQUO. Too cruel anywhere.— Dear Duff, I pr’ythee, contradict thyself, And say it is not so. Enter Macbeth and Lennox with Ross. MACBETH. Had I but died an hour before this chance, I had liv’d a blessed time; for, from this instant There’s nothing serious in mortality. All is but toys: renown and grace is dead; The wine of life is drawn, and the mere lees Is left this vault to brag of. Enter Malcolm and Donalbain. DONALBAIN. What is amiss? MACBETH. You are, and do not know’t: The spring, the head, the fountain of your blood Is stopp’d; the very source of it is stopp’d. MACDUFF. Your royal father’s murder’d. MALCOLM. O, by whom? LENNOX. Those of his chamber, as it seem’d, had done’t: Their hands and faces were all badg’d with blood; So were their daggers, which, unwip’d, we found Upon their pillows. They star’d, and were distracted; No man’s life was to be trusted with them. MACBETH. O, yet I do repent me of my fury, That I did kill them. MACDUFF. Wherefore did you so? MACBETH. Who can be wise, amaz’d, temperate, and furious, Loyal and neutral, in a moment? No man: Th’ expedition of my violent love Outrun the pauser, reason. Here lay Duncan, His silver skin lac’d with his golden blood; And his gash’d stabs look’d like a breach in nature For ruin’s wasteful entrance: there, the murderers, Steep’d in the colours of their trade, their daggers Unmannerly breech’d with gore. Who could refrain, That had a heart to love, and in that heart Courage to make’s love known? LADY MACBETH. Help me hence, ho! MACDUFF. Look to the lady. MALCOLM. Why do we hold our tongues, That most may claim this argument for ours? DONALBAIN. What should be spoken here, where our fate, Hid in an auger hole, may rush, and seize us? Let’s away. Our tears are not yet brew’d. MALCOLM. Nor our strong sorrow Upon the foot of motion. BANQUO. Look to the lady:—  [_Lady Macbeth is carried out._]  And when we have our naked frailties hid, That suffer in exposure, let us meet, And question this most bloody piece of work To know it further. Fears and scruples shake us: In the great hand of God I stand; and thence Against the undivulg’d pretence I fight Of treasonous malice.

Enter Lord Aurelian and Sir Edmund.
AURELIAN. Is our sovereign yet awake, good sir?
EDMUND. Not so, my lord. He charged me to attend him at first light, yet I fear the hour runs fast.
AURELIAN. I shall conduct you to his chamber.
EDMUND. The duty is a welcome burden, though a burden still.
AURELIAN. Service performed with love sits lightly on the shoulders. Here is the door.
EDMUND. Then with your leave I’ll call upon him.
Exit Edmund.
RICHARD. Will the king depart this morning?
AURELIAN. So he intended when last we spoke.
RICHARD. The heavens were troubled through the night. Fierce winds battered the towers, and many swore they heard strange cries upon the air. Dogs howled without cease, and the ravens gathered before dawn as though they scented calamity.
AURELIAN. The storm was harsh indeed.
RICHARD. In all my years I do not recall its equal.
Re-enter Edmund.
EDMUND. O miserable sight! O dreadful, dreadful day!
AURELIAN, RICHARD. What has befallen?
EDMUND. Ruin has entered where honour slept. A most foul crime has struck at majesty itself and robbed this kingdom of its living heart.
AURELIAN. Explain yourself!
RICHARD. Speak plainly, man.
EDMUND. Go to the chamber and behold it with your own eyes. My tongue refuses what my eyes have witnessed.
Aurelian and Richard exit.
Awake! Awake! Ring the bell! Treason! Murder! Let every loyal soul arise! Prince Henry! Prince Edward! Wake from sleep and look upon the horror that has visited this house!
A bell rings.
Enter Queen Eleanor.
ELEANOR. What summons breaks the silence of the dawn? Why does this fearful noise disturb the household?
EDMUND. Madam, forgive me. The news is too terrible for gentle ears.
Enter Lord Stanley.
STANLEY. What means this uproar?
EDMUND. Our gracious king is slain!
ELEANOR. Heaven defend us! Slain?
STANLEY. No, say not so. Let those words be false.
Re-enter Aurelian and Richard with Lords.
AURELIAN. Had death taken me before this hour, I should have counted myself fortunate. From this moment all joy seems hollow. Honour lies dead beside our sovereign.
Enter Prince Henry and Prince Edward.
HENRY. What disaster has occurred?
AURELIAN. The source from which your lives were nourished is cut off. Your father is no more.
EDMUND. The king has been murdered.
HENRY. By whose hand?
RICHARD. His attendants appeared guilty. Their garments were stained with blood, and the weapons were found beside them.
AURELIAN. In grief and rage I struck them down before they could answer.
EDMUND. Why did you act so quickly?
AURELIAN. Who could remain calm before such a sight? There lay our noble king, his wounds crying out for vengeance. Reason yielded to loyalty and sorrow.
ELEANOR. I grow faint.
STANLEY. Attend the queen.
HENRY. Why do we stand silent while danger gathers around us?
EDWARD. We know not where the blow was forged. It may be aimed at us next.
STANLEY. Let us withdraw for a time, compose ourselves, and then seek the truth. Fear clouds every judgment.
HENRY. Agreed. We must learn who profits from this evil deed.
STANLEY. Until then, I place my trust in Heaven and stand ready against whatever treachery remains concealed.
Exeunt.

Enter Lord Aurelian and Sir Edmund.
AURELIAN. Has the king stirred yet?
EDMUND. Not that I know of, my lord. He asked me to attend him early, and I fear I am close to missing the appointed hour.
AURELIAN. Come then. I’ll take you to his chamber.
EDMUND. A pleasant duty, though duties seldom come without trouble.
AURELIAN. Trouble weighs little when the heart is willing. The door is here.
EDMUND. I’ll announce myself.
Exit Edmund.
RICHARD. Does His Majesty leave today?
AURELIAN. That was his intention.
RICHARD. The night gave ill signs of it. The wind shook the walls, and more than one man claimed to hear cries carried through the darkness. Even the dogs would not rest. Before sunrise the ravens circled the courtyard in great numbers.
AURELIAN. A bitter night, certainly.
RICHARD. Stranger than any I remember.
Edmund returns suddenly.
EDMUND. Horror! O God, what horror!
AURELIAN. What has happened?
RICHARD. Speak!
EDMUND. Wickedness has done its worst. The king is struck down. The crown itself has been assaulted.
AURELIAN. What are you saying?
EDMUND. Look for yourselves. I cannot tell it properly.
Aurelian and Richard hurry away.
Awake! Awake! Ring the bell! Treason and murder! Princes, rise! Throw off your sleep and see what dreadful thing has been done!
A bell sounds throughout the castle.
Enter Queen Eleanor.
ELEANOR. What alarm is this? Why is the house in such confusion?
EDMUND. Madam, the tale is too dreadful to repeat.
Enter Lord Stanley.
STANLEY. What misfortune stirs everyone so?
EDMUND. Our king has been murdered.
ELEANOR. Murdered?
STANLEY. No. Let that not be true.
Aurelian and Richard return with several lords.
AURELIAN. Had my life ended an hour ago, I should have died happier than I stand now. Everything seems diminished after this.
Enter Prince Henry and Prince Edward.
HENRY. What has occurred?
AURELIAN. The man who gave you life is gone.
EDMUND. Your father has been slain.
HENRY. By whom?
RICHARD. Suspicion falls upon those who guarded his chamber. They were covered in blood, and bloody daggers lay beside them.
AURELIAN. I killed them in my fury.
EDMUND. Before they were questioned?
AURELIAN. Yes. I saw the king's wounds and lost command of myself. Grief moved faster than judgment.
ELEANOR. I cannot bear this.
STANLEY. Help the queen.
HENRY. We speak too little when the matter concerns us most.
EDWARD. And perhaps too openly. If this plot reaches the throne, who can say where it ends?
STANLEY. Let us gather ourselves before we act. Fear makes poor counsel.
HENRY. Yet the truth must be found.
STANLEY. It shall be. Until then, I trust in Heaven and keep watch against whatever treason still hides in shadow.
Exeunt.

He loves you well that holds his life of you. The second Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa. Who is the second that presents himself? THAISA. A prince of Macedon, my royal father; And the device he bears upon his shield Is an arm’d knight that’s conquer’d by a lady; The motto thus, in Spanish, _Piu por dulzura que por forza._  The third Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa. SIMONIDES. And what’s the third? THAISA. The third of Antioch; And his device, a wreath of chivalry; The word, _Me pompae provexit apex._  The fourth Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa. SIMONIDES. What is the fourth? THAISA. A burning torch that’s turned upside down; The word, _Quod me alit me extinguit._  SIMONIDES. Which shows that beauty hath his power and will, Which can as well inflame as it can kill. The fifth Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa. THAISA. The fifth, an hand environed with clouds, Holding out gold that’s by the touchstone tried; The motto thus, _Sic spectanda fides._  The sixth Knight, Pericles, passes in rusty armour with bases, and unaccompanied. He presents his device directly to Thaisa. SIMONIDES. And what’s the sixth and last, the which the knight himself With such a graceful courtesy deliver’d? THAISA. He seems to be a stranger; but his present is A wither’d branch, that’s only green at top; The motto, _In hac spe vivo._  SIMONIDES. A pretty moral; From the dejected state wherein he is, He hopes by you his fortunes yet may flourish. FIRST LORD. He had need mean better than his outward show Can any way speak in his just commend; For by his rusty outside he appears To have practised more the whipstock than the lance. SECOND LORD. He well may be a stranger, for he comes To an honour’d triumph strangely furnished. THIRD LORD. And on set purpose let his armour rust Until this day, to scour it in the dust. SIMONIDES. Opinion’s but a fool, that makes us scan The outward habit by the inward man. But stay, the knights are coming. We will withdraw into the gallery. [_Exeunt. Great shouts within, and all cry_ ‘The mean Knight!’]    SCENE III. The same. A hall of state: a banquet prepared. Enter Simonides, Thaisa, Lords, Attendants and Knights, from tilting. SIMONIDES. Knights, To say you’re welcome were superfluous. To place upon the volume of your deeds, As in a title-page, your worth in arms, Were more than you expect, or more than’s fit, Since every worth in show commends itself. Prepare for mirth, for mirth becomes a feast: You are princes and my guests. THAISA. But you, my knight and guest; To whom this wreath of victory I give, And crown you king of this day’s happiness. PERICLES. ’Tis more by fortune, lady, than by merit. SIMONIDES. Call it by what you will, the day is yours; And here, I hope, is none that envies it. In framing an artist, art hath thus decreed, To make some good, but others to exceed; And you are her labour’d scholar. Come queen of the feast,— For, daughter, so you are,—here take your place: Marshal the rest, as they deserve their grace. KNIGHTS. We are honour’d much by good Simonides. SIMONIDES. Your presence glads our days; honour we love; For who hates honour hates the gods above. MARSHALL. Sir, yonder is your place. PERICLES. Some other is more fit. FIRST KNIGHT. Contend not, sir; for we are gentlemen Have neither in our hearts nor outward eyes Envied the great, nor shall the low despise. PERICLES. You are right courteous knights. SIMONIDES. Sit, sir, sit. By Jove, I wonder, that is king of thoughts, These cates resist me, he but thought upon. THAISA. By Juno, that is queen of marriage, All viands that I eat do seem unsavoury, Wishing him my meat. Sure, he’s a gallant gentleman. SIMONIDES. He’s but a country gentleman; Has done no more than other knights have done; Has broken a staff or so; so let it pass. THAISA. To me he seems like diamond to glass. PERICLES. Yon king’s to me like to my father’s picture, Which tells me in that glory once he was; Had princes sit, like stars, about his throne, And he the sun, for them to reverence; None that beheld him, but, like lesser lights, Did vail their crowns to his supremacy: Where now his son’s like a glow-worm in the night, The which hath fire in darkness, none in light: Whereby I see that time’s the king of men, He’s both their parent, and he is their grave, And gives them what he will, not what they crave.

The second Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Princess Helena.
KING LEONTES. Who is this second champion?
HELENA. A duke from Sicily, honoured father;
Upon his shield appears a soaring eagle
That climbs above the storm and seeks the sun;
The motto, Virtus superat fata.
LEONTES. A noble thought. True courage oft aspires
Beyond the reach of fortune's harsh command.
The third Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield.
LEONTES. And who comes next?
HELENA. A lord of Venice.
His device is a laurel growing from a rock;
The word, Per dura ad gloriam.
LEONTES. A fitting emblem for a steadfast man.
The fourth Knight passes.
HELENA. The fourth bears a broken sword
Bound fast with golden cords;
The motto, Fides reparat.
LEONTES. Loyalty can mend what force destroys.
The fifth Knight passes.
HELENA. Upon the fifth knight's shield
A ship rides safely through a violent sea;
Beneath is written, Spem sequor.
LEONTES. Hope is a pilot many men have trusted.
The sixth Knight enters alone, his armour plain and weathered. He presents his shield himself.
LEONTES. And what says this last device,
Which the knight delivers with such modest grace?
HELENA. A single flower rising through the snow;
The motto, Dum vivo spero.
LEONTES. A simple lesson, yet a pleasing one.
Though winter presses hard upon his state,
He trusts that spring may yet return.
FIRST LORD. His appearance promises but little.
His armour seems more acquainted with neglect
Than with the honours of the field.
SECOND LORD. Yet strangers often wear disguises
That hide their worth.
THIRD LORD. Or perhaps he kept that rust
To polish it with glory in the lists.
LEONTES. Men judge too swiftly by what they can see.
The garment does not always reveal the man.
But look—the contest ends. Let us retire.
Exeunt. Great shouts within. Voices cry, “The Stranger Knight! The Stranger Knight!”

SCENE III. A Banquet Hall
Enter King Leontes, Helena, Lords, Attendants, and Knights.
LEONTES. My lords and noble knights,
To welcome you would waste unnecessary words.
Your deeds have spoken loudly for themselves.
This hall is prepared for joy, and joy becomes
Such company as yours.
Take now your places.
HELENA. And you, good sir,
To whom this garland of victory belongs,
Receive it as the honour of the day.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Lady, fortune has been kinder than my merits.
LEONTES. Call it fortune if you wish;
The prize remains deserved.
Not all men are fashioned in equal measure.
Some achieve honour, while others seem born
To surpass it.
Come, daughter, lead the feast.
KNIGHTS. We thank Your Majesty.
LEONTES. You do us honour by your presence.
MARSHAL. Sir, your seat awaits.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Another may deserve it more.
FIRST KNIGHT. Not so.
We who have lost bear no resentment,
Nor should excellence be denied its due.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Your generosity exceeds my deserts.
LEONTES. Sit, sir, sit.
Aside.
By heaven, I marvel at my daughter.
The feast stands before her untouched,
Yet all her thoughts seem fixed elsewhere.
HELENA. Aside.
The richest dishes lose their savour.
My eyes have feasted where my heart would dine.
Surely this knight possesses uncommon virtue.
LEONTES. Aside.
He is but a gentleman,
No different from the rest.
A broken lance or two has earned him praise.
Let that suffice.
HELENA. Aside.
To compare the others with him
Is to compare crystal with a star.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Aside.
That king recalls my father's image,
Majestic in the days of his prosperity.
Around him gathered nobles like bright planets
Circling a sun.
Now I remain, diminished by misfortune,
A lonely spark where once a fire burned.
Thus time exalts and humbles whom it pleases;
It grants its gifts, then asks them back again.

The second Knight passes, and his Squire presents a shield before Helena.
LEONTES. Who offers himself next?
HELENA. A duke from Sicily, my lord.
His shield bears an eagle climbing through dark clouds toward the sun.
The motto reads, Virtus superat fata.
LEONTES. A bold sentiment. Some men would rather challenge fate than submit to it.
A third Knight approaches.
LEONTES. And this one?
HELENA. A Venetian nobleman.
Upon his shield grows a laurel from bare stone.
His word is Per dura ad gloriam.
LEONTES. Glory won through hardship. An old lesson, but a worthy one.
The fourth Knight presents his device.
HELENA. A shattered sword bound together with golden cords.
The motto, Fides reparat.
LEONTES. Faith and loyalty often repair what strength cannot.
The fifth Knight advances.
HELENA. He bears a vessel crossing rough seas.
Below it stands the phrase Spem sequor.
LEONTES. Hope has carried many safely through greater storms.
The last Knight enters without attendant or display. His armour is worn and dull with age.
LEONTES. And what of this final challenger?
HELENA. His shield shows a flower breaking through snow.
The words are Dum vivo spero.
LEONTES. Plain enough, yet full of meaning. A man brought low still believes better days may come.
FIRST LORD. His appearance gives little cause for confidence.
SECOND LORD. Appearances have deceived wiser men than us.
THIRD LORD. Perhaps the rust is intentional. Victory might shine brighter beside it.
LEONTES. We are all too eager to read character from clothing. Let us wait for proof instead.
Shouts erupt offstage.
VOICES. The Stranger Knight! The Stranger Knight!
LEONTES. It seems the field has already given its answer.
Exeunt.

SCENE III. A Hall Prepared for Feasting
Enter King Leontes, Helena, Lords, Attendants, and Knights.
LEONTES. Noble knights, welcome.
Yet your actions have welcomed you better than any words I could offer.
The day calls for celebration, not ceremony.
Take your places and enjoy what has been prepared.
HELENA. And you, sir,
Accept this wreath.
The honour belongs to you.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Lady, I fear fortune deserves more credit than I do.
LEONTES. Fortune may help, but she rarely wins tournaments alone.
Today is yours.
HELENA places the wreath upon him.
LEONTES. Daughter, sit beside me and guide the rest to their seats.
KNIGHTS. Your kindness honours us.
LEONTES. The honour is shared.
MARSHAL. Sir, this place has been reserved for you.
STRANGER KNIGHT. There are worthier men present.
FIRST KNIGHT. We disagree.
No gentleman should envy another's success, especially when it has been fairly earned.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Then I thank you for your courtesy.
He sits.
LEONTES. Aside.
My daughter hardly touches her meal.
The victor occupies more of her attention than the feast.
HELENA. Aside.
Nothing on the table pleases me.
My thoughts remain fixed elsewhere.
There is something rare about this man.
LEONTES. Aside.
A capable knight, certainly, but no miracle.
Young hearts are quick to exaggerate.
HELENA. Aside.
The difference seems greater to me.
The others shine, yet he draws the eye without trying.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Aside.
The king reminds me of my father as he once stood:
powerful, admired, surrounded by loyal nobles.
Time has altered everything.
Where he blazed like the sun, I wander as a small light in darkness.
So it goes with men. Time raises them high, then lowers them again, caring little for what they desire.
The feast continues.

He heareth not, he stirreth not, he moveth not; The ape is dead, and I must conjure him. I conjure thee by Rosaline’s bright eyes, By her high forehead and her scarlet lip, By her fine foot, straight leg, and quivering thigh, And the demesnes that there adjacent lie, That in thy likeness thou appear to us. BENVOLIO. An if he hear thee, thou wilt anger him. MERCUTIO. This cannot anger him. ’Twould anger him To raise a spirit in his mistress’ circle, Of some strange nature, letting it there stand Till she had laid it, and conjur’d it down; That were some spite. My invocation Is fair and honest, and, in his mistress’ name, I conjure only but to raise up him. BENVOLIO. Come, he hath hid himself among these trees To be consorted with the humorous night. Blind is his love, and best befits the dark. MERCUTIO. If love be blind, love cannot hit the mark. Now will he sit under a medlar tree, And wish his mistress were that kind of fruit As maids call medlars when they laugh alone. O Romeo, that she were, O that she were An open-arse and thou a poperin pear! Romeo, good night. I’ll to my truckle-bed. This field-bed is too cold for me to sleep. Come, shall we go? BENVOLIO. Go then; for ’tis in vain To seek him here that means not to be found. [_Exeunt._]  SCENE II. Capulet’s Garden. Enter Romeo. ROMEO. He jests at scars that never felt a wound. Juliet appears above at a window. But soft, what light through yonder window breaks? It is the east, and Juliet is the sun! Arise fair sun and kill the envious moon, Who is already sick and pale with grief, That thou her maid art far more fair than she. Be not her maid since she is envious; Her vestal livery is but sick and green, And none but fools do wear it; cast it off. It is my lady, O it is my love! O, that she knew she were! She speaks, yet she says nothing. What of that? Her eye discourses, I will answer it. I am too bold, ’tis not to me she speaks. Two of the fairest stars in all the heaven, Having some business, do entreat her eyes To twinkle in their spheres till they return. What if her eyes were there, they in her head? The brightness of her cheek would shame those stars, As daylight doth a lamp; her eyes in heaven Would through the airy region stream so bright That birds would sing and think it were not night. See how she leans her cheek upon her hand. O that I were a glove upon that hand, That I might touch that cheek. JULIET. Ay me. ROMEO. She speaks. O speak again bright angel, for thou art As glorious to this night, being o’er my head, As is a winged messenger of heaven Unto the white-upturned wondering eyes Of mortals that fall back to gaze on him When he bestrides the lazy-puffing clouds And sails upon the bosom of the air. JULIET. O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo? Deny thy father and refuse thy name. Or if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love, And I’ll no longer be a Capulet. ROMEO. [_Aside._] Shall I hear more, or shall I speak at this? JULIET. ’Tis but thy name that is my enemy; Thou art thyself, though not a Montague. What’s Montague? It is nor hand nor foot, Nor arm, nor face, nor any other part Belonging to a man. O be some other name. What’s in a name? That which we call a rose By any other name would smell as sweet; So Romeo would, were he not Romeo call’d, Retain that dear perfection which he owes Without that title. Romeo, doff thy name, And for thy name, which is no part of thee, Take all myself. ROMEO. I take thee at thy word. Call me but love, and I’ll be new baptis’d; Henceforth I never will be Romeo. JULIET. What man art thou that, thus bescreen’d in night So stumblest on my counsel? ROMEO. By a name I know not how to tell thee who I am: My name, dear saint, is hateful to myself, Because it is an enemy to thee. Had I it written, I would tear the word. JULIET. My ears have yet not drunk a hundred words Of thy tongue’s utterance, yet I know the sound.

BALTHAZAR. He hears us not, nor answers to our call. Love hath transformed him into some strange creature.
LUCIUS. Let him alone. Such men are better companions to their thoughts than to their friends.
BALTHAZAR. Yet I shall summon him once more. I conjure thee by Celia's golden hair, by her bright smile and gentle voice, by every grace that nature placed within her, appear before us and confess thy bondage.
LUCIUS. If he hears that, he'll curse thee for it.
BALTHAZAR. Not so. A lover is never angered by praise of his beloved. He would sooner quarrel with the wind than with the sound of her name.
LUCIUS. Look there. He has hidden himself beside the garden wall. The night suits his humour.
BALTHAZAR. Love delights in shadows. Yet if love cannot see, how comes it always to find trouble?
LUCIUS. Come, let us leave him. He seeks solitude, and we waste our breath.
BALTHAZAR. Agreed. Good night to our wandering friend.
Exeunt.
SCENE II. A Garden by Moonlight
Enter ADRIAN.
ADRIAN. They laugh at longing who have never suffered it.
A window opens above. CELIA appears.
What radiance breaks upon the darkness there?
The moon grows pale beside her.
She stands above the garden like a star
That has forgotten heaven and wandered here.
If beauty had a voice, it would be hers.
Yet she speaks not.
Her silence itself is music.
How softly she rests her hand upon her cheek.
Would that I were the breeze that touched her face.
CELIA. Alas.
ADRIAN. She speaks. Let every other sound be still.
CELIA. Why must affection choose such difficult paths?
Why should the one I love belong to those
Whom my family calls enemies?
Were he named differently,
Would he be less kind,
Less worthy,
Less himself?
A name is but a word.
The flower blooming in the garden
Would smell as sweet if called by any other title.
So too the man I love.
What power lies in a name
That it should divide two hearts?
ADRIAN. Aside.
Shall I remain silent, or reveal myself?
CELIA. If he would cast aside that hated name,
I would surrender all hesitation.
ADRIAN. Then hear me.
Call me what you will,
And I shall answer.
The name I bear matters little
If it displeases you.
CELIA. Who speaks from the darkness?
ADRIAN. One who fears his own name
Only because it troubles you.
Were it written before me,
I would gladly blot it out.
CELIA. Though I have heard but little,
The voice is known to me.
You are Adrian.
ADRIAN. And happier now than ever that I am.

BALTHAZAR. He neither hears nor stirs. Love has carried him so far from ordinary company that I doubt he remembers we exist.
LUCIUS. Then leave him to his dreaming.
BALTHAZAR. One call more. By Celia's bright eyes, by the sweetness of her smile, by every charm she possesses, I summon him forth.
LUCIUS. You'll earn his displeasure if he catches you.
BALTHAZAR. Hardly. Lovers forgive much when their lady's name is involved.
LUCIUS. There, among the vines. He's hidden himself away again.
BALTHAZAR. The night is a faithful friend to lovers.
LUCIUS. And an unfaithful one to common sense.
BALTHAZAR. Come. We shall find warmer beds than this garden path.
They leave.
Exeunt.
SCENE II. The Garden
Enter ADRIAN alone.
ADRIAN. Easy enough to mock a wound you've never felt.
A light appears at an upper window. CELIA steps forward.
What light is that?
The whole garden seems altered by it.
The moon, which a moment ago ruled the sky, now looks pale and unnecessary.
There she stands.
If those stars above exchanged places with her eyes, I think the heavens themselves would shine no brighter.
Even the birds might mistake this night for morning.
She leans upon her hand.
To be that hand's companion for a moment—what fortune that would be.
CELIA. Ah me.
ADRIAN. At last, her voice.
CELIA. Why should the person dearest to me carry a name that stands against my own house?
If he were called something else, would anything important change?
Would his heart be different?
His nature?
Names seem powerful, yet they are only sounds.
A rose would remain a rose regardless of what people chose to call it.
Its fragrance would not fade.
Neither would his worth.
ADRIAN. Aside.
Do I wait, or answer her?
CELIA. If he would abandon that single obstacle, I could give him everything.
ADRIAN. Then take me at my word.
Give me another name, and I shall wear it gladly.
CELIA. Who is there?
ADRIAN. A man who has grown tired of hearing his own name spoken as your enemy.
If I could erase it with a stroke, I would.
CELIA. I have not heard many words, yet I know that voice.
It is Adrian.
ADRIAN. Then I am discovered—and fortunate to be so.
They gaze at one another as the night grows quiet.

PISTOL. Die and be damn’d! and _fico_ for thy friendship! FLUELLEN. It is well. PISTOL. The fig of Spain. [_Exit._]  FLUELLEN. Very good. GOWER. Why, this is an arrant counterfeit rascal. I remember him now; a bawd, a cutpurse. FLUELLEN. I’ll assure you, ’a uttered as prave words at the pridge as you shall see in a summer’s day. But it is very well; what he has spoke to me, that is well, I warrant you, when time is serve. GOWER. Why, ’t is a gull, a fool, a rogue, that now and then goes to the wars, to grace himself at his return into London under the form of a soldier. And such fellows are perfect in the great commanders’ names; and they will learn you by rote where services were done; at such and such a sconce, at such a breach, at such a convoy; who came off bravely, who was shot, who disgrac’d, what terms the enemy stood on; and this they con perfectly in the phrase of war, which they trick up with new-tuned oaths: and what a beard of the general’s cut and a horrid suit of the camp will do among foaming bottles and ale-wash’d wits, is wonderful to be thought on. But you must learn to know such slanders of the age, or else you may be marvellously mistook. FLUELLEN. I tell you what, Captain Gower; I do perceive he is not the man that he would gladly make show to the world he is. If I find a hole in his coat, I will tell him my mind. [_Drum heard._] Hark you, the King is coming, and I must speak with him from the pridge. Drum and colours. Enter King Henry, Gloucester and his poor soldiers. God bless your Majesty! KING HENRY. How now, Fluellen! cam’st thou from the bridge? FLUELLEN. Ay, so please your Majesty. The Duke of Exeter has very gallantly maintain’d the pridge. The French is gone off, look you; and there is gallant and most prave passages. Marry, th’ athversary was have possession of the pridge; but he is enforced to retire, and the Duke of Exeter is master of the pridge. I can tell your Majesty, the Duke is a prave man. KING HENRY. What men have you lost, Fluellen? FLUELLEN. The perdition of the athversary hath been very great, reasonable great. Marry, for my part, I think the Duke hath lost never a man, but one that is like to be executed for robbing a church, one Bardolph, if your Majesty know the man. His face is all bubukles, and whelks, and knobs, and flames o’ fire; and his lips blows at his nose, and it is like a coal of fire, sometimes plue and sometimes red; but his nose is executed, and his fire’s out. KING HENRY. We would have all such offenders so cut off; and we give express charge, that in our marches through the country, there be nothing compell’d from the villages, nothing taken but paid for, none of the French upbraided or abused in disdainful language; for when lenity and cruelty play for a kingdom, the gentler gamester is the soonest winner. Tucket. Enter Montjoy. MONTJOY. You know me by my habit. KING HENRY. Well then I know thee. What shall I know of thee? MONTJOY. My master’s mind. KING HENRY. Unfold it. MONTJOY. Thus says my King: Say thou to Harry of England: Though we seem’d dead, we did but sleep; advantage is a better soldier than rashness. Tell him we could have rebuk’d him at Harfleur, but that we thought not good to bruise an injury till it were full ripe. Now we speak upon our cue, and our voice is imperial. England shall repent his folly, see his weakness, and admire our sufferance. Bid him therefore consider of his ransom; which must proportion the losses we have borne, the subjects we have lost, the disgrace we have digested; which in weight to re-answer, his pettishness would bow under. For our losses, his exchequer is too poor; for the effusion of our blood, the muster of his kingdom too faint a number; and for our disgrace, his own person, kneeling at our feet, but a weak and worthless satisfaction. To this add defiance; and tell him, for conclusion, he hath betrayed his followers, whose condemnation is pronounc’d. So far my King and master; so much my office. KING HENRY. What is thy name? I know thy quality. MONTJOY.

PISTOL.
Curse thee and all thy counsel! Take thy friendship and choke on it!
FLUELLEN.
That is spoken. It shall be remembered.
PISTOL.
A Spanish fig for thee and thy discipline!
[Exit.]
FLUELLEN.
Very well. He is gone in anger.
GOWER.
That fellow is no soldier, but a wanderer in borrowed armor. I know him now. A thief of words and a thief of coin both.
FLUELLEN.
He did speak stoutly enough upon the rampart, I will maintain that much. Yet words and deeds are not one thing, no.
GOWER.
He is one of those idle companions who haunt the wars only to adorn themselves with other men’s honor. They learn the names of captains as children learn songs, and later sing them at taverns as if they were present at the deeds.
FLUELLEN.
If I find him again, I shall examine his courage more closely. There may be cracks in his show of valor.
[Drums heard.]
GOWER.
The King approaches.
FLUELLEN.
From the bridge?
GOWER.
Aye.
Enter KING HENRY, GLOUCESTER, and Soldiers.
FLUELLEN.
God preserve your Majesty! The passage of the bridge is most nobly kept.
KING HENRY.
Speak, Fluellen. How stands the field?
FLUELLEN.
The enemy has withdrawn, your Majesty, with some disorder. Our men held firm. The Duke of Exeter behaved with great resolution, as any captain might desire.
KING HENRY.
What loss have we sustained?
FLUELLEN.
Very little in truth. A few wounded, none of principal account.
Though there is one matter, if it please your Majesty.
KING HENRY.
Proceed.
FLUELLEN.
A certain soldier, one Bardolph, was taken in theft against a church, and by the law of war he hath been executed.
KING HENRY.
So.
FLUELLEN.
He was a man of loud countenance, his face much inflamed, as if fire were lodged within it. Yet such outward appearance is no defense before justice.
KING HENRY.
Let it be known throughout the army: no plundering, no violence against the people of France. What is taken shall be paid for. Discipline shall be our strength, not disorder.
FLUELLEN.
It shall be done.
[Tucket sounded.]
Enter MONTJOY.
MONTJOY.
I come from my sovereign with message.
KING HENRY.
Speak freely. I know thy office.
MONTJOY.
Thus says the King of France: though fortune has not favored us in the field, the matter is not yet concluded. He bids you consider the losses you have sustained, and the long labor of your march, and to weigh whether further resistance serves your advantage.
He offers terms, though not generous ones, for the return of your forces.
KING HENRY.
Is this all?
MONTJOY.
There is more. He reminds you that your position grows weaker with time, and that endurance is not victory. He urges you to accept composition before greater ruin follows.
KING HENRY.
And what of his own condition?
MONTJOY.
He stands prepared for war, yet would prefer resolution by treaty.
KING HENRY.
Then return to him and say this: we are not yet diminished to the point he imagines. Our strength is measured not only in numbers, but in will.
Tell him England has not yet spent her breath. And though we bleed, we do not bend for threats.
As for his terms, we shall consider them in our own time, not his.
MONTJOY.
I shall carry your answer.
KING HENRY.
Go then.
[Exit MONTJOY.]
GLOUCESTER.
Your Majesty speaks firmly.
KING HENRY.
Necessity teaches the tongue its shape.
Come, let us see what the next hour brings. War does not pause for negotiation, nor does honor wait upon convenience.
[Exeunt.]

PISTOL.
Rot thee and all thy counsel! Keep thy friendship for fools.
FLUELLEN.
So be it. I have heard thee.
PISTOL.
A fig of Spain for thee and all thy discipline!
[Exit.]
FLUELLEN.
He is gone off in heat.
GOWER.
He is no soldier. I have seen such men before—vagabonds that creep into wars to clothe themselves in borrowed honor. I know him now well enough: a thief of reputation more than of coin.
FLUELLEN.
He spoke bravely enough upon the bridge, I will say that. Yet a brave tongue is not always a brave heart.
GOWER.
No. These fellows learn the names of captains as one learns a song, then sing it in alehouses afterward, as though they had stood where steel was crossing steel.
FLUELLEN.
If he returns my way, I shall look more closely at his courage. Words are easy things to wear.
[Drums within.]
GOWER.
The King comes on.
FLUELLEN.
From the bridge, is it?
GOWER.
Aye.
Enter KING HENRY, GLOUCESTER, and SOLDIERS.
FLUELLEN.
God save your Majesty. The bridge holds well.
KING HENRY.
Tell me then, Fluellen, how goes the field?
FLUELLEN.
In truth, sir, the enemy has drawn back somewhat disorderly. Our men stood firm. Exeter bore himself like a man born to command.
KING HENRY.
Losses?
FLUELLEN.
Not many. A few wounded, nothing that touches the chief strength of your army.
There is one matter I must report, though.
KING HENRY.
Speak it.
FLUELLEN.
A soldier named Bardolph was taken in theft against a church. He has been executed according to the law of war.
KING HENRY.
So.
FLUELLEN.
A man with a face all inflamed and broken-looking, as though fire had once lived in him and burned itself out.
KING HENRY.
Let it be proclaimed through the camp: no plunder, no violence against the townsfolk. All must be paid for. Discipline first, always.
FLUELLEN.
It shall be done.
[A tucket sounds.]
Enter MONTJOY.
MONTJOY.
I bring a message from the French King.
KING HENRY.
We know thee. Speak.
MONTJOY.
He says this: though fortune has gone against him in the field, the war is not yet settled. He bids you weigh your losses and consider whether further resistance is worth the cost.
He offers terms for your withdrawal and the return of your men.
KING HENRY.
And that is all?
MONTJOY.
He warns that your strength will diminish if you continue, and urges you to end matters before greater harm follows.
KING HENRY.
What does he think remains of us?
MONTJOY.
He believes you weakened, though still dangerous.
KING HENRY.
Then return and tell him he miscounts us. We are fewer perhaps, but not less resolved.
England has not yet spent itself, and what remains is not for bargaining under pressure.
Say to him also: we will answer his proposals in our own time.
MONTJOY.
I shall carry it.
KING HENRY.
Go.
[Exit MONTJOY.]
GLOUCESTER.
Your Majesty holds firm.
KING HENRY.
There is little choice in it. One either stands, or yields.
Come. The day is not finished yet, and neither is the work before us.
[Exeunt.]

VARRO’S SERVANT. Thou art not altogether a fool. FOOL. Nor thou altogether a wise man. As much foolery as I have, so much wit thou lack’st. APEMANTUS. That answer might have become Apemantus. VARRO’S SERVANT. Aside, aside, here comes Lord Timon. Enter Timon and Flavius. APEMANTUS. Come with me, fool, come. FOOL. I do not always follow lover, elder brother, and woman; sometime the philosopher. [_Exeunt Apemantus and Fool._]  FLAVIUS. Pray you walk near. I’ll speak with you anon. [_Exeunt Servants._]  TIMON. You make me marvel wherefore ere this time Had you not fully laid my state before me, That I might so have rated my expense As I had leave of means. FLAVIUS. You would not hear me, At many leisures I proposed. TIMON. Go to. Perchance some single vantages you took When my indisposition put you back, And that unaptness made your minister Thus to excuse yourself. FLAVIUS. O my good lord, At many times I brought in my accounts, Laid them before you; you would throw them off And say you found them in mine honesty. When for some trifling present you have bid me Return so much, I have shook my head and wept, Yea, ’gainst th’ authority of manners, prayed you To hold your hand more close. I did endure Not seldom nor no slight checks, when I have Prompted you in the ebb of your estate And your great flow of debts. My loved lord, Though you hear now, too late, yet now’s a time. The greatest of your having lacks a half To pay your present debts. TIMON. Let all my land be sold. FLAVIUS. ’Tis all engaged, some forfeited and gone, And what remains will hardly stop the mouth Of present dues; the future comes apace. What shall defend the interim? And at length How goes our reckoning? TIMON. To Lacedaemon did my land extend. FLAVIUS. O my good lord, the world is but a word; Were it all yours to give it in a breath, How quickly were it gone! TIMON. You tell me true. FLAVIUS. If you suspect my husbandry or falsehood, Call me before th’ exactest auditors And set me on the proof. So the gods bless me, When all our offices have been oppressed With riotous feeders, when our vaults have wept With drunken spilth of wine, when every room Hath blazed with lights and brayed with minstrelsy, I have retired me to a wasteful cock And set mine eyes at flow. TIMON. Prithee, no more. FLAVIUS. Heavens, have I said, the bounty of this lord! How many prodigal bits have slaves and peasants This night englutted? Who is not Timon’s? What heart, head, sword, force, means, but is Lord Timon’s? Great Timon, noble, worthy, royal Timon! Ah, when the means are gone that buy this praise, The breath is gone whereof this praise is made. Feast-won, fast-lost; one cloud of winter showers, These flies are couched. TIMON. Come, sermon me no further. No villainous bounty yet hath passed my heart; Unwisely, not ignobly, have I given. Why dost thou weep? Canst thou the conscience lack To think I shall lack friends? Secure thy heart. If I would broach the vessels of my love And try the argument of hearts by borrowing, Men and men’s fortunes could I frankly use As I can bid thee speak. FLAVIUS. Assurance bless your thoughts! TIMON. And in some sort these wants of mine are crowned, That I account them blessings. For by these Shall I try friends. You shall perceive how you Mistake my fortunes. I am wealthy in my friends. Within there! Flaminius! Servilius! Enter Flaminius, Servilius and a third Servant. SERVANTS. My lord, my lord. TIMON. I will dispatch you severally. [_To Servilius_.] You to Lord Lucius; [_To Flaminius_.] to Lord Lucullus you, I hunted with his honour today; [_To the third Servant_.] you to Sempronius. Commend me to their loves; and I am proud, say, that my occasions have found time to use ’em toward a supply of money. Let the request be fifty talents. FLAMINIUS. As you have said, my lord. [_Exeunt Servants._]  FLAVIUS. [_Aside_.] Lord Lucius and Lucullus? Humh! TIMON. Go you, sir, to the senators, Of whom, even to the state’s best health, I have Deserved this hearing, Bid ’em send o’ th’ instant A thousand talents to me. FLAVIUS. I have been bold— For that I knew it the most general way— To them to use your signet and your name, But they do shake their heads, and I am here No richer in return.

SERVANT TO AURELIUS.
You speak more wisely than you look.
FOOL.
And thou look’st wiser than thou speak’st, which is a harder deceit to maintain.
SERVANT.
You answer quickly for one held a fool.
FOOL.
Speed is the last refuge of those who have no estate in argument.
SERVANT.
Here comes Lord Aurelius.
Enter AURELIUS and MARCUS.
MARCUS.
Keep near him. I would speak with you apart.
FOOL.
Apart is the safest company.
SERVANT.
I had rather stand aside than be found standing wrong.
[Exeunt Servants.]
AURELIUS.
I marvel at it still. Why was my estate not set before me in full,
that I might measure my spending against my means?
I was made to believe the ground beneath me firmer than it proved.
MARCUS.
My lord, I spoke at several times, and in as plain a manner as I could devise.
AURELIUS.
You spoke, yes. Yet never at the right hour, or so it seemed to me then.
Some occasions found me unwilling, others found me distracted, and your counsel was set aside as if it were no more than noise at a feast.
MARCUS.
I brought accounts before you. I laid them open without disguise.
You would sometimes cast them back and say they were written to frighten you rather than to inform you.
When gifts were asked for, I hesitated, and you thought it refusal out of neglect. Yet I often begged you to hold your hand, to consider the drain upon your coffers.
AURELIUS.
And now you speak of coffers as if they still held breath.
MARCUS.
They do not, my lord. They are emptied. Some lands are already pledged, others lost outright. What remains can scarcely answer the debts that press upon the present hour.
AURELIUS.
Then let the remainder go also. I would not be haunted by fragments.
MARCUS.
It is already nearly nothing to sell.
AURELIUS.
Nothing? I was told I possessed abundance.
MARCUS.
Abundance that moved too freely is soon called memory rather than possession.
AURELIUS.
So I am poor without knowing the passage into poverty.
MARCUS.
Poverty is not a sudden arrival. It advances while a man is looking elsewhere.
AURELIUS.
And what would you have me do now? Defend the gap between what I am and what I thought myself to be?
MARCUS.
I would have you pause. Consider those to whom you owe. Consider also those who depend upon your house.
AURELIUS.
Pause. That word comes always too late.
MARCUS.
Better late than never spoken.
AURELIUS.
Yet spoken is not the same as answered.
MARCUS.
True.
Enter a MESSENGER.
MESSENGER.
My lord, the creditors press again. They demand settlement without delay.
AURELIUS.
Let them press. There is little left to yield.
MESSENGER.
They threaten suit.
AURELIUS.
Suit may be made of cloth or complaint; I possess neither in plenty now.
MESSENGER.
They ask for what is owed.
AURELIUS.
And I ask for what is possible.
[Exit MESSENGER.]
MARCUS.
The hour grows sharper.
AURELIUS.
Then let it sharpen me. I have been soft too long in my own judgement.
If I trusted too easily, it was not without reason. Men spoke fairly. Promises stood like pillars in my hearing. It is only now, when the roof leans, that I begin to question the strength of those supports.
MARCUS.
The roof has not fallen yet.
AURELIUS.
Not yet. But I hear it complain.
MARCUS.
There is still time to rebuild if you will allow it.
AURELIUS.
Rebuild from what? From absence?
MARCUS.
From what remains.
AURELIUS.
Very little remains.
MARCUS.
Enough to begin.
AURELIUS.
Then begin carefully. I would not lose even the act of beginning.
[Exeunt.]

SERVANT TO AURELIUS.
You speak more wisely than you seem.
FOOL.
And you seem wiser than you are. I call that an uneven trade.
SERVANT.
For a fool, you move quickly in reply.
FOOL.
Slowness is a luxury for those who can afford better arguments.
SERVANT.
Here comes Lord Aurelius.
Enter AURELIUS and MARCUS.
MARCUS.
Stay near him a while. I would have speech with you when I can.
FOOL.
I prefer standing near nothing at all. Less chance of error.
SERVANT.
And I prefer stepping aside before I am asked to stand in judgment.
[Exeunt Servants.]
AURELIUS.
I cannot put my mind to rest on this matter.
Why was my estate never set plainly before me? I might have governed my spending differently had I known the truth in time.
Instead I was led to believe I had more beneath me than I truly did.
MARCUS.
My lord, I spoke of it more than once. I set the accounts before you when there was still room to act upon them.
AURELIUS.
You did speak, yes. But not in the moment it might have mattered most. Or so it seemed then.
There were times I would not hear it, other times I put it aside, thinking it could wait.
MARCUS.
I brought you the reckoning itself. Not once, but many times. You dismissed it as caution or alarm, not fact.
When I urged restraint, you thought it resistance to your will.
AURELIUS.
And now restraint arrives too late to be of use.
MARCUS.
I am afraid so, my lord.
AURELIUS.
So the foundation was thinner than I was told.
MARCUS.
Thinner, or more strained than it appeared. Some lands are gone, others pledged. The rest can barely meet what is already owed.
AURELIUS.
Then there is no remainder worth speaking of.
MARCUS.
There is little that can be turned into coin.
AURELIUS.
Strange. I was thought to be rich.
MARCUS.
Riches that are spent freely often remain only in memory.
AURELIUS.
So I arrive at poverty without noticing the road.
MARCUS.
It rarely announces itself.
AURELIUS.
What would you have me do now?
MARCUS.
Face it as it stands. And consider those who depend upon you still.
AURELIUS.
Even now you ask me to pause.
MARCUS.
Because there is nothing gained by rushing further.
AURELIUS.
Pause has become a word that always arrives after damage is done.
MARCUS.
It is still the only word left that can change direction.
Enter MESSENGER.
MESSENGER.
My lord, the men to whom you owe money demand immediate payment.
AURELIUS.
Tell them I am in debt even to delay.
MESSENGER.
They speak of legal action.
AURELIUS.
Let them speak. Speech is cheaper than settlement.
MESSENGER.
They expect what is owed.
AURELIUS.
And I expect what I no longer have.
[Exit MESSENGER.]
AURELIUS.
So it narrows to this.
I did not think myself careless, only fortunate. Men around me spoke as if fortune were stable, as if wealth stood still once gained.
It did not.
MARCUS.
Nothing does.
AURELIUS.
No, I see that now. Too clearly.
A silence.
If I was misled, it was not by silence but by comfort. Everyone spoke well while there was something to gain by it.
MARCUS.
There is still time to repair part of it.
AURELIUS.
Repair with what? The structure is already half gone.
MARCUS.
With what remains. Small things can still hold weight if placed carefully.
AURELIUS.
Then we begin with what is left, however small it is.
MARCUS.
That is all I ask.
AURELIUS.
Begin, then. And be careful how you set each stone.
[Exeunt.]

This is the law, and this Duke Humphrey’s doom. SOMERSET. I humbly thank your royal Majesty. HORNER. And I accept the combat willingly. PETER. Alas, my lord, I cannot fight; for God’s sake, pity my case! The spite of man prevaileth against me. O Lord, have mercy upon me! I shall never be able to fight a blow. O Lord, my heart! GLOUCESTER. Sirrah, or you must fight or else be hanged. KING HENRY. Away with them to prison; and the day Of combat shall be the last of the next month. Come, Somerset, we’ll see thee sent away. [_Flourish. Exeunt._]  SCENE IV. Gloucester’s Garden  Enter the Witch Margery Jourdain, the two Priests, Hume, Southwell and Bolingbroke. HUME. Come, my masters. The duchess, I tell you, expects performance of your promises. BOLINGBROKE. Master Hume, we are therefore provided. Will her ladyship behold and hear our exorcisms? HUME. Ay, what else? Fear you not her courage. BOLINGBROKE. I have heard her reported to be a woman of an invincible spirit. But it shall be convenient, Master Hume, that you be by her aloft while we be busy below; and so, I pray you go, in God’s name, and leave us. [_Exit Hume._]  Mother Jourdain, be you prostrate and grovel on the earth. John Southwell, read you; and let us to our work. Enter Duchess aloft, Hume following. ELEANOR. Well said, my masters; and welcome all. To this gear, the sooner the better. BOLINGBROKE. Patience, good lady; wizards know their times. Deep night, dark night, the silent of the night, The time of night when Troy was set on fire, The time when screech-owls cry and ban-dogs howl, And spirits walk and ghosts break up their graves; That time best fits the work we have in hand. Madam, sit you and fear not. Whom we raise We will make fast within a hallowed verge. [_Here they do the ceremonies belonging, and make the circle; Bolingbroke or Southwell reads_ “Conjuro te”, _etc. It thunders and lightens terribly; then the Spirit riseth._]  SPIRIT. _Adsum_. M. JOURDAIN. Asnath, By the eternal God, whose name and power Thou tremblest at, answer that I shall ask; For till thou speak thou shalt not pass from hence. SPIRIT. Ask what thou wilt. That I had said and done! BOLINGBROKE. [_Reads_.] _First of the King: what shall of him become?_  SPIRIT. The duke yet lives that Henry shall depose, But him outlive and die a violent death. [_As the Spirit speaks, Southwell writes the answer._]  BOLINGBROKE. [_Reads_.] _What fates await the Duke of Suffolk?_  SPIRIT. By water shall he die and take his end. BOLINGBROKE. [_Reads_.] _What shall befall the Duke of Somerset?_  SPIRIT. Let him shun castles. Safer shall he be upon the sandy plains Than where castles mounted stand. Have done, for more I hardly can endure. BOLINGBROKE. Descend to darkness and the burning lake! False fiend, avoid! [_Thunder and lightning. Exit Spirit._]  Enter the Duke of York and the Duke of Buckingham with their Guard, and Sir Humphrey Stafford, and break in. YORK. Lay hands upon these traitors and their trash. Beldam, I think we watched you at an inch. What, madam, are you there? The King and commonweal Are deeply indebted for this piece of pains. My Lord Protector will, I doubt it not, See you well guerdoned for these good deserts. ELEANOR. Not half so bad as thine to England’s king, Injurious duke, that threatest where’s no cause. BUCKINGHAM. True, madam, none at all. What call you this? Away with them! Let them be clapped up close And kept asunder.—You, madam, shall with us.— Stafford, take her to thee. [_Exit Stafford._]  [_Exeunt above, Duchess and Hume, guarded._]  We’ll see your trinkets here all forthcoming. All, away! [_Exeunt guard with  Jourdain, Southwell, Bolingbroke, etc._]  YORK. Lord Buckingham, methinks you watched her well. A pretty plot, well chosen to build upon! Now, pray, my lord, let’s see the devil’s writ. What have we here? [_Reads_.] _The duke yet lives that Henry shall depose. But him outlive and die a violent death._ Why, this is just _Aio te, Aeacida, Romanos vincere posse._ Well, to the rest: _Tell me what fate awaits the Duke of Suffolk? By water shall he die and take his end. What shall betide the Duke of Somerset? Let him shun castles; Safer shall he be upon the sandy plains Than where castles mounted stand._ Come, come, my lords, these oracles Are hardly attained, and hardly understood.

SCENE I. A court of judgement.
HERALD.
By sentence of the realm it is determined: the matter stands concluded, and no appeal shall alter it.
SOMERSET.
I accept the King’s grace and judgment.
HORNER.
And I accept the trial appointed me, without reluctance.
PETER.
Alas, my lord, I am no fighter. I cannot stand in combat. Have pity upon me, for I am undone before I begin. My limbs fail me, and my heart will not serve me. O Lord, deliver me!
GLOUCESTER.
You shall either fight or suffer the law.
KING HENRY.
Take them hence to prison. The day appointed for combat shall fall upon the last day of next month.
Come, Somerset, you shall be conveyed away.
[Flourish. Exeunt.]

SCENE II. A garden within Gloucester’s house. Night.
Enter MARGERY, HUME, SOUTHWELL, and BOLINGBROKE.
HUME.
The duchess awaits what was promised. There must be no delay.
BOLINGBROKE.
All instruments are prepared. Nothing is lacking.
HUME.
She will indeed be present to witness this work?
BOLINGBROKE.
She will. Though you need not fear her. Her courage is spoken of everywhere.
HUME.
Then I shall leave you to it. I will not be seen when the matter begins.
[Exit Hume.]
BOLINGBROKE.
Mother Jourdain, lie close and attend the ground. Southwell, begin your reading when all is set.
JOURDAIN.
Proceed, my masters. I have long desired this hour.
SOUTHWELL.
The time is suitable. Night itself lends authority to such operations.
BOLINGBROKE.
Set the circle. Make it firm. Let nothing pass its bounds.
[The Duchess appears above; Hume follows unseen.]
DUCHESS.
You are bold men to call upon powers you do not understand.
SOUTHWELL.
Stand ready.
[Ceremonies performed. Thunder.]
SPIRIT.
I am here. Speak.
JOURDAIN.
By the power we invoke, answer truly what is demanded of thee.
SPIRIT.
Ask.
BOLINGBROKE.
What becomes of the King?
SPIRIT.
He shall be cast down by one yet living, and shall not long survive his fall.
SOUTHWELL.
And Suffolk?
SPIRIT.
He shall perish upon the water.
BOLINGBROKE.
And Somerset?
SPIRIT.
He shall find safety in no stronghold; open ground shall serve him better than walls.
SOUTHWELL.
Enough. The answers grow dark.
DUCHESS.
No, I will hear more.
SPIRIT.
No more is permitted.
[Thunder. The spirit vanishes.]
HUME.
We are observed. This place is no longer secure.
BOLINGBROKE.
Break off the rite. Away.

SCENE III. The same garden. Enter YORK and BUCKINGHAM with guards.
YORK.
Seize them all without exception.
BUCKINGHAM.
So this was the practice—hidden arts within the court itself.
YORK.
Madam, you are taken in the very act.
DUCHESS.
You call it act. I call it inquiry.
BUCKINGHAM.
Enough. Remove them.
YORK.
Search the ground thoroughly. Leave nothing concealed.
[They search and gather writings.]
BUCKINGHAM.
Here are papers.
YORK.
Read them.
BUCKINGHAM.
Prophecies concerning the crown… and the downfall of the King.
YORK.
So the design is plain enough.
DUCHESS.
You read what you wish to find.
YORK.
We read what is written.
BUCKINGHAM.
Take them hence.
[Exeunt guards with prisoners.]
YORK.
This night has uncovered more than was expected.
BUCKINGHAM.
And yet less than is certain. These writings speak, but not clearly.
YORK.
Even so, they are dangerous in any hand.
BUCKINGHAM.
Especially when men begin to believe them.
YORK.
Come. We must report this to the King.
[Exeunt.]

SCENE I. A hall of judgement.
HERALD.
The matter is resolved by royal decree. No further appeal shall be heard.
SOMERSET.
I accept the King’s sentence.
HORNER.
And I accept the combat laid upon me.
PETER.
My lord, I cannot fight. I am no man for such work. Have mercy, I beg you. My strength fails me before I even begin. I am undone by fear alone.
GLOUCESTER.
Fight, or answer with your life.
KING HENRY.
To prison with them. The day of combat shall fall upon the last day of the coming month.
Somerset, you shall be removed from hence.
[Flourish. Exeunt.]

SCENE II. A garden within Gloucester’s house. Night.
Enter MARGERY, HUME, SOUTHWELL, and BOLINGBROKE.
HUME.
The duchess waits. There is no turning back from what was promised.
BOLINGBROKE.
Everything is prepared. The work may proceed at once.
HUME.
She will be present to witness it?
BOLINGBROKE.
She will. Though I see no cause for doubt in her resolve.
HUME.
Then I will not remain. I prefer to be elsewhere when these matters begin.
[Exit Hume.]
BOLINGBROKE.
Mother Jourdain, take your place. Southwell, read when the circle is set.
JOURDAIN.
Do not delay on my account. I am ready.
SOUTHWELL.
Night itself seems made for such proceedings.
BOLINGBROKE.
Set the circle close. Nothing must pass its bounds.
[The Duchess appears above; Hume follows unseen.]
DUCHESS.
So this is the work you hide yourselves for.
SOUTHWELL.
Be steady. Begin.
[Ceremonies performed. Thunder.]
SPIRIT.
I am here. Speak.
JOURDAIN.
By the power invoked, you will answer truthfully.
SPIRIT.
Ask.
BOLINGBROKE.
What becomes of the King?
SPIRIT.
He shall be overthrown by one who yet lives, and shall not long remain after.
SOUTHWELL.
And Suffolk?
SPIRIT.
He dies upon the water.
BOLINGBROKE.
And Somerset?
SPIRIT.
He shall trust no fortress. The open field is safer for him than stone walls.
SOUTHWELL.
That is enough.
DUCHESS.
I will hear more.
SPIRIT.
No more is permitted.
[Thunder. The spirit disappears.]
HUME.
We are known. This place is no longer secure.
BOLINGBROKE.
End it. Now.

SCENE III. The same garden. Enter YORK and BUCKINGHAM with guards.
YORK.
Seize them.
BUCKINGHAM.
So this is the practice—hidden dealings within the court.
YORK.
Madam, you are taken in plain discovery.
DUCHESS.
You call it discovery. I call it inquiry.
BUCKINGHAM.
Take them away.
YORK.
Search every corner. Nothing is to be left behind.
[They gather writings.]
BUCKINGHAM.
These papers here.
YORK.
Read them.
BUCKINGHAM.
Prophecies touching the crown… and the King’s fall.
YORK.
Then the intent is clear enough.
DUCHESS.
Clear only to those who choose their reading.
YORK.
We read what stands before us.
BUCKINGHAM.
Remove them.
[Exeunt guards with prisoners.]
YORK.
This night has drawn much into the open.
BUCKINGHAM.
And still leaves much uncertain. The words are dangerous, though unclear.
YORK.
Dangerous enough.
BUCKINGHAM.
Especially where belief begins to follow them.
YORK.
Come then. We must lay this before the King.
[Exeunt.]

Hadst thou beheld—  MARTIUS. Pray now, no more. My mother, Who has a charter to extol her blood, When she does praise me grieves me. I have done As you have done—that’s what I can; Induced as you have been—that’s for my country. He that has but effected his good will Hath overta’en mine act. COMINIUS. You shall not be The grave of your deserving. Rome must know The value of her own. ’Twere a concealment Worse than a theft, no less than a traducement, To hide your doings and to silence that Which, to the spire and top of praises vouched, Would seem but modest. Therefore, I beseech you— In sign of what you are, not to reward What you have done—before our army hear me. MARTIUS. I have some wounds upon me, and they smart To hear themselves remembered. COMINIUS. Should they not, Well might they fester ’gainst ingratitude And tent themselves with death. Of all the horses— Whereof we have ta’en good and good store—of all The treasure in this field achieved and city, We render you the tenth, to be ta’en forth Before the common distribution At your only choice. MARTIUS. I thank you, general, But cannot make my heart consent to take A bribe to pay my sword. I do refuse it; And stand upon my common part with those That have beheld the doing. [_A long flourish. They all cry “Martius, Martius!” and cast up their caps and lances. Cominius and Lartius stand bare._]  May these same instruments which, you profane, Never sound more! When drums and trumpets shall I’ th’ field prove flatterers, let courts and cities be Made all of false-faced soothing! When steel grows soft Soft as the parasite’s silk, let him be made An ovator for the wars! No more, I say. For that I have not washed my nose that bled, Or foiled some debile wretch—which, without note, Here’s many else have done—you shout me forth In acclamations hyperbolical, As if I loved my little should be dieted In praises sauced with lies. COMINIUS. Too modest are you, More cruel to your good report than grateful To us that give you truly. By your patience, If ’gainst yourself you be incensed, we’ll put you, Like one that means his proper harm, in manacles, Then reason safely with you. Therefore be it known, As to us to all the world, that Caius Martius Wears this war’s garland, in token of the which My noble steed, known to the camp, I give him, With all his trim belonging. And from this time, For what he did before Corioles, call him, With all th’ applause and clamour of the host, Caius Martius Coriolanus! Bear Th’ addition nobly ever! [_Flourish. Trumpets sound, and drums._]  ALL. Caius Martius Coriolanus! CORIOLANUS. I will go wash; And when my face is fair, you shall perceive Whether I blush or no. Howbeit, I thank you. I mean to stride your steed and at all times To undercrest your good addition To th’ fairness of my power. COMINIUS. So, to our tent, Where, ere we do repose us, we will write To Rome of our success.—You, Titus Lartius, Must to Corioles back. Send us to Rome The best, with whom we may articulate For their own good and ours. LARTIUS. I shall, my lord. CORIOLANUS. The gods begin to mock me. I, that now Refused most princely gifts, am bound to beg Of my lord general. COMINIUS. Take’t, ’tis yours. What is’t? CORIOLANUS. I sometime lay here in Corioles At a poor man’s house; he used me kindly. He cried to me; I saw him prisoner; But then Aufidius was within my view, And wrath o’erwhelmed my pity. I request you To give my poor host freedom. COMINIUS. O, well begged! Were he the butcher of my son, he should Be free as is the wind.—Deliver him, Titus. LARTIUS. Martius, his name? CORIOLANUS. By Jupiter, forgot! I am weary; yea, my memory is tired. Have we no wine here? COMINIUS. Go we to our tent. The blood upon your visage dries; ’tis time It should be looked to. Come. [_A flourish of cornets. Exeunt._]  SCENE X. The camp of the Volsces  A flourish. Cornets. Enter Tullus Aufidius, bloody, with two or three soldiers. AUFIDIUS. The town is ta’en. SOLDIER. ’Twill be delivered back on good condition. AUFIDIUS. Condition? I would I were a Roman, for I cannot, Being a Volsce, be that I am.

GENERAL. Had you but seen him in the breach—
VALERIUS. I pray you, speak no further.
My father, when he praises me,
Makes me ashamed of what little I have done.
I acted as necessity required,
No more than any loyal man should do.
He whose intentions served the state as well
Deserves as much remembrance as my deeds.
GENERAL. You wrong yourself.
The kingdom must be told who won this day.
To bury merit is a kind of theft,
And silence may dishonour noble actions
As surely as false witness.
Therefore permit me,
Not as reward but recognition,
To speak before the army.
VALERIUS. My wounds ache enough already.
They need no herald to remind them why.
GENERAL. Then let them ache with gratitude instead.
Of all the spoils recovered in this campaign,
The horses, gold, and treasures taken here,
The tenth part shall be yours to choose before
The common distribution.
VALERIUS. My thanks, my lord,
But I cannot persuade myself to profit
From service freely given.
My sword was never hired.
I claim no larger share than any soldier
Who stood beside me in the fight.
A flourish. The soldiers cheer and cry, “Valerius! Valerius!”
VALERIUS. Peace, I beg you!
If trumpets praise where duty only acted,
Then honour loses value.
Because I bled,
Because I happened to stand nearest danger,
You raise me higher than I deserve.
Many brave men have done the same today
Without these loud acclamations.
GENERAL. Your modesty does greater injury
To your reputation than our praise can mend.
Yet whether you approve or not,
The army shall know what it owes you.
Before all gathered here,
I grant you this renowned warhorse,
Whose service is known throughout the camp.
And from this day forward,
For your defence of Blackstone Gate,
You shall be called Valerius Blackstone.
Trumpets and drums.
ALL. Valerius Blackstone!
BLACKSTONE. I thank you.
When next I ride that noble horse,
I shall endeavour to deserve the name
You place upon me.
GENERAL. Come, let us retire.
Before we rest, dispatches must be sent
To tell the capital of our success.
CAPTAIN. They shall be written at once.
BLACKSTONE. The gods mock me still.
I refused rich gifts,
Yet now I find myself compelled to ask one favour.
GENERAL. Speak freely.
BLACKSTONE. During the siege,
I lodged within a poor citizen's house.
The man showed kindness when I had little comfort.
Today I saw him taken prisoner,
Yet battle drew my eye elsewhere.
I ask his freedom.
GENERAL. Gladly granted.
Were he my enemy's closest ally,
Your request would win him liberty.
CAPTAIN. What is the prisoner's name?
BLACKSTONE. Strange.
The exhaustion of war has robbed me of it.
My memory fails me.
GENERAL. Then rest first.
Your face is caked with blood and dust.
Come.
Exeunt.
SCENE X. The Enemy Camp
Flourish. Enter ARDEN, wounded, with soldiers.
ARDEN. The fortress is lost.
SOLDIER. Terms are being discussed. It may yet be surrendered back under agreement.
ARDEN. Agreement?
Would that I belonged to the victors.
For being what I am,
I cannot be content with what remains.

GENERAL. If you had witnessed what he accomplished—
VALERIUS. No more of that, I ask you.
When my own family praises me,
