"How should I know. I paid two copecks to the boy who brought it, out of my own pocket. You'll give it back?"
"Bring it here, for heaven's sake — bring it at once!" cried Volkov, suddenly agitated. "Bring it now!"
A moment later she placed it in his hands. He saw at once what it was: a letter from his sister, from the town of K——. He felt the colour leave his face. It had been a very long time. But beyond the simple shock of it, something else moved in him too, something he could not immediately name.
"Fedosya, go — please — here are your two copecks, but go, go quickly, please."
The letter trembled in his fingers. He could not bring himself to open it while she was still in the room; he needed to be entirely alone with it. When at last her shuffling steps had faded down the corridor, he raised the letter to his lips and held it there for a moment. Then he turned it over and studied the address — the careful, upright hand of his sister, each letter formed with that particular deliberateness he had known since childhood. He hesitated. Some obscure dread seemed to hold him back. Then he broke the seal.
It was a substantial letter, three sheets covered on both sides in a close and careful hand.
"My dear Aleksei," his sister had written, "it is nearly three months since I last wrote to you, and I know that must have caused you worry, though I hope you will not think too harshly of me for my silence once you have read everything I have to tell you. You know I think of you constantly. You and mother are everything to me — my one certainty in all this uncertainty.
"I was very sorry to learn, through mother's last letter to me, that you have had to abandon your position at the office and that the private tutoring has also come to nothing. I cannot tell you how it grieved me. My own situation here has not permitted me to send you anything, though God knows I have wished to. I earn thirty roubles a year in this household, of which I was advanced twenty when I first arrived, and as you will understand, I have been paying that back a little each month and am only now nearly clear of it. The Starovoitovs are not unkind people, on the whole, but they are careful with money as only provincial merchants know how to be, and there is little room to ask for more than what was agreed.
"But now, dear Aleksei, I have something to tell you which I hope will be, at least in part, good news. I am not to remain here much longer. The arrangement is coming to an end at Michaelmas, and I find I am not sorry for it. There are things I have not told you — things I did not want to put into a letter, not knowing whose hands it might pass through before reaching you — but I will tell you everything now, plainly and in order, so that you may understand what the past months have truly been like and why I kept silent about them for so long.
"When you wrote in the spring asking whether all was well, and saying you had heard certain things from Petrov — I scarcely knew how to answer you. Had I told you the truth, I am quite sure you would have done something rash. I know your nature, Alyosha. You would not have stayed where you were. And what would have become of your own situation then? I could not be the cause of that. So I told you only that things were tolerable, which was true enough, up to a point.
"But now I can tell you everything."

OTHELLO. I gave her such a one, ’twas my first gift. IAGO. I know not that: but such a handkerchief (I am sure it was your wife’s) did I today See Cassio wipe his beard with. OTHELLO. If it be that,—  IAGO. If it be that, or any that was hers, It speaks against her with the other proofs. OTHELLO. O, that the slave had forty thousand lives! One is too poor, too weak for my revenge! Now do I see ’tis true. Look here, Iago; All my fond love thus do I blow to heaven. ’Tis gone. Arise, black vengeance, from thy hollow hell! Yield up, O love, thy crown and hearted throne To tyrannous hate! Swell, bosom, with thy fraught, For ’tis of aspics’ tongues! IAGO. Yet be content. OTHELLO. O, blood, Iago, blood! IAGO. Patience, I say. Your mind perhaps may change. OTHELLO. Never, Iago. Like to the Pontic Sea, Whose icy current and compulsive course Ne’er feels retiring ebb, but keeps due on To the Propontic and the Hellespont; Even so my bloody thoughts, with violent pace Shall ne’er look back, ne’er ebb to humble love, Till that a capable and wide revenge Swallow them up. Now by yond marble heaven, In the due reverence of a sacred vow [_Kneels._] I here engage my words. IAGO. Do not rise yet. [_Kneels._] Witness, you ever-burning lights above, You elements that clip us round about, Witness that here Iago doth give up The execution of his wit, hands, heart, To wrong’d Othello’s service! Let him command, And to obey shall be in me remorse, What bloody business ever. [_They rise._]  OTHELLO. I greet thy love, Not with vain thanks, but with acceptance bounteous, And will upon the instant put thee to ’t. Within these three days let me hear thee say That Cassio’s not alive. IAGO. My friend is dead. ’Tis done at your request. But let her live. OTHELLO. Damn her, lewd minx! O, damn her, damn her! Come, go with me apart, I will withdraw To furnish me with some swift means of death For the fair devil. Now art thou my lieutenant. IAGO. I am your own for ever. [_Exeunt._]  SCENE IV. Cyprus. Before the Castle. Enter Desdemona, Emilia and Clown. DESDEMONA. Do you know, sirrah, where Lieutenant Cassio lies? CLOWN. I dare not say he lies anywhere. DESDEMONA. Why, man? CLOWN. He’s a soldier; and for one to say a soldier lies is stabbing. DESDEMONA. Go to. Where lodges he? CLOWN. To tell you where he lodges is to tell you where I lie. DESDEMONA. Can anything be made of this? CLOWN. I know not where he lodges; and for me to devise a lodging, and say he lies here, or he lies there, were to lie in mine own throat. DESDEMONA. Can you inquire him out, and be edified by report? CLOWN. I will catechize the world for him, that is, make questions and by them answer. DESDEMONA. Seek him, bid him come hither. Tell him I have moved my lord on his behalf, and hope all will be well. CLOWN. To do this is within the compass of man’s wit, and therefore I will attempt the doing it. [_Exit._]  DESDEMONA. Where should I lose that handkerchief, Emilia? EMILIA. I know not, madam. DESDEMONA. Believe me, I had rather have lost my purse Full of crusadoes. And but my noble Moor Is true of mind and made of no such baseness As jealous creatures are, it were enough To put him to ill thinking. EMILIA. Is he not jealous? DESDEMONA. Who, he? I think the sun where he was born Drew all such humours from him. EMILIA. Look, where he comes. Enter Othello. DESDEMONA. I will not leave him now till Cassio Be call’d to him. How is’t with you, my lord? OTHELLO. Well, my good lady. [_Aside._] O, hardness to dissemble! How do you, Desdemona? DESDEMONA. Well, my good lord. OTHELLO. Give me your hand. This hand is moist, my lady. DESDEMONA. It yet hath felt no age nor known no sorrow. OTHELLO. This argues fruitfulness and liberal heart. Hot, hot, and moist. This hand of yours requires A sequester from liberty, fasting and prayer, Much castigation, exercise devout; For here’s a young and sweating devil here That commonly rebels. ’Tis a good hand, A frank one. DESDEMONA. You may indeed say so, For ’twas that hand that gave away my heart. OTHELLO. A liberal hand. The hearts of old gave hands, But our new heraldry is hands, not hearts.

LEONTES. I gave her that ring with my own hand — it was the first token I ever placed on her finger.
CAMILLO. I cannot speak to that; but such a ring — and I am certain it was hers — I saw upon Polixenes this very morning, worn openly on his hand.
LEONTES. If it be that—
CAMILLO. If it be that, or any jewel once belonging to her, it joins with all the rest against her.
LEONTES. O, that the traitor had a thousand lives to spend! One death is nothing — nothing to appease what burns in me! Now is the veil torn from it. Mark me, Camillo — all my tender love I fling from me, up to the cold air. It is gone. Rise up, black hatred, from thy sunless pit! Love, yield thy place, thy throne within my breast, to iron revenge! Fill, heart, fill — for the poison is in thee now, and it is of the deadliest sort.
CAMILLO. Yet hold yourself in patience.
LEONTES. No patience — there is blood in this, Camillo, blood!
CAMILLO. I urge you — wait. Your mind may alter when the heat has passed.
LEONTES. It shall not alter. As the great northern river in the depths of winter drives its frozen current without pause or turning, heedless of the rocks and narrow straits, pressing forward to the open sea — so do my thoughts run now with that same cold compulsion, forward, always forward, and shall not retreat to the soft ground of love until a full and sweeping vengeance has consumed them utterly. Now, by this silent sky above me, I bind myself with sacred words. [Kneels.]
CAMILLO. Do not yet rise. [Kneels.] Hear it, you watching stars, you vast and encompassing heavens — hear that here Camillo pledges all his cunning, all his hands, all the loyalty of his heart, to the service of a wronged king. Command me, and to obey shall be my only purpose, be the work never so dark or terrible. [They rise.]
LEONTES. I receive thy pledge not with empty gratitude but with full and earnest acceptance, and will set thee to work this very hour. Before three days are out, let me hear that Polixenes breathes no more.
CAMILLO. The man is as good as dead — say only the word. But the woman — let her live.
LEONTES. Curse her — false, corrupted creature! Curse her! Come, walk with me, and I will find the means to deal swiftly with the fair deceiver. From this hour thou art my right hand.
CAMILLO. And thine I am, now and beyond all ending. [Exeunt.]

SCENE III. Sicily. The outer courtyard of the palace. Enter Hermione, Paulina, and a Servant.
HERMIONE. Sirrah, do you know where Lord Archidamus lodges at present?
SERVANT. I dare not say he lodges anywhere comfortable, my lady.
HERMIONE. Why not?
SERVANT. A man in his position, my lady — to say precisely where he lies at any hour would be to lay oneself open to contradiction, and contradiction is a quarrelsome bedfellow.
HERMIONE. Enough of that. Where does he keep his rooms?
SERVANT. To tell you where he keeps his rooms, I must needs know where he keeps himself, and that, my lady, is a thing that shifts with the wind.
HERMIONE. Can you find nothing useful to say?
SERVANT. I can say this with confidence: he is somewhere within the walls of Sicily, and that narrows it considerably.
HERMIONE. Go find him. Tell him I have spoken in his favour and that he should come to me directly.
SERVANT. That much lies within the compass of my legs, if not my wit, and I will set about it. [Exit.]
HERMIONE. Paulina, I cannot think where I set down that brooch.
PAULINA. I could not say, madam.
HERMIONE. I would sooner have lost a purse of gold. And yet — were my lord other than he is, were there any shadow of suspicion in his nature, so small a thing might be enough to kindle it. But he is not made of that stuff.
PAULINA. Is he entirely free of jealousy, then?
HERMIONE. Him? The very climate that formed him burned all such weakness out. He is not capable of it.
PAULINA. See — he comes this way.
Enter Leontes.
HERMIONE. I shall not stir from here until I have secured him on Archidamus's behalf. My lord, how do you do?
LEONTES. Well enough, my lady. [Aside.] God, what torment it is to wear a pleasant face. — And you, Hermione?
HERMIONE. Well, my lord, I thank you.
LEONTES. Give me your hand. [Takes it.] It is warm.
HERMIONE. It has known neither age nor grief enough to cool it.
LEONTES. Warm hands speak of a warm and open nature. Very warm. This hand of yours, Hermione, stands in some need of discipline — of fasting, of cool reflection, of the curb of devotion. There is a spirit in it that does not easily submit.
HERMIONE. You may say so if you will. It was that same hand, after all, that gave you my heart.
LEONTES. A generous hand indeed. Once, a heart given was a heart kept. Now it seems the hand moves faster than the heart can follow.

LEONTES. I placed that ring on her finger myself — it was the first thing I ever gave her.
CAMILLO. I cannot speak to that. But such a ring — and I am certain it was hers — I saw on Polixenes this very morning, on his hand, worn openly.
LEONTES. If it be that—
CAMILLO. If it be that ring, or any jewel once belonging to her, it stands against her with the rest.
LEONTES. O, that the traitor had a thousand lives! One is not near enough — nothing near enough for what I feel. Now I see it plain. Mark me, Camillo — all my love, all of it, I throw from me into the empty air. Gone. Rise up, black hatred, from whatever pit holds thee. Love, yield thy throne within my breast to something harder. Fill, heart — fill with it — the poison is in, and it is of the bitterest kind.
CAMILLO. Yet hold — patience—
LEONTES. No patience. There is blood in this, Camillo.
CAMILLO. I beg you — wait. Your mind may cool.
LEONTES. It will not cool. As the great northern river in the grip of winter drives its current without pause or turning, pressing through narrows and past rocks to reach the open sea — so my thoughts move now with that same blind force and will not turn back toward love, will not soften or retreat, until a full and final vengeance has swallowed everything. Now, by the sky above me, I bind myself with a vow. [Kneels.]
CAMILLO. Do not rise yet. [Kneels.] Hear it, you fixed and watching stars — hear that here Camillo pledges all his cunning, all his hands, all his heart, to the service of a wronged king. Let him command, and obedience shall be my whole purpose, be the work never so dark. [They rise.]
LEONTES. I receive thy bond not with hollow thanks but with full acceptance, and will put thee to it this very hour. Within three days, tell me Polixenes lives no more.
CAMILLO. The man is as good as dead — say only the word. But let her live.
LEONTES. Damn her — false, corrupted thing! Damn her! Come, walk with me, and I'll find swift means to deal with this fair deceiver. From this hour you are my right hand.
CAMILLO. And yours I am, now and for ever. [Exeunt.]

SCENE III. Sicily. The outer courtyard of the palace. Enter Hermione, Paulina, and a Servant.
HERMIONE. Sirrah, do you know where Lord Archidamus is lodged?
SERVANT. I dare not say he lies anywhere, my lady.
HERMIONE. Why not?
SERVANT. A man of his standing — to say precisely where he lies at any given hour is to court contradiction, and contradiction is a quarrelsome companion.
HERMIONE. Never mind that. Where does he keep his rooms?
SERVANT. To tell you where he keeps his rooms, I must know where he keeps himself, and that shifts rather freely, my lady.
HERMIONE. Is there nothing useful in you?
SERVANT. This much I'll swear to: he is somewhere within the walls of Sicily, which does narrow the field considerably.
HERMIONE. Go and find him. Tell him I have spoken for him and that he should come to me at once.
SERVANT. That lies well within the reach of my legs, if not my learning, and I'll set about it directly. [Exit.]
HERMIONE. Paulina, I cannot think where I have put that brooch.
PAULINA. I couldn't say, madam.
HERMIONE. I would sooner have lost a purse full of gold. And yet — were my lord other than he is, were there any shadow of suspicion in his nature — a thing so small as this might be enough to kindle it. But he is not built that way.
PAULINA. He is not jealous, then? Not at all?
HERMIONE. Him? The very country that shaped him burned all such weakness out long ago.
PAULINA. Look — he comes.
Enter Leontes.
HERMIONE. I'll not leave him until I have spoken for Archidamus. My lord, how are you?
LEONTES. Well enough, my lady. [Aside.] God, what agony it is to wear this face. — And you, Hermione?
HERMIONE. Very well, my lord.
LEONTES. Give me your hand. It is warm.
HERMIONE. It has not yet known enough age or sorrow to make it otherwise.
LEONTES. Warm hands speak of a warm nature. Very warm. This hand of yours, I think, wants discipline — fasting, cool reflection, the check of prayer and devotion. There is something in it that doesn't readily submit.
HERMIONE. You may say so. It was that same hand that gave you my heart.
LEONTES. A generous hand. Once, a heart given was a heart kept. Now it seems the hand moves rather faster than the heart behind it.

O Lorenzo, If thou keep promise, I shall end this strife, Become a Christian and thy loving wife. [_Exit._]  SCENE IV. The same. A street. Enter Gratiano, Lorenzo, Salarino and Solanio. LORENZO. Nay, we will slink away in supper-time, Disguise us at my lodging, and return All in an hour. GRATIANO. We have not made good preparation. SALARINO. We have not spoke us yet of torch-bearers. SOLANIO. ’Tis vile, unless it may be quaintly order’d, And better in my mind not undertook. LORENZO. ’Tis now but four o’clock, we have two hours To furnish us. Enter Launcelet with a letter. Friend Launcelet, what’s the news? LAUNCELET. And it shall please you to break up this, it shall seem to signify. LORENZO. I know the hand, in faith ’tis a fair hand, And whiter than the paper it writ on Is the fair hand that writ. GRATIANO. Love news, in faith. LAUNCELET. By your leave, sir. LORENZO. Whither goest thou? LAUNCELET. Marry, sir, to bid my old master the Jew to sup tonight with my new master the Christian. LORENZO. Hold here, take this. Tell gentle Jessica I will not fail her, speak it privately. Go, gentlemen,  [_Exit Launcelet._]  Will you prepare you for this masque tonight? I am provided of a torch-bearer. SALARINO. Ay, marry, I’ll be gone about it straight. SOLANIO. And so will I.  LORENZO. Meet me and Gratiano At Gratiano’s lodging some hour hence. SALARINO. ’Tis good we do so. [_Exeunt Salarino and Solanio._]  GRATIANO. Was not that letter from fair Jessica? LORENZO. I must needs tell thee all. She hath directed How I shall take her from her father’s house, What gold and jewels she is furnish’d with, What page’s suit she hath in readiness. If e’er the Jew her father come to heaven, It will be for his gentle daughter’s sake; And never dare misfortune cross her foot, Unless she do it under this excuse, That she is issue to a faithless Jew. Come, go with me, peruse this as thou goest; Fair Jessica shall be my torch-bearer. [_Exeunt._]  SCENE V. The same. Before Shylock’s house. Enter Shylock the Jew and Launcelet his man that was the clown. SHYLOCK. Well, thou shalt see, thy eyes shall be thy judge, The difference of old Shylock and Bassanio.— What, Jessica!—Thou shalt not gormandize As thou hast done with me;—What, Jessica!— And sleep, and snore, and rend apparel out. Why, Jessica, I say! LAUNCELET. Why, Jessica! SHYLOCK. Who bids thee call? I do not bid thee call. LAUNCELET. Your worship was wont to tell me I could do nothing without bidding. Enter Jessica. JESSICA. Call you? What is your will? SHYLOCK. I am bid forth to supper, Jessica. There are my keys. But wherefore should I go? I am not bid for love, they flatter me. But yet I’ll go in hate, to feed upon The prodigal Christian. Jessica, my girl, Look to my house. I am right loath to go; There is some ill a-brewing towards my rest, For I did dream of money-bags tonight. LAUNCELET. I beseech you, sir, go. My young master doth expect your reproach. SHYLOCK. So do I his. LAUNCELET. And they have conspired together. I will not say you shall see a masque, but if you do, then it was not for nothing that my nose fell a-bleeding on Black Monday last at six o’clock i’ th’ morning, falling out that year on Ash-Wednesday was four year in th’ afternoon. SHYLOCK. What, are there masques? Hear you me, Jessica, Lock up my doors, and when you hear the drum And the vile squealing of the wry-neck’d fife, Clamber not you up to the casements then, Nor thrust your head into the public street To gaze on Christian fools with varnish’d faces, But stop my house’s ears, I mean my casements. Let not the sound of shallow fopp’ry enter My sober house. By Jacob’s staff I swear I have no mind of feasting forth tonight. But I will go. Go you before me, sirrah. Say I will come. LAUNCELET. I will go before, sir. Mistress, look out at window for all this. There will come a Christian by Will be worth a Jewess’ eye. [_Exit Launcelet._]  SHYLOCK. What says that fool of Hagar’s offspring, ha? JESSICA. His words were “Farewell, mistress,” nothing else. SHYLOCK. The patch is kind enough, but a huge feeder, Snail-slow in profit, and he sleeps by day More than the wild-cat. Drones hive not with me, Therefore I part with him, and part with him To one that I would have him help to waste His borrowed purse.

O Fabian, If thou keep faith, I shall resolve this doubt, Forsake my father's house and be thy spouse. [Exit.]

SCENE IV. The same. A street. Enter Valentine, Fabian, Curio, and Sebastian.
FABIAN. Nay, we must slip away while they sit at table, change our dress at my lodging, and be back again before the second course is cleared.
VALENTINE. We have scarce thought the business through.
CURIO. We have said nothing yet of who shall carry the lights.
SEBASTIAN. It is a shabby thing unless it be neatly contrived, and in my judgement better left alone than done in this disorder.
FABIAN. It lacks but five o'clock — we have time enough, if we use it.
Enter Gobbo with a letter.
Friend Gobbo, what have you there?
GOBBO. If it shall please you to break the seal, it will make itself known well enough.
FABIAN. I know this writing — in good faith, it is a fine hand, and fairer than the paper is the hand that penned it.
VALENTINE. Love's business, by the look of it.
GOBBO. By your leave, sir—
FABIAN. Where do you go?
GOBBO. Marry, sir, to bid my old master the moneylender to dine tonight with my new master, who is a gentleman of some Christian virtue and considerable appetite.
FABIAN. Here, take this. Tell sweet Isabetta I will not fail her — speak it to her alone and to no one else. Go, gentlemen—
[Exit Gobbo.]
Will you make ready for this evening's entertainment? I have settled the matter of the torch-bearer.
CURIO. Ay, I'll be about it presently.
SEBASTIAN. And I the same.
FABIAN. Come to Valentine's lodging within the hour, the both of you.
CURIO. That we shall. [Exeunt Curio and Sebastian.]
VALENTINE. Was that letter from Isabetta?
FABIAN. I must tell you everything. She has set out exactly how I am to bring her from her father's keeping, what money and jewels she has made ready, and what disguise she has prepared. If ever her father in his closeness and his cold suspicion finds any mercy shown to him, it will be on her account entirely, and for no desert of his own. Misfortune would want a sharp excuse to light on one so gentle. Come, read as we walk — Isabetta herself shall bear our light tonight. [Exeunt.]

SCENE V. The same. Before the house of Mosca, a usurer. Enter Mosca and Gobbo.
MOSCA. Well, thou shalt see it with thine own eyes — see the difference between a house that is kept with order and one that is squandered away in foolishness. What, Lisetta! Thou shalt not eat me out of house and home as thou hast lately taken to doing — What, Lisetta! — sleeping till midday and wearing out good linen and leaving candles to burn to nothing.
GOBBO. What, Lisetta!
MOSCA. Who asked thee to call? I did not bid thee call.
GOBBO. Your worship has always told me I am fit for nothing unless I am told what to do.
Enter Lisetta.
LISETTA. You called? What is it you want?
MOSCA. I am bidden to supper, Lisetta. There are the keys. And yet why should I go? They do not want me — they flatter me to my face and mock me behind my back. But I'll go all the same, if only to see what that spendthrift sets on his table. Look to the house while I am gone. I go against my better judgement. Something sits ill with me tonight — I dreamed last night of my strongbox, and not pleasantly.
GOBBO. I beseech you, sir, go. My young master does expect your arrival, and has spoken of it.
MOSCA. As I have spoken of his.
GOBBO. There is entertainment planned as well. I will not say outright there is to be a masque, but if there is, it is no mere accident that my left eye began twitching on St. Crispin's eve at the very stroke of midnight, which fell that year upon a Wednesday that had been a fast day for three years running.
MOSCA. What — a masque, do you say? Hark you, Lisetta — bolt the shutters, and when you hear the drum begin and the shrieking of those infernal pipes, do not go climbing up to peer out of the windows, nor push your head into the street to gape at fools with painted faces. Shut the house up, stop its ears. Let none of that shallow nonsense in. I swear I have no stomach for feasting tonight, and yet I'll go. Go ahead of me, sirrah, and tell them I am coming.
GOBBO. I go before, sir. [Aside to Lisetta.] Mistress, keep one eye on the street regardless. There will pass by tonight someone well worth a young woman's notice. [Exit Gobbo.]
MOSCA. What was that the rascal was muttering?
LISETTA. Only "farewell, mistress" — nothing more.
MOSCA. He is not a bad fellow at bottom, but he eats like three men, profits like none, and sleeps away half the working day besides. I keep no drones in my house. I have already found him a new place — with a master who, I trust, will let him eat his way through a borrowed fortune at his leisure.

O Fabian, If thou keep faith, I'll end this wretched strife And leave my father's house to be thy wife. [Exit.]

SCENE IV. The same. A street. Enter Valentine, Fabian, Curio, and Sebastian.
FABIAN. No — we slip out during supper, change our dress at my lodging, and return before anyone has noticed. The whole business fits in an hour.
VALENTINE. We've barely thought it through.
CURIO. We haven't even settled on torch-bearers.
SEBASTIAN. The thing's not worth doing unless it's done properly. Honestly I'd sooner leave it.
FABIAN. It's not yet five. We have time enough.
Enter Gobbo with a letter.
Gobbo — what have you there?
GOBBO. Break the seal, sir, and it will speak for itself.
FABIAN. I know this hand. A fine hand too — fairer than the paper it's written on, if you take my meaning.
VALENTINE. Love's work, plainly enough.
GOBBO. By your leave, sir—
FABIAN. Where are you off to?
GOBBO. To bid my old master the moneylender to supper tonight with my new master, who is a Christian gentleman of considerable appetite.
FABIAN. Here — take this. And tell sweet Isabetta I will not fail her. Say it to her privately. Go, gentlemen—
[Exit Gobbo.]
Will you get yourselves ready for tonight? I've settled the torch-bearer.
CURIO. Ay, I'll see to it at once.
SEBASTIAN. And I.
FABIAN. Come to Valentine's lodging within the hour.
CURIO. Done. [Exeunt Curio and Sebastian.]
VALENTINE. That was from Isabetta, was it not?
FABIAN. I'll tell you everything. She's laid out exactly how I'm to get her out of her father's house — what money and jewels she's put aside, what disguise she's prepared. If her father ever finds any mercy in this world, it'll be on her account alone, and God knows not for any deserving of his own. Come — read as we walk. Isabetta herself shall be our torch-bearer tonight. [Exeunt.]

SCENE V. The same. Before the house of Mosca, a usurer. Enter Mosca and Gobbo.
MOSCA. Well, thou shalt see it plain enough — see the difference between a house governed with sense and one thrown away in waste and idleness. What, Lisetta! Thou shalt not eat me bare as thou hast lately done — What, Lisetta! — lying abed till noon and tearing through good linen and leaving candles to burn themselves to stubs—
GOBBO. What, Lisetta!
MOSCA. Who told thee to call? I did not bid thee call.
GOBBO. Your worship has always said I'm fit for nothing without being told.
Enter Lisetta.
LISETTA. You called? What do you want?
MOSCA. I am bidden to supper, Lisetta. Take the keys. And yet — why should I go? They don't want me there; they flatter me to my face and laugh behind my back. But I'll go in spite of it, if only to watch that spendthrift's candles burn. Look to the house. I go against my own judgement. Something sits ill with me tonight — I dreamed last night of my strongbox, and not pleasantly.
GOBBO. Go, sir, I beseech you. My young master is expecting you, and has spoken of it.
MOSCA. As I have spoken of him.
GOBBO. There's entertainment planned as well. I won't say outright there's to be a masque — but if there is, it was no accident that my left eye began twitching on St. Crispin's eve at the stroke of midnight, which that year fell on a Wednesday that had been a fast day three years running.
MOSCA. A masque — what? Listen to me, Lisetta. Bolt the shutters. And when you hear the drum start up and those infernal pipes squealing, don't go climbing to the window to gape at painted fools in the street. Stop the house's ears. Let none of that nonsense in. I swear I have no appetite for feasting tonight — and yet I'll go. Go ahead, sirrah, tell them I am coming.
GOBBO. I go, sir. [Aside to Lisetta.] Mistress — keep one eye on the street all the same. There'll be someone worth a young woman's notice passing by before long. [Exit.]
MOSCA. What was that the wretch was muttering?
LISETTA. Only "farewell, mistress." Nothing more.
MOSCA. He's not a bad creature at bottom — but he eats like three men, profits like none, and sleeps away half the day besides. I keep no drones. I've already found him a new place, with a master who will no doubt let him eat his way cheerfully through a borrowed fortune.

The fresh air revived me, and with renewed determination I approached the door of their cottage. “I knocked. ‘Who is there?’ said the old man. ‘Come in.’  “I entered. ‘Pardon this intrusion,’ said I; ‘I am a traveller in want of a little rest; you would greatly oblige me if you would allow me to remain a few minutes before the fire.’  “‘Enter,’ said De Lacey, ‘and I will try in what manner I can to relieve your wants; but, unfortunately, my children are from home, and as I am blind, I am afraid I shall find it difficult to procure food for you.’  “‘Do not trouble yourself, my kind host; I have food; it is warmth and rest only that I need.’  “I sat down, and a silence ensued. I knew that every minute was precious to me, yet I remained irresolute in what manner to commence the interview, when the old man addressed me. ‘By your language, stranger, I suppose you are my countryman; are you French?’  “‘No; but I was educated by a French family and understand that language only. I am now going to claim the protection of some friends, whom I sincerely love, and of whose favour I have some hopes.’  “‘Are they Germans?’  “‘No, they are French. But let us change the subject. I am an unfortunate and deserted creature, I look around and I have no relation or friend upon earth. These amiable people to whom I go have never seen me and know little of me. I am full of fears, for if I fail there, I am an outcast in the world for ever.’  “‘Do not despair. To be friendless is indeed to be unfortunate, but the hearts of men, when unprejudiced by any obvious self-interest, are full of brotherly love and charity. Rely, therefore, on your hopes; and if these friends are good and amiable, do not despair.’  “‘They are kind—they are the most excellent creatures in the world; but, unfortunately, they are prejudiced against me. I have good dispositions; my life has been hitherto harmless and in some degree beneficial; but a fatal prejudice clouds their eyes, and where they ought to see a feeling and kind friend, they behold only a detestable monster.’  “‘That is indeed unfortunate; but if you are really blameless, cannot you undeceive them?’  “‘I am about to undertake that task; and it is on that account that I feel so many overwhelming terrors. I tenderly love these friends; I have, unknown to them, been for many months in the habits of daily kindness towards them; but they believe that I wish to injure them, and it is that prejudice which I wish to overcome.’  “‘Where do these friends reside?’  “‘Near this spot.’  “The old man paused and then continued, ‘If you will unreservedly confide to me the particulars of your tale, I perhaps may be of use in undeceiving them. I am blind and cannot judge of your countenance, but there is something in your words which persuades me that you are sincere. I am poor and an exile, but it will afford me true pleasure to be in any way serviceable to a human creature.’  “‘Excellent man! I thank you and accept your generous offer. You raise me from the dust by this kindness; and I trust that, by your aid, I shall not be driven from the society and sympathy of your fellow creatures.’  “‘Heaven forbid! Even if you were really criminal, for that can only drive you to desperation, and not instigate you to virtue. I also am unfortunate; I and my family have been condemned, although innocent; judge, therefore, if I do not feel for your misfortunes.’  “‘How can I thank you, my best and only benefactor? From your lips first have I heard the voice of kindness directed towards me; I shall be for ever grateful; and your present humanity assures me of success with those friends whom I am on the point of meeting.’  “‘May I know the names and residence of those friends?’  “I paused. This, I thought, was the moment of decision, which was to rob me of or bestow happiness on me for ever. I struggled vainly for firmness sufficient to answer him, but the effort destroyed all my remaining strength; I sank on the chair and sobbed aloud. At that moment I heard the steps of my younger protectors.

The cold night air steadied me somewhat, and gathering what resolve I could, I made my way toward the low light burning in the window of the dwelling. I knocked softly upon the door.
"Who is there?" called a voice from within — an old voice, careful and unhurried.
"A stranger," I replied. "One who means no harm and asks only a moment's shelter."
"Come in, then."
I entered. The room was small and warm, lit by a single lamp. An elderly man sat by the hearth, his hands resting on his knees, his eyes turned toward me without seeing.
"Forgive me for disturbing your evening," I said. "I have been walking a long while and am very tired. If you would permit me to sit by your fire for a short time, I would ask nothing more."
"Sit, sit," said the old man — Vernet, as I later learned he was called. "I am sorry I can offer you little else. My daughter has gone to the village and will not return before dark, and as you may perceive, I am not well placed to be of great practical use to a guest."
"You have already given me more than you know simply by opening your door. I have food enough — it is rest and a little warmth that I need, nothing more."
He nodded, and for a time neither of us spoke. I felt the moments passing with terrible weight, knowing that what I had to do could not be long delayed — and yet I could not find the beginning of it. At last the old man spoke first.
"You have an unusual manner of speaking," he said, not unkindly. "I cannot place you. Are you from this country?"
"No. I was raised in circumstances that gave me your language, but I come from nowhere that has a name I could give you. I am on my way to find certain people — people who are very dear to me, and from whom I hope, though I am not certain, to receive some welcome."
"They are relations?"
"Not by blood. They are the only beings in the world toward whom I feel anything that might be called attachment. I have no others. I have never had others. And these — they do not know me as I truly am, and I fear that when they see me, all my hopes will end in a single moment."
The old man was quiet for a while. "That is a hard thing to carry," he said at last. "But you must not give way to despair before the moment itself arrives. I have lived long enough to know that the worst we imagine seldom falls out exactly as we dread it."
"You are kind to say so. But my case is not an ordinary one. These people have cause to fear what I am — or what they believe me to be. They have been told things, or have formed impressions, that stand entirely against me. And when they see me face to face, that impression will not be softened. It will, I am afraid, be confirmed in the most terrible way."
"And yet you intend to go to them."
"I must. I have no other hope in the world. And I have not been idle in my waiting — for a long time, longer than they know, I have done what small good I could for them, quietly and without any acknowledgement. I had hoped that this might count for something. I had hoped that kindness, even when its source is unknown, leaves some trace in the heart that receives it."
"I believe that it does," said Vernet slowly. "I believe that deeply. Goodness recognised or not is never entirely without effect." He turned his unseeing face toward me. "You say these people live nearby?"
"Very near."
He was silent again. Then: "If you were to tell me the whole of your situation — plainly, and withholding nothing — it is possible I might be of some small use to you. I cannot see your face, and so I must judge you by your words alone. But there is something in the way you speak that makes me trust you. I am not a man of any influence or resource — I have known exile and poverty — but if I could do anything to ease your way, I would do it gladly."
Something swelled in me that I had not felt before — or had felt so rarely that I no longer recognised it. I could not at once speak.
"You cannot know," I said at last, "what it means to hear those words. I have lived so long without them. I will tell you everything — I will hold nothing back — and I will trust in your goodness as I have not yet been able to trust in anyone's."
"Then tell me. But first — these friends of yours. Will you tell me their names?"
I stopped. Here it was — the moment I had been moving toward for so long, through cold and darkness and loneliness beyond any telling. I tried to answer. I formed the words in my mind and brought them forward, but they dissolved before they reached my tongue. The effort of it undid me entirely. I felt myself sink back into the chair, and a sound came out of me that I could not stop — something between a cry and a sob, raw and without dignity.
And at that moment I heard footsteps on the path outside.

The cold night air helped. My head cleared a little, and I walked toward the light in the window with something closer to resolution than I had felt all day. I knocked.
"Who's there?" An old voice — careful, unhurried.
"A stranger. I mean no harm. I ask only a moment's shelter."
"Come in."
The room was small and warm, a single lamp burning on the table. An elderly man sat by the hearth, hands resting on his knees, his eyes directed toward me without seeing.
"Forgive me for disturbing you," I said. "I have been walking a long while and am very tired. If you would let me sit by your fire for a short time, I would ask nothing more."
"Sit down," said the old man — Vernet, I later learned. "I am sorry I cannot offer you more. My daughter has gone to the village and will not be back before dark, and as you can perhaps see, I am not well placed to be of much practical help to a visitor."
"You have given me more than you know by simply opening the door. I have food — it is warmth and rest I need, nothing else."
He nodded. For a while neither of us spoke. I felt the time pressing on me, knowing I could not put off much longer what I had come to do — and yet I could not find where to begin. The old man spoke first.
"You speak in an unusual way," he said, not unkindly. "I cannot quite place you. Are you from this country?"
"No. I was raised in circumstances that gave me your language, but I have no country I could name. I am travelling to find certain people — people who mean everything to me — and from whom I hope, though I am not sure, to receive some welcome."
"Relations?"
"Not by blood. They are the only beings in this world toward whom I feel anything like attachment. I have no others. And these — they do not know me as I am. When they see me, I fear my hopes will end in a single moment."
The old man was quiet. "That is a heavy thing to carry," he said at last. "But do not give way before the moment comes. I have lived long enough to know the worst we imagine seldom falls out quite as we dread."
"You are kind. But my situation is not an ordinary one. These people have been given reason to fear what I am — or what they believe me to be. When they see me, that belief will not soften. I am afraid it will be confirmed in the worst possible way."
"And yet you mean to go to them."
"I have no other hope. And I have not spent my waiting time in idleness — for a long while, much longer than they know, I have done what quiet good I could for them, without acknowledgement or thanks. I had hoped that kindness, even when its source is unknown, leaves some trace in the heart that receives it."
"I believe that it does," said Vernet, slowly. "Goodness, whether it is recognised or not, is never entirely without effect." He turned his blind face toward me. "You say these people live nearby?"
"Very near."
A pause. Then: "If you were to tell me your full situation — plainly and without holding anything back — I might be able to help. I cannot see your face, so I must judge by your words alone. But there is something in the way you speak that inclines me to trust you. I have no influence, no resources to speak of — I have known exile and poverty well enough — but if there is anything I could do, I would do it willingly."
Something moved in me that I had not felt in a very long time. I could not speak immediately.
"You cannot know what it means to hear those words," I managed at last. "I will tell you everything. I will hold nothing back. I will trust in your goodness as I have not been able to trust in anyone's before now."
"Then tell me. But first — your friends. Will you give me their names?"
I stopped. Here it was — the moment everything had been moving toward, through all the cold and dark and loneliness beyond telling. I tried to answer. I formed the words and brought them forward and they came to nothing. The effort of it simply broke something in me. I felt myself fall back against the chair, and a sound came out of me — raw, helpless, beyond any controlling.
And at that moment I heard footsteps on the path outside.

“You were inquiring for me... of the porter?” Raskolnikov said at last, but in a curiously quiet voice. The man made no answer; he didn’t even look at him. Again they were both silent. “Why do you... come and ask for me... and say nothing.... What’s the meaning of it?”  Raskolnikov’s voice broke and he seemed unable to articulate the words clearly. The man raised his eyes this time and turned a gloomy sinister look at Raskolnikov. “Murderer!” he said suddenly in a quiet but clear and distinct voice. Raskolnikov went on walking beside him. His legs felt suddenly weak, a cold shiver ran down his spine, and his heart seemed to stand still for a moment, then suddenly began throbbing as though it were set free. So they walked for about a hundred paces, side by side in silence. The man did not look at him. “What do you mean... what is.... Who is a murderer?” muttered Raskolnikov hardly audibly. “_You_ are a murderer,” the man answered still more articulately and emphatically, with a smile of triumphant hatred, and again he looked straight into Raskolnikov’s pale face and stricken eyes. They had just reached the cross-roads. The man turned to the left without looking behind him. Raskolnikov remained standing, gazing after him. He saw him turn round fifty paces away and look back at him still standing there. Raskolnikov could not see clearly, but he fancied that he was again smiling the same smile of cold hatred and triumph. With slow faltering steps, with shaking knees, Raskolnikov made his way back to his little garret, feeling chilled all over. He took off his cap and put it on the table, and for ten minutes he stood without moving. Then he sank exhausted on the sofa and with a weak moan of pain he stretched himself on it. So he lay for half an hour. He thought of nothing. Some thoughts or fragments of thoughts, some images without order or coherence floated before his mind--faces of people he had seen in his childhood or met somewhere once, whom he would never have recalled, the belfry of the church at V., the billiard table in a restaurant and some officers playing billiards, the smell of cigars in some underground tobacco shop, a tavern room, a back staircase quite dark, all sloppy with dirty water and strewn with egg-shells, and the Sunday bells floating in from somewhere.... The images followed one another, whirling like a hurricane. Some of them he liked and tried to clutch at, but they faded and all the while there was an oppression within him, but it was not overwhelming, sometimes it was even pleasant.... The slight shivering still persisted, but that too was an almost pleasant sensation. He heard the hurried footsteps of Razumihin; he closed his eyes and pretended to be asleep. Razumihin opened the door and stood for some time in the doorway as though hesitating, then he stepped softly into the room and went cautiously to the sofa. Raskolnikov heard Nastasya’s whisper:  “Don’t disturb him! Let him sleep. He can have his dinner later.”  “Quite so,” answered Razumihin. Both withdrew carefully and closed the door. Another half-hour passed. Raskolnikov opened his eyes, turned on his back again, clasping his hands behind his head. “Who is he? Who is that man who sprang out of the earth? Where was he, what did he see? He has seen it all, that’s clear. Where was he then? And from where did he see? Why has he only now sprung out of the earth? And how could he see? Is it possible? Hm...” continued Raskolnikov, turning cold and shivering, “and the jewel case Nikolay found behind the door--was that possible? A clue? You miss an infinitesimal line and you can build it into a pyramid of evidence! A fly flew by and saw it! Is it possible?” He felt with sudden loathing how weak, how physically weak he had become. “I ought to have known it,” he thought with a bitter smile. “And how dared I, knowing myself, knowing how I should be, take up an axe and shed blood! I ought to have known beforehand.... Ah, but I did know!” he whispered in despair. At times he came to a standstill at some thought. “No, those men are not made so. The real _Master_ to whom all is permitted storms Toulon, makes a massacre in Paris, _forgets_ an army in Egypt, _wastes_ half a million men in the Moscow expedition and gets off with a jest at Vilna.

"You were watching me... from the corner of the street?" Svidrigailov said at last, in a voice that was almost too composed. The other man offered no reply, nor did he glance in his direction. The silence between them stretched long and heavy. "Why follow me here... and then stand there saying nothing... what is it you want?"
Svidrigailov's voice faltered slightly at the edges. The other man turned then and fixed upon him a look of such cold and steady contempt that Svidrigailov felt it like a hand pressed flat against his chest.
"Thief," the man said quietly. The word was unhurried, almost gentle in its delivery, but it landed with extraordinary precision.
Svidrigailov continued walking. His feet carried him forward by some automatic function wholly separate from his will. A strange numbness spread through his hands and up into his jaw. They walked on together for perhaps two hundred paces, neither speaking, the man keeping always at the same distance from him, like a shadow with a separate intention.
"I don't understand you," Svidrigailov said at last. "You use that word as though you witnessed something."
"I did," the man replied with calm and unhurried certainty. Then he stopped, turned down a narrow side street, and was gone.
Svidrigailov stood at the entrance to the lane for a long moment. A cab rolled past. A woman with a basket crossed at the far end of the road. The city continued around him with total indifference.
He returned to his rooms in a state he could not properly name. It was not fear exactly—he had always prided himself on his relationship with fear, on having looked at it steadily enough to rob it of authority. No, it was something more unsettling than fear. It was the sensation of having been accurately read, as one reads a letter that was not meant to be found.
He sat at his desk without lighting the lamp. The room grew slowly darker around him. He thought of nothing with any continuity—there came instead a disordered procession of images, faces without names, a garden in summer, a corridor in some house he had visited years ago and never thought of since, the sound of water somewhere below, a child's shoe lying in mud. The images did not distress him. They moved past with a kind of dreary indolence.
After some time he lit a cigarette and sat smoking in the dark.
"A man who saw," he said aloud to no one. "But what exactly did he see?"
He turned the question over with methodical patience, the way one might dismantle a mechanism to understand why it had stopped. The man's face had been composed. Not the face of someone acting on impulse or private grievance—a face that had already concluded something, had long since passed through whatever agitation the knowledge had initially produced and arrived now at some settled and implacable resolution.
That was the detail that troubled him. Not the accusation itself, which might be managed, which might be traced back to its source and handled. It was the composure that troubled him. It suggested something finished, something already set irrevocably in motion.
He pressed the end of the cigarette against the ashtray and watched the ember die. "I should have been more careful," he said, in a tone neither of regret nor self-reproach, but simply of accurate retrospective assessment. "Though of course I was never the sort to be careful." He almost smiled. "That was always the difficulty."

"You were watching me... from the corner of the street?" Svidrigailov said at last, his voice almost too composed. The other man didn't reply, didn't even glance at him. The silence between them went on and on. "Why follow me here and then stand there saying nothing—what do you want?"
There was a slight fracture at the edge of his voice. The man turned and fixed him with a look of such cold, steady contempt that Svidrigailov felt it like a hand pressed flat against his chest.
"Thief." Quietly said. Unhurried, almost gentle—but landing with extraordinary precision.
Svidrigailov kept walking. His feet carried him forward by some automatic function separate from his will, and a strange numbness moved through his hands and up into his jaw. They went on together perhaps two hundred paces without a word, the man always at the same distance, like a shadow with its own intention.
"I don't understand you," Svidrigailov said finally. "You use that word as though you actually witnessed something."
"I did." Calm. Unhurried. Then the man stopped, turned into a narrow side street, and was gone.
Svidrigailov stood at the entrance to the lane. A cab rolled past. A woman with a basket crossed at the far end of the road. The city went on around him with complete indifference.
He returned to his rooms in a state he couldn't properly name—not fear, exactly. He had always prided himself on his relationship with fear, on having looked at it long enough to rob it of its authority. This was something more unsettling. The sensation of having been accurately read, like a letter found that was never meant to be.
He sat at his desk without lighting the lamp, the room darkening slowly around him. His thoughts came in fragments: a garden somewhere in summer, a corridor in a house visited years ago and never thought of since, the sound of water below, a child's shoe lying in mud. The images weren't distressing. They drifted past with a kind of dreary indolence.
He lit a cigarette eventually and sat smoking in the dark.
"A man who saw," he said aloud, to no one. "But what exactly did he see?"
He turned the question over with methodical patience—the way you take apart a mechanism to understand why it's stopped. The man's face had been composed. Not the face of someone acting on impulse or private grievance, but a face that had already concluded something, had long passed through whatever agitation the knowledge first produced and arrived at some settled, implacable resolution.
That was what troubled him. Not the accusation itself, which might be traced back and handled. The composure was what troubled him—it suggested something finished, already set irrevocably in motion.
He pressed the cigarette into the ashtray and watched the ember go out. "I should have been more careful," he said, in a tone that was neither regret nor self-reproach—just accurate retrospective assessment. "Though of course I was never the sort to be careful." He almost smiled. "That was always the difficulty."

BIONDELLO. I told him that your father was at Venice, And that you look’d for him this day in Padua. TRANIO. Th’art a tall fellow; hold thee that to drink. Here comes Baptista. Set your countenance, sir. Enter Baptista and Lucentio. Signior Baptista, you are happily met. [_To the Pedant_] Sir, this is the gentleman I told you of; I pray you stand good father to me now; Give me Bianca for my patrimony. PEDANT. Soft, son! Sir, by your leave: having come to Padua To gather in some debts, my son Lucentio Made me acquainted with a weighty cause Of love between your daughter and himself: And,—for the good report I hear of you, And for the love he beareth to your daughter, And she to him,—to stay him not too long, I am content, in a good father’s care, To have him match’d; and, if you please to like No worse than I, upon some agreement Me shall you find ready and willing With one consent to have her so bestow’d; For curious I cannot be with you, Signior Baptista, of whom I hear so well. BAPTISTA. Sir, pardon me in what I have to say. Your plainness and your shortness please me well. Right true it is your son Lucentio here Doth love my daughter, and she loveth him, Or both dissemble deeply their affections; And therefore, if you say no more than this, That like a father you will deal with him, And pass my daughter a sufficient dower, The match is made, and all is done: Your son shall have my daughter with consent. TRANIO. I thank you, sir. Where then do you know best We be affied, and such assurance ta’en As shall with either part’s agreement stand? BAPTISTA. Not in my house, Lucentio, for you know Pitchers have ears, and I have many servants; Besides, old Gremio is hearkening still, And happily we might be interrupted. TRANIO. Then at my lodging, and it like you: There doth my father lie; and there this night We’ll pass the business privately and well. Send for your daughter by your servant here; My boy shall fetch the scrivener presently. The worst is this, that at so slender warning You are like to have a thin and slender pittance. BAPTISTA. It likes me well. Cambio, hie you home, And bid Bianca make her ready straight; And, if you will, tell what hath happened: Lucentio’s father is arriv’d in Padua, And how she’s like to be Lucentio’s wife. LUCENTIO. I pray the gods she may, with all my heart! TRANIO. Dally not with the gods, but get thee gone. Signior Baptista, shall I lead the way? Welcome! One mess is like to be your cheer; Come, sir; we will better it in Pisa. BAPTISTA. I follow you. [_Exeunt Tranio, Pedant and Baptista._]  BIONDELLO. Cambio! LUCENTIO. What say’st thou, Biondello? BIONDELLO. You saw my master wink and laugh upon you? LUCENTIO. Biondello, what of that? BIONDELLO. Faith, nothing; but has left me here behind to expound the meaning or moral of his signs and tokens. LUCENTIO. I pray thee moralize them. BIONDELLO. Then thus: Baptista is safe, talking with the deceiving father of a deceitful son. LUCENTIO. And what of him? BIONDELLO. His daughter is to be brought by you to the supper. LUCENTIO. And then? BIONDELLO. The old priest at Saint Luke’s church is at your command at all hours. LUCENTIO. And what of all this? BIONDELLO. I cannot tell, except they are busied about a counterfeit assurance. Take your assurance of her, _cum privilegio ad imprimendum solum_; to the church! take the priest, clerk, and some sufficient honest witnesses. If this be not that you look for, I have more to say, But bid Bianca farewell for ever and a day. [_Going._]  LUCENTIO. Hear’st thou, Biondello? BIONDELLO. I cannot tarry: I knew a wench married in an afternoon as she went to the garden for parsley to stuff a rabbit; and so may you, sir; and so adieu, sir. My master hath appointed me to go to Saint Luke’s to bid the priest be ready to come against you come with your appendix. [_Exit._]  LUCENTIO. I may, and will, if she be so contented. She will be pleas’d; then wherefore should I doubt? Hap what hap may, I’ll roundly go about her; It shall go hard if Cambio go without her:  [_Exit._]  SCENE V. A public road. Enter Petruchio, Katherina, Hortensio and Servants.

GRUMIO. I told the steward that your uncle was in Florence, And that you looked for his arrival here by Thursday.
FABIAN. Thou art a clever rogue; take that for thy trouble. But here comes old Orsino. Look lively, man.
Enter Orsino and Valentio.
Good Signior Orsino, well encountered, sir. (To the Merchant) This is the gentleman of whom I spoke at length; I prithee, play the father to me here, And grant me fair Sophia for my wife.
MERCHANT. Gently, son. Sir, by your leave: I came to Verona On business of some weight concerning debts, When my son Valentio brought to my attention The tender and most earnest cause of love Between your niece and his most willing heart. And for the good report that travels with you, And for the love my son doth bear the lady, I am resolved, as any careful father, To see him settled well and honestly. If you shall like the terms as well as I, You'll find me neither difficult nor cold; I come prepared to hear and to agree, And give my blessing freely to the match.
ORSINO. Sir, hear me out, for I must speak my mind. Your honest manner and plain speech please me well. It is most true my niece Sophia here Doth love your son, or both do counterfeit With more than ordinary art and patience. If then you mean to deal as fathers deal— With substance, settlement, and honest care— And pass Sophia a sufficient dower, I am content, the match is sealed and done: She shall be his, and with my full consent.
FABIAN. I thank you, sir. But where shall we confirm These articles and set our seals upon them, That both sides may be satisfied and bound?
ORSINO. Not in this house; you'll understand me, sir. Walls are not always walls, and servants hear Far more than one would wish. Besides, old Curio Is forever loitering near where he's not wanted.
FABIAN. Then at my lodgings, if it please you well: My father rests there; we shall do the business Quietly tonight, without interruption. Send for Sophia; let my boy go fetch A scrivener who can set the matter down. The worst is only this: at such short notice Our supper may be somewhat plain and spare.
ORSINO. That suits me well enough. You, Feste, go— Ride home at once and bid Sophia prepare, And if you will, acquaint her with the news: Valentio's father is arrived in Verona, And she is like to be Valentio's wife.
VALENTIO. With all my heart I pray the heavens grant it!
FABIAN. Leave off your prayers, man, and get thee gone. Signior Orsino, shall I show the way? You're welcome, though our table may be modest— We'll make it right when next we meet in Padua.
ORSINO. I follow you.
Exeunt Fabian, Merchant, and Orsino.
GRUMIO. Valentio!
VALENTIO. What is it, Grumio?
GRUMIO. Did you mark how Fabian winked and smiled upon you just now?
VALENTIO. I marked it well. But what of it?
GRUMIO. Nothing of great consequence, only I have been left standing here to untangle the knot of his signs and gestures, if I can.
VALENTIO. Then untangle it, by all means.
GRUMIO. Thus then: Orsino is at this moment contentedly occupied in conversation with a false father on behalf of a son who is not what he claims.
VALENTIO. And what follows?
GRUMIO. His niece is to be brought by you this evening to supper.
VALENTIO. Go on.
GRUMIO. The chaplain at Saint Ambrose's chapel is entirely at your disposal and keeps no particular hours.
VALENTIO. And what is the sum of all this?
GRUMIO. I cannot say with any certainty, except that the business upstairs is a counterfeit settlement designed to occupy all interested parties. You, meanwhile, should secure your own settlement—take the lady to the chapel, take the priest, take a witness or two of good reputation. If that is not the thing you've been wanting, I have nothing further to offer, and you may bid Sophia a long and final farewell.
(Moving to go.)
VALENTIO. Wait, Grumio—
GRUMIO. I cannot linger. A woman I once knew was married on a Tuesday afternoon while she was sent to the kitchen garden to pick mint for a sauce; and there is no reason you should fare any worse. My master has sent me ahead to Saint Ambrose's to have the priest standing ready by the time you arrive with your company.
Exit.
VALENTIO. I will, and gladly, if she is willing. She will be willing; why should I doubt it? Come what may, I'll go to her directly— It shall go hard if I come home without her.
Exit.

GRUMIO. I told the steward your uncle was in Florence, expected here by Thursday at the latest.
FABIAN. Sharp work—here, take that for thy trouble. Now look to yourself, here comes old Orsino.
Enter Orsino and Valentio.
Good Signior Orsino, well met indeed. (To the Merchant) This is the very gentleman I spoke of, sir. Stand as a father to me now, I beg you, and help me win fair Sophia for my wife.
MERCHANT. Softly, son. Sir, your pardon—I came to Verona on a matter of debts when my son Valentio made me aware of this weighty business between himself and your niece. Given your good name, and the love they bear each other, I am content—as any father who wishes his son well settled—to hear the terms. You'll find me willing, and not hard to please.
ORSINO. I must speak plainly, sir, and I trust you'll not take offence. Your directness suits me. It is true enough that Sophia loves your son, or else they are both accomplished liars—and I do not think them so. If you mean to deal in earnest, with proper settlement and a sufficient dower, then the matter is concluded: she shall be his, and with my blessing.
FABIAN. I thank you heartily. But where shall we draw up these articles and set our hands to them, so that all is properly witnessed and binding?
ORSINO. Not here—servants have long memories and longer ears, and besides, old Curio will be sniffing about before the ink is dry.
FABIAN. Then at my lodgings, if that please you. My father is there; we'll conclude the whole affair tonight, quietly, and without interruption. Send for Sophia at once; I'll have a scrivener fetched directly. I warn you only—at such short notice, supper may be a slender affair.
ORSINO. That's no matter. You, Feste—ride home this instant and tell Sophia to make ready. And if you like, tell her why: Valentio's father has come to Verona, and she is as good as Valentio's wife.
VALENTIO. God grant it, with all my heart!
FABIAN. Save your prayers for later and get thee gone. Signior Orsino, shall I lead the way? A plain welcome tonight—we'll do better when we meet again in Padua.
ORSINO. I follow you.
Exeunt Fabian, Merchant, and Orsino.
GRUMIO. Valentio!
VALENTIO. What is it, Grumio?
GRUMIO. Did you see Fabian winking at you just now?
VALENTIO. I saw it. What of it?
GRUMIO. Nothing much—only he's left me here to make sense of it all, so here it is: Orsino is at this moment happily talking with a false father on behalf of a son who is nothing of the sort.
VALENTIO. And?
GRUMIO. His niece is to be brought to supper by you.
VALENTIO. Yes, and?
GRUMIO. The chaplain at Saint Ambrose's keeps no fixed hours and answers to nobody in particular.
VALENTIO. Come to the point, man.
GRUMIO. The point is this: that business indoors is a counterfeit, meant to keep everyone occupied. Your business is of a different kind—take the lady to the chapel, take the priest, take two honest witnesses. If that is not what you have been waiting for, I have nothing more to say, and you can say your goodbyes to Sophia here and now.
(Turning to go.)
VALENTIO. Grumio, wait—
GRUMIO. I cannot stop. I once knew a woman married on a Tuesday afternoon while she was sent out to fetch mint for a sauce—and you have more to work with than she did. My master has sent me ahead to Saint Ambrose's to have the priest ready and waiting.
Exit.
VALENTIO. I will go—gladly, if she is willing. And she will be. Why should I doubt it? Come what may, I mean to have her.
Exit.

Before I arrived in sight of it, all that remained of day was a beamless amber light along the west: but I could see every pebble on the path, and every blade of grass, by that splendid moon. I had neither to climb the gate nor to knock—it yielded to my hand. That is an improvement, I thought. And I noticed another, by the aid of my nostrils; a fragrance of stocks and wallflowers wafted on the air from amongst the homely fruit-trees. Both doors and lattices were open; and yet, as is usually the case in a coal-district, a fine red fire illumined the chimney: the comfort which the eye derives from it renders the extra heat endurable. But the house of Wuthering Heights is so large that the inmates have plenty of space for withdrawing out of its influence; and accordingly what inmates there were had stationed themselves not far from one of the windows. I could both see them and hear them talk before I entered, and looked and listened in consequence; being moved thereto by a mingled sense of curiosity and envy, that grew as I lingered. “Con-_trary_!” said a voice as sweet as a silver bell. “That for the third time, you dunce! I’m not going to tell you again. Recollect, or I’ll pull your hair!”  “Contrary, then,” answered another, in deep but softened tones. “And now, kiss me, for minding so well.”  “No, read it over first correctly, without a single mistake.”  The male speaker began to read: he was a young man, respectably dressed and seated at a table, having a book before him. His handsome features glowed with pleasure, and his eyes kept impatiently wandering from the page to a small white hand over his shoulder, which recalled him by a smart slap on the cheek, whenever its owner detected such signs of inattention. Its owner stood behind; her light, shining ringlets blending, at intervals, with his brown locks, as she bent to superintend his studies; and her face—it was lucky he could not see her face, or he would never have been so steady. I could; and I bit my lip in spite, at having thrown away the chance I might have had of doing something besides staring at its smiting beauty. The task was done, not free from further blunders; but the pupil claimed a reward, and received at least five kisses; which, however, he generously returned. Then they came to the door, and from their conversation I judged they were about to issue out and have a walk on the moors. I supposed I should be condemned in Hareton Earnshaw’s heart, if not by his mouth, to the lowest pit in the infernal regions if I showed my unfortunate person in his neighbourhood then; and feeling very mean and malignant, I skulked round to seek refuge in the kitchen. There was unobstructed admittance on that side also; and at the door sat my old friend Nelly Dean, sewing and singing a song; which was often interrupted from within by harsh words of scorn and intolerance, uttered in far from musical accents. “I’d rayther, by th’ haulf, hev’ ’em swearing i’ my lugs fro’h morn to neeght, nor hearken ye hahsiver!” said the tenant of the kitchen, in answer to an unheard speech of Nelly’s. “It’s a blazing shame, that I cannot oppen t’ blessed Book, but yah set up them glories to sattan, and all t’ flaysome wickednesses that iver were born into th’ warld! Oh! ye’re a raight nowt; and shoo’s another; and that poor lad ’ll be lost atween ye. Poor lad!” he added, with a groan; “he’s witched: I’m sartin on’t. Oh, Lord, judge ’em, for there’s norther law nor justice among wer rullers!”  “No! or we should be sitting in flaming fagots, I suppose,” retorted the singer. “But wisht, old man, and read your Bible like a Christian, and never mind me. This is ‘Fairy Annie’s Wedding’—a bonny tune—it goes to a dance.”  Mrs. Dean was about to recommence, when I advanced; and recognising me directly, she jumped to her feet, crying—“Why, bless you, Mr. Lockwood! How could you think of returning in this way? All’s shut up at Thrushcross Grange. You should have given us notice!”  “I’ve arranged to be accommodated there, for as long as I shall stay,” I answered. “I depart again to-morrow. And how are you transplanted here, Mrs. Dean? tell me that.”  “Zillah left, and Mr. Heathcliff wished me to come, soon after you went to London, and stay till you returned.

Before the house came into view, the last of the evening had already withdrawn behind the treeline, leaving only a pale residue of light along the horizon; but the lane was bright enough beneath the full moon, and I could make out the ruts in the mud and the Queen Anne's lace trembling at the verge. The gate stood unlatched—someone had been through it recently, or perhaps it was simply never fastened anymore—and I passed through without difficulty. The garden had been attended to since my last visit: I noticed the smell first, something sweet and almost embarrassingly abundant, roses perhaps, or honeysuckle climbing the near wall, and the grass on either side of the path had been cut low. The windows were lit and standing open to the warm air, their curtains lifting at intervals, and though the evening was mild there was a fire somewhere within—I could see its red suggestion at the far end of the hall—and the smell of woodsmoke mingled pleasantly with the garden.
I did not go in directly. Something in the arrangement of the light and the sound of voices checked my step, and I remained on the path a moment, not quite willing to announce myself.
There were two of them in the room nearest the window. A young woman sat in the larger chair with her feet drawn up and a book open but neglected in her lap, and a man—older than her by perhaps a decade, with a dark, careful face—sat across from her on a low stool, his elbows on his knees, watching her with an attention he may not have known was visible. They were arguing, lightly, in the way people argue when they are more interested in the other person than in the argument.
"You said yesterday you did not mind it," the woman said.
"I said I did not mind it then. That is a different thing entirely."
"You are always making these distinctions."
"One of us ought to."
She laughed, and the sound came clearly through the open window to where I stood, and I felt that particular variety of loneliness that is not quite sadness—something closer to the sensation of arriving late to a place one had expected to belong to.
I went around to the back, as I had always done, and found the kitchen door open and old Mrs. Alderton at the table with her mending, spectacles down on her nose, a tallow candle burning steadily beside her. She looked up at the sound of my step and her expression passed through surprise, pleasure, and reproof in quick succession.
"Lord above, Mr. Ashford—walking in like a ghost with no word sent ahead! I might have put you up at the Lamb and Flag without any trouble if only I'd known. As it is I can't say what state the room is in."
"I shan't need much," I told her. "I mean to leave again by Thursday. I only came to see how matters stood. How long have you been keeping the house?"
She set down her mending and folded her hands in the manner of someone who has a considerable amount to say and is deciding where to begin.
"Since Michaelmas," she said. "After the other woman went—you'll have heard about that, I expect—and the new arrangement was settled. It's a different sort of place now than when you were last here, Mr. Ashford. Very different."
I drew out the chair opposite and sat down, and she rose to put the kettle on without my asking, which I thought was a good sign.
"Tell me everything," I said.

The house wasn't yet in sight when the last of the evening withdrew behind the treeline, leaving only a pale residue of light along the horizon. But the lane was bright under the full moon—I could make out the ruts in the mud, the Queen Anne's lace trembling at the verge. The gate was unlatched, recently passed through or simply never fastened anymore, and I went through without difficulty.
The garden had been attended to since my last visit. I noticed the smell first—something sweet and almost embarrassingly abundant, roses perhaps, or honeysuckle on the near wall—and the grass had been cut low on either side of the path. The windows stood open to the warm air, curtains lifting at intervals, and though the evening was mild a fire burned somewhere within; I could see its red suggestion at the far end of the hall, and woodsmoke mingled with the garden.
I didn't go in. Something in the arrangement of light and voices checked my step, and I stood on the path a moment, unwilling to announce myself.
Two people occupied the room nearest the window. A young woman sat in the larger chair, feet drawn up, a book open but forgotten in her lap. A man—older by perhaps a decade, with a dark, careful face—sat across from her on a low stool, elbows on his knees, watching her with an attention he probably didn't know was visible. They were arguing, lightly, in the way people argue when they are more interested in each other than in the argument itself.
"You said yesterday you didn't mind it," the woman said.
"I said I didn't mind it then. That is a different thing entirely."
"You are always making these distinctions."
"One of us ought to."
She laughed. The sound came cleanly through the open window to where I stood, and I felt that particular kind of loneliness that is not quite sadness—closer to the sensation of arriving late somewhere you had expected to belong.
I went round to the back, as I had always done, and found the kitchen door open and old Mrs. Alderton at the table with her mending, spectacles low on her nose, a tallow candle burning beside her. She looked up, and her expression moved through surprise, pleasure, and reproof in quick succession.
"Lord above, Mr. Ashford—walking in like a ghost and no word sent! I could have put you up at the Lamb and Flag easily enough if only I'd known. I can't answer for the state of the room as it is."
"I shan't need much," I said. "I mean to leave again by Thursday—I only came to see how things stood. How long have you been keeping the house?"
She set down her mending and folded her hands in the manner of someone who has a great deal to say and is deciding where to begin.
"Since Michaelmas. After the other woman went—you'll have heard about that—and the new arrangement was settled. It's a different sort of place now, Mr. Ashford. Very different from when you were last here."
I pulled out the chair opposite and sat down. Without my asking, she rose to put the kettle on, which I took as a good sign.
"Tell me everything," I said.

Nay, in good faith; for mine ease, in good faith. Sir, here is newly come to court Laertes; believe me, an absolute gentleman, full of most excellent differences, of very soft society and great showing. Indeed, to speak feelingly of him, he is the card or calendar of gentry; for you shall find in him the continent of what part a gentleman would see. HAMLET. Sir, his definement suffers no perdition in you, though I know, to divide him inventorially would dizzy th’arithmetic of memory, and yet but yaw neither, in respect of his quick sail. But, in the verity of extolment, I take him to be a soul of great article and his infusion of such dearth and rareness as, to make true diction of him, his semblable is his mirror and who else would trace him his umbrage, nothing more. OSRIC. Your lordship speaks most infallibly of him. HAMLET. The concernancy, sir? Why do we wrap the gentleman in our more rawer breath? OSRIC. Sir? HORATIO. Is’t not possible to understand in another tongue? You will do’t, sir, really. HAMLET. What imports the nomination of this gentleman? OSRIC. Of Laertes? HORATIO. His purse is empty already, all’s golden words are spent. HAMLET. Of him, sir. OSRIC. I know you are not ignorant,—  HAMLET. I would you did, sir; yet in faith if you did, it would not much approve me. Well, sir? OSRIC. You are not ignorant of what excellence Laertes is,—  HAMLET. I dare not confess that, lest I should compare with him in excellence; but to know a man well were to know himself. OSRIC. I mean, sir, for his weapon; but in the imputation laid on him, by them in his meed he’s unfellowed. HAMLET. What’s his weapon? OSRIC. Rapier and dagger. HAMLET. That’s two of his weapons. But well. OSRIC. The King, sir, hath wager’d with him six Barbary horses, against the which he has imponed, as I take it, six French rapiers and poniards, with their assigns, as girdle, hangers, and so. Three of the carriages, in faith, are very dear to fancy, very responsive to the hilts, most delicate carriages, and of very liberal conceit. HAMLET. What call you the carriages? HORATIO. I knew you must be edified by the margin ere you had done. OSRIC. The carriages, sir, are the hangers. HAMLET. The phrase would be more german to the matter if we could carry cannon by our sides. I would it might be hangers till then. But on. Six Barbary horses against six French swords, their assigns, and three liberal conceited carriages: that’s the French bet against the Danish. Why is this all imponed, as you call it? OSRIC. The King, sir, hath laid that in a dozen passes between you and him, he shall not exceed you three hits. He hath laid on twelve for nine. And it would come to immediate trial if your lordship would vouchsafe the answer. HAMLET. How if I answer no? OSRIC. I mean, my lord, the opposition of your person in trial. HAMLET. Sir, I will walk here in the hall. If it please his Majesty, it is the breathing time of day with me. Let the foils be brought, the gentleman willing, and the King hold his purpose, I will win for him if I can; if not, I will gain nothing but my shame and the odd hits. OSRIC. Shall I re-deliver you e’en so? HAMLET. To this effect, sir; after what flourish your nature will. OSRIC. I commend my duty to your lordship. HAMLET. Yours, yours. [_Exit Osric._]  He does well to commend it himself, there are no tongues else for’s turn. HORATIO. This lapwing runs away with the shell on his head. HAMLET. He did comply with his dug before he suck’d it. Thus has he,—and many more of the same bevy that I know the drossy age dotes on,— only got the tune of the time and outward habit of encounter; a kind of yeasty collection, which carries them through and through the most fanned and winnowed opinions; and do but blow them to their trial, the bubbles are out. Enter a Lord. LORD. My lord, his Majesty commended him to you by young Osric, who brings back to him that you attend him in the hall. He sends to know if your pleasure hold to play with Laertes or that you will take longer time. HAMLET. I am constant to my purposes, they follow the King’s pleasure.

Nay, upon my honour, purely for the occasion. Sir, there is newly arrived to this city one Silvano; believe me, a most accomplished gentleman, replete with every fine distinction, of very gracious address and remarkable presence.
