Ha, ha! we go the gait that leaves no dust behind!”  “There she blows—she blows!—she blows!—right ahead!” was now the mast-head cry. “Aye, aye!” cried Stubb, “I knew it—ye can’t escape—blow on and split your spout, O whale! the mad fiend himself is after ye! blow your trump—blister your lungs!—Ahab will dam off your blood, as a miller shuts his watergate upon the stream!”  And Stubb did but speak out for well nigh all that crew. The frenzies of the chase had by this time worked them bubblingly up, like old wine worked anew. Whatever pale fears and forebodings some of them might have felt before; these were not only now kept out of sight through the growing awe of Ahab, but they were broken up, and on all sides routed, as timid prairie hares that scatter before the bounding bison. The hand of Fate had snatched all their souls; and by the stirring perils of the previous day; the rack of the past night’s suspense; the fixed, unfearing, blind, reckless way in which their wild craft went plunging towards its flying mark; by all these things, their hearts were bowled along. The wind that made great bellies of their sails, and rushed the vessel on by arms invisible as irresistible; this seemed the symbol of that unseen agency which so enslaved them to the race. They were one man, not thirty. For as the one ship that held them all; though it was put together of all contrasting things—oak, and maple, and pine wood; iron, and pitch, and hemp—yet all these ran into each other in the one concrete hull, which shot on its way, both balanced and directed by the long central keel; even so, all the individualities of the crew, this man’s valor, that man’s fear; guilt and guiltiness, all varieties were welded into oneness, and were all directed to that fatal goal which Ahab their one lord and keel did point to. The rigging lived. The mast-heads, like the tops of tall palms, were outspreadingly tufted with arms and legs. Clinging to a spar with one hand, some reached forth the other with impatient wavings; others, shading their eyes from the vivid sunlight, sat far out on the rocking yards; all the spars in full bearing of mortals, ready and ripe for their fate. Ah! how they still strove through that infinite blueness to seek out the thing that might destroy them! “Why sing ye not out for him, if ye see him?” cried Ahab, when, after the lapse of some minutes since the first cry, no more had been heard. “Sway me up, men; ye have been deceived; not Moby Dick casts one odd jet that way, and then disappears.”  It was even so; in their headlong eagerness, the men had mistaken some other thing for the whale-spout, as the event itself soon proved; for hardly had Ahab reached his perch; hardly was the rope belayed to its pin on deck, when he struck the key-note to an orchestra, that made the air vibrate as with the combined discharges of rifles. The triumphant halloo of thirty buckskin lungs was heard, as—much nearer to the ship than the place of the imaginary jet, less than a mile ahead—Moby Dick bodily burst into view! For not by any calm and indolent spoutings; not by the peaceable gush of that mystic fountain in his head, did the White Whale now reveal his vicinity; but by the far more wondrous phenomenon of breaching. Rising with his utmost velocity from the furthest depths, the Sperm Whale thus booms his entire bulk into the pure element of air, and piling up a mountain of dazzling foam, shows his place to the distance of seven miles and more.

The vessel pressed onward through the long heaving swells, cutting the ocean with a steady violence that sent spray flying from her bows. Every spar groaned with the strain, and yet the ship seemed eager for the pursuit, as though some hidden desire possessed her timbers. The men moved about the decks with restless expectation. Each glance cast toward the horizon carried the same question, and each moment of delay sharpened it.
“There again! There again! Off the larboard bow!” came the cry from above.
The words passed through the crew like a spark through dry grass. Heads turned at once. Hands tightened upon ropes and rails. Even those who had spoken little during the morning now stared ahead with shining eyes.
The captain stood unmoving for a moment. Then he stepped forward and fixed his gaze upon the distant sea.
“Keep her course,” he said. “Let no man lose sight of that place.”
The ocean before them appeared empty save for a patch of white disturbance far ahead. Yet all knew the sign. A whale had broken the surface and vanished again beneath the dark water.
Excitement spread among them. Old fears, doubts, and warnings were forgotten. The long chase, the weary watches, the uncertain days behind them—all seemed justified by that fleeting glimpse. Their purpose narrowed to a single point upon the sea.
The ship rushed onward. Wind filled the sails until they swelled like great clouds overhead. The masts bent slightly under the pressure, and the deck trembled beneath the continual drive forward.
Again the lookout shouted.
This time there could be no mistake.
The whale erupted from the depths in a vast arching leap. Water cascaded from its shining body. For an instant it hung against the sky, immense and magnificent, before crashing back into the sea with a report like distant artillery. Foam burst outward in every direction.
A great cry rose from the ship.
The crew surged toward the rails. Some pointed; others laughed aloud. The sight overwhelmed caution. The creature seemed less an animal than a force of nature, appearing suddenly from the hidden world below and declaring its presence to all who watched.
The captain’s eyes never left it.
“There is our quarry,” he said quietly.
The men heard him and answered with renewed energy. Whatever fate awaited them beyond that horizon, they were committed now. Ship, sea, wind, and crew had become joined in a single pursuit, driving after the mighty shape that moved before them through the endless blue.

The ship drove hard across the swells, her bow throwing up sheets of spray as she carved through the water. Every beam and spar complained under the strain, but there was something eager in her motion. She seemed to lean into the chase. Around the deck the crew worked with growing impatience, their attention returning again and again to the horizon.
A cry rang down from the masthead.
“There! Off the larboard bow!”
At once the mood changed. Men stopped what they were doing. A few stepped to the rail. Others shaded their eyes and searched the distance. The call had awakened the same hope in all of them.
The captain looked ahead without speaking. After a brief pause he gave his order.
“Hold the course. Keep your eyes on that water.”
Far off, a patch of white turbulence marked the sea. It was small enough to vanish if one looked away for a moment, yet everyone aboard understood what it meant. Something immense had surfaced and slipped below again.
The sight was enough. Days of uncertainty faded from memory. Fatigue no longer mattered. The chase, which had stretched on through long watches and empty horizons, suddenly felt alive again.
Wind pressed heavily into the sails. The vessel answered, surging forward. The rigging hummed, the masts flexed, and a faint shudder ran beneath every footstep on deck.
Then the lookout shouted once more.
This time nobody doubted.
The whale burst from the sea in a tremendous leap. Water streamed from its body as it rose, suspended for a heartbeat against the bright sky. Then it crashed down. The impact rolled across the water like cannon fire, and a wide circle of foam exploded around it.
A roar went up from the crew.
Men pointed and called to one another. Some laughed from sheer excitement. The creature seemed almost unreal—less a living thing than a sudden manifestation of the ocean itself.
The captain watched in silence.
At last he spoke.
“There is the one we seek.”
His words carried across the deck. No speech was needed after that. Every man knew his part. Drawn together by the same purpose, ship and crew pressed on through the wind and sunlight, following the dark shape moving ahead across the open sea.

Yet, indeed, I am in earnest. I speak nothing but the truth. He still loves me, and we are engaged.”  Jane looked at her doubtingly. “Oh, Lizzy! it cannot be. I know how much you dislike him.”  “You know nothing of the matter. _That_ is all to be forgot. Perhaps I did not always love him so well as I do now; but in such cases as these a good memory is unpardonable. This is the last time I shall ever remember it myself.”  Miss Bennet still looked all amazement. Elizabeth again, and more seriously, assured her of its truth. “Good heaven! can it be really so? Yet now I must believe you,” cried Jane. “My dear, dear Lizzy, I would, I do congratulate you; but are you certain--forgive the question--are you quite certain that you can be happy with him?”  “There can be no doubt of that. It is settled between us already that we are to be the happiest couple in the world. But are you pleased, Jane? Shall you like to have such a brother?”  “Very, very much. Nothing could give either Bingley or myself more delight. But we considered it, we talked of it as impossible. And do you really love him quite well enough? Oh, Lizzy! do anything rather than marry without affection. Are you quite sure that you feel what you ought to do?”  “Oh, yes! You will only think I feel _more_ than I ought to do when I tell you all.”  “What do you mean?”  “Why, I must confess that I love him better than I do Bingley. I am afraid you will be angry.”  “My dearest sister, now be, _be_ serious. I want to talk very seriously. Let me know everything that I am to know without delay. Will you tell me how long you have loved him?”  “It has been coming on so gradually, that I hardly know when it began; but I believe I must date it from my first seeing his beautiful grounds at Pemberley.”  Another entreaty that she would be serious, however, produced the desired effect; and she soon satisfied Jane by her solemn assurances of attachment. When convinced on that article, Miss Bennet had nothing further to wish. “Now I am quite happy,” said she, “for you will be as happy as myself. I always had a value for him. Were it for nothing but his love of you, I must always have esteemed him; but now, as Bingley’s friend and your husband, there can be only Bingley and yourself more dear to me. But, Lizzy, you have been very sly, very reserved with me. How little did you tell me of what passed at Pemberley and Lambton! I owe all that I know of it to another, not to you.”  Elizabeth told her the motives of her secrecy. She had been unwilling to mention Bingley; and the unsettled state of her own feelings had made her equally avoid the name of his friend: but now she would no longer conceal from her his share in Lydia’s marriage. All was acknowledged, and half the night spent in conversation. “Good gracious!” cried Mrs. Bennet, as she stood at a window the next morning, “if that disagreeable Mr. Darcy is not coming here again with our dear Bingley! What can he mean by being so tiresome as to be always coming here? I had no notion but he would go a-shooting, or something or other, and not disturb us with his company. What shall we do with him? Lizzy, you must walk out with him again, that he may not be in Bingley’s way.”  Elizabeth could hardly help laughing at so convenient a proposal; yet was really vexed that her mother should be always giving him such an epithet. As soon as they entered, Bingley looked at her so expressively, and shook hands with such warmth, as left no doubt of his good information; and he soon afterwards said aloud, “Mrs. Bennet, have you no more lanes hereabouts in which Lizzy may lose her way again to-day?”  “I advise Mr. Darcy, and Lizzy, and Kitty,” said Mrs. Bennet, “to walk to Oakham Mount this morning. It is a nice long walk, and Mr. Darcy has never seen the view.”  “It may do very well for the others,” replied Mr. Bingley; “but I am sure it will be too much for Kitty. Won’t it, Kitty?”  Kitty owned that she had rather stay at home.

“I assure you, there is no mistake,” said Marianne. “I have never spoken with greater sincerity. He has declared himself, and I have accepted him.”
Her sister stared in astonishment. “You cannot expect me to believe it immediately. Only a short time ago, you could scarcely mention his name with patience.”
“That belongs entirely to the past,” replied Marianne. “If I judged him harshly before, I was mistaken. I see him differently now. Indeed, I think it would be unjust to dwell upon former opinions when everything has so completely changed.”
Still her sister seemed uncertain.
“You are serious?” she asked at last. “Perfectly serious?”
“Perfectly. I would not trifle with you on such a subject.”
“My dear Marianne!” she cried, taking her hand. “Then I wish you every happiness. Yet tell me honestly—are you certain of your own feelings? Do you esteem him enough? Do you love him as you ought?”
“I do. More than I imagined possible.”
“Then I am content,” said her sister. “Nothing else is necessary. But you have concealed this wonderfully well. Looking back, I can now understand many things that puzzled me, though at the time I suspected nothing.”
“There was little to tell at first,” said Marianne. “Even to myself, the change was gradual. I did not know my own heart until quite recently.”
They continued talking for some time, reviewing conversations and incidents that now appeared in a different light. What had once seemed insignificant acquired fresh meaning. By the end of their discussion, every doubt had disappeared.
The following morning, their mother observed from the drawing-room window that two gentlemen were approaching the house.
“There they are again!” she exclaimed. “I declare, they seem determined to spend every day here. Well, I cannot complain, for one of them is always welcome. But the other surprises me by his persistence.”
Marianne coloured slightly and turned away.
A few moments later the visitors entered. One addressed her sister with unusual warmth, while the other approached Marianne with a composure that concealed, though only imperfectly, his satisfaction.
Their mother, entirely unaware of the true state of affairs, soon proposed an excursion.
“The weather is delightful,” she said. “You should all walk to the hill beyond the meadow. The view is particularly fine this time of year.”
Her younger daughter immediately declined.
“I would rather remain at home,” she said.
“Then the rest of you must go without her,” replied their mother. “There is no reason everyone should lose the pleasure.”
The suggestion was received with more approval than she realized, and several exchanged glances that were not wholly accidental.

“There is nothing uncertain about it,” said Marianne. “I mean every word. He asked me to marry him, and I agreed.”
Her sister looked at her for a long moment.
“Marianne, I can hardly comprehend it. Not very long ago you spoke of him with so little favour.”
Marianne smiled.
“Then you must forget what I said before. I have done so myself. People change their opinions when they learn more of one another. At least, I have.”
The explanation did not entirely remove her sister’s surprise.
“You truly mean it?”
“Entirely.”
“My dear Marianne,” she said, squeezing her hand, “then I am happy for you. But allow me one question. Are you quite certain this attachment is strong enough to make you happy?”
“It is stronger than I ever expected it could be.”
That answer seemed to satisfy her.
“Then I have no further concerns. Still, you have been remarkably secretive. I see now that there were signs, but I never understood them.”
“I scarcely understood them myself,” Marianne admitted. “The change happened so gradually that I cannot point to any exact moment when it began.”
The conversation continued well into the evening. Together they revisited recent events, discovering meanings that had escaped them at the time. By the end, there was little left unexplained.
The next morning their mother happened to glance from the drawing-room window.
“Well!” she cried. “Here come our visitors again.”
She watched them approach the house with evident amusement.
“One might think they had no other destination. Not that I object. Their company is always agreeable enough.”
Marianne lowered her eyes, hoping no one would notice the colour rising in her cheeks.
When the gentlemen entered, greetings were exchanged all around. One spoke with particular warmth to her sister, while the other directed most of his attention toward Marianne, though with studied restraint.
Before long their mother proposed a walk.
“You should all go to the hill beyond the meadow,” she said. “The air is pleasant, and the view is worth seeing.”
Her younger daughter immediately shook her head.
“No, thank you. I would rather stay here.”
“Then stay,” said her mother. “The rest need not be deprived of an outing on your account.”
The proposal appeared perfectly innocent. Yet the quiet looks exchanged across the room suggested that some among the party found it especially welcome.

You must not be left without occupation, and so, work and a definite aim set before you might, I fancy, be very beneficial.”  “Yes, yes; you are perfectly right.... I will make haste and return to the university: and then everything will go smoothly....”  Zossimov, who had begun his sage advice partly to make an effect before the ladies, was certainly somewhat mystified, when, glancing at his patient, he observed unmistakable mockery on his face. This lasted an instant, however. Pulcheria Alexandrovna began at once thanking Zossimov, especially for his visit to their lodging the previous night. “What! he saw you last night?” Raskolnikov asked, as though startled. “Then you have not slept either after your journey.”  “Ach, Rodya, that was only till two o’clock. Dounia and I never go to bed before two at home.”  “I don’t know how to thank him either,” Raskolnikov went on, suddenly frowning and looking down. “Setting aside the question of payment--forgive me for referring to it (he turned to Zossimov)--I really don’t know what I have done to deserve such special attention from you! I simply don’t understand it... and... and... it weighs upon me, indeed, because I don’t understand it. I tell you so candidly.”  “Don’t be irritated.” Zossimov forced himself to laugh. “Assume that you are my first patient--well--we fellows just beginning to practise love our first patients as if they were our children, and some almost fall in love with them. And, of course, I am not rich in patients.”  “I say nothing about him,” added Raskolnikov, pointing to Razumihin, “though he has had nothing from me either but insult and trouble.”  “What nonsense he is talking! Why, you are in a sentimental mood to-day, are you?” shouted Razumihin. If he had had more penetration he would have seen that there was no trace of sentimentality in him, but something indeed quite the opposite. But Avdotya Romanovna noticed it. She was intently and uneasily watching her brother. “As for you, mother, I don’t dare to speak,” he went on, as though repeating a lesson learned by heart. “It is only to-day that I have been able to realise a little how distressed you must have been here yesterday, waiting for me to come back.”  When he had said this, he suddenly held out his hand to his sister, smiling without a word. But in this smile there was a flash of real unfeigned feeling. Dounia caught it at once, and warmly pressed his hand, overjoyed and thankful. It was the first time he had addressed her since their dispute the previous day. The mother’s face lighted up with ecstatic happiness at the sight of this conclusive unspoken reconciliation. “Yes, that is what I love him for,” Razumihin, exaggerating it all, muttered to himself, with a vigorous turn in his chair. “He has these movements.”  “And how well he does it all,” the mother was thinking to herself. “What generous impulses he has, and how simply, how delicately he put an end to all the misunderstanding with his sister--simply by holding out his hand at the right minute and looking at her like that.... And what fine eyes he has, and how fine his whole face is!... He is even better looking than Dounia.... But, good heavens, what a suit--how terribly he’s dressed!... Vasya, the messenger boy in Afanasy Ivanitch’s shop, is better dressed! I could rush at him and hug him... weep over him--but I am afraid.... Oh, dear, he’s so strange! He’s talking kindly, but I’m afraid! Why, what am I afraid of?...”  “Oh, Rodya, you wouldn’t believe,” she began suddenly, in haste to answer his words to her, “how unhappy Dounia and I were yesterday! Now that it’s all over and done with and we are quite happy again--I can tell you. Fancy, we ran here almost straight from the train to embrace you and that woman--ah, here she is! Good morning, Nastasya!... She told us at once that you were lying in a high fever and had just run away from the doctor in delirium, and they were looking for you in the streets. You can’t imagine how we felt! I couldn’t help thinking of the tragic end of Lieutenant Potanchikov, a friend of your father’s--you can’t remember him, Rodya--who ran out in the same way in a high fever and fell into the well in the court-yard and they couldn’t pull him out till next day.

“You must not remain idle,” said the doctor. “A settled routine, some occupation, a purpose that carries you forward—those things would do you more good than any medicine I could prescribe.”
“Yes, perhaps you are right,” replied Nikolai after a pause. “I shall return to my studies soon. Once I am occupied again, everything may become simpler.”
The doctor nodded with satisfaction, though he was secretly disappointed. He had expected gratitude, perhaps even admiration for his advice. Instead, when he looked closely at his patient, he thought he detected a trace of irony in his expression. The impression vanished almost immediately.
His mother hastened to fill the silence.
“We owe you so much already,” she said warmly. “Especially after your kindness in visiting us last night.”
“You saw them last night?” Nikolai asked, turning toward the doctor. “Then you sacrificed your rest as well.”
“It was nothing of consequence,” said the doctor. “I was awake regardless.”
Nikolai lowered his eyes.
“To tell the truth, I do not understand why everyone troubles themselves on my account. You have all shown me more concern than I deserve. I ought to be grateful, yet I find myself embarrassed by it. It is difficult to accept what I cannot explain.”
“You are overthinking the matter,” said the doctor with a laugh. “Young physicians become attached to their early patients. We imagine ourselves responsible for them. Besides, I have not yet the luxury of being overwhelmed with work.”
“And he,” Nikolai added, indicating his friend, “has received nothing in return for his efforts except inconvenience.”
“What absurdity!” his friend exclaimed. “You speak as though we are strangers.”
His sister, however, watched him carefully. There was something in his manner that unsettled her. His words were kind, but they seemed rehearsed, as if he had prepared them beforehand.
He turned toward his mother.
“As for you, I only realized today how much anxiety I caused. You must have suffered greatly yesterday.”
Then, unexpectedly, he extended his hand toward his sister.
The gesture was simple, but the feeling behind it appeared genuine. She grasped his hand immediately. Relief brightened her face. Their disagreement of the previous day seemed suddenly less important.
The mother's eyes filled with tears.
“There,” she thought, “that is the son I know. Whatever troubles him, his heart remains good.”
Her gaze lingered on him. He looked exhausted. His clothes were worn, his appearance neglected. Yet to her he remained dearer than anyone in the world.
Suddenly she began speaking again.
“You cannot imagine how frightened we were yesterday. We arrived full of hope and were told at once that you had left in a feverish state. They were searching the streets for you. I thought of every terrible possibility. I scarcely knew what to do.”
The memory still shook her.
“For hours I could think of nothing else. Every story of illness or misfortune returned to me. I feared we might lose you before we had even seen you properly again.”
The room fell silent. No one doubted the sincerity of her distress.

“You need something to occupy you,” said the doctor. “Work, study, any clear purpose. A man recovers faster when he has somewhere to direct his thoughts.”
Nikolai gave a faint nod.
“Yes. I suppose that is true. I will return to the university before long. Perhaps once I fall back into a routine, things will seem less complicated.”
The doctor appeared pleased by the answer, though only for a moment. When he glanced at his patient, he thought he caught a hint of amusement—or perhaps mockery—in his face. It disappeared so quickly that he could not be certain.
His mother intervened before the silence became awkward.
“We are deeply grateful to you,” she said. “Especially for calling on us last evening.”
Nikolai looked up.
“You visited them last night? Then you have not had much rest yourself.”
“It was no great sacrifice,” the doctor replied lightly.
Nikolai frowned and looked down at his hands.
“What puzzles me is why everyone concerns themselves so much with me. I know I ought to be thankful. Still, I cannot quite understand it. The kindness itself becomes a burden when I feel I have done nothing to earn it.”
The doctor laughed.
“There is no mystery. Young doctors become attached to their first patients. We take an unreasonable interest in them. And, to be honest, I am not so busy that I can afford indifference.”
Nikolai gestured toward his friend.
“And he has endured even more. Trouble, inconvenience, endless demands—nothing else.”
“Nonsense,” his friend said at once. “You speak as though I have been performing a great service.”
His sister remained quiet. She had been watching him all this time. There was warmth in what he said, yet something restrained beneath it, something she could not quite name.
After a moment he turned to his mother.
“I understand now how worried you must have been yesterday. At least a little. I did not think of it before.”
Then he reached out his hand to his sister.
Nothing dramatic accompanied the gesture, but it was enough. She took his hand immediately, her face brightening with relief. The quarrel that had stood between them seemed to dissolve without another word.
Their mother could hardly contain her happiness.
For a few moments she simply looked at him. His clothes were shabby, his face drawn from fatigue, and yet she found herself thinking how dear he was to her. No outward disorder could alter that.
The thought stirred her into speech once more.
“You have no idea what yesterday was like for us. We arrived eager to see you and learned almost at once that you had gone out while ill. They said you were wandering about in a fever. People were searching everywhere.”
She paused, remembering.
“I imagined every possible disaster. The longer you were gone, the worse my fears became. I do not think I have ever passed such anxious hours.”
No one interrupted her. The depth of her distress was evident, and the room grew quiet as she spoke.

My journey had been my own suggestion, and Elizabeth therefore acquiesced, but she was filled with disquiet at the idea of my suffering, away from her, the inroads of misery and grief. It had been her care which provided me a companion in Clerval—and yet a man is blind to a thousand minute circumstances which call forth a woman’s sedulous attention. She longed to bid me hasten my return; a thousand conflicting emotions rendered her mute as she bade me a tearful, silent farewell. I threw myself into the carriage that was to convey me away, hardly knowing whither I was going, and careless of what was passing around. I remembered only, and it was with a bitter anguish that I reflected on it, to order that my chemical instruments should be packed to go with me. Filled with dreary imaginations, I passed through many beautiful and majestic scenes, but my eyes were fixed and unobserving. I could only think of the bourne of my travels and the work which was to occupy me whilst they endured. After some days spent in listless indolence, during which I traversed many leagues, I arrived at Strasburgh, where I waited two days for Clerval. He came. Alas, how great was the contrast between us! He was alive to every new scene, joyful when he saw the beauties of the setting sun, and more happy when he beheld it rise and recommence a new day. He pointed out to me the shifting colours of the landscape and the appearances of the sky. “This is what it is to live,” he cried; “now I enjoy existence! But you, my dear Frankenstein, wherefore are you desponding and sorrowful!” In truth, I was occupied by gloomy thoughts and neither saw the descent of the evening star nor the golden sunrise reflected in the Rhine. And you, my friend, would be far more amused with the journal of Clerval, who observed the scenery with an eye of feeling and delight, than in listening to my reflections. I, a miserable wretch, haunted by a curse that shut up every avenue to enjoyment. We had agreed to descend the Rhine in a boat from Strasburgh to Rotterdam, whence we might take shipping for London. During this voyage we passed many willowy islands and saw several beautiful towns. We stayed a day at Mannheim, and on the fifth from our departure from Strasburgh, arrived at Mainz. The course of the Rhine below Mainz becomes much more picturesque. The river descends rapidly and winds between hills, not high, but steep, and of beautiful forms. We saw many ruined castles standing on the edges of precipices, surrounded by black woods, high and inaccessible. This part of the Rhine, indeed, presents a singularly variegated landscape. In one spot you view rugged hills, ruined castles overlooking tremendous precipices, with the dark Rhine rushing beneath; and on the sudden turn of a promontory, flourishing vineyards with green sloping banks and a meandering river and populous towns occupy the scene. We travelled at the time of the vintage and heard the song of the labourers as we glided down the stream. Even I, depressed in mind, and my spirits continually agitated by gloomy feelings, even I was pleased. I lay at the bottom of the boat, and as I gazed on the cloudless blue sky, I seemed to drink in a tranquillity to which I had long been a stranger. And if these were my sensations, who can describe those of Henry? He felt as if he had been transported to Fairy-land and enjoyed a happiness seldom tasted by man. “I have seen,” he said, “the most beautiful scenes of my own country; I have visited the lakes of Lucerne and Uri, where the snowy mountains descend almost perpendicularly to the water, casting black and impenetrable shades, which would cause a gloomy and mournful appearance were it not for the most verdant islands that relieve the eye by their gay appearance; I have seen this lake agitated by a tempest, when the wind tore up whirlwinds of water and gave you an idea of what the water-spout must be on the great ocean; and the waves dash with fury the base of the mountain, where the priest and his mistress were overwhelmed by an avalanche and where their dying voices are still said to be heard amid the pauses of the nightly wind; I have seen the mountains of La Valais, and the Pays de Vaud; but this country, Victor, pleases me more than all those wonders.

The decision to travel had been mine alone, though Clara consented to it with visible reluctance. She feared less for the hardships of the road than for the solitude into which I willingly cast myself. Her concern had procured for me the companionship of Ashton, whose cheerful temper she believed might draw me from my habitual gloom. Yet no kindness could wholly overcome the burden that oppressed my thoughts.
When at last I departed, she stood silent beside the gate, her composure maintained only by effort. I attempted words of reassurance, but they died before reaching my lips. The carriage bore me away through valleys bright with summer light, across hills clothed in forests, and beside rivers that reflected the clear sky. These scenes passed before me almost unnoticed. My mind remained fixed upon the object of my journey and the unfinished labor that awaited me.
Several days later I arrived at a small city upon the frontier and there remained until Ashton joined me. The contrast between us was striking. Every novelty delighted him. He spoke with enthusiasm of distant towers, crowded marketplaces, and the varied character of the people we encountered. Where I saw only passing forms, he discovered endless occasions for admiration.
“You observe nothing,” he said one evening as we walked beside the river. “The world spreads its treasures before you, yet you move among them as though in a dream.”
I could not deny the justice of his remark. The beauty that animated him seemed unable to penetrate the cloud that surrounded me. Nevertheless, I listened while he described the changing colors of the sunset and the reflections trembling upon the water. His pleasure was sincere and infectious, and at moments I almost believed myself capable of sharing it.
We continued our route by boat, allowing the current to carry us through a succession of fertile districts. Vineyards descended the hillsides in ordered rows. Ancient fortresses crowned rocky heights and overlooked the winding river below. In some places the landscape appeared severe and lonely; in others it opened suddenly into broad fields dotted with villages and church spires.
The season of harvest had begun. Along the banks we heard laborers singing as they worked, their voices carried faintly across the water. Children ran among the orchards, and carts laden with fruit moved slowly along the roads. Such signs of ordinary happiness awakened in me a feeling I had not experienced for many months. Though my anxieties remained, they seemed less absolute than before.
One afternoon, as our vessel drifted beneath a range of wooded cliffs, I reclined against the railing and watched clouds gathering in the western sky. The air grew cooler, and a gentle wind stirred the surface of the river. For a brief interval my thoughts loosened their hold upon me. I ceased to dwell upon the future and surrendered myself to the quiet motion of the voyage.
Ashton, meanwhile, appeared enchanted by everything around him. He spoke of distant countries he hoped to visit and of the many wonders still hidden beyond the horizon. His imagination transformed every scene into the beginning of some greater adventure. Observing his delight, I could not help envying the ease with which he embraced the world.
“Perhaps,” he said as evening descended, “the greatest privilege is not to discover new lands, but to retain the ability to wonder at them.”
The remark lingered with me long after the light had faded and the river had vanished into darkness.

The journey was my own idea, though Clara agreed to it reluctantly. What troubled her was not the travel itself but the thought of my being left alone with my thoughts. She had arranged for Ashton to accompany me, hoping his good spirits would keep me from sinking further into melancholy. It was a thoughtful plan, though at the time I doubted anyone could lighten the mood in which I found myself.
On the morning of my departure she tried to appear calm. A few words passed between us, neither of us saying quite what we meant. Then I was gone. The road led through pleasant country—wooded hills, bright rivers, fields warmed by the sun—but I paid little attention to any of it. My mind was occupied elsewhere, fixed on the purpose that had drawn me from home.
I waited several days at a frontier town before Ashton arrived. From the moment we resumed our travels together, the difference between us became impossible to ignore. Everything interested him. A marketplace, a ruined tower, even a crowded ferry landing could hold his attention for an hour. He had a way of finding something worth admiring wherever he looked.
One evening, while we walked beside the river, he laughed and told me I passed through the world half asleep.
“There is beauty everywhere,” he said. “You look directly at it and never seem to see it.”
The criticism was fair. I had become so accustomed to my own worries that I scarcely noticed what surrounded me. Still, as he pointed out the fading colors in the sky and the reflections scattered across the water, I found myself listening with unexpected interest.
We traveled onward by boat. The river wound through fertile country where vineyards climbed the slopes and old stone fortresses stood watch above the banks. Sometimes the scenery was wild and austere, marked by cliffs and dark forests. A bend in the river would reveal an entirely different prospect—open fields, villages, and church spires rising from among clusters of trees.
Harvest season was underway. Songs drifted from the fields. We saw wagons heavy with fruit and workers gathering crops beneath the late afternoon sun. There was something reassuring in these ordinary scenes. For the first time in many months, I felt a faint easing of the pressure that had weighed upon me.
One afternoon clouds gathered over the western hills as we floated beneath a line of wooded heights. The breeze freshened, rippling the surface of the river. Leaning against the railing, I watched the changing light and, for a little while, forgot to dwell on what lay ahead. The relief was brief, but it was real.
Ashton remained delighted by everything. Every town suggested a story, every distant road another destination. He spoke eagerly of future travels and places he hoped one day to see. Listening to him, I envied the openness with which he met the world.
As darkness settled over the water, he made a remark that stayed with me.
“People spend their lives searching for wonders,” he said. “The rarer gift may be never losing the ability to be surprised by them.”
The river disappeared into the night, but those words remained long after the last light had gone.

(CNN)Over the past decade, Vladimir Putin's resurgent Russia has been a perpetual concern for many in the West.These concerns are not baseless. It's undeniable that Putin and the President's inner circle have grown in confidence during this period. Whether it be annexing parts of another nation, backing a dictator in a foreign war or poisoning dissidents on Russian soil, Putin's Kremlin seemingly no longer seeks validation from a West that has allowed Moscow's belligerence to grow with little effect on his behavior. The world has been reminded of Russia's confidence in recent weeks. As gas prices soar across Europe due to a reduced supply of Russian gas and Putin severs his nation's loose diplomatic ties to NATO, it's worth examining how gravely Western policymakers have misread Putin and ignored his willingness to use the weapons at his disposal. The Europe-wide energy crisis reveals a very powerful tool that provides Russia with leverage in its relationship with Europe: its gas reserves.It is no secret that many European countries, including Germany, are reliant on Russian supplies of natural gas. The recent shortages have hammered home not just the economic, but geopolitical risks of this dependency.Read MoreWhile Russia is meeting its existing obligations to supply European countries, analysts say it could increase exports to enable storage ahead of what could be a cold winter, thus reducing costs and calming nerves.Europe's energy crunch is giving Putin the upper hand The question from the Russian perspective is, why should we? Moscow is still awaiting German regulatory approval for Nord Stream 2, a controversial pipeline that would connect Russia to Germany and supply large amounts of gas to Western Europe. "If the German regulator hands its clearance for supplies tomorrow, supplies of 17.5 billion cubic metres will start the day after tomorrow," Putin told a televised forum on Thursday, blaming the recent gas crisis and high prices on the EU's energy policy, Reuters reported.The pipeline is controversial because many see it as a geopolitical influence project for Moscow, a fear that wasn't tempered when Russia's Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak said earlier this month that "early completion of the certification" for Nord Stream 2 would help "cool off the current situation."Aside from the financial and geopolitical advantages that might come from Europe's reliance on Russian gas, it also helps play into a domestic political narrative that has evolved over time in Russia: The West keeps getting things wrong.The Slavyanskaya compressor station, located in Russia's Leningrad region, is the starting point of the Nord Stream 2 pipeline. "The core of this narrative is that Europe and the West needs to rethink its broken policies, be they on energy, foreign intervention or nation building," says Oleg Ignatov, a senior analyst at Crisis Group in Russia."Ten years ago, this argument was more defensive, as the Kremlin wanted to protect itself from criticism from Western governments or NGOs. But now Russia can argue that Western policies failed in Libya, Syria and now Afghanistan so badly that Russia's approach has actually been correct all along," he adds.Western failure and Russian success are, of course, relative to the priorities of each party. Putin has said that the fall of the Soviet Union was the "greatest geopolitical tragedy" of the 20th century.When you factor this into so much of Putin's behavior over the last decade -- annexing Crimea, gaslighting the West over military action in Syria by denying Russia's activity, stirring tensions between NATO and Turkey -- it becomes easy to build an image of a leader trying to restore pride to his country and only too happy to exploit opportunities provided by naïve global counterparts.Russian President Vladimir Putin and Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan shake hands at the opening ceremony of the Turkstream gas pipeline project in in Istanbul, Turkey, in January 2020."Since the end of the Cold War, many in Putin's generation have believed it was still in a political war with the West," says Mark Galeotti, honorary professor at University College London, currently based in Moscow."This became more acute after the annexation of Crimea in 2014 and is why you now see a Russia more comfortable with putting troops on international borders, spreading disinformation and going after political dissidents. As far as they're concerned, this is a war footing," Galeotti says, before adding that "for the West, however, Russia is extremely irksome, but not actually that much of a threat."Some argue that Putin's relatively limited threat has bred a lackluster Western policy in the face of Russian aggression. This, in turn, has meant the Russian President can carry out hostile acts with very few consequences.The logic goes something like this. Russian agents poisoning a Russian ex-spy on British soil is of course sinister and menacing. However, it poses little actual threat to the UK, but going further than placing sanctions on individuals close to Putin could be more hassle that it's worth.Russia says Berlin could ease natural gas crisis by approving Nord Stream 2This, potentially, plays into Putin's hands, as it allows him to spin these events as proof that he is an untouchable strongman sticking it to the West, a theme he warmed to in a speech at the annual Valdai Discussion Club in Sochi on Thursday evening in which he slammed the US for the "mess" it created in Afghanistan."The West's long-term failure has been treating each hostile act as an isolated incident, rather than seeing the overall pattern of a Russia that has no desire or interest in playing by its rules," says Keir Giles, a senior fellow at Chatham House and author of the forthcoming book "How Russia Gets Its Way."This, Giles argues, is at the heart of everything happening right now."Russia is becoming more open and direct. When Russia exploits Europe's gas crisis to force through its Nord Stream pipeline project, or cuts all remaining links with NATO, it's done openly and there is no longer a pretense​ that Moscow is working towards good relations with the West. It's the same pattern that we see domestically within Russia -- the increased repression is now overt and accelerating, because the Kremlin no longer cares."Limited consequences for the West, of course, provide little comfort to those who oppose Putin inside and outside of Russia."Putin is an opportunist. NATO's disunity is the greatest gift he can receive," says Riho Terras, former commander of the Estonian Defense Forces. "German reliance on Russian gas is a problem for those of us who share a border as it undermines unity. Brexit might be good for the UK, but it raises questions of a European army which would obviously be weaker than NATO." Some believe that Putin's greatest asset has been hysteria and overstating of the threat he poses in some part of the West, combined with limited pushback from powerful nations, including the US, for his sincere hostility.Russia responsible for Alexander Litvinenko's assassination, European court rules"Every time an opportunity appears, he will take it. It happened in Ukraine, it happened in Georgia. He only understands strong messages and if we keep showing disunity he will respond in kind. He is a streetfighter. The West is trying to figure skate around Russia, but Putin plays ice hockey," says Terras.Opposition figures in Russia do believe that the West can take action that could weaken Putin's position."Personal sanctions against the people close to Putin, who are involved in corruption and human rights abuse, will go a long way towards achieving this goal," says Vladimir Ashurkov, an opposition politician and Executive Director of opposition leader Alexey Navalny's Anti-Corruption Foundation. However, the myth that has been fed by Western confusion and inaction as to exactly who Putin is, and what he wants, has gone some way to creating a domestic colossus who can increasingly act with impunity in a way that only serves to feed the myth surrounding him in Russia.For all the Russia hysteria over the past decade, it might be that the West's reluctance to really understand Putin has helped create the most dangerous version of the man that was ever possible.

Over the past decade, Xi Jinping's increasingly assertive China has been a constant source of anxiety for many in the West.
These concerns are deeply rooted in reality. It is undeniable that Xi and the Chinese Communist Party's inner circle have grown significantly in confidence during this era. Whether it entails militarizing artificial islands in the South China Sea, suppressing democratic institutions in Hong Kong, or employing economic coercion against sovereign nations, Beijing seemingly no longer seeks approval from a Western world that has allowed China's expansionism to proceed with minimal consequences to its behavior.
The global community has been starkly reminded of China's confidence in recent months. As technology sectors scramble due to a restricted supply of critical minerals and Beijing intensifies its military posturing around Taiwan, it is crucial to examine how significantly Western leaders have misunderstood Xi and ignored his readiness to utilize the geopolitical leverage he possesses.
The global supply chain vulnerability reveals a profoundly powerful tool that provides China with leverage in its relationship with the West: its dominance over rare earth elements.
It is no secret that many Western nations, including the United States, are heavily reliant on Chinese exports of the minerals necessary to manufacture everything from smartphones to electric vehicles. The recent export restrictions have emphasized not just the economic, but the geopolitical risks of this profound dependency.
While China continues to participate in global trade, analysts note that it could easily ease export quotas to stabilize global manufacturing ahead of a major technological transition, thereby reducing costs and calming international markets.
The question from the Chinese perspective is, why should they? Beijing is currently leveraging its economic power to discourage foreign interference in its domestic affairs. "If Washington removes its tariffs and technology sanctions tomorrow, normalized trade flows of these crucial components will resume the day after," state media outlets recently implied, blaming the current supply chain crisis and inflation on American protectionist policies.
These trade dynamics are controversial because many view them as a geopolitical influence mechanism for Beijing, a concern that was not alleviated when Chinese officials recently indicated that "mutual respect" regarding Taiwan would help "stabilize the global economic situation."
Aside from the financial and geopolitical advantages derived from the world's reliance on Chinese manufacturing, it also feeds into a domestic political narrative that has evolved significantly over time in China: The West is in a state of terminal decline.
"The core of this narrative is that the United States and its allies are paralyzed by internal divisions and failing democratic systems," says a senior analyst focusing on East Asian geopolitics. "A decade ago, this argument was largely defensive, utilized by the CCP to shield itself from Western lectures on human rights. But today, Beijing can point to political polarization in the US and sluggish economic growth in Europe to argue that the Chinese authoritarian model is actually superior."
Western decline and Chinese resurgence are, of course, viewed through the specific historical lens of the CCP. Xi has repeatedly emphasized the necessity of national rejuvenation to overcome the "Century of Humiliation."
When you factor this historical grievance into so much of Xi's behavior over the last decade—building up a blue-water navy, utilizing debt-trap diplomacy in developing nations, and aggressively confronting foreign militaries in international airspace—it becomes easy to construct an image of a leader determined to restore his nation to its rightful place as a global hegemon, perfectly willing to exploit the hesitation of naïve global counterparts.
"Since the 2008 financial crisis, many in the CCP leadership have believed that the balance of global power was permanently shifting," notes a prominent scholar of Chinese politics. "This became more acute after the consolidation of Xi's power, which is why you now see a China more comfortable with coercive diplomacy, intellectual property theft, and aggressive maritime patrols. As far as Beijing is concerned, this is a strategic competition," the scholar says, adding that "for the West, however, China's rise is viewed as an economic challenge rather than a comprehensive systemic threat."
Some argue that this failure to perceive the true nature of the threat has bred a lackluster Western policy in the face of Chinese aggression. This, in turn, has meant the Chinese Premier can carry out hostile acts with very few consequences.
"The West's long-term failure has been treating each aggressive maneuver as an isolated incident, rather than recognizing the overall pattern of a rising power that has no interest in abiding by the current rules-based international order," argues a senior fellow at a major geopolitical think tank.
This is at the heart of the current geopolitical tension. China is becoming much more open and direct in its ambitions. When China leverages its critical mineral monopoly to punish political rivals, or aggressively sanctions European politicians, it is done overtly. There is no longer a pretense that Beijing is working to assimilate into the Western liberal order.
Limited consequences from the West provide little comfort to those who share a geographic proximity to China. Regional allies warn that Western disunity is Xi's greatest advantage. Every time an opportunity arises to divide the US from its allies, Beijing takes it.
Ultimately, the myth that has been fed by Western confusion and economic entanglement has gone a long way toward creating a domestic colossus who can increasingly act with impunity. For all the anxiety over China's rise, it may be that the West's reluctance to truly understand Xi Jinping has helped create the most formidable version of the leader that was ever possible.

For a decade now, the West has wrung its hands over Xi Jinping. They have good reason to.
Xi and his inner circle have spent the last ten years growing steadily bolder. They've militarized artificial reefs in the South China Sea, dismantled Hong Kong's democratic guardrails, and weaponized trade to punish their critics. Through it all, Beijing has stopped pretending it cares what the West thinks. Why should it? Expansionism has cost them very little.
Lately, the world has been getting a masterclass in Chinese confidence. Tech supply chains are bottlenecking as Beijing squeezes critical mineral exports. Fighter jets routinely cross the median line in the Taiwan Strait. And as this happens, it’s worth asking how Western policymakers misread Xi so completely—and why they kept ignoring the weapons he had quietly stockpiled.
Chief among those weapons is a staggering leverage over the global supply chain, specifically rare earth elements.
It’s the worst-kept secret in global manufacturing. The US and Europe are hopelessly dependent on Chinese minerals to build everything from an iPhone to an electric vehicle. The recent export curbs didn't just expose an economic vulnerability; they laid bare a massive geopolitical risk.
Beijing could ease these quotas tomorrow. Analysts point out that flooding the market would stabilize global manufacturing and calm panicked tech sectors ahead of the green energy transition. But from Xi's perspective, what's the incentive?
China is using its market dominance to exact political silence. State media hasn't been subtle about it, heavily implying that if Washington drops its tech sanctions and tariffs, the minerals will flow again. They blame the current supply chain mess entirely on American protectionism. It's a strategy designed to force a choice: secure your economies, or keep lecturing us about Taiwan. Chinese officials even suggested recently that showing "mutual respect" regarding their territorial claims would magically "stabilize" the global economy.
But this isn't just about global leverage. It feeds a story the CCP has been telling its citizens for years: the West is dying.
"The core of the narrative is that democracies are paralyzed and failing," one East Asian geopolitical analyst told me recently. Ten years ago, the CCP used that argument defensively, mostly to deflect human rights criticism. Now, they point to America's polarized elections and Europe's stagnant growth as proof. They aren't just defending their authoritarian model anymore; they're marketing it as a superior alternative.
You have to look at this through the lens of the CCP's historical grievances. Xi's entire mandate is built on the idea of "national rejuvenation"—avenging the "Century of Humiliation."
Look at his track record through that specific filter. The blue-water navy build-up, the debt-trap loans in the global south, the aggressive intercepts of foreign aircraft. It’s the playbook of a leader desperate to claw back his country's status as the center of the world, perfectly happy to run over naïve Western diplomats to get there.
"Since the 2008 financial crisis, the CCP leadership decided the global balance of power had permanently fractured," notes a prominent scholar based in the region. Once Xi consolidated power, he accelerated the timeline. "That’s why you see coercive diplomacy and intellectual property theft happening out in the open. Beijing views this as a zero-sum strategic competition. The West is still trying to manage it like a tricky trade dispute."
That disconnect—treating China as an economic rival rather than a systemic threat—has bred a uniquely weak Western foreign policy. Xi takes aggressive steps, the West protests, and nothing fundamentally changes.
The primary failure of the US and Europe has been treating each Chinese provocation as a one-off event. They miss the forest for the trees. Beijing has absolutely no interest in integrating into the American-led international order. They want to rewrite the rules entirely.
And they are getting louder about it. When Beijing cuts off minerals to punish a political rival or slaps sanctions on European parliamentarians, they do it proudly. The era of "hide your strength, bide your time" is dead.
For the nations actually bordering China, the West's slow learning curve is terrifying. Regional allies constantly warn that Washington's internal disunity is a gift to Beijing. Xi is an opportunist; he exploits every crack in Western alliances.
By refusing to disentangle our economies, and by failing to understand exactly what Xi wants, the West has inadvertently helped build a geopolitical colossus. We spent ten years worrying about China's rise, but our reluctance to actually confront it may have just handed Xi the keys to the future.

(CNN)Poland will have to pay a €500,000 ($586,000) fine for each day it continues extracting coal at an open-pit mine near the Czech and German borders, Europe's top court ruled Monday. The European Commission ordered Poland to stop operations at the Turów lignite mine in May following a lawsuit launched by the Czech Republic. Poland has so far failed to comply with the order and the Polish government said in a statement Monday it would continue to do so -- even though the European Court of Justice ruling means it will have to pay the daily penalty."The Polish government will not close the KWB Turów mine. From the very beginning, we were of the opinion that the suspension of the works of the mine in Turów would threaten the stability of the Polish power system," Polish government spokesperson Piotr Mueller said in a statement. Not a single G20 country is in line with the Paris Agreement on climate, analysis showsTurów supplies power to around 2.3 million Polish households, according to PGE, the state-controlled company that runs the mine. It is also an important local employer, with one in every two jobs in the region directly or indirectly dependent on it.But it has long been at a center of an international dispute. The Turów mine is located in a tiny, appendix-like tip of Poland, right at the border with Germany and the Czech Republic. The two countries have been campaigning to have the mine shut over environmental concerns -- even though they also use coal for power. Read MoreAccording to the International Energy Agency, 80% of Poland's domestic energy came from coal in 2019, compared to 54% in the Czech Republic and 43% in Germany.The Turów lignite mine in southwestern Poland in 2020.The conflict escalated when PGE made it clear it was planning to expand the digging closer towards the Czech border, a plan that has caused tension between the two longtime allies. Negotiations on the issue have failed, with Poland refusing to change the plans. The Czech government has praised the court's decision on Monday. Foreign Minister Jakub Kulhanek said on Twitter he welcomed the judgment and added his country was ready to negotiate with Poland on the future of the mine -- although he stressed environmental issues would be a priority..Residents on the Czech border are worried about the mine's impact on ground water levels, dust and noise. "The wells are drying out. As the mine becomes deeper and inches closer to the border, more ground water flows away. There are people who -- in the 21st century -- are now left without water," Milan Starec, one of the residents on the Czech side, told CNN last year.This legislation is an attack on the free press in PolandPGE and the Polish government say the planned mining area is within the boundaries stipulated in the original 1994 permit. PGE says its studies have shown a minimal impact on ground water levels. To prevent drainage, the company is also building an underground barrier on the site.The climate implications of the mine are also significant. Lignite, sometimes called brown coal due to its color, is the least efficient and most polluting type of coal. It has lower fuel value and higher emissions intensity compared to the geologically older hard coal that is mined underground. The European Union has said it wants to phase out coal by 2030 -- something Poland said it won't be able to do, sparking yet another disagreement with the bloc. CNN's Antonia Mortensen contributed to this report.

(CNN)Germany will have to pay a €600,000 ($640,000) fine for each day it continues clearing land for the Garzweiler open-pit coal mine expansion in North Rhine-Westphalia, a European tribunal ruled Monday.
The European Commission ordered Germany to halt operations at the site following a lawsuit launched by environmental coalitions and neighboring municipalities. Germany has so far failed to comply with the order, and the regional government stated Monday it would continue the expansion -- even though the court ruling means it will incur the daily penalty.
"The state government will not halt the Garzweiler operations. From the very beginning, we maintained that suspending the extraction would threaten the immediate energy security of the industrial region," a regional government spokesperson said in a statement.
The Garzweiler mine supplies fuel to several major power plants, according to RWE, the energy company that operates the site. It is also a significant economic driver, with thousands of jobs in the region tied directly to the fossil fuel sector.
But it has long been at the center of an intense environmental dispute. The mine is located in a densely populated area of western Germany, near the Dutch border. Activists and neighboring countries have been campaigning to have the expansion halted over climate concerns -- even amid a broader European energy crunch.
According to the International Energy Agency, Germany still relied heavily on coal for power generation during recent winter months, despite ambitious long-term climate targets.
The conflict escalated when RWE made it clear it was planning to demolish several local villages, including Lützerath, to reach the coal beneath them, a plan that has caused widespread protests. Negotiations on the issue have failed, with the company refusing to change its timeline.
Environmental groups have praised the court's decision on Monday. A leading climate advocate said on Twitter they welcomed the judgment and added that the coalition is ready to monitor compliance, stressing that emission reductions must be the priority.
Residents near the site are worried about the mine's impact on air quality, structural stability, and the permanent destruction of historical land. "The villages are being swallowed. As the massive excavators inch closer to our homes, the community is torn apart. There are people who are losing their ancestral homes to a fuel we are supposed to be phasing out," one local resident told CNN last year.
RWE and the German authorities say the planned mining area is within the boundaries stipulated in decades-old permits. RWE says its studies have shown the expansion is necessary to bridge the gap until renewable sources are fully scaled. To compensate, the company is also investing in wind and solar projects.
The climate implications of the mine are also significant. Lignite, the type of brown coal extracted at Garzweiler, is the least efficient and most polluting type of coal. It has lower fuel value and higher emissions intensity compared to other fossil fuels. The European Union has said it wants to phase out coal by 2030 -- a target Germany has officially embraced, but short-term realities have sparked yet another disagreement over the transition speed.

Europe's top court just handed Germany a massive ultimatum: stop expanding the Garzweiler open-pit coal mine, or pay €600,000 every single day.
The ruling came down on Monday. It follows a fierce lawsuit from environmental coalitions and local municipalities, which resulted in a European Commission order to immediately halt land-clearing operations in North Rhine-Westphalia. Yet, German authorities aren't backing down. Despite the staggering daily fine, the regional government made it perfectly clear that the excavators will keep digging.
"We are not halting Garzweiler," a spokesperson for the regional government stated bluntly. Their argument is essentially that pulling the plug on the mine right now would severely compromise the energy grid across the country's industrial heartland.
For RWE, the energy giant operating the mine, Garzweiler is a critical asset. It feeds several major power plants and supports thousands of jobs in an area historically reliant on fossil fuels. But the site has become a massive environmental flashpoint. Carved into a densely populated stretch of western Germany not far from the Dutch border, the mine's relentless creep has sparked international backlash—which is especially awkward given the continent's broader push toward green energy.
Germany might have ambitious climate goals, but the International Energy Agency notes the country still leans heavily on coal to keep the lights on during the winter.
Things reached a boiling point when RWE confirmed it would bulldoze several villages, including the tiny hamlet of Lützerath, to get at the coal underneath. The plan triggered massive protests. Attempts to negotiate a compromise ultimately collapsed as RWE refused to budge on its timeline.
Unsurprisingly, environmental groups are thrilled with Monday's ruling. Climate advocates took to Twitter to celebrate, promising to hold the government’s feet to the fire regarding compliance and carbon cuts.
On the ground, the anxiety is palpable. Locals worry about toxic dust, the structural safety of their remaining homes, and the total erasure of centuries-old communities.
"They are swallowing our villages whole," one resident said in an interview last year. "The machines just keep getting closer. It's surreal to lose your family home in the 21st century for a dirty fuel we're supposed to be leaving behind."
Both RWE and German officials defend the expansion, pointing out that the digging is fully covered by permits issued decades ago. The company insists this coal is a necessary bridge to keep power flowing while they scale up wind and solar alternatives.
Still, the climate cost is impossible to ignore. Garzweiler produces lignite—a soft, brown coal that ranks as the absolute dirtiest and least efficient fossil fuel on the market. It flies directly in the face of the European Union's push to phase out coal by 2030. Germany has technically signed on to that goal, but as the active excavators at Garzweiler show, getting there is proving to be a messy, deeply contested process.

(CNN)The Democratic Party's early 2000s dream of an emerging majority based on a diversifying electorate has run into reality. Democrats lost the 2016 presidential election, and they barely won in 2020. Part of their problem was declining support among White voters.But the 2020 election also pointed to another problem: Hispanic voters (who are growing as a portion of the electorate) moving toward the Republican Party. Recent polling -- and now this week's Texas primaries -- show that these Republican gains don't seem to be going away anytime soon.Texas is a heavily Hispanic state relative to the country as a whole. There are 16 counties in Texas where Hispanics make up at least 80% of the citizen voting age population, according to the latest data available from Census Bureau. The county with the highest percentage of Hispanic adult citizens (Starr County) backed now-President Joe Biden by 5 points in 2020, after voting for Hillary Clinton by 60 points four years earlier. (That's not a misprint. It really was a 55-point swing.)Regularly scheduled primaries, of course, don't feature a Democratic candidate versus a Republican candidate. We can, however, look at the relative turnout between the Democratic and Republican primaries. This is instructive in Texas where voters don't register by party and can choose which party's primary to vote in.Democrats look to win back Latino voters after Trump's inroads in South TexasAll told, 27% of the Texans who voted Tuesday in the 16 most Hispanic counties cast a ballot in the Republican primary. This may not seem like a lot but consider that in 2018, just 15% of those who voted in either the Democratic or Republican primary cast a ballot on the Republican side.Read MoreIn Starr County, 24% of primary votes were cast on the Republican side Tuesday. It was basically nothing in 2018, with a mere 0.2% of primary votes being cast on the Republican side. That's a 24-point shift.Compare this with what happened statewide in Texas this year. There was slight movement toward Republicans: Of the votes cast on the Democratic or Republican side, 65% were Republican primary votes. In 2018, it was 60%. This was a 4-point shift without rounding.Put another way, the shift in Republican primary participation in those 16 heavily Hispanic counties was nearly three times the statewide shift.More signs of a shiftTexas, though, is only one state. Further, it's just a primary.But even on the national level, Texas does not seem to be that much of an outlier.While it's not clear that Hispanic Americans have moved even more toward the Republicans relative to how Americans overall are shifting, it's clear that Republicans are holding their gains from 2020.Trump made big in-roads in Hispanic areas across the nationNationally, the three polls that meet CNN standards for reporting (CNN/SSRS, Fox and Quinnipiac University) have produced generic congressional ballot results since the beginning of the year and provided a crosstab on Hispanic voters' midterm preferences. The generic ballot asks respondents some form of the following question: "If the elections for Congress were held today, would you vote for the Democratic or Republican Party?"Democrats have held a 23-point advantage among Hispanics on the generic congressional ballot in the average of these polls so far this year.An average of three estimates of how Americans voted in House races in 2020 (Catalist, the CNN exit poll and the Pew Research Center) found that Democrats won the House vote by about 28 points in 2020 among Hispanics.This 5-point shift toward Republicans among Hispanics is in line with the 5-point shift we see among voters overall -- Republicans ahead by 2 points on the generic congressional ballot, compared with losing the national House vote by 3 points in 2020.The key thing to realize, though, is that Democrats did worse -- and Republicans did better -- among Hispanic voters in 2020 House races relative to the national vote than in any House election since 2004. So while not losing additional ground is not a bad thing for Democrats, it's not a good thing either.The Biden factorIf anything, the picture gets better for Republicans when you examine Biden's popularity. Across the CNN, Fox and Quinnipiac polls this year, the President's net approval rating averages +2 points with Hispanic Americans. That's 17 points better than his net approval rating with voters overall in these polls (-15 points).CNN Poll: Most Biden detractors say he's done nothing they like since becoming presidentIn the 2020 election, Biden's margin with Hispanic voters was about 23 points better than it was overall.So compared with 2020, Biden is doing worse with Hispanics relative to how he is doing with voters overall. That's notable given that earlier in his presidency, Biden was doing significantly better among Hispanics relative to how he was doing overall and compared with how he did in 2020.This fits with an analysis from FiveThirtyEight's Geoffrey Skelley, which found that Biden's approval rating had fallen disproportionately among Hispanics over the course of his presidency.The fact that the political preferences of Hispanic Americans are jumping around may get at something larger: Their votes are up for grabs more so than the average voter's. While they may still be more Democratic-leaning, both parties have a good chance of making up ground among the Hispanic electorate.I'd expect a lot of attention to be focused on this growing bloc of voters in the midterms.

(CNN)The Democratic Party's early 2000s dream of an emerging majority based on a diversifying electorate has run into reality. Democrats lost the 2016 presidential election, and they barely won in 2020. Part of their problem was declining support among White voters.
But the 2020 election also pointed to another problem: Asian American voters (who are growing as a portion of the electorate) moving toward the Republican Party. Recent polling -- and now this week's New York primaries -- show that these Republican gains don't seem to be going away anytime soon.
New York has a significant Asian American population relative to the country as a whole. There are five assembly districts in New York City where Asian Americans make up at least 50% of the citizen voting age population, according to the latest data available from the Census Bureau. The district with the highest percentage of Asian American adult citizens (in Flushing, Queens) backed now-President Joe Biden by 15 points in 2020, after voting for Hillary Clinton by 40 points four years earlier. (That's not a misprint. It really was a 25-point swing.)
Regularly scheduled primaries, of course, don't feature a Democratic candidate versus a Republican candidate. We can, however, look at the relative turnout between the Democratic and Republican primaries. This is instructive in New York where, despite closed primaries, enthusiasm shifts are often visible in party turnout patterns.
All told, 32% of the New Yorkers who voted Tuesday in the five most heavily Asian districts cast a ballot in the Republican primary. This may not seem like a lot but consider that in 2018, just 14% of those who voted in either the Democratic or Republican primary cast a ballot on the Republican side. 
In the Flushing-based district, 35% of primary votes were cast on the Republican side Tuesday. It was basically nothing in 2018, with a mere 8% of primary votes being cast on the Republican side. That's a 27-point shift. 
Compare this with what happened citywide in New York this year. There was slight movement toward Republicans: Of the votes cast on the Democratic or Republican side, 22% were Republican primary votes. In 2018, it was 18%. This was a 4-point shift without rounding.
Put another way, the shift in Republican primary participation in those five heavily Asian districts was nearly seven times the citywide shift.
New York, though, is only one state. Further, it's just a primary.
But even on the national level, New York does not seem to be that much of an outlier.
While it's not clear that Asian Americans have moved even more toward the Republicans relative to how Americans overall are shifting, it's clear that Republicans are holding their gains from 2020. 
Nationally, the three polls that meet CNN standards for reporting (CNN/SSRS, Fox and Quinnipiac University) have produced generic congressional ballot results since the beginning of the year and provided a crosstab on Asian American voters' midterm preferences. The generic ballot asks respondents some form of the following question: "If the elections for Congress were held today, would you vote for the Democratic or Republican Party?" 
Democrats have held a 27-point advantage among Asian Americans on the generic congressional ballot in the average of these polls so far this year.
An average of three estimates of how Americans voted in House races in 2020 (Catalist, the CNN exit poll and the Pew Research Center) found that Democrats won the House vote by about 34 points in 2020 among Asian Americans.
This 7-point shift toward Republicans among Asian Americans is slightly larger than the 5-point shift we see among voters overall -- Republicans ahead by 2 points on the generic congressional ballot, compared with losing the national House vote by 3 points in 2020.
The key thing to realize, though, is that Democrats did worse -- and Republicans did better -- among Asian American voters in 2020 House races relative to the national vote than in any House election since 2014. So while not losing additional ground is not a bad thing for Democrats, it's not a good thing either.
If anything, the picture gets better for Republicans when you examine Biden's popularity. Across the CNN, Fox and Quinnipiac polls this year, the President's net approval rating averages +8 points with Asian Americans. That's 23 points better than his net approval rating with voters overall in these polls (-15 points).
In the 2020 election, Biden's margin with Asian American voters was about 30 points better than it was overall.
So compared with 2020, Biden is doing worse with Asian Americans relative to how he is doing with voters overall. That's notable given that earlier in his presidency, Biden was doing significantly better among Asian Americans relative to how he was doing overall and compared with how he did in 2020.
This fits with an analysis from FiveThirtyEight's Geoffrey Skelley, which found that Biden's approval rating had fallen disproportionately among non-White voters, including Asian Americans, over the course of his presidency.
The fact that the political preferences of Asian Americans are jumping around may get at something larger: Their votes are up for grabs more so than the average voter's. While they may still be more Democratic-leaning, both parties have a good chance of making up ground among the Asian American electorate.
I'd expect a lot of attention to be focused on this growing bloc of voters in the midterms.

The Democratic Party spent the early 2000s banking on a simple demographic theory: a diversifying country would naturally deliver them a permanent electoral majority. That dream collided with reality pretty quickly. The party lost the presidency in 2016 and only squeaked by in 2020. A major piece of the math simply didn't add up because of bleeding support among White working-class voters.
But the last few election cycles have highlighted a totally different headache for the left. Asian American voters, who represent one of the fastest-growing segments of the electorate, are shifting rightward. And judging by recent polling—along with turnout numbers from this week's New York primaries—the GOP’s inroads are sticky. 
Take New York, a state with a massive Asian American population. According to Census data, there are five state assembly districts in New York City alone where Asian Americans represent at least half the citizen voting-age population. Look closely at the numbers in the most concentrated of those districts, located in Flushing, Queens. In 2016, voters there backed Hillary Clinton by a whopping 40 points. Fast forward to 2020, and Joe Biden won it by just 15 points. That 25-point swing isn't a typo. It was a massive political earthquake.
Now, primaries are weird. You aren't watching a Democrat run head-to-head against a Republican. But relative primary turnout tells a compelling story about voter enthusiasm. In New York's closed primary system, we can easily track which party is drawing voters to the polls.
