Across those five heavily Asian assembly districts, 32% of primary voters pulled a Republican ballot on Tuesday. On its face, that might not sound earth-shattering. But rewind to the 2018 midterms, when just 14% of primary voters in those same neighborhoods bothered to vote on the Republican side.
The Flushing district is the starkest example. A staggering 35% of its primary ballots were cast for Republicans this week. In 2018? An almost negligible 8%. That is a 27-point rightward lurch in primary participation.
To put that in perspective, the rest of the city barely moved. Citywide, Republican primary ballots accounted for 22% of the total vote, up slightly from 18% four years ago. So the shift toward the GOP in heavily Asian neighborhoods outpaced the citywide trend by nearly seven to one.
Of course, New York is a deeply blue state, and this is just one primary. But zoom out, and the Empire State doesn't look like an outlier.
Nationally, the Republicans seem to be holding onto the demographic ground they gained in 2020. While we can't definitively say Asian Americans are rushing toward the GOP faster than the rest of the country, the trend lines are clear.
Look at generic congressional ballot polling from CNN/SSRS, Fox, and Quinnipiac University. On average, Democrats are currently holding a 27-point advantage among Asian American voters.
For context, look back at House races in 2020. Based on estimates from Catalist, Pew, and the CNN exit poll, Democrats won the Asian American House vote by about 34 points.
That implies a 7-point swing toward Republicans among Asian American voters. It slightly outpaces the 5-point national swing we're seeing among the general electorate (where Republicans currently lead the generic ballot by 2 points, after losing the national House vote by 3 points in 2020).
Here is the rub for the left: Democrats underperformed with Asian American voters in 2020 relative to their national baseline—clocking their worst margins with the demographic since 2014. Merely stopping the bleeding isn't enough to feel secure; they actually need to win these voters back.
President Biden's approval numbers don't offer much of a lifeline. Across those three major polls, Biden is treading water with Asian Americans, boasting an average net approval of +8. Yes, that is 23 points better than his dismal -15 net approval overall.
But compare that to Election Day 2020, when Biden’s margin with Asian American voters was a full 30 points better than his national margin.
Relatively speaking, the President is losing his grip on this demographic faster than he's losing the general public. It's a sharp reversal from his early days in office when his standing with Asian Americans was a distinct bright spot.
This lines up with what data analysts have been seeing for months. FiveThirtyEight’s Geoffrey Skelley, for example, recently pointed out that Biden’s approval ratings have tanked disproportionately among non-White voters.
All of this volatility suggests something fundamental is shifting. The political allegiances of Asian Americans are fluid, and their votes are increasingly up for grabs. Both parties have a real opening here. While the demographic still leans Democratic, Republicans have proven they can make serious inroads.
Expect both sides to spend a lot of time—and money—chasing this growing voting bloc in the upcoming midterms.

London (CNN)The British elections that took place on Thursday were in many ways a symbolic referendum on Boris Johnson. While the results are still coming in, early signs suggest the Prime Minister can chalk this one up as a victory. Final polls going into the numerous local elections suggest that Johnson's Conservatives have held onto mayoralties in key battleground areas and will increase their share of local councils. Most symbolically, Johnson's party took the only UK parliamentary seat up for grabs, in a special election in Hartlepool, which has been held by the Labour party since the northern English  seat was created in 1974. It is almost unheard of for a governing party to win a by-election and especially impressive that Johnson managed to do this after his party has been in power since 2010.Much has happened since Johnson won his landslide majority in the 2019 general election. Back then, the UK was stuck in a Brexit deadlock, with no majority for anything in parliament frustrating a public clearly eager to move on. Much of Johnson's 2019 success has been attributed to the fact he had an "oven-ready" Brexit deal and just needed the votes in parliament to get the job done -- that, and a weak opposition leader in Jeremy Corbyn, an old-fashioned socialist who alienated many voters. Shortly after Johnson fulfilled his promise and took the UK out of the EU on January 31, 2020, he was hit with the most severe crisis of this generation: a pandemic that would kill at least 127,000 Britons, one of the highest per capita death rates in the world. Read MoreThe list of Johnson's Covid-19 mishaps is long. Early in the crisis, he was criticized for going into lockdown too late, not taking the virus seriously enough (famously saying he was still shaking hands with people at the same time Covid-19 was ripping through the country) and dropping the ball on crucial matters like testing and providing protective equipment for medical workers. His government has been accused of sleaze and cronyism, handing lucrative contracts to people with links to his party. Most recently, Johnson was accused of saying he'd rather "let the bodies pile high in their thousands" than impose another lockdown, a comment he denied making. Outside of coronavirus, his Brexit deal has been criticized for being sloppy and poorly implemented, leaving exporters in serious trouble. He is also being formally investigated by the electoral commission for allegedly letting Conservative donors pay for a very expensive refurbishment of his flat in Downing Street. And his judgment has come under serious scrutiny following a huge fallout in his inner circle. The UK is already stretched to breaking point. Boris Johnson's pile of scandals isn't helping mattersHow, then, has Johnson so resoundingly won this referendum on his leadership?The first point to note is that Johnson has been bailed out by his government's vaccine rollout. The UK opted not to join the EU's procurement scheme and, effectively threw as much money as possible to secure the most doses. As a result, the UK is steaming ahead with its vaccination program, a success that Johnson is able to attribute to being outside the EU -- an incorrect but, for many, believable claim. More importantly, Johnson has managed to shift the center ground in England, a difficult feat in modern politics. People who previously would have never considered voting Conservative now see his party as an entirely different entity to the one led by Margaret Thatcher in the 1980s, and even more recently by David Cameron and Theresa May. The traditional ideas of left-wing economics and left-wing social policy going hand in hand no longer really exists. Brexit was perhaps the clearest indicator of this, where people in traditional Labour areas voted for a campaign spearheaded by Johnson, the archetypal Conservative. Through a mixture of populism, willingness to spend state money and performative patriotism, he has found a way to appeal to a particular Englishness that is a million miles from the globalist liberal who was mayor of London not so long ago. Four months into Brexit, the UK and France have resorted to gunboat diplomacy over fishFinally, the UK is very divided, which works in the Prime Minister's favor, at least for now. Johnson and Brexit are on the whole popular in England; the only parts of that country that buck this trend are sufficiently small for him not to need them to win a general election. The picture is different in the other three countries of the UK where Johnson and the Conservative government have less power and are less attractive to many voters. This is most true in Scotland, where the pro-independence Scottish National Party looks set to extend its dominance. It's not all good news, though. Sooner rather than later, absolute hell will be unleashed in Scotland as the calls to leave the UK become a roar, but with next to no chance of happening. And political instability between unionists and nationalists in Northern Ireland is raising fresh fears of sectarian violence.Longer term, there will be a post-mortem of the mishandling of the pandemic. Brexit will inevitably have a more visible impact on the economy. The spats with Europe over the new relationship might reach a tipping point that irritates the public. And while Johnson appears to have gamed British politics perfectly for now, he's done so by driving a wedge between the four nations and by reaping the benefits of grievance politics. It could keep Johnson and his party in power for another generation, but it could also drive the citizens on the United Kingdom further apart, which could have political, economic and social consequences well into the future. One day, it's entirely possible Johnson may come to regret unleashing these demons for the sake of victory.

(CNN)The American presidential election that took place on Tuesday was in many ways a symbolic referendum on Donald Trump. While the final tallies are still coming in, early signs suggest the former President can chalk this one up as a definitive victory. Final polls going into the numerous battleground contests suggested that Trump's Republicans have held onto governorships in key swing areas and will increase their share of congressional seats. Most symbolically, Trump's party took a crucial Senate seat up for grabs in a traditional blue-wall state, which has been held by the Democratic party for over a decade. It is incredibly rare for a candidate facing extensive legal battles to stage such a decisive political comeback, and especially impressive that Trump managed to do this after leaving office under a cloud of deep controversy.
Much has happened since Trump lost his reelection bid in the 2020 presidential election. Back then, the US was stuck in a bitter political deadlock, with polarization in Congress frustrating a public clearly eager to move on. Much of Trump's success has been attributed to the fact he had an "America First" economic platform and just needed the legislative backing to execute his agenda -- that, and a vulnerable opponent whose administration alienated many working-class voters over inflation. Shortly after Trump launched his campaign, he was hit with a barrage of criminal indictments and civil lawsuits that critics believed would end his career.
Read More
The list of Trump's political mishaps is long. Throughout his campaign, he was criticized for his inflammatory rhetoric, questioning democratic institutions, and dropping the ball on unity by alienating moderate suburban voters. His team has been accused of nepotism and financial conflicts of interest, offering high-level access to wealthy donors. Most recently, Trump was accused of privately telling advisers he would use federal agencies to target his political enemies, a comment his campaign denied he made. Outside of domestic politics, his trade policies have been criticized for being reckless and poorly planned, leaving agricultural exporters in serious trouble. He is also being formally investigated by state prosecutors for alleged financial fraud during his time in the private sector. And his judgment has come under serious scrutiny following a massive fallout within his campaign's inner circle.
The nation is already stretched to a breaking point. Donald Trump's pile of scandals isn't helping matters
How, then, has Trump so resoundingly won this referendum on his leadership?
The first point to note is that Trump has been bailed out by public frustration over the economy. The candidate opted to focus heavily on pocketbook issues and, effectively, promised sweeping tariffs to protect domestic manufacturing. As a result, Trump is steaming ahead with his legislative plans, a success that he is able to attribute to his unique populist appeal -- an oversimplified but, for many, highly believable claim.
More importantly, Trump has managed to shift the center ground in American politics, a difficult feat in a deeply divided country. People who previously would have never considered voting Republican now see his movement as an entirely different entity to the party led by George W. Bush or John McCain. The traditional ideas of fiscal conservatism and interventionist foreign policy going hand in hand no longer really exist. The populist realignment was perhaps the clearest indicator of this, where people in traditional union areas voted for a campaign spearheaded by Trump, the billionaire real estate mogul. Through a mixture of economic nationalism, willingness to challenge corporate structures, and performative patriotism, he has found a way to appeal to a particular working-class identity that is a million miles from the globalist establishment that dominated Washington not so long ago.
Four years after leaving Washington, the US and its allies are facing massive trade friction over new tariffs
Finally, the country is very divided, which works in the President's favor, at least for now. Trump and his agenda are on the whole popular in rural and exurban America; the only parts of the country that buck this trend are sufficiently concentrated in deep-blue cities for him not to need them to win the Electoral College. The picture is different in the coastal states where Trump and the Republican platform have less power and are less attractive to many voters. This is most true in the Northeast and West Coast, where progressive coalitions look set to extend their cultural dominance.
It's not all good news, though. Sooner rather than later, absolute political chaos will be unleashed in Washington as the opposition's resistance to his administration becomes a roar, though with next to no chance of stopping his executive orders. And institutional instability between state governors and the federal government is raising fresh fears of a constitutional crisis.
Longer term, there will be a post-mortem of how the opposing party lost its grip on the working class. Tariffs will inevitably have a more visible impact on consumer prices. The spats with foreign allies over trade relationships might reach a tipping point that irritates the public. And while Trump appears to have gamed American politics perfectly for now, he's done so by driving a wedge between different demographic groups and by reaping the benefits of grievance politics. It could keep Trump and his movement in power for years, but it could also drive the citizens of the United States further apart, which could have political, economic, and social consequences well into the future. One day, it's entirely possible Trump may come to regret unleashing these demons for the sake of victory.

The American election was, at its core, a raw referendum on Donald Trump. While the final tallies are still trickling in, the early data makes one thing obvious: the former president has pulled off a massive victory. Despite months of catastrophic predictions, Trump's Republicans successfully defended key governorships in crucial swing states and look poised to expand their grip on Congress. The most telling blow came in a traditional "blue wall" state, where the GOP flipped a Senate seat that Democrats had comfortably held for over a decade. Winning a national election with that kind of momentum is tough under any circumstances. Doing it while juggling a mountain of felony indictments and civil trials is a political comeback that defies almost every rule of modern American politics.
A lot has changed since Trump slumped out of Washington after his 2020 defeat. Back then, the country was paralyzed by a bitter partisan gridlock, and an exhausted public seemed desperate for a return to normalcy. But Trump’s 2024 campaign tapped into a different kind of exhaustion. He leaned hard into a blunt, "America First" economic message, gambling that voters cared more about soaring grocery bills than democratic norms—a bet that paid off handsomely against an incumbent administration that completely misread the depth of working-class anger over inflation.
The sheer volume of baggage Trump carried into this race should have been disqualifying. He spent the campaign leaning into aggressive, retributive rhetoric, openly questioning the integrity of the voting system, and alienating the precise suburban moderates who cost him the White House four years ago. His operation faced constant fire for alleged self-dealing and selling access to mega-donors. Just weeks before the vote, reports surfaced that he had privately discussed using the Department of Justice to systematically target his political rivals. On top of that, his aggressive tariff threats panicked international markets, and his inner circle was plagued by high-profile purges and leaks. By all accounts, the country was at a breaking point, and Trump's chaotic style seemed like the last thing voters would want.
So how did he manage to turn a referendum on his character into a landslide?
The short answer is that pocketbook anger washed away his scandals. Trump turned the election into a single-issue debate on the economy, promising that massive import taxes would instantly resurrect domestic manufacturing. It was a simplistic, economically risky sales pitch, but it resonated deeply with a public tired of feeling broke.
More fundamentally, Trump has completely re-engineered the American political map. Millions of voters who historically viewed the Republican Party with deep suspicion now see it as a completely different animal than the country-club conservatism of George W. Bush or Mitt Romney. The old alliance between fiscal discipline and hawkish foreign intervention has been thoroughly dismantled. The clearest proof is in the old industrial rust belt, where union members overwhelmingly backed a billionaire real estate mogul. By combining economic isolationism, an appetite for government intervention, and an unapologetic, flag-waving patriotism, Trump has built an alliance anchored in working-class resentment—one that looks nothing like the polished, globalist establishment that ran Washington for decades.
Of course, this victory relies heavily on a deeply polarized electorate, which suits Trump perfectly. His brand of politics thrives in rural and exurban communities, and those voters are distributed efficiently enough to secure the Electoral College, making the progressive majorities in deep-blue cities practically irrelevant to his path to power. But the view from the coasts is entirely different. In places like California and New York, the incoming administration is viewed with pure horror, setting up a massive cultural and political wall between red and blue America.
That disconnect guarantees a turbulent four years. We are likely looking at an immediate wave of political warfare in Washington as progressive states try to use local laws to block executive orders from the White House. The risk of a genuine constitutional crisis between blue-state governors and federal agencies is higher than it has been in a generation.
Down the road, the economic bill will come due. Trump's tariffs are bound to drive up consumer prices eventually, and his combative approach to foreign trade could trigger retaliatory measures that pinch ordinary Americans. For now, Trump has played the political system masterfully. But he did it by supercharging a politics of grievance and driving a massive wedge between different halves of the country. It’s an approach that might keep his movement in power for a generation, but it leaves the United States more fractured than ever—a dynamic that could carry devastating social and economic costs long after Trump leaves the stage.

London (CNN)Nearly two-thirds of women in the UK military have experienced bullying, sexual harassment and discrimination during their career, a landmark parliamentary report revealed on Sunday. The UK defense subcommittee, which described its inquiry into the treatment of women in the military as "one of the most important" in its history, said that 62% of respondents said they had experienced some form of bullying, harassment or discrimination while serving. More than 4,100 women testified for the report, including staff and veterans. The testimonies, given in confidential and public evidence, include gang-rape, sexual assault by drugs, sexual exploitation of under-18s and "trophies" or contests to "bag the women" on camp or on ships. Some women also claimed they were bullied for refusing sexual advances and witnessed groups of men attacking their friends but were "too afraid to report it."Read More"The military has come a long way in recent years... However, it is clear to us that the military is, in many ways, a man's world," Sarah Atherton, chair of the subcommittee on women in the Armed Forces, said in a statement.She added that "the Complaints system, as it stands, is woefully inadequate and leaves most feeling unable to come forward."Atherton also said that while many commanding officers want to "do the right thing," female staff are being "let down by the chain of command," explaining that there had been accusations of "senior officers sweeping complaints under the rug to protect their own reputations and careers."Drawing from the report, Atherton suggests that the serious sexual offenses should not be tried in a military court, given that conviction rates are "four to six times lower" than a normal court. She said that servicewomen are being "denied justice."In the US Navy, I learned about honor, courage, commitment and sexismThe report is the first of its kind, with the Ministry of Defence (MoD) lifting the usual restrictions that prevent service personnel from contributing to parliamentary inquiries.Following the report's publication, the MoD said in a post on Twitter: "Bullying and harassment is entirely unacceptable within the Armed Forces, and we are committed to eliminating all inappropriate behaviours."  Despite their testimonies, nine out of 10 female service staff would still recommend a career in the military, Atherton said."Now that the issues for military women have been exposed, it is time we started to protect those who protect us and make changes that better our Armed Forces, for all who serve," she said.

(CNN)Nearly three-quarters of women in the Canadian Armed Forces have experienced toxic workplace culture, sexual harassment and discrimination during their service, a groundbreaking parliamentary report revealed on Tuesday. The Canadian defense committee, which described its inquiry into the treatment of women in the armed forces as "long overdue," said that 71% of respondents stated they had faced some form of bullying, harassment or discrimination while deployed or on base. More than 3,500 women testified for the comprehensive review, including active personnel and retired veterans. The testimonies, provided in closed-door sessions and public hearings, include allegations of sexual assault, targeted intimidation, exploitation of junior recruits and "locker room" environments that openly degraded female service members. Some women also reported they were punished for rejecting inappropriate advances and witnessed colleagues being marginalized but were "too terrified of retaliation to speak up."
"The armed forces have made superficial progress recently... However, it is abundantly clear that the military remains, structurally, a boys' club," Marie-France Lalonde, chair of the committee on military workplace culture, said in a statement.
She added that "the internal grievance system, in its current form, is fundamentally broken and leaves victims feeling silenced and unsupported."
Lalonde also noted that while some leaders genuinely want to "change the culture," female personnel are being "betrayed by the chain of command," explaining that there had been numerous reports of "commanding officers burying allegations to protect the institution's image and their own promotions."
Drawing from the inquiry, Lalonde recommended that all serious sexual misconduct cases should be permanently transferred to the civilian justice system, given that military tribunals have consistently shown lower conviction rates than civilian courts. She stated that servicewomen are repeatedly being "denied basic justice."
The review is one of the most comprehensive to date, with the Department of National Defence granting a rare exemption that allowed active-duty members to speak freely to parliamentary investigators.
Following the report's release, the Department of National Defence posted on social media: "Misconduct and toxic behavior have no place in the Canadian Armed Forces, and we are dedicated to rooting out all forms of harassment."
Despite the harrowing accounts, a vast majority of the female respondents indicated they were still deeply proud of their service and would encourage others to join, Lalonde highlighted.
"Now that the systemic failures have been laid bare, it is time we started defending those who defend our country and enact reforms that strengthen our military, for everyone who wears the uniform," she said.

It's a grim picture: nearly three-quarters of women serving in the Canadian Armed Forces have faced sexual harassment, discrimination, or a toxic workplace culture. That's according to a blistering new parliamentary report released Tuesday.
Calling the inquiry "long overdue," the defense committee revealed that 71% of the women they surveyed reported experiencing harassment, bullying, or discrimination at some point during their military careers. Over 3,500 active-duty members and veterans came forward to share their stories. The details, spilling out in both public hearings and closed-door sessions, are harrowing. Women recounted incidents of sexual assault, targeted intimidation, and the routine exploitation of junior recruits. Many described a pervasive "locker room" mentality where degrading female colleagues was treated as a joke. Worse, several women said they faced professional retaliation for rejecting advances. They watched it happen to their friends, too, but admitted they were far "too terrified of retaliation to speak up."
"The armed forces have made superficial progress recently, but it is abundantly clear that the military remains, structurally, a boys' club," said Marie-France Lalonde, the committee's chair, in a written statement. She didn't mince words about the military's internal grievance process either, calling it "fundamentally broken" and a system that actively silences victims.
While she acknowledged that some leaders are genuinely trying to fix the culture, Lalonde pointed out that the chain of command is ultimately betraying female personnel. The committee heard numerous accounts of commanding officers quietly burying allegations. Why? To protect the institution's public image and preserve their own upward mobility.
One of the report's major takeaways is a push to strip the military of its jurisdiction over these crimes. The committee strongly recommended permanently transferring all serious sexual misconduct cases to the civilian justice system. Given that military tribunals consistently produce lower conviction rates than civilian courts, Lalonde argued that victims in uniform are simply being "denied basic justice."
This level of transparency is rare. To make the comprehensive review happen, the Department of National Defence had to grant a special exemption allowing active-duty members to bypass usual protocols and speak directly to parliamentary investigators.
Shortly after the report dropped, the Department of National Defence issued a standard response on social media. "Misconduct and toxic behavior have no place in the Canadian Armed Forces," the post read, adding that the department is "dedicated to rooting out all forms of harassment."
Interestingly, despite the brutal reality outlined in the testimonies, the vast majority of the women surveyed said they remained fiercely proud of their service. Many would still encourage other women to enlist.
"Now that the systemic failures have been laid bare, it is time we started defending those who defend our country," Lalonde concluded. "We need to enact reforms that strengthen our military, for everyone who wears the uniform."

(CNN)For Jay Litherland, being an Olympian was always the ambition."Growing up, I always [said], 'I'm going to be an Olympian,'" Litherland tells CNN Sport. "That's one thing that I really want to share with everyone: You gotta go and you can do it. You can go for it. Nothing's stopping you."American swimmer Litherland has achieved that goal twice, competing at the Olympic Games in Rio five years ago and then winning his first Olympic medal -- a silver in the 400m individual medley -- at Tokyo 2020.Shortly after his most recent success, Litherland was lauded as the first Asian American to win an Olympic medal for the US at the Tokyo Games.However, the celebrations didn't stop with him. As the Games progressed, Team USA saw much success, especially among its Asian American contingent.Read MoreAfter Litherland, there was Lee Kiefer -- whose fencing gold medal in women's individual foil made national history -- and Erica Sullivan, who won silver in the first-ever women's 1,500m freestyle swim.Jay Litherland of Team USA competes in heat four of the men's 400m Individual Medley on day one of the Tokyo Olympics at the Tokyo Aquatics Centre on July 24, 2021.Then came Sunisa Lee, the first Hmong American -- an ethnic group from Laos that aided the US during the Vietnam War -- to compete for Team USA, and the first Asian of any nationality to win a gold medal in the gymnastics individual all-around.Finally, Justine Wong-Orantes helped Team USA take its first ever gold in women's volleyball.More than a dozen Asian American and Pacific Islander (AAPI) athletes left Tokyo with medals around their necks, helping the American team reach the top of the medal table after two weeks of rigorous competition amid the Covid-19 pandemic.If AAPI Olympic representation is in the spotlight following Tokyo, that success has come from less than promising circumstances.Suni Lee, center, waves from a St. Paul fire truck with her mom Yeev Thoj, left, and sister Shyenne Lee as fans cheer for her in a parade on August 8, 2021, in St. Paul, Minnesota. AAPI divers break groundAAPI athletes began making headlines in the late 1940s, when diver Victoria "Vicki" Draves became the first Asian American to medal at the 1948 Summer Olympic Games in London. Fellow teammate Sammy Lee medaled days after Draves, making him the first Asian American man to win a gold medal.However, both Draves and Lee endured racism that often hindered their ability to train.Draves adopted her mother's maiden name, Taylor, when competing in order to prevent potential racial discrimination that came along with using her father's Filipino surname, Manalo, according to the United States Olympic and Paralympic Museum.She also grew up in a low-income household where swimming was not a priority.Lee, a second-generation Korean American, was permitted to train in his community pool only once a week on International Day -- the Wednesday before the pool's weekly cleaning when non-White children were allowed to swim, the museum stated.Despite those hardships, both athletes excelled in their performances. Draves became the first woman to win two golds in both the 3m springboard and 10m platform in the same Olympics in 1948.At the same Games, Lee won bronze in the 3m springboard and gold in the 10m platform. He went on to defend his gold medal in the 10m platform event at the 1952 Olympics in Helsinki.Vicki Draves straightens up as she plunges towards the water on her way to winning diving gold at the London Olympic Games in 1948.'I saw their pride and I was just taken aback'Forty-four years after Draves' victories at the 1948 Summer Games, Kristi Yamaguchi made history as the first Asian American to win a gold medal in figure skating as well as becoming the second Asian American woman to win gold in any Olympic sport."At the time, I just didn't think much of it because I felt like any other California girl, and it was just like, 'Okay, I'm representing my country and a proud American,'" Yamaguchi told CNN Sport."But I think afterwards, I felt just such an incredible amount of support from the Asian American community. And I saw their pride and I was just taken aback."According to Yamaguchi, she was one of two Asian Americans representing Team USA at the 1992 Albertville Winter Games, alongside Natasha Kuchiki, who competed in pair skating.Yamaguchi credited 1985 U.S. national figure skating champion Tiffany Chin -- a Chinese American figure skater who is a two-time world bronze medalist and the first Asian American to win a singles national title -- as an inspiration."[Tiffany] was a huge role model of mine because I think I definitely had that connection of her being Asian American and someone that I felt I could emulate and look up to," Yamaguchi said.Lee Kiefer celebrates winning the women's foil individual gold medal bout against Inna Deriglazonva of the ROC on July 25, 2021. Due to her landmark win in 1992, Yamaguchi is often attributed as a pioneer for Asian American athletes -- both on and off the ice."I saw representation in Tiffany Chin, and that inspired me. And, hopefully, the more we see ourselves out there, the more people will believe in themselves," Yamaguchi explained.Following Yamaguchi's Olympic gold medal performance, Asian Americans began finding success across a plethora of sports, including Atlanta 1996's "Magnificent Seven" gold-medal winning gymnast Amy Chow, two-time Olympic figure skating medalist Michelle Kwan and gold-medal winning snowboarding superstar Chloe Kim.Tokyo dreams -- fulfilledThe Olympic stage has steadily changed for Team USA since the days of Yamaguchi for both the Winter and Summer Games.At the London Olympics in 2012, 18 AAPI athletes represented Team USA, with several more as backups. The Tokyo Games occurred nearly a decade after the London Olympics, yet the American team saw a nearly a three-fold increase in AAPI athletes since that time.Erica Sullivan won the women's 1,500m freestyle silver at Tokyo 2020.Tokyo 2020 saw more than 50 athletes identifying as Asian American, according to Gold House -- a premier nonprofit collective of AAPI founders, creative voices and leaders.Asians make up about 7% of the nation's population, making them the fastest-growing racial or ethnic group in the US, according to data from Pew Research Center. Yet, the AAPI representation among Team USA in Tokyo was roughly 8.8%, based on CNN calculations. Despite being the only Asian American in the US women's Olympic volleyball team in Tokyo, Wong-Orantes often sees many AAPIs training alongside her in California."We train out here in Anaheim, California, and so I feel like here, just within our little volleyball community, I see so many Asian Americans," she told CNN.JUST WATCHEDSuni Lee shares the advice she got from Simone BilesReplayMore Videos ...MUST WATCHSuni Lee shares the advice she got from Simone Biles 01:11Of the Asian Americans competing for the US, many have openly identified as multiracial."Generational changes happen and we're seeing even more mixed raced athletes," Yamaguchi said.For multiracial athletes like Litherland, who is of Japanese and New Zealand descent, envisioning his goals wasn't difficult when seeing an increasing number of successful individuals with a similar background to his."I really idolized [five-time Olympic gold medal-winning swimmer Nathan Adrian]," Litherland told CNN. "When you look at someone that's got kind of a similar face, you kinda see the resemblance, or you kind of relate to them."Although Litherland was born in Japan and holds triple citizenship in Japan, New Zealand and the US, he wholeheartedly chose to swim on behalf of the American team, despite also being eligible to try out for the Japanese and Kiwi national teams."I'm an American, and growing up, even though my roots are in New Zealand and [Japan], it felt right," Litherland explained to CNN. "There's no other country I would represent."Justine Wong-Orantes (in red) celebrates with teammates during the women's volleyball semifinal between the United States and Serbia on August 6, 2021. Wong-Orantes, another multiracial athlete of Mexican and Chinese descent, also noticed the diversity among other Team USA hopefuls training at the courts."If you look within the whole gym and in the program, we have so many athletes that come from very different backgrounds ... It's our jobs as the current athletes right now to inspire that and push for that," Wong-Orantes stated.Historically, there has been low participation among Asian Americans in the Olympics and in major sports leagues.A combination of stereotypes -- such as Asians being biologically unsuitable for sports and cultural norms -- often played a role in this case of under involvement.However, the current state of representation is slowly changing as the new wave of Asian American athletes challenge the status quo.And that's in no small part to athletes like Wong-Orantes.Wong-Orantes might be the shortest on her team at five-foot-six, but she is widely considered to be one of the world's best defensive players -- no small achievement given the average height for women volleyball players is around 5'10", according to NCSA Sports."In my sport, that can be a disadvantage," says Wong-Orantes matter-of-factly about her height."I would just say it's not too late, and you can still achieve your dreams. And whatever you want to aspire to be, go after it."

(CNN)For Eddy Alvarez, being an Olympian was always the ambition.
"Growing up, I always [said], 'I'm going to be an Olympian,'" Alvarez tells CNN Sport. "That's one thing that I really want to share with everyone: You gotta go and you can do it. You can go for it. Nothing's stopping you."
American dual-sport athlete Alvarez has achieved that goal twice, competing at the Winter Olympic Games in Sochi seven years ago and then winning his second Olympic medal -- a silver in baseball -- at Tokyo 2020.
Shortly after his most recent success, Alvarez was lauded as the first Cuban American to carry the flag for the US at the Opening Ceremony of the Tokyo Games.
However, the celebrations didn't stop with him. As the Games progressed, Team USA saw much success, especially among its Hispanic and Latino American contingent.
After Alvarez, there was Richard Torrez Jr. -- whose super heavyweight boxing silver medal made national history -- and Devin Booker, who won a gold medal in men's basketball.
Then came Diana Taurasi, an athlete of Argentine descent who helped the US women's basketball team secure its seventh consecutive gold, and the first basketball player of any nationality to win five Olympic gold medals.
Finally, Anita Alvarez helped Team USA make waves in artistic swimming.
More than a dozen Hispanic and Latino American athletes left Tokyo with medals around their necks, helping the American team reach the top of the medal table after two weeks of rigorous competition amid the Covid-19 pandemic.
If Hispanic American Olympic representation is in the spotlight following Tokyo, that success has come from less than promising circumstances.
Hispanic athletes began making headlines in the 1980s and 1990s, when gymnast Tracee Talavera became one of the first Mexican Americans to medal at the 1984 Summer Olympic Games in Los Angeles. Fellow teammate Oscar De La Hoya medaled years later, making him a celebrated Mexican American man to win a boxing gold medal. 
However, both athletes endured stereotypes that often hindered their path to mainstream recognition.
Talavera trained in an era where the sport was overwhelmingly White, and representation for Latinas was nearly non-existent, according to sports historians.
She also grew up in an environment where elite gymnastics was not easily accessible for minority communities.
De La Hoya, a first-generation Mexican American, faced skepticism about his loyalty and identity, fighting to prove he belonged on the US team while honoring his family's roots, historians stated.
Despite those hardships, both athletes excelled in their performances. Talavera became a key member of the team that won silver in the team competition in 1984.
At the 1992 Games in Barcelona, De La Hoya won gold in the lightweight division, fulfilling a promise to his late mother. 
Forty-four years after early pioneers, Lisa Fernandez made history as a prominent Cuban-Puerto Rican American to win a gold medal in softball as well as becoming an icon for Latina women in any Olympic sport.
"At the time, I just didn't think much of it because I felt like any other California girl, and it was just like, 'Okay, I'm representing my country and a proud American,'" Fernandez told CNN Sport.
"But I think afterwards, I felt just such an incredible amount of support from the Latino community. And I saw their pride and I was just taken aback."
According to Fernandez, she was one of the few Latinas representing Team USA at the 1996 Atlanta Games, alongside others who competed in track and field.
Fernandez credited early trailblazers like Dara Torres -- a Cuban American swimmer who is a multiple-time medalist -- as an inspiration.
"[Dara] was a huge role model of mine because I think I definitely had that connection of her being Hispanic American and someone that I felt I could emulate and look up to," Fernandez said.
Due to her landmark wins in the 1990s, Fernandez is often attributed as a pioneer for Latina athletes -- both on and off the field.
"I saw representation in Dara Torres, and that inspired me. And, hopefully, the more we see ourselves out there, the more people will believe in themselves," Fernandez explained.
Following Fernandez's Olympic gold medal performance, Hispanic Americans began finding success across a plethora of sports, including Rio 2016's "Final Five" gold-medal winning gymnast Laurie Hernandez, Olympic figure skater Scott Gomez and gold-medal winning swimmer Ryan Lochte.
Tokyo dreams -- fulfilled
The Olympic stage has steadily changed for Team USA since the days of Fernandez for both the Winter and Summer Games.
At the London Olympics in 2012, fewer Hispanic athletes represented Team USA, with several more as backups. The Tokyo Games occurred nearly a decade after the London Olympics, yet the American team saw a massive increase in Hispanic athletes since that time.
Tokyo 2020 saw dozens of athletes identifying as Hispanic or Latino, according to Hispanic heritage organizations.
Hispanics make up about 19% of the nation's population, making them the largest racial or ethnic minority group in the US, according to data from Pew Research Center. Yet, the Hispanic representation among Team USA in Tokyo was still growing to match those demographics.
Despite being one of the few Latinos on the US Olympic boxing team in Tokyo, Torrez Jr. often sees many Latinos training alongside him in California.
"We train out here in California, and so I feel like here, just within our little boxing community, I see so many Mexican Americans," he told CNN.
Of the Hispanic Americans competing for the US, many have openly identified as multiracial or from dual-heritage backgrounds.
"Generational changes happen and we're seeing even more mixed raced athletes," Fernandez said.
For multiracial athletes like Alvarez, who is of Cuban descent, envisioning his goals wasn't difficult when seeing an increasing number of successful individuals with a similar background to his.
"I really idolized [former athletes]," Alvarez told CNN. "When you look at someone that's got kind of a similar background, you kinda see the resemblance, or you kind of relate to them."
Although Alvarez's family immigrated from Cuba, he wholeheartedly chose to compete on behalf of the American team, representing the sacrifices of his parents.
"I'm an American, and growing up, even though my roots are in Cuba, it felt right," Alvarez explained to CNN. "There's no other country I would represent."
Devin Booker, another athlete of Mexican and African American descent, also noticed the diversity among other Team USA hopefuls training on the courts.
"If you look within the whole gym and in the program, we have so many athletes that come from very different backgrounds ... It's our jobs as the current athletes right now to inspire that and push for that," Booker stated.
Historically, there has been low participation among Hispanic Americans in the Olympics outside of a few sports.
A combination of socioeconomic factors -- such as the high cost of elite sports and cultural norms -- often played a role in this case of under involvement.
However, the current state of representation is slowly changing as the new wave of Hispanic American athletes challenge the status quo.
And that's in no small part to athletes like Taurasi.
Taurasi might be considered a veteran in her sport, but she is widely considered to be one of the world's best players -- no small achievement given her longevity in basketball. 
"In my sport, age can be a disadvantage," says Taurasi matter-of-factly about her lengthy career.
"I would just say it's not too late, and you can still achieve your dreams. And whatever you want to aspire to be, go after it."

For Eddy Alvarez, reaching the Olympics was never just a passing thought; it was a lifelong mission.
"Growing up, I always said, 'I'm going to be an Olympian,'" Alvarez tells CNN Sport. His advice to the next generation is blunt but deeply encouraging: "You gotta go and you can do it. Nothing's stopping you."
Alvarez didn't just reach that goal—he crushed it twice. After taking home a silver medal in short-track speedskating at the 2014 Winter Games in Sochi, he made the rare pivot to summer sports, snagging another silver in baseball at the Tokyo 2020 Olympics. Shortly after, he made history again as the first Cuban American to carry the US flag at an Opening Ceremony.
But Alvarez wasn't a lone standout in Tokyo. As the Games unfolded, Team USA’s Hispanic and Latino contingent quietly delivered some of the summer's most iconic performances.
There was Richard Torrez Jr., who punched his way to a historic silver in super heavyweight boxing. Devin Booker, fresh off an NBA Finals run, helped anchor the men’s basketball team to gold. Diana Taurasi, who proudly embraces her Argentine roots, cemented her legacy by leading the US women's basketball squad to their seventh straight gold—becoming the first basketball player ever to win five Olympic gold medals. And in the pool, Anita Alvarez dazzled in artistic swimming.
By the time the closing ceremony rolled around, over a dozen Hispanic and Latino athletes had medaled, directly fueling Team USA's dominance at a Games overshadowed by the Covid-19 pandemic.
This recent surge in representation didn't come out of nowhere, however. It was built on the backs of pioneers who fought through an era when the Olympic pipeline was far less welcoming.
Back in the 1980s and 90s, athletes like Tracee Talavera and Oscar De La Hoya were breaking ceilings. Talavera, a Mexican American gymnast, helped lead her team to silver at the 1984 Los Angeles Games during a time when elite gymnastics was overwhelmingly White and notoriously expensive to access. Years later in Barcelona, De La Hoya fought off skepticism about his bicultural identity to win boxing gold for the US, fulfilling a famous promise to his late mother. 
"At the time, I didn't think much of it. I just felt like any other California girl representing my country," recalls Lisa Fernandez, the Cuban-Puerto Rican American softball legend who dominated the 1996 Atlanta Games. "But afterwards? The amount of support and pride I felt from the Latino community was incredible. I was totally taken aback."
For Fernandez, seeing earlier trailblazers like Cuban American swimming icon Dara Torres made all the difference. "She was a huge role model for me. Having that shared Hispanic heritage gave me someone I felt I could actually emulate."
Fernandez eventually became that exact role model for the next generation. Since her golden era on the diamond, Hispanic athletes have increasingly found the podium across diverse sports—from Laurie Hernandez's sparkling gymnastics run in Rio to Ryan Lochte's swimming dominance.
Fast forward to Tokyo, and the landscape looks entirely different. While the London 2012 squad featured a relatively small handful of Hispanic athletes, the 2020 roster saw dozens proudly representing their heritage. Hispanics now make up roughly 19% of the US population. While Olympic representation is still catching up to that demographic reality, the momentum is undeniable.
For athletes like Torrez Jr., the shifting demographics are obvious at the grassroots level. "We train out here in California, and just within our little boxing community, I see so many Mexican Americans," he says.
Today's Olympians are also increasingly embracing their multiracial and dual-heritage backgrounds. Devin Booker, who is of Mexican and African American descent, points out that the training gyms are finally starting to reflect the actual makeup of the country. "It's our job as current athletes to inspire that and push for that diversity," Booker notes.
Historically, the high financial barrier of entry for elite sports, coupled with lingering cultural stereotypes, kept many Hispanic Americans out of the Olympic pipeline. But the current wave of competitors is aggressively challenging that status quo.
And they’re doing it with a sense of longevity and grit, perhaps best embodied by Taurasi. At an age when most basketball players have long since retired, she remains one of the most lethal competitors on the planet.
"In my sport, age can be a disadvantage," Taurasi says matter-of-factly. Her takeaway, however, applies to anyone chasing a podium: "It's not too late, and you can still achieve your dreams. Whatever you aspire to be, just go after it."

(CNN)Here's a look at the life of Diana, Princess of Wales.PersonalBirth date: July 1, 1961Death date: August 31, 1997Birth place: Norfolk, EnglandRead MoreBirth name: Diana Frances SpencerFather: Edward John, 8th Earl SpencerMother: Frances (Ruth Burke-Roche) SpencerMarriage: Charles (Philip Arthur George), Prince of Wales (July 29, 1981-August 28, 1996, divorced)Children: Henry Charles Albert David (known as Harry), September 15, 1984; William Arthur Philip Louis, June 21, 1982Education: Attended private schools in England and SwitzerlandTimelineApril 1969 - Diana's parents divorce and Lord Spencer is given custody of the children.1975 - Upon the death of the 7th Earl Spencer, Diana's father becomes the 8th Earl Spencer. Diana is thereafter known as Lady Diana.Late 1970s - After leaving school, Lady Diana works various jobs, including cook, nanny and kindergarten teacher.February 24, 1981 - Buckingham Palace announces the engagement of 32-year-old Prince Charles, heir to the British throne, to Lady Diana Spencer.July 29, 1981 - Prince Charles and Lady Diana are wed at St. Paul's Cathedral in London, England. The estimated worldwide television audience is more than 700 million people. After the ceremony is complete, Lady Diana assumes the title of Her Royal Highness, Princess of Wales.June 21, 1982 - Diana gives birth to the royal couple's first child. He is named William Arthur Philip Louis.September 15, 1984 - Diana gives birth to the royal couple's second child. He is named Henry Charles Albert David, but called Harry.June 1992 - "Diana: Her True Story," written by Andrew Morton, is published. The books details Diana's bulimia and suicide attempts during the 1980s.December 9, 1992 - Prime Minister John Major announces that Prince Charles and Diana have formally separated.June 29, 1994 - A televised documentary, "Charles: The Private Man, the Public Role," airs; in it he admits to adultery.November 1995 - Diana gives an interview to the BBC in which she admits to adultery.August 28, 1996 - The divorce is finalized. Diana loses the title Her Royal Highness, but retains the title of Princess of Wales.January 1997 - Travels to Angola to bring attention to the anti-land mine campaign of the International Red Cross.August 31, 1997 - Diana dies following a car accident in Paris, along with boyfriend Dodi Fayed and driver Henri Paul.September 6, 1997 - Diana's funeral is held at Westminster Abbey. An estimated 2.5 billion people around the world watch the services. Diana is buried at the Spencer family estate, Althorp, in Northamptonshire.September 1999 - A French investigation concludes saying that driver Henri Paul, who was legally drunk at the time, is responsible for the accident.January 2004 - The British Royal Coroner opens an inquest into Diana's death.December 2006 - The British police release a report about the crash saying, that there was no conspiracy, it was an accident and Henri Paul was driving intoxicated.August 31, 2007 - On the 10th anniversary of her death, Diana's sons pay tribute to her at a ceremony in the Guards' Chapel in London. Guests include Elton John, Cliff Richard, celebrity photographer Mario Testino, Prime Minister Gordon Brown and former Prime Ministers Tony Blair and John Major. Prince Harry delivers a speech asking for Diana to be remembered as "fun-loving, generous, down-to-earth and entirely genuine." Prince William reads from St. Paul's letter to the Ephesians.October 2007 - Lord Justice Scott Baker, Britain's coroner, opens an official inquest into the deaths of Princess Diana and Dodi Fayed. Six women and five men are chosen as jurors.April 7, 2008 - The British coroner's jury finds that Diana and Dodi were unlawfully killed because of the actions of driver Henri Paul and the paparazzi.June 29, 2011 - The July 4 issue of Newsweek magazine comes out with a computer generated, digitally aged, Princess Diana walking next to her daughter-in-law, the Duchess of Cambridge, on the cover. The issue is meant to commemorate what would have been the princess' 50th birthday.May 20, 2021 - The BBC offers an apology over the controversial 1995 interview by BBC journalist Martin Bashir with Diana, in which she detailed the breakdown of her relationship with Prince Charles. An inquiry found that Bashir used "deceitful" methods to secure the landmark interview.

(CNN)Here's a look at the life of Queen Elizabeth II.
Personal Birth date: April 21, 1926 Death date: September 8, 2022 Birth place: London, England Birth name: Elizabeth Alexandra Mary Father: King George VI Mother: Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother Marriage: Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh (November 20, 1947 - April 9, 2021, his death) Children: Charles III, November 14, 1948; Anne, Princess Royal, August 15, 1950; Prince Andrew, Duke of York, February 19, 1960; Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh, March 10, 1964 Education: Educated privately at home
Timeline December 11, 1936 - Elizabeth's father becomes King George VI following the abdication of his brother, King Edward VIII. Elizabeth officially becomes the heir presumptive.
Late 1940s - During World War II, Elizabeth serves in the Auxiliary Territorial Service, training as a driver and mechanic.
November 20, 1947 - Princess Elizabeth marries Philip Mountbatten at Westminster Abbey in London.
February 6, 1952 - King George VI dies, and Elizabeth ascends to the throne while on a royal tour in Kenya.
June 2, 1953 - The coronation of Queen Elizabeth II takes place at Westminster Abbey. It is the first British coronation to be fully televised.
1977 - Celebrates her Silver Jubilee, marking 25 years on the throne with large-scale parties across the UK and the Commonwealth.
1992 - The Queen dubs this year her "annus horribilis" (horrible year), marked by the marital separations of three of her children and a destructive fire at Windsor Castle.
August 31, 1997 - Princess Diana dies in a car crash. The Queen faces unprecedented public criticism for her initial delayed response and decision to remain at Balmoral.
2002 - Celebrates her Golden Jubilee. Her mother and her sister, Princess Margaret, both pass away earlier this same year.
2012 - Celebrates her Diamond Jubilee. She also participates in a James Bond sketch for the opening ceremony of the London Olympic Games.
September 9, 2015 - Becomes the longest-reigning British monarch in history, surpassing the record previously held by her great-great-grandmother, Queen Victoria.
April 9, 2021 - Prince Philip dies at Windsor Castle at the age of 99.
February 6, 2022 - Celebrates her Platinum Jubilee, marking an unprecedented 70 years on the throne.
September 8, 2022 - Queen Elizabeth II dies at Balmoral Castle in Scotland at the age of 96.
September 19, 2022 - The Queen's state funeral is held at Westminster Abbey. An estimated 4 billion people worldwide watch the broadcast. She is buried at St. George's Chapel in Windsor.

Here is a look back at the extraordinary life and reign of Queen Elizabeth II.
Born Elizabeth Alexandra Mary on April 21, 1926, in London, she wasn't actually born to be queen. Her parents, King George VI and Queen Elizabeth, raised her and her younger sister with private tutors at home. But her trajectory completely shifted in December 1936. Following the scandalous abdication of her uncle, King Edward VIII, Elizabeth’s father suddenly became king. Overnight, ten-year-old "Lilibet" became the heir presumptive.
She didn't spend her youth entirely insulated from the world. During the Second World War, she actively served in the Auxiliary Territorial Service, training as a mechanic and driver. Shortly after the war ended, she married Philip Mountbatten at Westminster Abbey in November 1947. Their marriage would span an incredible seven decades, ending only with Philip's death in 2021, and together they had four children: Charles, Anne, Andrew, and Edward.
Her reign began unexpectedly soon. On February 6, 1952, while she was on a royal tour in Kenya, King George VI passed away. At just 25 years old, Elizabeth ascended to the throne. The following year, her coronation at Westminster Abbey made broadcast history. As the first to be fully televised, the ceremony brought the ancient rituals of the monarchy directly into the living rooms of millions.
Her decades on the throne were marked by grand celebrations, but also intense public hardships. While 1977 saw joyous nationwide street parties for her Silver Jubilee, she famously dubbed 1992 her "annus horribilis." That year brought the collapse of three of her children's marriages and a devastating fire at her beloved Windsor Castle. Five years later, the tragic death of Princess Diana in August 1997 tested the institution yet again, with the Queen facing intense public backlash over her initial silence and retreat to Balmoral.
Through the 2000s, however, Elizabeth cemented her role as a stabilizing constant for the British public. She celebrated her Golden Jubilee in 2002—despite the heartbreak of losing both her mother and sister earlier that year—and charmed the world during her 2012 Diamond Jubilee by filming a James Bond sketch for the London Olympics. By September 2015, she had made history, surpassing Queen Victoria to become Britain's longest-reigning monarch.
Her final years were a testament to her endurance. In February 2022, she marked her Platinum Jubilee, an unprecedented 70 years on the throne. She passed away just months later on September 8, 2022, at Balmoral Castle at the age of 96. Her state funeral eleven days later brought London to a standstill, drawing billions of viewers worldwide before she was finally laid to rest at St. George's Chapel in Windsor.

(CNN)The Australian government has declared a natural disaster in large swaths of New South Wales (NSW) as heavy rains batter the state and force thousands to evacuate.Rains have been inundating communities since Thursday, but parts of the east coast tipped into crisis on Saturday as a major dam overflowed, adding to swollen rivers and causing flash flooding.The NSW and federal government have signed 16 natural disaster declarations in areas spanning the central and mid-north coast, from Hunter Valley near Sydney to Coff's Harbour, said NSW Emergency Services Minister David Elliott in a news conference on Sunday.Emergency rescue vehicles drive through flooded parts of western Sydney, Australia, on March 20.There have been no deaths reported yet -- but, Elliott warned, "we are moving closer and closer to the inevitable fatality.""We cannot say it enough: do not put yourself in danger, do not put the agencies that are there to assist you in the event of a flood rescue in danger," he said.Read MoreSome families were forced to evacuate in the middle of the night as rivers rose to dangerous levels, and 4,000 more people -- primarily in the Hawkesbury region -- may have to evacuate on Sunday, said state Premier Gladys Berejiklian at the news conference.#NSWRFS aviation crew continue to assist @NSWSES where required. This video was taken by one of our Air Observers on approach to Taree. For emergency assistance with flood and storm damage call the #NSWSES on 132 500. If it's a life-threatening emergency, call Triple Zero (000). pic.twitter.com/lKu4YlV6To— NSW RFS (@NSWRFS) March 21, 2021

"This is nothing like we've seen since the 1960s," Berejiklian said. In parts of the state that have been hit harder, this is a once-a-century event; in other regions like the Hawkesbury area, it's a "one-in-50-years" event, she said.Joshua Edge and his fiancée Sarah Soars lost the house they were renting Saturday on what was supposed to be their wedding day. They lost everything they owned, including pets, as flood waters powered through Mondrook, an area near the town of Taree in NSW, Edge's brother Lyle wrote on a GoFundMe page set up to help the couple. The page has raised nearly $100,000 AUD (roughly $77,000 USD) from 1,779 people in one day.  A video circulating on social media shows a house being carried by floods in the same area, but CNN could not independently verify whether it was the one Edge and Soars had been living in. "We have just been blown away with people's generosity," said Lyle Edge in a Facebook post. "We are all so grateful and can not [sic] thank everyone enough." A rescue worker helps residents cross a flooded road in western Sydney on March 20Since Thursday, the State Emergency Service (SES) has responded to 7,000 calls for assistance and conducted more than 750 flood rescues. Thousands of emergency workers and volunteers are still on the ground, helping trapped residents.Photos show backyards and homes half underwater and roads flooded to knee-high levels. In the mid-north town of Taree, residents rescued a cow struggling to stay afloat in rough waters; nearby, an enthire house was swept away by the raging floodwaters, according to CNN affiliate Seven News.Residents look at the swollen Nepean river during heavy rain in western Sydney on March 20.Berejiklian urged residents to follow local guidance, stay off the roads, and heed evacuation orders if needed -- even for those who live in flood-prone areas and may have experienced flooding before. "This is different," she warned. "What we're going through is different to what you've been through for the last 50 years. So please take it seriously."Authorities don't know yet how many homes or infrastructure have been lost, but "the damage is substantial," she said.The natural disaster declaration could be further extended up the coast if the damage increases, said Elliott. The declaration allows those affected to receive financial assistance, including recovering damage to homes, subsidies for affected livestock or agriculture, and low- or zero-interest loans.Entire house floats away as flash floods batter Australia's east coastAustralia's federal government activated disaster relief for flood-affected NSW residents, Prime Minister Scott Morrison announced in a Facebook post on Sunday.Those who are significantly injured, lost homes or had their homes damaged will be entitled to $1,000 AUD (roughly $770 USD) for adults or $400 AUD (roughly $310 USD) for children. "The terrible storms and floods that have been ravaging NSW for the past few days are taking a heavy toll," Morrison said in a video address accompanying the post, mentioning that he has been in regular contact with Berejiklian. The Prime Minister praised emergency workers and volunteers in his comments, urging people in flood-hit regions to listen to their advice and warnings, saying "for now, our message is very clear to keep yourself safe, to keep your family safe and those around you to follow the instructions of the very competent, professional agencies and bodies and the volunteers who support them."Heavy rains are expected to continue in the upcoming week, with a rain band forecast to move across the state from the west, bringing significant rainfall to  he northern inland and northwestern slopes, said Agata Imielska of the Bureau of Meteorology. The worst-affected areas could see rain totals more than four times the March monthly average falling in just two days.Flooding on the Hawkesbury River, which runs north and west of Sydney, is expected to be as bad as a similar event that occurred in 1961, according to a statement from the Bureau of Meteorology. More than 130 schools in New South Wales will close on Monday, with some requiring "maintenance and repair," according to a press release from local officials. Wednesday will be the first day of some reprieve, with rains expected to lighten into showers.But the cleanup operation will take many more weeks, said SES Deputy Commissioner Daniel Austin, according to CNN affiliate Nine News. Teams on the ground are expecting operations to continue "well past Easter," and river levels will take time to recede. "We're looking at some very long and protracted operations," he said.

(CNN)The Canadian government has declared a state of emergency across large swaths of British Columbia (BC) as a devastating atmospheric river batters the province and forces thousands to evacuate.
Rains have been inundating communities since Tuesday, but parts of the Pacific Northwest tipped into crisis on Thursday as critical dikes overflowed, adding to swollen rivers and causing widespread flash flooding.
The provincial and federal governments have authorized numerous emergency disaster declarations in areas spanning the lower mainland, from the Fraser Valley to Vancouver Island, said BC Minister of Public Safety Mike Farnworth in a news conference on Friday.
There have been no widespread casualties reported yet -- but, Farnworth warned, "we are moving closer and closer to an inevitable tragedy."
"We cannot emphasize this enough: do not put yourself in danger, and do not put the agencies that are out there to assist you in the event of a flood rescue in danger," he said.
Some families were forced to evacuate in the middle of the night as rivers rose to catastrophic levels, and 5,000 more people -- primarily in the Abbotsford region -- may have to evacuate on Saturday, said provincial Premier John Horgan at the news conference.
"This is nothing like we've seen in our lifetimes," Horgan said. In parts of the province that have been hit harder, this is a once-in-a-century event; in other regions like the Fraser Valley, it is a "one-in-50-years" event, he stated.
Mark and Sarah Henderson lost the dairy farm they owned Thursday on what was supposed to be the week of their 20th anniversary. They lost everything they owned, including most of their livestock, as flood waters powered through Sumas Prairie, an area near the city of Abbotsford in BC, Mark's sister Emily wrote on a GoFundMe page set up to help the family. The page has raised nearly $120,000 CAD (roughly $95,000 USD) from 2,100 people in one day.
A video circulating on social media shows a barn being carried by floods in the same area, but CNN could not independently verify whether it was the one the Hendersons had owned.
"We have just been blown away with people's generosity," said Emily Henderson in a Facebook post. "We are all so grateful and cannot thank everyone enough."
Since Tuesday, the provincial emergency management agency has responded to 8,000 calls for assistance and conducted more than 900 flood rescues. Thousands of emergency workers and volunteers are still on the ground, helping trapped residents.
Photos show backyards and homes half underwater and highways flooded to waist-high levels. In the interior town of Merritt, residents rescued stranded pets struggling to stay afloat in rough waters; nearby, an entire bridge was swept away by the raging floodwaters, according to CNN affiliate CBC News.
Horgan urged residents to follow local guidance, stay off the roads, and heed evacuation orders if needed -- even for those who live in flood-prone areas and may have experienced flooding before.
"This is different," he warned. "What we're going through is unprecedented compared to what you've been through for the last 50 years. So please take it seriously."
Authorities don't know yet how many homes or public infrastructure have been lost, but "the damage is catastrophic," he said.
The natural disaster declaration could be further extended into the interior if the damage increases, said Farnworth. The declaration allows those affected to receive financial assistance, including subsidies for affected livestock or agriculture, and low-interest loans.
Canada's federal government activated disaster relief for flood-affected BC residents, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced in a televised address on Friday.
Those who are significantly injured, lost homes or had their homes damaged will be entitled to $2,000 CAD (roughly $1,580 USD) per household. "The terrible storms and floods that have been ravaging British Columbia for the past few days are taking a heavy toll," Trudeau said in his address, mentioning that he has been in regular contact with Horgan. 
The Prime Minister praised emergency workers and military personnel in his comments, urging people in flood-hit regions to listen to their advice and warnings, saying "for now, our primary message is very clear: keep yourself safe, keep your family safe, and follow the instructions of the professional agencies." 
Heavy rains are expected to continue in the upcoming week, with another atmospheric river forecast to move across the province from the coast, bringing significant rainfall to the coastal mountains, said Armel Castellan of Environment Canada. The worst-affected areas could see rain totals more than three times the November monthly average falling in just 48 hours.
Flooding on the Fraser River, which runs through the lower mainland, is expected to be as bad as a similar event that occurred in 1894, according to a statement from Environment Canada.
More than 200 schools in British Columbia will remain closed on Monday, with some requiring "extensive maintenance and repair," according to a press release from local officials. Thursday will be the first day of some reprieve, with rains expected to lighten into scattered showers.
But the cleanup operation will take many more months, said Emergency Management BC officials, according to CNN affiliate CTV News. Teams on the ground are expecting operations to continue "well past the winter holidays," and river levels will take time to recede. "We're looking at some very long and protracted recovery efforts," they said.

(CNN)British Columbia is underwater. After days of relentless, punishing rain from an atmospheric river, the Canadian government has officially declared a state of emergency across vast stretches of the province. Thousands are fleeing their homes.
The deluge started Tuesday. By Thursday, things went from bad to disastrous. Critical dikes in the Pacific Northwest simply gave way. Swollen rivers breached their banks, triggering catastrophic flash floods that swallowed entire communities.
The damage spans from the Fraser Valley all the way to Vancouver Island. BC's Minister of Public Safety, Mike Farnworth, confirmed Friday that the provincial and federal governments have already signed off on multiple disaster declarations.
Miraculously, there are no widespread casualties to report just yet. But Farnworth wasn't mincing words during Friday's press conference: "We are moving closer and closer to an inevitable tragedy."
His message to the public was blunt. "We cannot emphasize this enough. Do not put yourself in danger, and do not put the rescue agencies in danger."
For thousands of residents, the nightmare peaked in the dead of night. Families were jolted awake and forced to evacuate as water levels surged. Premier John Horgan noted that another 5,000 people—mostly around Abbotsford—might need to pack up and leave by Saturday.
"This is nothing like we've seen in our lifetimes," Horgan said. He called the destruction in the hardest-hit zones a once-in-a-century event. Even for places used to high water, like the Fraser Valley, it’s a one-in-50-years catastrophe.
For Mark and Sarah Henderson, the timing couldn't have been more cruel. Thursday was supposed to mark their 20th wedding anniversary. Instead, they watched their dairy farm in Sumas Prairie get completely wiped out. The floodwaters took everything—their home, their equipment, and most of their livestock. Mark's sister, Emily, quickly launched a GoFundMe to help the couple get back on their feet. In a single day, over 2,100 people chipped in, raising nearly $120,000 CAD (about $95,000 USD).
Footage of a massive barn floating down a submerged highway in the area has been making the rounds on social media. (CNN hasn't independently verified if it belonged to the Hendersons).
"We have just been blown away with people's generosity," Emily wrote on Facebook. "We are all so grateful."
Emergency responders are stretched to the absolute limit. Since Tuesday, the provincial emergency agency has fielded over 8,000 calls for help and pulled off more than 900 water rescues. Armies of volunteers and first responders are still wading through flooded neighborhoods trying to reach trapped locals.
The visuals coming out of the province are staggering. Backyards look like lakes. Major highways are buried under waist-deep water. Over in Merritt, locals were seen pulling stranded pets from the freezing rapids, and CBC News reported that an entire bridge was swept away by the crushing current.
Horgan's plea to residents was simple: listen to the authorities. Stay off the roads. If you're told to evacuate, go.
"This is different," the Premier warned, specifically addressing locals who might think they can ride it out. "What we're going through is unprecedented compared to what you've been through for the last 50 years. Please take it seriously."
Officials are still trying to tally the exact number of destroyed homes and washed-out roads. But Horgan already knows the final bill will be steep, calling the damage "catastrophic."
Help is on the way. The disaster declaration unlocks federal and provincial cash, meaning victims can access subsidies for lost livestock and low-interest loans to rebuild.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took to the airwaves Friday to announce the activation of federal disaster relief. Households dealing with major injuries or severe property damage can claim a $2,000 CAD emergency payout.
"The terrible storms and floods that have been ravaging British Columbia for the past few days are taking a heavy toll," Trudeau said, adding he’s been in constant contact with Premier Horgan. He commended the military personnel and local rescue crews, urging everyone to keep their heads down and follow instructions.
Unfortunately, the sky isn't clearing up anytime soon. Environment Canada's Armel Castellan warned that another atmospheric river is barreling toward the coast. In the worst spots, 48 hours of rain could dump three times the typical November average. The Fraser River is swelling to levels not seen since the historic floods of 1894.
Come Monday, over 200 schools across BC will keep their doors shut, with officials noting that many need extensive repairs. Thursday might offer a brief window of relief as the downpours finally transition to scattered showers.
But drying out is just the beginning. Emergency Management BC officials told CTV News that the cleanup will take months. With river levels receding at a crawl, recovery operations are expected to stretch well past the winter holidays.

(CNN)The Tokyo 2020 Paralympics will not have any spectators as the Covid-19 situation in Japan remains serious, according to organizers. The announcement was made on Monday, following a remote meeting of four Paralympic and Japanese government groups responsible for the Games."In light of the current emergency declaration issued for Tokyo, Saitama and Chiba Prefectures, another declaration of the state of emergency being requested by Shizuoka prefecture and the current infection situation broadly, more stringent measures will be taken for competitions to be held in these prefectures, including having no spectators," the International Paralympic Committee (IPC), the Tokyo 2020 Organizing Committee, the Tokyo Metropolitan Government and the Government of Japan announced in a joint press statement. Members of the public have been urged to refrain from attending road events, but school programs will be allowed to continue with Covid-19 safety measures.The news comes as Tokyo announced 2,962 new daily cases on Monday, after a record 5,773 on Friday.Read MoreSpectators were absent for the majority of events during the Tokyo Olympics. The government is set to prolong the state of emergency in Tokyo and other regions -- which is due to expire on 31 August -- to September 12, according to NHK, Japan's public broadcaster. There was an absence of spectators for 97% of competitions during the Olympics -- including in all Tokyo venues, with limited capacity in several other prefectures."We very much regret that this situation has impacted the Paralympic Games, following the Olympic Games, and we sincerely apologize to all ticket buyers who were looking forward to watching the Games at the venues. We hope that you understand that these measures are unavoidable and being implemented in order to prevent the spread of infection. Everyone is encouraged to watch the Games at home," the statement added. The statement also said that organizers will have a further four-party meeting "in the event of any major changes in the infection situation."The Paralympic Games are scheduled to begin on August 24 and run through to September 5.

(CNN)The Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics will not sell tickets to the general public as the Covid-19 situation globally and within China remains complex, according to organizers. The announcement was made on Monday, following a finalized plan by the local organizing committee and international Olympic bodies.
"In light of the current pandemic situation globally and to ensure the safety of all participants and spectators, it has been decided that tickets should not be sold anymore to spectators from outside China's mainland," the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and the Beijing 2022 Organizing Committee announced in a joint press statement. While the general public is excluded, groups of targeted domestic spectators will be invited to venues under strict Covid-19 safety measures.
