The news comes as several Chinese cities announced tightened lockdown measures on Monday, after a cluster of new cases was identified over the weekend.
Foreign spectators were also absent for the entirety of the Tokyo Olympics a year prior. The government is set to enforce a strict "closed-loop" management system across Beijing and other competition zones -- which will begin well before the opening ceremony -- to prevent any cross-infection, according to state media. This means there will be an absence of regular fans for 100% of the ticketed events during the Winter Games, with only pre-approved groups allowed inside the bubble.
"We very much regret that this situation has impacted the Winter Games, and we sincerely apologize to all international sports fans who were looking forward to traveling to watch the Games at the venues. We hope that you understand that these measures are unavoidable and being implemented in order to prevent the spread of infection. Everyone is encouraged to watch the Games at home," the statement added.
The statement also said that organizers will continuously monitor the situation and have further remote meetings "in the event of any major changes in the infection situation."
The Winter Games are scheduled to begin on February 4 and run through to February 20.

There won't be any tickets sold to the general public for the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics. Organizers confirmed on Monday that the ongoing complexities of the global Covid-19 pandemic have forced them to shut out regular fans, opting instead for a highly restricted spectator model.
The decision was finalized after consultations between the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and local Beijing officials. In a joint press release, the groups made it clear that international travel for the event was officially off the table. Citing the need to protect the safety of athletes and locals alike, they stated that "tickets should not be sold anymore to spectators from outside China's mainland."
Even within China, average citizens won't be able to just buy a ticket. Only specific, targeted groups of domestic spectators will be invited to attend the events, and they will be subject to intense Covid-19 protocols.
The announcement coincides with a fresh wave of localized lockdowns across several Chinese cities, triggered by a weekend flare-up in virus cases.
It’s a familiar scenario for the Olympic community. Just months prior, foreign spectators were completely banned from the Tokyo Summer Olympics. To avoid a major outbreak, Beijing is implementing a massive "closed-loop" bubble system. State media reports that this isolation network will go into effect long before the opening ceremony, completely separating athletes, media, and event staff from the wider Chinese public.
For the average sports fan, it means a total lockout from standard ticketing.
Organizers expressed their dismay over the situation, issuing a formal apology to international fans who had hoped to make the trip. They stressed that the sweeping measures are simply unavoidable if they want to stop the virus from spreading through the Olympic village. "Everyone is encouraged to watch the Games at home," the statement noted.
Officials plan to keep a close eye on the data, leaving room to tweak protocols if the infection rate shifts dramatically in the coming months.
The Winter Olympics are slated to kick off on February 4, wrapping up on February 20.

(CNN)The North Carolina State Wolfpack is out of the College World Series due to Covid-19 protocols, allowing the Vanderbilt Commodores to advance to the finals.The Wolfpack was scheduled to play the Commodores Saturday afternoon in the winner-take-all bracket final, but the NCAA declared the game a no-contest early Saturday morning because of health and safety protocols."This decision was made based on the recommendation of the Championship Medical Team and the Douglas County Health Department. As a result, Vanderbilt will advance to the CWS Finals," NCAA said in a statement. "The NCAA and the committee regret that NC State's student-athletes and coaching staff will not be able to continue in the championship in which they earned the right to participate."Former University of Michigan basketball players accuse late Dr. Robert Anderson of abuseThe NCAA could not provide additional details due to privacy issues, they added."The last 24 hours have been extremely difficult for everyone involved and my heart goes out to the student-athletes, coaches and staff of our baseball program," NC State Athletics Director Boo Corrigan said in a statement. "This is an excruciating way to have their season come to a conclusion, we appreciate their efforts all year long, as well as the incredible support from Wolfpack Nation."Read MoreOn Friday, North Carolina State had only 13 players available in the team's 3-1 loss to Vanderbilt.The Commodores will face either the Texas Longhorns or Mississippi State Bulldogs in the best-of-three championship series beginning Monday in Omaha, Nebraska.Vanderbilt won the 2019 CWS. Last year's tournament was canceled due to the pandemic.The Wolfpack is not the only team whose season was cut short because of pandemic protocols. University of Michigan's men's hockey team, Virginia Commonwealth University's men's basketball team, and Rice University's women's volleyball team were also forced out of NCAA championship tournaments due to Covid-19 protocols.

(CNN)The Oregon Ducks are out of the NCAA Women's Final Four due to severe health and safety protocols, allowing the South Carolina Gamecocks to advance to the national championship.
The Ducks were scheduled to play the Gamecocks Sunday evening in the national semifinal, but the NCAA declared the game a no-contest early Sunday morning because of medical protocols.
"This decision was made based on the recommendation of the Tournament Medical Staff and the local Department of Public Health. As a result, South Carolina will advance to the Championship," the NCAA said in a statement. "The NCAA and the committee regret that Oregon's student-athletes and coaching staff will not be able to continue in the tournament in which they earned the right to participate."
The NCAA could not provide additional details due to medical privacy laws, they added.
"The last 48 hours have been incredibly heartbreaking for everyone involved and my heart goes out to the student-athletes, coaches and staff of our basketball program," Oregon Athletics Director Rob Mullens said in a statement. "This is a devastating way to have their historic season come to an end, we appreciate their dedication all year long, as well as the unwavering support from Ducks fans."
On Friday, Oregon had only seven players available in the team's 72-68 victory over Stanford.
The Gamecocks will face either the UConn Huskies or the Iowa Hawkeyes in the national title game beginning Tuesday in Dallas, Texas.
South Carolina won the 2022 National Championship.
The Ducks are not the only team whose postseason was cut short because of unexpected health protocols. Texas A&M's men's golf team, Florida State's women's soccer team, and Ohio State's wrestling team were also forced to withdraw from recent NCAA championship tournaments due to severe illness outbreaks.

The Oregon Ducks' remarkable run in the NCAA Women's Tournament is over, and they didn't even get to take the court for their final game. A severe virus outbreak has forced the team to withdraw from the Final Four, sending the South Carolina Gamecocks straight through to the national championship.
Early Sunday morning, the NCAA officially called off the highly anticipated semifinal matchup. Citing strict health and safety guidelines, officials declared the game a no-contest.
"This decision was made based on the recommendation of the Tournament Medical Staff and the local Department of Public Health," the NCAA shared in a brief statement. Because of the cancellation, South Carolina automatically advances. The organization expressed deep regret for the players and coaching staff, acknowledging how hard the team fought to reach this stage of the tournament.
Citing medical privacy laws, officials declined to share specific details about the players' conditions or the exact nature of the outbreak.
For Oregon, the news is a crushing blow.
Athletics Director Rob Mullens didn't mince words, calling the past two days "incredibly heartbreaking." In a released statement, he praised the team's resilience. "This is a devastating way to have their historic season come to an end. My heart goes out to the student-athletes, coaches, and staff. We appreciate their dedication all year long, as well as the unwavering support from Ducks fans."
The warning signs were already there on Friday. During their gritty Elite Eight win over Stanford, the Ducks were severely depleted, managing to pull off the victory with a roster of just seven healthy players.
Now, South Carolina moves on. They will face the winner of the UConn-Iowa matchup for the national title on Tuesday night in Dallas. The Gamecocks are certainly no strangers to this stage, having recently won the championship in 2022.
Sadly, Oregon joins a growing list of college programs derailed by sudden medical emergencies. Over the past few seasons, Texas A&M men's golf, Florida State women's soccer, and Ohio State wrestling all saw their title hopes abruptly dashed by unexpected health protocol withdrawals.

(CNN)Cystic fibrosis did not define Claire Wineland. She did.No matter the obstacles placed in front of her, of which there were many, she refused to be pitied and was determined to live a life that mattered. She inspired countless people, invited -- no, demanded -- honest talk about illness and mortality, and brightened the worlds of those she touched with her smile, spunk and spirit.On Sunday evening, after being taken off life support and using the newly transplanted lungs she received just one week earlier, Claire took her last breath. The cause of death was a massive stroke she suffered soon after the transplant surgery. She was 21.'Love what is'A quarter of Claire's life was spent in the hospital. The medical team that tended to her became family. She played hide-and-seek with nurses and left explosions of glitter in her wake. She watched one of her doctors squirm as he gave her the safe-sex talk. She took great care to decorate her hospital room so it felt and looked like home.Read MoreShe expected to die young. Then came the call that offered new lifeHer parents, Melissa Nordquist Yeager and John Wineland, split up when Claire was 3, but they remained friends and partners in her care.Her father credits Claire with teaching him "to not be afraid of what hasn't happened yet" and to learn to "love what is."Yeager, who lost and quit jobs as hospital stays dictated, always marveled at her daughter's aura and her ability to lift up those around her.In 2017, Yeager recalled a conversation with Claire about death. At one point, Claire looked at her mom and said, "After you die, you're closer to everyone you love because you're part of everything," Yeager remembered.These words were a gift, a reminder that Claire would remain with her always, even after she was gone.Trusting ClaireMore than 30,000 people in the United States, more than 70,000 worldwide, have cystic fibrosis, according to the Cystic Fibrosis Foundation. The median survival age is 40, the foundation reports, which is a great improvement from the 1950s, when surviving long enough to attend elementary school was rare.Claire Wineland spent a quarter of her life in the hospital.The genetic and progressive disease creates an overabundance of mucus, which traps infections and blocks airways in the lungs. It also complicates digestion, affects the pancreas and other organs and, eventually, leads to respiratory failure.There is no cure, but dutiful breathing treatments -- which eat up hours each day -- can help with symptoms and complications. A double-lung transplant, when successful, can add years to a patient's life.Claire's parents learned to trust their independent and strong-willed daughter. When it came to her care, she knew her body best -- what worked for her, what didn't and how far she was willing to go.So when she became a legal adult and told them she had no intention of getting a double-lung transplant, they had to accept her decision, even if it pained them."I had to be honest," she once explained. "It's not for me and never has been."A change of heartClaire took the gift of life and her health seriously, but she didn't take herself too seriously. She once escaped from the hospital so she could attend a Bernie Sanders rally. She laughed at the absurdities that often swirled around her, including those moments in the produce aisle at Whole Foods when shoppers would prescribe her unsolicited "cures," telling her to eat more pineapple or that a mushroom cleanse would take care of everything.Claire Wineland's greatest wish, her mother said, was that "her foundation will live on, even in her absence."She was of the mind that she would leave this world with the body she came in with. She'd travel, answer calls for speaking engagements and put energy into her foundation, which she set up at age 13 after coming out of a 16-day medically induced coma. She'd work on a book -- promising it wouldn't be "another happy sick person book" -- and appreciate the small things like swims in the ocean for as long as she was able.At a TEDx talk she gave last year, Claire made a point of saying how cystic fibrosis helped give her a quality of life."Life isn't just about being happy. ... It's not about how you feel second to second," she said. "It's about what you're making of your life and whether you can find a deep pride in who you are and what you've given." Claire had a change of heart about transplant earlier this year, prompted by a steep decline in her health that robbed her of the energy and ability to do what gave her joy and purpose. She wasn't done contributing. It was a welcome, albeit terrifying, development for those who loved her.Living while dying: 'Little Buddha' wisdom from a terminally ill 'goofball'To get on the list for new lungs, she had to be sick enough to need them, yet strong enough to withstand the surgery and recovery. Some people worried that she'd waited too long and wouldn't be given the chance at continued life.But Claire took on the evaluation process to get on the list with laser focus. She sat in an educational meeting at UC San Diego Health's transplant center and diligently took notes. Revealed on her left ankle was a tattoo: the thumbs-up "Don't Panic" logo from "The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy."She listened to the risks and the long list of side effects and emerged unfazed."None of it spooked me," Claire said afterward. "Now that I'm looking at it as something I have to do ... I'm willing to deal with anything."'It's a GO!!!'In late May, she made the list and shared the exciting news with her social media followers who dot the globe. But then, a mix of health and life complications took a toll and knocked her off the list, temporarily. By mid-August, she was back on and feeling ready.Claire and her mother beamed in a photo posted on social media after they learned her transplant was a go. She'd worked hard to get there, was focused on her self-care and getting stronger. She joked that the squats she was doing, upon doctor's orders, would help get her more than lungs. She'd also get a butt.  She knew that the call could come at any minute, and on August 26, it did."It's a GO!!!" she posted on Twitter, not long before she was wheeled into the operating room in San Diego. "See y'all on [the] other side."The nine-hour surgery went well, and her mother reported that the lungs were working great. Yeager posted a video of herself doing a happy dance with friends in the waiting room. Why a terminally ill young woman has changed her mind about livingBut not long after the successful surgery, hope turned to fear. Claire suffered a stroke when a blood clot cut off blood flow to the right side of her brain. She never emerged from her medically induced coma. Despite emergency surgeries, and what her mother described as "Herculean efforts" to try to save her, the daughter she knew was gone. Given the severity of the stroke and Claire's advance directive, it became clear that it was time to let her go. She passed away peacefully, with her parents by her side. "They saw her into this world for her first breath and were with her for her last," Laura McHolm, the board chair for Claire's foundation, wrote in a Facebook post. Less than 3% of lung transplant recipients have a stroke between the surgery and hospital discharge, according to the United Network for Organ Sharing, which operates the nation's organ transplant system.Claire, who understood the importance of organ donation, was a donor herself. On Monday afternoon, her mother received word that Claire had already made a difference. "Claire was able to save the life of two people, her right kidney was transplanted to a 44 year old woman in San Diego, and her left kidney was transplanted to a 55 year old male in Northern California. Also, Claire's corneas and tissue was recovered and she will be able to enhance the life of up to 50 people," a family services specialist wrote to Yeager in an email message. "Claire's gift is huge, I want your family to know that your daughter is a hero."The family intends to honor her memory by continuing to advance Claire's Place Foundation, which she established to financially support others affected by her lifelong disease. 'Go enjoy it'In one of the last videos Claire posted, she went where she hadn't before.A self-described "goofball," she usually engaged people with humor and optimism. This time, she was raw and allowed herself to cry. As she faced the prospect of getting a double-lung transplant, she understood how desperate she was to live -- and give -- more."It hurts everything inside of me to make this video," she said into the camera. "I didn't realize how much I didn't expect to live this long. I didn't expect to have a chance. ... The years of telling myself I can do it on my own are over."Get CNN Health's weekly newsletter Sign up here to get The Results Are In with Dr. Sanjay Gupta every Tuesday from the CNN Health team.She had plenty of emotional support, but she worried what transplant surgery might mean for her future and her parents' future if it didn't go well. What if they poured everything into her transplant, and she didn't make it or was not able to work again, she explained later. She needed financial help. By asking for it, she said, she could relax and get in the headspace for whatever would come next.Before signing off, Claire implored viewers to do one thing."Go enjoy your life. Really. I mean that seriously," she said with her signature smile and laugh, her eyes not yet dry. "Go enjoy it, 'cause there are people fighting like hell for it."

Part 1: Generated Text
(CNN)Osteosarcoma did not dictate Liam Thorne’s life. He did.
No matter the obstacles placed in front of him, of which there were many, he refused to be pitied and was determined to live a life that mattered. He inspired countless people, invited -- no, demanded -- honest talk about chronic illness and mortality, and brightened the worlds of those he touched with his humor, resilience, and spirit.
On Sunday morning, after battling a sudden onset of sepsis following an experimental immunotherapy treatment he started just two weeks earlier, Liam took his last breath. The cause of death was systemic organ failure triggered by the infection. He was 23.
'Embrace the present'
A significant portion of Liam's life was spent in oncology wards. The medical team that tended to him became a second family. He played practical jokes on the nursing staff and left trails of neon stickers in his wake. He watched one of his surgeons laugh as he gave him advice on dating with a prosthetic leg. He took great care to decorate his hospital room so it felt and looked like a sanctuary, complete with his acoustic guitar.
He expected to die young. Then came the opportunity that offered a potential reprieve.
His parents, Sarah Davies and Mark Thorne, divorced when Liam was 7, but they remained united and partnered in his care.
His mother credits Liam with teaching her "to not fear the inevitable" and to learn to "embrace the present."
Davies, who shifted to freelance work as hospital stays dictated her schedule, always marveled at her son's energy and his ability to uplift those around him.
In 2021, Davies recalled a conversation with Liam about legacy. At one point, Liam looked at his mom and said, "When I'm gone, I'll be in the music you listen to and the art you see, because I'm leaving a piece of myself in all of it," Davies remembered.
These words were a comfort, a reminder that Liam would remain with her always, even after he was gone.
Trusting Liam
More than 1,000 new cases of osteosarcoma are diagnosed in the United States each year, according to the American Cancer Society. The five-year survival rate varies depending on the spread, the organization reports, which is a significant improvement from previous decades when limb-salvaging surgeries were rarely successful.
The rare bone cancer often originates in the long bones of the arms and legs, creating aggressive tumors that can quickly metastasize to the lungs. It complicates mobility, requires intense chemotherapy, and, eventually, can lead to severe respiratory or systemic failure if the spread cannot be contained.
There is no definitive cure for advanced stages, but diligent treatments -- which demand grueling months of physical therapy and targeted radiation -- can help manage the progression. Clinical immunotherapy trials, when successful, can offer periods of remission.
Liam's parents learned to trust their independent and strong-willed son. When it came to his care, he knew his body best -- what therapies worked for him, what side effects were unbearable, and how far he was willing to push himself.
So when he became a legal adult and told them he had no intention of pursuing another round of traditional chemotherapy after his second relapse, they had to accept his decision, even if it pained them.
"I had to be realistic," he once explained on his blog. "It strips away my quality of life, and it's just not for me anymore."
A shift in perspective
Liam took his limited time and his health seriously, but he didn't take himself too seriously. He once snuck out of his hospital ward so he could attend a local indie rock concert. He laughed at the absurdities that often swirled around him, including those moments in coffee shops when strangers would prescribe him unsolicited "miracle cures," telling him to try alkaline water or that a strict vegan diet would reverse the cancer.
Liam Thorne's greatest wish, his father said, was that "his advocacy work will continue to fund pediatric cancer research, even in his absence."
He was of the mind that he would leave this world on his own terms. He'd travel, answer calls for podcast interviews, and put energy into his non-profit, which he established at age 17 after losing his right leg above the knee. He'd work on a documentary -- promising it wouldn't be "just another sad cancer story" -- and appreciate the small things like playing his guitar for as long as he was able.
At a university symposium he spoke at last year, Liam made a point of saying how cancer helped clarify his purpose.
"Life isn't just about existing comfortably. ... It's not about avoiding pain," he said. "It's about what you're creating while you're here and whether you can find a deep satisfaction in the connections you've made."
Liam had a shift in perspective about experimental trials earlier this year, prompted by a rapid spread to his lungs that robbed him of the breath and ability to do what gave him joy. He wasn't done creating. It was a welcome, albeit terrifying, development for those who loved him.
'Let's do this!!!'
To qualify for the new immunotherapy trial, he had to match the specific genetic markers, yet be strong enough to withstand the severe immune responses the drug would trigger. Some people worried that his immune system was already too compromised and wouldn't be able to handle the aggressive regimen.
But Liam took on the screening process with laser focus. He sat in a clinical meeting at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute and diligently recorded the session. Displayed on his guitar case was a sticker: the bold "Keep Moving Forward" motto from his favorite animated film.
He listened to the risks and the long list of potential adverse reactions and emerged unfazed.
"None of it scared me," Liam said afterward. "Now that I'm looking at it as a real chance to stick around ... I'm willing to face the fire."
In late October, he was approved for the trial and shared the exciting news with his thousands of online followers. But then, a mix of fluctuating blood counts and fevers delayed his start date, temporarily. By mid-November, his numbers stabilized and he was ready.
Liam and his father beamed in a photo posted on social media after they learned his first infusion was scheduled. He'd worked hard to maintain his weight, was focused on his physical therapy, and was getting stronger. He joked that the extra protein shakes he was drinking, upon doctor's orders, would give him the muscles he always wanted.
He knew the treatment would be intense, and on November 20, it began.
"Let's do this!!!" he posted on Instagram, not long before the IV was hooked up in Boston. "See you guys on the flip side."
The initial infusions went well, and his father reported that the tumors seemed to be responding. Thorne posted a video of himself playing a celebratory song in the clinic.
But not long after the early success, hope turned to tragedy. Liam developed a severe, unforeseen infection that triggered septic shock. He was placed on a ventilator in the intensive care unit. Despite aggressive antibiotics, and what his father described as "heroic efforts" to try to stabilize his blood pressure, the son he knew was fading. Given the severity of the organ damage and Liam's advance directive, it became clear that it was time to let him go. He passed away peacefully, with his family by his side.
"They welcomed him into this world with boundless love and surrounded him with it as he left," Sarah Jenkins, the director of Liam's non-profit, wrote in a blog post.
The family intends to honor his memory by continuing to advance the Thorne Foundation, which he established to financially support teenagers affected by limb-loss and cancer.
'Make some noise'
In one of the last videos Liam posted, he went where he hadn't before.
A self-described "optimist," he usually engaged people with sarcastic wit and bravery. This time, he was vulnerable and allowed his fear to show. As he faced the prospect of the grueling clinical trial, he understood how desperate he was to live -- and play music -- more.
"It is terrifying to make this video," he said into the camera. "I didn't realize how much I was banking on this working. I didn't expect to feel this hopeful again. ... The phase of just accepting the end is over."
He had plenty of emotional support, but he worried what the trial might mean for his family's finances if it required prolonged ICU stays, he explained later. He needed help raising awareness for his foundation so he could relax and focus on the battle ahead.
Before signing off, Liam implored viewers to do one thing.
"Go make some noise. Seriously. Create something," he said with his signature crooked smile, his voice shaking slightly. "Go do it, 'cause there are people fighting like hell just for the chance to try."

(CNN)Liam Thorne wasn’t defined by osteosarcoma. He made sure of that.
The obstacles in front of him were relentless, but he refused to be an object of pity. Instead, he fiercely carved out a life that mattered. He sparked difficult, honest conversations about what it means to be young and dying, and he did it while bringing a rare, chaotic joy to the people in his orbit.
He died Sunday morning. He was 23. The cause was systemic organ failure, brought on by sepsis shortly after he began an experimental immunotherapy trial.
'Embrace the present'
Liam practically grew up on oncology wards, and the medical staff eventually just became an extension of his family. He’d prank the nurses, leaving a trail of neon stickers stuck to the walls behind his wheelchair. He’d proudly display his acoustic guitar and movie posters, insisting that his hospital room feel like a real sanctuary, not a sterile clinic. He even managed to make his surgeon blush while asking for dating advice after his leg amputation.
He always assumed he would die young. Then, out of nowhere, a clinical trial offered a sliver of a future.
His parents, Sarah Davies and Mark Thorne, split up when Liam was 7. But when it came to their son, they were a united front. His mother says Liam taught her how to stop fearing the inevitable. "He showed me how to embrace the present," she said.
Davies ended up shifting to freelance work just to accommodate Liam’s unpredictable hospital stays. Through it all, she marveled at his energy. He had an uncanny ability to lift up the people around him, even when he was the one in the hospital bed.
She still thinks about a conversation they had back in 2021 about what he’d leave behind. "When I'm gone," Liam told her, "I'll be in the music you listen to and the art you see, because I'm leaving a piece of myself in all of it."
Those words stuck with her. They were a gift.
Trusting Liam
Osteosarcoma is rare. The American Cancer Society notes that only about 1,000 new cases are diagnosed in the U.S. each year. Survival rates vary wildly depending on how far the cancer has spread—though things are much better now than decades past, when limb-salvaging surgeries almost never worked.
The disease usually starts in the long bones of the arms and legs, spawning aggressive tumors that love to travel to the lungs. It strips away mobility. It demands brutal rounds of chemotherapy. Eventually, if it can't be stopped, it leads to respiratory or systemic failure.
There is no magic cure for the advanced stages. Patients endure grueling physical therapy and radiation just to manage the spread. Sometimes, if you're lucky, an immunotherapy trial buys a little more time.
Mark and Sarah had to learn to trust their son. Liam was fiercely independent, and he knew his body better than anyone—what worked, what didn't, and exactly how much suffering he was willing to tolerate.
So, when he turned 18 and announced he was done with traditional chemotherapy after his second relapse, his parents had to swallow their pain and back him up.
"I had to be realistic," Liam wrote on his blog at the time. "It strips away my quality of life, and it's just not for me anymore."
A shift in perspective
He took his illness seriously, but he absolutely refused to take himself seriously. Once, he actually snuck out of his hospital ward to catch a local indie rock show. He found the absurdity of his situation hilarious—especially the strangers in coffee shops who would confidently prescribe him "miracle cures," insisting that alkaline water or a vegan diet would fix his mutated cells.
He wanted to go out on his own terms. He traveled when he could, did podcast interviews, and poured his remaining energy into the non-profit he started at 17, right after losing his right leg. He was also shooting a documentary, swearing up and down that it wouldn't be "just another sad cancer story."
During a university symposium last year, Liam explained how the disease had actually sharpened his focus.
"Life isn't just about existing comfortably, and it's not about avoiding pain," he told the crowd. "It's about what you're creating while you're here. It's about finding a deep satisfaction in the connections you've made."
But his perspective shifted earlier this year. The cancer spread rapidly to his lungs, stealing his breath and, with it, his ability to play music. He realized he wasn't done creating yet. So, he decided to look into experimental trials—a terrifying but welcome pivot for everyone who loved him.
'Let's do this!!!'
Getting into the trial wasn't simple. He needed the right genetic markers, and he had to be just healthy enough to survive the massive immune response the drugs would provoke. Friends worried his body was already too battered for such an aggressive regimen.
Liam didn't care. He tackled the screening process with total tunnel vision. During a clinical meeting at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, he sat there quietly recording the session, a "Keep Moving Forward" sticker slapped prominently on his guitar case.
He listened to the terrifying list of potential side effects and didn't blink.
"None of it scared me," he said later. "Now that I'm looking at it as a real chance to stick around, I'm willing to face the fire."
He got the green light in late October, sharing the news with his thousands of online followers. Fevers and fluctuating blood counts delayed the start, but by mid-November, his numbers finally leveled out.
He posted a photo with his dad, both of them beaming, right after the first infusion was scheduled. He had been drinking extra protein shakes on doctor's orders, joking that he was finally going to get ripped.
On November 20, the treatment began.
"Let's do this!!!" he posted on Instagram from his hospital bed in Boston. "See you guys on the flip side."
At first, it worked. The initial infusions went smoothly, and his dad reported that the tumors were actually shrinking. Liam even posted a video of himself playing his guitar in the clinic to celebrate.
Then everything collapsed. Liam contracted a severe, unforeseen infection that threw his body into septic shock. He was rushed to the ICU and put on a ventilator. Despite massive doses of antibiotics and what his father called "heroic efforts" to stabilize his blood pressure, his organs simply couldn't recover. Following his advance directives, the medical team withdrew life support. He died peacefully with his parents beside him.
"They welcomed him into this world with boundless love and surrounded him with it as he left," Sarah Jenkins, the director of Liam's non-profit, wrote online.
His family plans to keep the Thorne Foundation running to financially support teenagers dealing with cancer and limb loss.
'Make some noise'
In one of his final videos, Liam showed a side of himself he usually kept hidden.
Normally a sarcastic, brave optimist, he let his guard down. Facing the brutal reality of the clinical trial, he admitted just how desperately he wanted to live.
"It is terrifying to make this video," he told the camera, allowing himself to be vulnerable. "I didn't realize how much I was banking on this working. I didn't expect to feel this hopeful again. The phase of just accepting the end is over."
He knew he had emotional support, but he was terrified of bankrupting his family with prolonged ICU stays. He asked his followers for help raising awareness for his foundation, just so he could clear his head for the fight ahead.
Before turning the camera off, his voice shook just a little bit. But that signature crooked smile was still there.
"Go make some noise. Seriously. Create something," he urged. "Go do it, 'cause there are people fighting like hell just for the chance to try."

SydneyFour of Australia's eight capital cities are now under Covid-19 lockdown, as authorities struggle to contain fresh outbreaks of the highly infectious Delta variant.Perth, the capital of Western Australia state, began a four-day lockdown at midnight Tuesday, while Brisbane, the capital of Queensland, will enter a three-day lockdown starting Tuesday evening.They join New South Wales' capital, Sydney, and Darwin, capital of the Northern Territory, which are already under lockdown.Collectively, the four cities have a population of more than 10.2 million people, according to government data. As of Monday afternoon, the country has 271 active cases, the majority of which are from New South Wales, according to the national health departmentAustralia was celebrated for its initial response to the pandemic and for getting its economy more or less back on track long ago.Read MoreBut with that security has come complacency, particularly in the federal government, which failed to secure enough vaccine doses to prevent the regular "circuit breaker" lockdowns that come every time a handful of cases emerge, or even the longer restrictions the capital cities are experiencing now. Residents of Perth and the neighboring Peel region must stay home except for urgent reasons after officials detected a third case, linked to the outbreak in Sydney.And in Brisbane, the country's third largest city, people will be required to stay home except for essential work, health care, grocery shopping or exercise.Sydney in lockdown, borders shut and hardly anyone vaccinated. How long can Australia go on like this?Sydney, home to a fifth of Australia's 25 million population, is under a two-week lockdown until July 9, while the lockdown in Darwin was extended for another 72 hours until Friday. Concerns the Delta strain could spark significant outbreaks have also seen some form of restrictions in most other cities, with more than 20 million Australians, or about 80% of the population affected.Authorities late on Monday said people age under 60 would now be able to get the AstraZeneca Covid-19 vaccine if approved by their doctor, who will be covered by a no-fault indemnity scheme, to ramp up a sluggish immunization drive.Vaccinations have also been made mandatory for high-risk aged-care workers and employees in quarantine hotels."I'm absolutely comfortable about the rollout through the aged care sector," Australia's chief medical officer Paul Kelly told the Australian Broadcasting Corporation on Tuesday.Lockdowns, swift contact tracing and strict social distancing rules have largely helped Australia keep its Covid-19 numbers relatively low, with just over 30,500 cases and 910 deaths -- but its vaccine rollout has hit several roadblocks.Officials two weeks ago limited the use of AstraZeneca vaccines only to people over 60 due to blood clot concerns, while recommending Pfizer shots to everyone under 60 in a major change to its immunization drive.Australia has fully vaccinated nearly 5% of its population, compared with more than 46% in the United States and 48% in the UK, according to Our World in Data. Its rates are more comparable with Indonesia and India, which, like much of the developing world, were left out of the agreements with pharmaceutical companies that secured hundreds of millions of vaccine doses for most of the rich world.CNN's Angela Dewan contributed to this report.

Toronto. Three of Canada's most populous provinces are now under strict stay-at-home orders, as health authorities struggle to contain rapid outbreaks of a highly transmissible new viral variant.
Toronto, the capital of Ontario, began a two-week lockdown at midnight Sunday, while Montreal, the largest city in Quebec, will enter a ten-day lockdown starting Monday evening.
They join British Columbia's largest city, Vancouver, which is already under heavy restrictions.
Collectively, the affected metropolitan areas have a population of more than 12.5 million people, according to national census data. As of Tuesday morning, the country has 4,320 active cases, the majority of which are concentrated in Ontario, according to the federal health ministry.
Canada was initially praised for its high vaccination uptake and for safely reopening its borders months ago.
But with that success has come a relaxation of precautions, particularly from provincial governments, which failed to adequately prepare hospital capacity to prevent the sudden emergency measures that are implemented every time a cluster of variant cases emerges.
Residents of Toronto and the surrounding Greater Toronto Area must stay home except for essential reasons after officials detected widespread community transmission, linked to an outbreak in neighboring regions.
And in Montreal, the country's second-largest city, people will be required to work from home and leave only for essential health care, grocery shopping, or solitary exercise.
Toronto, home to nearly 3 million of Canada's 38 million population, is under a strict lockdown until late next month, while the restrictions in Vancouver were extended for another 48 hours until Thursday. Concerns that the new strain could spark nationwide outbreaks have also seen some form of capacity limits in most other provinces, with more than 25 million Canadians affected.
Authorities late on Monday said vulnerable populations would now be eligible to receive a modified booster shot if approved by their physician, who will be supported by a newly expanded public health initiative, to ramp up a stalling booster drive.
Booster mandates have also been introduced for frontline healthcare workers and employees in long-term care facilities.
"I am entirely confident in the rollout across the healthcare sector," Canada's chief public health officer told national broadcasters on Tuesday.
Targeted lockdowns, rapid antigen testing, and strict indoor masking rules have largely helped Canada keep its severe hospitalization numbers relatively low—but its booster rollout has hit several logistical roadblocks.
Officials two weeks ago limited the distribution of the latest vaccine formulations only to high-risk individuals due to supply chain bottlenecks, while recommending standard doses to everyone else in a major shift to its immunization strategy.
Canada has administered the updated booster to nearly 15% of its population, compared with more than 35% in the United Kingdom and 40% in Israel, according to global health trackers. Its rates are more comparable with parts of Western Europe, which, despite high initial vaccination rates, were caught off guard by the rapid spread of the latest variant.

Three of Canada's biggest provinces are slamming the brakes. Facing a surge of a highly contagious new viral variant, authorities have ordered strict stay-at-home mandates, scrambling to get the infection numbers under control.
Starting Sunday at midnight, Toronto plunged into a two-week lockdown. Montreal followed suit shortly after, preparing for a ten-day shutdown to begin Monday night. Both cities join Vancouver, where heavy restrictions are already a daily reality for residents.
Between them, these metropolitan hubs account for over 12.5 million people. The federal health ministry reported 4,320 active cases by Tuesday morning, with Ontario bearing the brunt of the infections.
Not long ago, Canada was taking a victory lap for its sky-high vaccination rates and newly reopened borders. But that early success seemingly bred complacency. Provincial governments, in particular, are facing intense criticism for failing to shore up hospital capacity. Now, instead of managing small clusters, officials are relying on the same sweeping emergency restrictions they used at the height of the pandemic.
In Toronto and the broader Greater Toronto Area, the rules are strict: stay home. After contact tracers uncovered widespread community transmission bleeding in from neighboring regions, exceptions to the lockdown have been strictly limited to essential errands. Montreal's rules are looking very similar. The country's second-largest city has mandated remote work, allowing residents out only for medical care, groceries, or solo exercise.
Toronto alone is home to nearly 3 million people, and they're looking at restrictions lasting until late next month. Out west, Vancouver just tacked another 48 hours onto its current protocols. The ripple effects are being felt everywhere. Fearing the strain will jump provincial borders, health officials have slapped capacity limits on businesses nationwide, impacting upwards of 25 million Canadians.
To fight the surge, authorities announced late Monday that vulnerable groups can now get a modified booster dose. Physicians, backed by a newly expanded public liability initiative, are being urged to authorize the shots to help kickstart a sluggish campaign. Frontline healthcare workers and long-term care staff no longer have a choice; for them, the boosters are mandatory.
"I am entirely confident in the rollout across the healthcare sector," Canada's chief public health officer assured national broadcasters on Tuesday.
Historically, Canada has relied on targeted lockdowns, widespread rapid antigen testing, and strict mask mandates to keep hospitalizations from spiraling. But this latest booster campaign has been a logistical mess.
Just two weeks ago, supply chain bottlenecks forced officials to restrict the newest vaccine formulations entirely to high-risk individuals. Everyone else was told to settle for standard doses—a major, and highly scrutinized, shift in public health strategy.
So far, only about 15% of the Canadian population has received the updated booster. Compare that to 35% in the UK or 40% in Israel. Right now, Canada's numbers look a lot more like those in Western Europe—regions that boasted high initial vaccination rates but ultimately found themselves totally unprepared for the speed of this latest wave.

(CNN)Anticipation will reach a fever pitch at the Kaseya Center on Saturday as a stacked UFC 287 card delivers a blockbuster world title rematch. Middleweight champion Alex Pereira (7-1) faces Israel Adesanya (23-2) in Miami in one of the sport's most highly anticipated second acts ever, just months after their grueling clash in November 2022.
That fight last year -- at UFC 281 -- saw the Brazilian striking specialist defeat the Nigerian-New Zealander via a stunning fifth-round technical knockout (TKO) to claim the undisputed belt. Pereira has not fought since capturing the title, while Adesanya immediately triggered his rematch clause in his sole focus since losing the championship he held for three years.
Read More
It is a fight that needs no additional hyping, but Thursday's intense pre-fight press conference in Florida has only added to the anticipation as the pair exchanged icy glares in front of a raucous crowd.
"I wouldn't say I'm treating this any differently," Adesanya said. "He is a massive challenge, he hits hard and he comes to fight each and every time, but in the environment he stepped into, he has to live with the fact that I have ruled this division for a reason."
Pereira knocks down Adesanya during their first championship bout at UFC 281 in New York.
Fighting talk
Last month, Pereira promised to deliver a "career-ending knockout" upon his rival and criticized Adesanya for his alleged mental weakness following their first epic MMA bout. "I'm not going to leave it to the judges. I'm going to give the people what they want," Pereira told MMA Fighting.
"If he wants this rematch, I'll do it right away. The thing is, after I finish him next weekend, he's not going to want to fight again. It's going to be a career-ending defeat.
"He's not going to want to come back for the trilogy, he's probably going to retire."
At yesterday's press conference, the Brazilian doubled down, alleging that Adesanya was forced into the rematch by his ego.
"He avoided the reality of that knockout at all costs," Pereira said.
"His pride and his coaches had to force his hand -- he was trying to delay the inevitable, he was trying to fight other contenders, he wanted to fight anybody but this rematch.
"He knows who the champion is, he knows who really won that fight. I don't live in the past, Saturday night's the future, and I'm destroying him again."
'Just look at his resume'
The co-main event tees up a crucial welterweight clash as hometown favorite Jorge Masvidal (35-16) seeks to defend his turf against the relentless contender Gilbert Burns (21-5). Masvidal pulled off a stunning string of knockouts earlier in his career to become a massive draw, but faces a tough test in the grappling-heavy Brazilian.
Masvidal and Burns face off during the UFC 287 press conference.
Perhaps unsurprising given the ferocity of the other main event's press conference, Masvidal's and Burns' exchanges were markedly more respectful, with a handshake at the end of the face-off.
"Just look at his [Burns'] resume and look what he's done to his opponents, I've got to protect myself at all times and stay sharp," Masvidal said.
It might not be a title fight, but the bantamweight bout between Rob Font (19-6) and Adrian Yanez (16-3) promises to be another entertaining watch for fans.
Kicking off the main card, an explosive matchup pits a versatile striker in Holland against a veteran brawler in Ponzinibbio. A bantamweight bout between Raul Rosas Jr. and Christian Rodriguez rounds off the main card.
The main card is slated to begin at 10 p.m. ET, with the preliminary card starting at 8 p.m. ET.
Here's how to watch the fights on Saturday: Viewers in Australia can watch on Main Event, Kayo, and Fetch TV. In Canada, the event is broadcast on BELL, Rogers, Shaw, and TSN. Fans in the UK and Republic of Ireland can tune into BT Sport 1. In the US, the main card is available exclusively on ESPN+ Pay-Per-View.

The tension inside Miami’s Kaseya Center is going to be thick enough to cut with a knife this Saturday. UFC 287 has finally arrived, bringing with it a middleweight title rematch that combat sports fans have been obsessing over for months. Alex Pereira and Israel Adesanya are officially running it back.
It’s only been five months since their last meeting at UFC 281, but the fallout from that November night still lingers. Pereira, the heavy-handed Brazilian kickboxer, shocked the world by stopping Adesanya with a brutal fifth-round TKO to snatch the belt. Before that sequence, Adesanya had ruled the 185-pound division with an iron fist for three years. Now, he wants his throne back.
Neither man really needed to sell this fight—their bitter kickboxing history does that on its own. Still, Thursday’s press conference in Florida gave the raucous crowd exactly what they came for: icy glares and plenty of bad blood.
Adesanya seemed completely unbothered by the pressure. "I wouldn't say I'm treating this any differently," he shrugged, acknowledging Pereira's raw power before quickly reminding everyone of his own championship pedigree. "He has to live with the fact that I have ruled this division for a reason."
Pereira, meanwhile, has been relentless in his trash talk. A few weeks ago, he openly questioned his rival's mental toughness and promised another devastating finish. "I'm going to give the people what they want," Pereira recently told reporters, swearing he wouldn't let the judges decide the outcome. He even went as far as suggesting that another knockout would send the former champion straight into retirement.
During the press conference, the newly crowned king didn't back down an inch. Speaking through a translator, Pereira claimed Adesanya was letting his ego drive him into a fight he couldn't win. "He avoided the reality of that knockout," Pereira said, adding that Adesanya's pride forced his hand into taking an immediate rematch instead of rebuilding his confidence against other contenders.
Just before the main event, the Miami crowd will get to roar for one of their own. Hometown hero Jorge Masvidal is stepping into the cage against the always-dangerous Gilbert Burns for a high-stakes welterweight showdown.
Unlike the main event, the build-up here has been almost entirely respectful. Both veterans shared a quiet nod and a handshake during their staredown. Masvidal knows exactly what he's up against, praising Burns' lethal grappling resume and noting he'll need to stay sharp from the opening bell to avoid a submission.
If you're looking for fireworks outside of the marquee names, keep an eye on the bantamweight scrap between Rob Font and Adrian Yanez. That fight has "Fight of the Night" written all over it. Kevin Holland taking on Santiago Ponzinibbio at welterweight is just the icing on the cake to round out the main card.
The main action kicks off at 10 p.m. ET, with the prelims setting the stage at 8 p.m. ET.
Fans in the US can catch the pay-per-view on ESPN+, while UK and Irish viewers can tune in via BT Sport 1. Australian fans can watch on Kayo or Main Event, and Canadian viewers have access through TSN and major cable providers.

(CNN)Heat, humidity and tropical systems have been the nemesis of athletes during these Olympic Games, and the closing weekend will bring more of the same.While heat and humidity in July are common, these levels were above average. Tropical systems can also happen in July, but traditionally the peak months in Japan are August and September. Organizers tried to help athletes to escape the heat and humidity by shifting venues and event times for the first few weeks, but it will still plague the upcoming marathon.Heat and humidity: a critical combination It's not just the temperatures but also the humidity. When both are high, conditions can be oppressive for athletes.Why summer is spooky season in JapanRead MoreWhen your body gets too hot, you sweat to cool off. But if the sweat cannot evaporate, the body cannot regulate its temperature. You need evaporation in order to effectively cool your body. When the relative humidity is high, the rate of evaporation from the body decreases, according to the National Weather Service. In other words, the human body feels warmer in humid conditions.To find cooler temperatures, the Olympics marathon events are taking place this weekend in Sapporo. Sapporo, about 500 miles north of Tokyo, can be as much as 40 degrees Fahrenheit cooler.Damian Warner: Three-time Olympian says Tokyo 2020 is the 'hottest' Games' he's competed inBut the high temperatures hitting Tokyo are spreading throughout the entire country. It will be cooler in Tokyo on Saturday than in Sapporo. Due to rain in the forecast, Tokyo's high temperature Saturday will be 88, and Sapporo's will be 93. The women's marathon takes place Saturday morning while the men race Sunday. This weekend Sapporo will see morning low temperatures of around 77 and afternoon highs of 90 to 93.  Theses temperatures are 9 to 13 degrees above normal.

Temperatures during both races will likely be between 80 and 86 degrees. 80-86° F). Relative humidity of 70% to 80% will make it feel warmer. Organizers moved the start of the women's race an hour earlier, to 6 a.m., to avoid the heat."Athletes' health and well-being are the organisers' top priorities. Having taken advice from World Athletic's medical team and reviewed all other considerations, World Athletics (WA), the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and the Tokyo 2020 Organising Committee had a series of discussions today and confirmed the schedule change," a Tokyo 2020 statement read.But will that one hour make a difference? Sunrise in Sapporo is around 4:30 a.m. now. With the race starting at 7 a.m., the sun will have been up for 2.5 hours before the race starts.

For comparison's sake, let's say you're a runner who tries to beat the heat with an early workout in Atlanta, where sunrise is around 6:50 a.m. This would be like doing your workout between 9:30 a.m. and 11:30 a.m. -- not exactly the ideal time to be running.The only temperature relief may come from a tropical storm that will dump buckets of water on athletes this weekend. Tropical storm to impact Tokyo this weekendThe Olympics have already been plagued by one tropical system when Nepartak made landfall during the first week. Now another, Tropical Storm Mirinae, is set to impact the final weekend. Events such as rowing were postponed two days due to the storm. 

The center of the storm is expected to pass just southeast of Tokyo on Sunday morning.For now, it looks like the majority of the rain will be Saturday night into the first half of Sunday. Some showers and scattered storms could linger into the evening for the closing ceremony. Winds will be a bit gusty, especially the first half of Sunday, but it shouldn't be too bad as Mirinae is expected to remain a mid-range tropical storm.The Closing Ceremony takes place Sunday at 8 p.m local time (7 a.m. EDT).

(CNN)Extreme heat, humidity and warming trends have been the nemesis of athletes during these Olympic Games, and the closing weekend will bring more of the same.
While heat and humidity in August are common, these levels were record-breaking. Heatwaves can also happen in August, but traditionally Paris does not experience the oppressive humidity seen in other host cities. Organizers tried to help athletes to escape the heat and humidity by adding cooling breaks and shifting event times for the first few weeks, but it will still plague the upcoming marathon. 
Heat and humidity: a critical combination
It's not just the temperatures but also the humidity. When both are high, conditions can be oppressive for athletes. 
When your body gets too hot, you sweat to cool off. But if the sweat cannot evaporate, the body cannot regulate its temperature. You need evaporation in order to effectively cool your body. When the relative humidity is high, the rate of evaporation from the body decreases, according to the National Weather Service. In other words, the human body feels warmer in humid conditions. 
To find cooler temperatures, the Olympics marathon events are taking place early in the morning. 
But the high temperatures hitting France are spreading throughout the entire country. The men's marathon takes place Saturday morning while the women race Sunday. This weekend Paris will see morning low temperatures of around 72 and afternoon highs of 90 to 95. These temperatures are 10 to 15 degrees above normal.
Temperatures during both races will likely be between 75 and 85 degrees. Relative humidity of 60% to 70% will make it feel warmer. Organizers moved the start of the marathon races to 8 a.m., to avoid the peak heat. 
"Athletes' health and well-being are the organisers' top priorities. Having taken advice from World Athletic's medical team and reviewed all other considerations, World Athletics (WA), the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and the Paris 2024 Organising Committee had a series of discussions today and confirmed the schedule," a Paris 2024 statement read. 
But will that earlier time make a difference? Sunrise in Paris is around 6:30 a.m. now. With the race starting at 8 a.m., the sun will have been up for 1.5 hours before the race starts. 
For comparison's sake, let's say you're a runner who tries to beat the heat with an early workout in New York, where sunrise is around 6:00 a.m. This would be like doing your workout between 7:30 a.m. and 9:30 a.m. -- not exactly the ideal time to be running.
The only temperature relief may come from a weak cold front that will bring scattered showers to athletes this weekend.
The Olympics have already been plagued by one sweltering heatwave during the first week. Now another, combined with the Urban Heat Island effect, is set to impact the final weekend. Events such as the triathlon were impacted by both heat and water conditions. 
The center of the high-pressure system is expected to sit directly over Paris on Sunday morning. 
For now, it looks like the majority of the heat will peak Saturday afternoon into the first half of Sunday. Some cloud cover and scattered showers could linger into the evening for the closing ceremony. Winds will be mostly calm, especially the first half of Sunday, but it shouldn't be too bad as temperatures are expected to drop slightly by nightfall. 
The Closing Ceremony takes place Sunday at 9 p.m. local time (3 p.m. EDT).

Extreme heat and suffocating humidity have been absolute nightmares for athletes at these Olympic Games. And as we head into the closing weekend in Paris, there’s no real relief in sight.
While it’s no secret that August can get hot, the levels we're seeing right now are historic. Heatwaves happen, sure, but Paris doesn't usually get slapped with the kind of oppressive, tropical humidity that defines summer in cities like Tokyo or Atlanta. Olympic organizers have been scrambling for weeks, trying to dodge the worst of it by tweaking event schedules and mandating cooling breaks. Still, the brutal weather is going to be a massive problem for the upcoming marathons. 
It really is a punishing combination. When soaring temps crash into high humidity, the conditions become downright dangerous for endurance athletes. 
Here's why: when your core overheats, you sweat. That sweat evaporates, cooling the skin and bringing your body temperature back down. But when the air is already thick with moisture—when relative humidity spikes—that sweat has nowhere to go. It just clings to you. The evaporation process stalls out, and your body essentially loses its natural air conditioning system. 
To beat the heat, organizers had to get creative with the marathon schedule. The men race on Saturday, and the women follow on Sunday. But the blistering heat isn't just a localized problem; it's baking the entire country. Paris is looking at morning lows hovering around 72 degrees and miserable afternoon highs pushing into the mid-90s—easily 10 to 15 degrees hotter than a normal Parisian summer. 
By the time the runners hit the pavement, temperatures will already be sitting somewhere between 75 and 85 degrees. Factor in the 60% to 70% humidity, and it’s going to feel significantly hotter. To mitigate the risk, the starting gun for both races was bumped to 8 a.m.
“Athletes' health and well-being are the organisers' top priorities,” read a joint statement from World Athletics, the IOC, and the Paris 2024 committee, noting that medical experts signed off on the revised schedule.
But honestly, is an 8 a.m. start going to be enough? The sun rises over Paris around 6:30 right now. That means the pavement will have been baking in direct sunlight for an hour and a half before the athletes even take their first strides.
Think about it like this: if you live on the East Coast and try to sneak in a morning run during a heatwave, starting at 8 a.m. means you’re finishing closer to 10:30. By then, the sun is high and the air is heavy. It's far from ideal for an elite marathon.
The only sliver of hope is a weak cold front drifting toward the city, which might drop a few scattered showers over the weekend.
These Games have already been rocked by one brutal heatwave, leading to scary scenes of athletes collapsing after finishing their events. Now, thanks in part to the Urban Heat Island effect trapping the hot air inside the city limits, this second wave is perfectly timed to disrupt the final weekend of competition.
A high-pressure system is expected to park itself right over the French capital by Sunday morning. The worst of the heat will likely peak Saturday afternoon and roll right into Sunday. Thankfully, some cloud cover and a breeze might roll in just in time for the Closing Ceremony, offering a tiny bit of relief before the Games officially wrap up at 9 p.m. local time.

(CNN)A small private plane crashed into an empty building on the outskirts of Milan, northern Italy, on Sunday, killing all eight people onboard, police said.The plane, which took off from Milan's Linate city airport, was headed for the island of Sardinia, officials said.The crash occurred just outside a suburban metro station.People in the area could hear a blast when the plane collided with the two-story building, which was empty at the time as it was being renovated.Flames and smoke shot in the air and several vehicles parked along the street caught fire, witnesses said, but no other casualties were reported.

(CNN)A twin-engine private plane crashed into a vacant warehouse on the outskirts of Dallas, northern Texas, on Tuesday, killing all six people onboard, authorities said.
The plane, which took off from Dallas Love Field airport, was headed for the coast of Florida, officials said.
The crash occurred just outside a local transit hub.
People in the vicinity could hear a loud explosion when the plane collided with the three-story structure, which was empty at the time as it was undergoing demolition.
Flames and debris shot into the air and several trucks parked along the alleyway caught fire, witnesses said, but no other casualties were reported.

Six people died Tuesday when a twin-engine plane slammed into an empty warehouse in suburban Dallas.
According to authorities, the aircraft had just taken off from Dallas Love Field and was en route to the Florida coast when it went down near a busy transit hub.
Witnesses reported hearing a massive explosion as the plane struck the three-story commercial building. Thankfully, the structure was completely vacant ahead of a planned demolition.
The impact sent a thick plume of black smoke into the sky and sparked secondary fires that engulfed several trucks parked in an adjacent alley. Despite the severe wreckage and the proximity to the commuter station, first responders confirmed there were no other casualties on the ground.

(CNN)Novak Djokovic has pulled out of next week's Miami Open following the birth of his third child.
Edoardo Artaldi, agent for the six-time Miami winner, told CNN Friday that reports that Djokovic's wife, Jelena, had given birth and that Djokovic had withdrawn from the tournament in Florida as a result were true, but he declined to comment further.
Earlier Friday, Rafael Nadal, the 22-time grand slam champion, said he was withdrawing from the same tournament in order to focus on his physical recovery.
Djokovic and Jelena, his high school sweetheart, tied the knot in July 2014. They already have one son, Stefan, 7, and a 4-year-old daughter, Tara. It is not yet known whether the new arrival is a boy or a girl.
Last year, the Serbian star opened up about fatherhood in an interview with the UK's Guardian newspaper.
"It can be a challenge because the older ones are always developing new interests and needing your guidance, whereas you have to balance your travel schedule with being present," the 34-year-old said. "Stefan just wants to play tennis and run around the whole time," Djokovic added, "whereas Tara is a little bit more drawn to arts and crafts -- so it's a beautiful dynamic."
World no. 1 Djokovic underwent elbow surgery in 2018 to save his career.

Djokovic is a 20-time grand slam champion and current world No. 1.

Novak Djokovic won't be competing at the Miami Open next week. The reason, however, is a happy one: the birth of his third child.
On Friday, the Serbian tennis star's agent, Edoardo Artaldi, confirmed the news to CNN. While he verified that Jelena Djokovic had indeed given birth—prompting Novak's sudden withdrawal from the Florida tournament—Artaldi kept the specifics under wraps and declined to comment further.
The Miami draw is certainly taking a hit this week. Just hours before Djokovic's announcement, 22-time grand slam champion Rafael Nadal also pulled out, citing a need to focus on his physical recovery rather than competing.
Djokovic and his high school sweetheart, Jelena, married back in the summer of 2014. The couple already shares a seven-year-old son, Stefan, and a four-year-old daughter, Tara. At this point, the family hasn't revealed whether the newest addition is a boy or a girl.
Balancing a grueling ATP tour schedule with raising kids is something the 34-year-old has spoken about candidly. In a Guardian interview last year, he touched on the chaotic beauty of fatherhood.
"It can be a challenge because the older ones are always developing new interests and needing your guidance," he explained. The dynamic at home is constantly shifting. "Stefan just wants to play tennis and run around the whole time, whereas Tara is a little bit more drawn to arts and crafts. It's a beautiful dynamic, but you really have to balance your travel schedule with being present."
It's been a turbulent few years for the current world No. 1. Back in 2018, he needed a medical intervention for a lingering elbow issue to get his career back on track. More recently, a high-profile visa cancellation forced him out of this year's Australian Open, paving the way for Nadal to capture the first grand slam title of the season.
With 20 major championships under his belt, Djokovic can easily afford to take a brief hiatus to focus on his growing family.

A stray visor tear-off—of all things—was the culprit behind Charles Leclerc’s frustrating Sunday at the Belgian Grand Prix. His Ferrari team confirmed the discarded plastic strip basically torpedoed the title contender's chances of fighting for a podium. 
Leclerc had to dive into the pits much earlier than planned because the front-right brakes on his F1-75 were overheating to dangerous levels. 
Ferrari’s team principal, Mattia Binotto, explained that the plastic tear-off had lodged itself perfectly inside the brake duct during the chaotic opening stint. Not only did it cause massive temperature spikes, but it also ended up damaging the car's sensor system, forcing the team to bring him in purely for safety.

Starting down in 15th due to engine penalties, Leclerc fought hard but ultimately had to settle for a disappointing sixth-place finish.

(CNN)The organizers of a half-marathon in the American Midwest have apologized after the course for Saturday's race was nearly a mile too short.
The Chicago Spring Half Marathon returned to the city's lakefront after severe weather canceled last year's event, but participants were forced to run less than the regulation 13.1 miles.
"We would like to apologize to our race participants that the course today measured 0.8 miles too short," the organizers said in a statement on Facebook.
"We are incredibly disappointed that this has affected our runners and hope that it hasn't ruined the experience, at what has been a wonderful community event after a long hiatus."
Sarah Jenkins won the women's division with a time of 1:08:12, with Maria Lopez in second with a time of 1:08:45.
Jenkins had been trailing Lopez until the final mile, sparking debate on social media.
"The runner who was leading was caught off guard when the finish line appeared early. So basically, she was pacing for a longer race," tweeted one spectator.
Race director David Chen explained the mistake in a statement published Sunday.
"Despite the half-marathon route being mapped correctly in the lead-up to race day, a basic human error in placing a turn sign meant that the final course was shortened by 0.8 miles on the day," Chen said.
"We offer our sincerest apologies for this error, which was only spotted once participants had crossed the finish line, and when a correction was not possible," he added.
Chen said finishers will be given adjusted pacing metrics, and the course length would be noted for results and qualifying times.
Saturday's half-marathon isn't the first race to feature an accidental distance adjustment. In October 2021, the organizers of the Portland 10K in Oregon apologized after officials directed runners the wrong way, adding around 400 meters (1,312 feet) to the expected distance.
"We're really sorry, and we're reviewing our protocols," Pacific Runs, which organizes the Portland 10K and numerous other events in the Pacific Northwest, posted on Twitter at the time. "We hope it didn't take away from your race day and we'll be in touch with all participants directly."
And in April 2018, an Italian cycling official was reprimanded after he redirected the peloton away from the final sprint, in a mistake that almost changed the outcome of the stage.
Belgian cyclist Thomas De Gendt was leading the breakaway in the local classic, and had entered the final kilometer when the police escort he was following exited the circuit.
Despite the chasing group being only seconds behind him, De Gendt followed the motorcycles off the road. Event organizers quickly directed him back on course, and De Gendt managed to win by a few wheel lengths despite the diversion.
Here is the second text, rewritten for a more natural style.

Organizers of a popular Chicago half-marathon are issuing major apologies this weekend. The reason? Saturday's race route came up nearly a full mile short.
The Chicago Spring Half Marathon was supposed to be a triumphant return to the city's lakefront following last year's weather-related cancellation. Instead, thousands of runners crossed the finish line much earlier than expected, missing out on the standard 13.1-mile distance.
"We would like to apologize to our race participants that the course today measured 0.8 miles too short," organizers admitted in a Facebook statement. They added they were "incredibly disappointed" by the blunder but hoped it didn't completely ruin the community event.
The shortened route actually impacted the podium. Sarah Jenkins took the women's division with a time of 1:08:12, edging out Maria Lopez at 1:08:45. Because Jenkins had been trailing Lopez right up until the premature finish, social media quickly lit up with debate.
"The runner who was leading was caught off guard when the finish line appeared early," one spectator pointed out on Twitter. "She was pacing for a longer race."
On Sunday, race director David Chen stepped up to explain what went wrong. It boiled down to a simple, costly mistake: a volunteer placed a crucial turn sign in the wrong spot.
"Despite the half-marathon route being mapped correctly in the lead-up to race day, a basic human error in placing a turn sign meant that the final course was shortened," Chen explained. The mistake wasn't caught until the leading pack was already crossing the finish line, making it impossible to fix in real time. Officials promised to provide runners with adjusted pacing metrics and add an asterisk for qualifying times.
Of course, this is hardly the first time a major race has fumbled its measuring tape. Back in 2021, a 10K in Portland, Oregon, accidentally tacked on an extra 400 meters when course marshals sent the pack down the wrong street. Organizers scrambled to apologize and review their protocols.
Even professional cycling isn't immune. During an Italian classic in 2018, a police escort famously drove off the circuit during the final kilometer, taking breakaway leader Thomas De Gendt with them. The Belgian rider lost precious seconds to the chasing group but somehow managed to salvage the victory by a few tire lengths.

"There is a growing tendency to withdraw, to do it all by ourselves, to stop making an effort to encounter others and do things together," he added.The Pope's traditional "Urbi et Orbi" or "To the City and the World" Christmas address was affected by the pandemic for the second year running. Pope Francis says domestic violence against women is 'almost satanic'  Read MoreUnlike in 2020, people were able to come to the square to listen to the traditional message this year, but the number of attendees was only about a fifth of what it was before the pandemic, because of the current increase in coronavirus cases in Italy.
