On Christmas Eve, the Pope led a vigil mass in St. Peter's Basilica with about 2,000 people in attendance, Holy See Press Office Director Matteo Bruni told CNN.The Pope said on Saturday that the pandemic has also affected dialogue, in relation to international conflict, leading people to take "shortcuts rather than setting out on the longer paths" for talks."Sisters and brothers, what would our world be like without the patient dialogue of the many generous persons who keep families and communities together? In this time of the pandemic, we have come to realize this more and more," he said.Pope Francis decries 'shipwreck of civilization' as he visits refugees on Greek island of LesbosHe urged the world to "open its hearts" to ensure necessary medical care, particularly vaccines, is provided to vulnerable people."God-with-us, grant health to the infirm and inspire all men and women of good will to seek the best ways possible to overcome the current health crisis and its effects. Open hearts to ensure that necessary medical care -- and vaccines in particular -- are provided to those peoples who need them most. Repay those who generously devote themselves to caring for family members, the sick and the most vulnerable in our midst," he said. The leader of the Catholic Church added that the world has become so used to immense tragedies that "we hardly even notice them anymore." He called for an end to conflicts throughout the Middle East and Africa, listing several places -- including Syria, Yemen, Iraq, Lebanon, Sudan and Ethiopia.The 85-year-old pontiff also used his Christmas message to address violence against women, which he said has increased during the pandemic. In a speech that marked his ninth Christmas as pontiff, Francis also highlighted the plight of refugees and migrants.

(CNN)UN Secretary-General António Guterres has dedicated a significant portion of his annual State of the Planet address to reflect on the climate crisis and its impact on global inequality.
Speaking from the rostrum of the General Assembly hall in New York, the Secretary-General called the environmental emergency a "generational test" that has strained international alliances and fueled isolationist policies.
"Our capacity for global solidarity is severely fractured," Guterres told the delegates in the hall, as well as the millions of citizens watching the broadcast around the world. "There is an alarming trend to retreat inward, to prioritize national interests, to abandon the difficult work of multilateralism and building sustainable solutions together," he added.
The Secretary-General's traditional keynote address was impacted by extreme weather events for the second consecutive year.
Unlike in 2024, delegations were able to travel to the headquarters to listen to the critical message in person, but the number of attendees was noticeably reduced due to unprecedented flight cancellations across the eastern seaboard, triggered by a severe late-season hurricane. The region reported record-breaking rainfall on Monday, marking the highest daily precipitation since tracking began, according to meteorological data.
Last year, the UN Chief delivered the address virtually from a remote studio, with the General Assembly completely closed to the public and diplomats. On Monday morning, Guterres led a closed-door climate summit with about 500 policy experts and leaders in attendance, a UN spokesperson told CNN.
The Secretary-General noted on Tuesday that the climate emergency has also undermined diplomatic dialogue, pushing nations to embrace "short-term economic gains rather than committing to the harder path" of green transition.
"Excellencies and delegates, what would our future look like without the relentless advocacy of the countless individuals who fight to keep our ecosystems and vulnerable communities intact? In this era of climate disruption, we are witnessing this necessity more than ever," he said.
He urged the international community to "open its resources" to ensure critical financial aid, particularly climate adaptation funds, is directed to developing nations.
"We must grant support to the most affected and inspire all governments of good will to pursue the necessary measures to survive the current environmental crisis and its economic fallout. Open treasuries to ensure that essential funding -- and clean technology in particular -- are distributed to those regions that need them most. Support those who tirelessly dedicate themselves to rebuilding communities, the displaced, and the most marginalized among us," he said.
The UN leader added that the global population has become so accustomed to catastrophic weather events that "we barely register the headlines anymore." He called for an immediate cessation of fossil fuel subsidies and highlighted several regions facing severe ecological crises -- including the Horn of Africa, the Amazon basin, Southeast Asia, and island nations in the Pacific.
The 76-year-old Secretary-General also used his address to highlight the displacement of indigenous populations, which he said has worsened during the climate crisis. In a speech that marked one of his most urgent pleas yet, Guterres also emphasized the plight of climate refugees worldwide.

UN Secretary-General António Guterres didn't mince words during his annual State of the Planet address on Tuesday. Instead of a standard policy briefing, he used the platform to deliver a stark warning about the climate crisis and how it continues to deepen global inequality.
Taking the podium at the General Assembly in New York, Guterres framed the environmental collapse as a "generational test." It’s a crisis, he argued, that isn't just destroying ecosystems—it’s actively unraveling international alliances and pushing countries toward isolationism.
"Our capacity for global solidarity is severely fractured," Guterres warned the delegates gathered in the hall and the millions tuning in worldwide. He pointed to a growing, dangerous reflex among nations to pull up the drawbridge. "There is an alarming trend to retreat inward, to prioritize national interests, to abandon the difficult work of multilateralism and building sustainable solutions together."
Ironically, the weather itself disrupted the keynote address for the second year in a row.
While diplomats were finally allowed back inside the UN headquarters—a stark contrast to 2024's virtual-only event—the room was visibly empty. A massive, late-season hurricane battered the eastern seaboard on Monday, grounding flights and triggering record-breaking rainfall across the region.
Just hours before his main speech, the UN Chief held a private, closed-door climate summit with roughly 500 leaders and policy experts, navigating the immediate logistical chaos of the storm outside.
When he finally addressed the wider assembly, Guterres pointed out that the climate emergency is actively poisoning diplomatic dialogue. Too many nations, he said, are chasing short-term economic wins instead of committing to the grueling, necessary work of transitioning to green energy.
"What would our future look like without the relentless advocacy of the countless individuals who fight to keep our ecosystems and vulnerable communities intact?" he asked the room. "In this era of climate disruption, we are witnessing this necessity more than ever."
A major focal point of his speech was money. Guterres pleaded with wealthier nations to unlock their resources, specifically demanding that climate adaptation funds finally reach the developing world.
He called on governments to step up, urging them to distribute essential funding and clean technology to the regions bearing the brunt of the crisis. "Support those who tirelessly dedicate themselves to rebuilding communities, the displaced, and the most marginalized among us," he urged.
Perhaps the most sobering moment of the address was his observation about public numbness. According to the UN leader, catastrophic weather events have become so routine that people barely register the headlines anymore. Demanding an immediate end to fossil fuel subsidies, he ran through a grim list of regions currently fighting for survival, from the drought-stricken Horn of Africa and the burning Amazon basin to the sinking island nations of the Pacific.
Before stepping down, the 76-year-old Secretary-General made a final, urgent pivot to the human cost of the crisis. In what stands as one of his most impassioned pleas to date, he spotlighted the escalating displacement of indigenous populations and the rapidly growing, yet often ignored, crisis of global climate refugees.

London (CNN)A British lawmaker has asked for an investigation into the rules around bringing babies into the House of Commons, after a member of Parliament was told she could no longer bring her child into the chamber.Stella Creasy, an MP for the opposition Labour Party, said she was told by a representative of Parliament's lower house that it was against the rules to bring a child to a debate at Westminster Hall after she attended with her son on Tuesday. Creasy shared an email sent to her by the private secretary to the chairman of ways and means, which references rules published in September: "You should not take your seat in the Chamber when accompanied by your child," and added that this also applied to Westminster Hall, the oldest building on the Parliamentary estate, used for state occasions and important ceremonies.  Offices are reopening. For parents, that raises a childcare problemCommons Speaker Lindsay Hoyle has asked the Commons Procedure Committee to look at the rules around bringing babies into the House following the incident, the PA Media news agency reported."Mothers in the mother of all parliaments are not to be seen or heard it seems..." Creasy wrote on Twitter following the incident.Read More"My son is 13 weeks old, so I can't really leave him on his own and I don't have any maternity cover. So I kind of can't win here," Creasy told the BBC's Victoria Derbyshire on Wednesday."I need to go in and I need to be able to speak but I also can't leave a baby that small, which I'm feeding at this point in time. "I've been told very clearly that apparently Parliament has taken time to write a law that it's a parliamentary faux pas and against the courtesies of the house to bring a child with you. "But we don't seem at the moment to have made a rule about wearing masks. It does seem to be a bit of a reflection of how Parliament was set up for another era when perhaps, you know, most MPs were men of a certain age and independent means," she added.A spokesperson for the House of Commons told CNN in an email that it is vital that all democratically elected MPs are able to carry out their duties in and around Parliament."Members can at any time consult with the Speaker, Deputy Speakers, Clerks and Doorkeepers about their requirements while in the Chamber or in Westminster Hall at any time," the spokesperson said. 'You're meant to be in bed!' -- Jacinda Ardern's toddler interrupts Facebook livestream "We are currently in communication with Stella Creasy about this matter," they added.Creasy told the BBC that while she wouldn't bring her other child, a toddler, into work "because she would find everything breakable or spreadable in the parliamentary chamber within five minutes and cause havoc," her infant son was "completely silent." Referencing the new rules published in September, Creasy, who said she had brought her first child to the House, said: "I don't understand what has changed. What I do understand is that there are barriers to getting mums involved in politics and I think that damages our political debates." Creasy lost a battle with the Independent Parliamentary Standards Authority this summer after she was told she could not hire a locum to cover her maternity leave after the birth of her second child.Responding to a suggestion Monday by journalist Julia Hartley-Brewer that she should "go and enjoy her maternity leave with her baby," Creasy tweeted: "Without maternity cover I don't actually get maternity leave as no one else to do my job."New Zealand speaker feeds lawmaker's baby during debate in Parliament In the US, Senator Tammy Duckworth made history in 2018 as she became the first senator to cast a vote on the Senate floor with her newborn by her side, days after the Senate changed longstanding rules to allow newborns onto the Senate floor during votes for the first time. The rule change, voted through unanimously, was made to accommodate senators with newborn babies, allowing them to bring a child under the age of 1 onto the Senate floor and breastfeed them during votes.In 2019, New Zealand Labour MP Tāmati Coffey brought his six-week-old son to the debating chamber, where the child was later held by the house speaker. And Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern, who became New Zealand's first premier to take maternity leave and the world's second elected leader to give birth in office, made history by bringing her three-month-old daughter into the United Nations assembly hall in 2018.But lawmakers have been criticized for caring for their children at work, including Spanish MP Carolina Bescansa, who in 2016 provoked criticism by taking her baby into parliament and breastfeeding him during its first session.

(CNN)A Canadian provincial lawmaker has requested a formal review of legislative rules regarding childcare, after she was informed she could no longer bring her infant daughter into the assembly chamber.
Sarah Lin, a Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) in British Columbia for the opposition party, said she was told by the Sergeant-at-Arms that it was a violation of parliamentary decorum to bring a child to a voting session after she attended with her daughter on Thursday. Lin shared a memorandum sent to her by the clerk's office, which referenced standing orders updated last year: "Non-members, including dependents, may not occupy a seat on the floor during official proceedings," adding that this also applied to committee rooms during broadcasted debates.
The Speaker of the House has asked the Committee on Parliamentary Privilege to examine the guidelines surrounding infants in the legislature following the incident, national broadcasters reported.
"Working mothers are told to lean in, but are literally locked out of the room it seems..." Lin wrote on Twitter following the incident.
"My daughter is barely two months old, so I cannot leave her at home, and the building does not offer drop-in childcare. So I am in an impossible situation," Lin told a local news network on Friday.
"I need to be present to represent my constituents, but I also have a nursing infant who relies on me. I was told unequivocally that the legislature considers the presence of a baby a breach of decorum and a distraction to the assembly.
"Yet, we routinely excuse members taking loud phone calls in the gallery. It feels like these rules were designed for a time when politics was exclusively the domain of older men of independent means with stay-at-home spouses," she added.
A spokesperson for the Legislative Assembly told CNN in an email that it is essential for all elected officials to participate fully in parliamentary duties without disruption.
"Members are encouraged to work with the Speaker's office and administrative staff to accommodate their needs while maintaining the formal environment of the chamber," the spokesperson said. "We are currently in active discussions with MLA Lin regarding this matter."
Lin noted that while she would never bring her active four-year-old son to a debate because "he would undoubtedly disrupt the proceedings and run down the aisles within five minutes," her newborn was "fast asleep and completely silent."
Referencing the updated standing orders, Lin, who had previously brought her first child to caucus meetings without issue, stated: "I fail to see what has changed. What is clear is that institutional barriers remain for young parents entering politics, and that ultimately hurts our democratic representation."
Lin previously clashed with the legislative administrative board earlier this year when her request for a remote proxy vote during her designated maternity leave was denied.
Responding to a conservative commentator's suggestion on Monday that she should "stay home and focus on being a mother," Lin tweeted: "Without a proxy system, my district goes unrepresented if I stay home. I am doing both my jobs."
In the US, Senator Tammy Duckworth made history in 2018 as she became the first senator to cast a vote on the Senate floor with her newborn by her side, days after the Senate changed longstanding rules to allow newborns onto the Senate floor during votes.
Similarly, in 2019, New Zealand MP Tāmati Coffey brought his six-week-old son into the debating chamber, where the baby was famously cradled by the house speaker. Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern also made headlines in 2018 when she brought her three-month-old daughter to the United Nations General Assembly.
However, the presence of children in political arenas has often sparked backlash. In 2015, European Parliament member Licia Ronzulli faced mixed reactions after frequently voting with her daughter in a sling, highlighting the ongoing global debate over work-life balance in high-level politics.

A Canadian lawmaker is pushing back against what she calls "archaic" workplace rules after being barred from bringing her newborn onto the legislative floor. 
British Columbia MLA Sarah Lin was reprimanded by the Sergeant-at-Arms on Thursday for bringing her two-month-old daughter to a voting session. Following the incident, Lin received a formal memo from the clerk’s office pointing to a recently updated standing order: "Non-members, including dependents, may not occupy a seat on the floor during official proceedings." The ban, she was informed, also extends to broadcasted committee rooms. 
The clash has prompted the Speaker of the House to ask the Committee on Parliamentary Privilege to review the assembly's childcare guidelines.
Taking to Twitter shortly after being told to leave, Lin didn't mince words. "Working mothers are told to lean in, but are literally locked out of the room," she wrote.
Speaking to local news networks on Friday, Lin highlighted the impossible bind she found herself in. With no drop-in childcare available at the legislature and a nursing infant who can't be left at home, she felt completely cornered. "I need to be present to represent my constituents, but I also have a nursing infant who relies on me," she said.
Lin found it particularly frustrating that a sleeping baby is viewed as a breach of decorum, pointing out that lawmakers frequently disrupt proceedings with loud phone calls without facing any consequences. To her, the rule smacks of an outdated political culture built around "older men with stay-at-home spouses."
While a spokesperson for the Legislative Assembly released a standard statement emphasizing the need to "accommodate needs while maintaining the formal environment," they confirmed they are in active discussions with Lin to find a solution.
For her part, Lin drew a sharp distinction between bringing her newborn and her energetic four-year-old son, who she joked would "undoubtedly disrupt the proceedings and run down the aisles." The infant, however, was completely silent. Having brought her first child to caucus meetings years ago without any pushback, she questioned what had actually changed—other than the enforcement of institutional barriers that actively keep young parents out of politics.
This isn't Lin's first fight over family-friendly policies. Earlier this year, she unsuccessfully petitioned the administrative board to allow remote proxy voting during her maternity leave. When a conservative pundit recently suggested she should just stay home and focus on motherhood, she quickly fired back online. Without a proxy system, she noted, staying home means her district loses its voice entirely.
The debate over babies in legislative chambers is hardly unique to Canada. In 2018, U.S. Senator Tammy Duckworth made history by casting a vote with her newborn after successfully lobbying to change Senate rules. New Zealand’s Parliament has also embraced the shift; in 2019, MP Tāmati Coffey brought his infant to a debate, and the baby was happily cradled by the Speaker of the House.
Still, progress is uneven. Politicians like European Parliament member Licia Ronzulli have faced both praise and heavy criticism for bringing their kids to work, proving that the global debate over balancing high-level politics with motherhood is far from settled.

(CNN)Paris Saint-Germain will play Real Madrid in a star-studded Champions League Round-of-16 tie after fixtures were redrawn following a technical error.European football's governing body UEFA declared the initial draw void earlier on Monday after Manchester United was drawn by mistake and the English Premier League club will probably be relieved. United was initially drawn against PSG but will now face a slightly easier two-legged tie against Spanish side Atlético Madrid -- although Diego Simeone's side is the reigning champion in Spain. Champions League last 16 draw Red Bull Salzburg vs. Bayern MunichSporting Clube de Portugal vs. Manchester CityBenfica vs. AjaxChelsea vs. LilleAtlético Madrid vs. Manchester UnitedVillarreal vs. JuventusInter Milan vs. LiverpoolPSG vs. Real MadridHowever, it means fans will be denied the mouthwatering prospect of watching Messi renew his rivalry with Ronaldo -- for now at least. READ: Lionel Messi wins seventh Ballon d'Or, while Alexia Putellas wins her first women's titleRead MoreThe French side will instead face the tricky prospect of playing current La Liga leader Real Madrid. Carlo Ancelotti's team is the competition's most successful side ever with 13 titles and has been in good form domestically of late.It will also see Messi, a long-term enemy of Madrid throughout his time at Barcelona, return to the Santiago Bernabéu.Sergio Ramos, Madrid's former captain, will also return to face the club where he won four Champions League titles. The defender moved to PSG at the start of the season but has seen limited action due to injury. Elsewhere, reigning champion Chelsea will still face French side Lille -- the only fixture that remained the same from the initial draw.
