Editor's Note: This article is part of an ongoing series examining the experiences of women in public and religious spaces around the world.
(CNN) Several women who attended a major religious gathering in South Asia have told CNN they experienced incidents of sexual harassment during the event, describing encounters that ranged from unwanted touching to verbal abuse in densely crowded areas.
The women said they decided to speak publicly after a social media post describing a similar experience gained widespread attention online earlier this year. The post prompted dozens of responses from women who said they had faced comparable situations during large-scale religious events.
The gathering attracts millions of worshipers annually and is considered one of the most significant spiritual occasions for participants. Organizers face major logistical challenges due to the size of the crowds, particularly in areas where pilgrims move through narrow routes or gather at key ceremonial locations.
Several women told CNN that the incidents occurred in crowded sections where movement was restricted and individuals were often pressed closely together. Some said they reported their experiences to security personnel, while others chose not to out of fear, embarrassment, or uncertainty about how their complaints would be received.
Event authorities said in a statement that harassment of any kind is unacceptable and that reports of misconduct are taken seriously. Officials added that additional security personnel, including more female officers, have been deployed in recent years to improve safety and encourage reporting.
The following accounts have been edited for clarity and length. Some interviewees requested anonymity because of concerns about public reaction.
Anonymous woman, Malaysia
I attended the gathering in 2018 with several relatives. During one of the busiest periods, I became separated from my family for a short time. While moving through a crowded passageway, I felt someone repeatedly touching my back and waist.
At first, I assumed it was accidental because there were so many people around me. But it continued even after I changed direction. When I turned around, the man moved away quickly and disappeared into the crowd.
I remember feeling shocked more than anything else. I had traveled there for spiritual reasons, and the last thing I expected was to be worried about my personal safety.
Anonymous woman, Turkey
My experience happened in broad daylight. I was walking with friends toward a prayer area when a man began making comments about my appearance. We ignored him and continued walking, but he followed us for several minutes.
Later that day, in a more crowded area, someone grabbed my arm from behind. I pulled away immediately, but the incident left me feeling anxious for the rest of the trip.
I never reported it because I assumed nothing would come of it. Looking back, I wish I had at least tried.
Anonymous woman, Nigeria
I was 19 years old when I attended the event for the first time. The crowds were enormous, and there were moments when it was difficult to move freely.
During one procession, a man standing behind me repeatedly pushed against me despite having plenty of space to step back. I felt uncomfortable and tried to move away, but there was little room.
For a long time, I convinced myself that I might have misunderstood what happened. Reading other women's stories later made me realize that many people had experienced similar situations.
Sara Ahmed, living in Canada
I attended with my parents several years ago. One incident happened near a marketplace where pilgrims often gathered after prayers.
A man approached and began asking personal questions. When I ignored him, he continued following me for several minutes. Eventually, I joined a larger group of women and he left.
Nothing physical happened, but the experience stayed with me because I felt vulnerable in a place where I expected to feel secure.
Many people assume these things cannot happen in religious settings, but large crowds can create opportunities for misconduct if safeguards are not strong enough.
Anonymous woman, Jordan
I have attended the gathering twice. During my first visit, I experienced unwanted touching in a crowded section near one of the main ritual sites.
I did not tell anyone at the time. I was young, embarrassed, and worried that people would question my actions rather than the behavior of the person responsible.
Years later, I shared the story with friends and discovered that some of them had experienced similar incidents. That was the first time I realized how common these conversations were becoming.

Editor's Note: This report is part of a broader series exploring women's experiences in public, cultural, and religious environments.
(CNN) Women from several countries have described experiences of harassment during a major religious pilgrimage in South Asia, saying that crowded conditions sometimes created situations in which inappropriate behavior went unchecked.
Their accounts surfaced after a widely shared social media post encouraged others to speak about incidents they had previously kept private. Many of the women said they had never discussed what happened until they saw similar stories from fellow pilgrims online.
The event draws millions of worshipers each year and holds deep spiritual significance for those who attend. Managing crowds on that scale presents obvious challenges, especially around popular ritual sites where movement can slow to a standstill.
Some of the women interviewed said the harassment occurred in exactly those areas. A few sought help from security personnel, while others stayed silent. Several said they felt uncertain about how a complaint would be handled, and some worried about negative reactions from their communities.
Organizers said they maintain a zero-tolerance approach to harassment and have increased security measures, including the presence of more female officers. They encouraged anyone experiencing misconduct to report it.
The following testimonies have been condensed and edited for clarity. Certain names have been withheld at the request of the interviewees.
Anonymous woman, Malaysia
I was attending with family members in 2018. At one point, we became separated in a very crowded section. While trying to make my way through, I noticed someone repeatedly touching my side and lower back.
Initially, I dismissed it as accidental contact. There were people everywhere. But when it kept happening even after I changed position, it became clear it wasn't random.
The person disappeared into the crowd before I could react. What stayed with me afterward was the surprise. I had never imagined I would encounter something like that during a religious journey.
Anonymous woman, Turkey
My experience started with comments. A man began speaking to me and a group of friends as we walked toward a prayer area. We ignored him, but he continued following us for a while.
Later, someone grabbed my arm in a crowded section of the site. It happened quickly, and I pulled away immediately.
I didn't report either incident. At the time, I felt it would be pointless, although now I'm not so sure.
Anonymous woman, Nigeria
I was attending for the first time when I was 19. The scale of the crowd was overwhelming, especially during one of the processions.
A man behind me kept pressing forward despite there being enough space not to. I remember feeling trapped because moving away wasn't easy.
For years I questioned my own interpretation of the situation. Only after reading other women's accounts did I realize my experience wasn't unique.
Sara Ahmed, Canada
One of my most uncomfortable moments happened near a busy market area after prayers.
A stranger started asking personal questions and continued following me when I chose not to answer. Eventually I moved closer to a larger group of women, and he stopped.
Compared with some stories, it may seem minor. Even so, it changed how I felt for the remainder of the trip. I became much more cautious about where I went and who was around me.
Anonymous woman, Jordan
I experienced unwanted touching during my first visit to the pilgrimage site. It happened in a densely packed area where people were moving slowly.
I never mentioned it to anyone. I was embarrassed and worried that the conversation would somehow focus on my behavior rather than the actions of the person involved.
Years later, I finally talked about it with friends. To my surprise, several had stories of their own. That realization made me understand why more women are choosing to speak publicly about these experiences today.

(CNN)She's been a fixture of life in Germany for almost 16 years. But now the political stability that the country has enjoyed for so long under Angela Merkel is coming to an end as she prepares to stand down as Chancellor.Until recently, the consensus was that despite standing down and letting her successor fight September's federal election, Merkel's Christian Democratic Union (CDU) and sister party, the Christian Social Union (CSU) in Bavaria, would still be the dominant force in German politics.  Last week, a shock poll placed the Green party ahead of the CDU by a margin of 7%. While CDU sources dismissed this as an expected spike in popularity after confirming Annalena Baerbock as their candidate for Chancellor that will die down, it's long been expected that Germany's next coalition would include the Green party in some way.  Subsequent polls have also placed the Greens ahead of Merkel's party on the "Sunday Question," a weekly survey that literally tracks how Germans would vote if elections were held this Sunday. "Even if the Greens don't win outright, a decent enough share of the vote would force the CDU to cut the Greens a good deal in a coalition deal as they don't have many options for partners," says Cathryn Clüver Ashbrook, executive director of the Project on Europe and the Transatlantic Relationship at the Harvard Kennedy School.Read MoreGermany to impose 'emergency brake' law across almost entire country to curb Covid infections Despite this Green surge, few are expecting any radical policy changes in Germany, as the CDU have adopted numerous Green policies over the past few years and the Greens have drifted rightwards to becoming a centrist party. Indeed, Cem Özdemir, a senior Green politician, said recently that his party would not be radically changing German policy on NATO, European policy or support for Israel, three issues that had been contentious in the past.  The second of those issues should be of comfort to the European Union's top brass in Brussels. Germany, as the wealthiest and largest member state, carries enormous influence in the overall direction of the European project. Under Merkel, Germany was broadly supportive of the EU's agenda, only occasionally throwing its weight around and blocking certain proposals. Despite the party seemingly having little appetite to make radical changes within the EU, a Green victory in Germany would mark a symbolic end of an era in Brussels.  The European People's Party (EPP), a pan-European center-right group with members from all EU member states, is the dominant political force in Brussels. It has more elected leaders than any other political bloc in the EU and is the most represented in both the European Parliament and the Commission.  To say that the EPP's leadership is closely aligned with the German Chancellor would be an understatement. And Ursula von der Leyen, the current Commission President and EPP member, previously served in Merkel's cabinet. No longer having a center-right conservative in the Bundeskanzleramt would be the strongest indicator yet that Europe's traditional parties are facing an uncertain future.  Ursula von der Leyen, right, the current European Commission President, previously served in Angela Merkel's cabinet.Daniel Freund, a German Green MEP, explains that two of the biggest forces in European politics, the rise of progressive politics versus right-wing nationalist populism, has squeezed parties like the CDU from both sides.  "The CDU has for a while been a shapeshifting party, adapting in response to whatever its biggest threat is. Not long ago that was the far-right AfD (Alternative für Deutschland) so it went anti-Europe and anti-immigration. Now we are eating up its votes, so it makes sense it would move more in line with us," he says.  Diplomats and officials say they now talk openly about the CDU being weaker than it was even five years ago and is looking like a different party. "In all honesty, von der Leyen could easily be a member of the Green party if you look at what she believes in," says a German diplomat.  Even in the event that the Greens don't win, a Green and Black (CDU/CSU) coalition looks increasingly likely and most observers in Brussels think it would be perfectly stable. However, in just over a year's time, it could run into the other volcano waiting to erupt in European politics.  France's next presidential election is looking far from safe for Emmanuel Macron. Politico's poll of polls for 2022 voting intentions places Marine Le Pen, leader of the far-right National Rally, one point ahead of Macron. Her party came out on top in the last European elections and is clearly spooking Macron, who is shifting towards Le Pen on issues such as immigration, and has been accused of Islamophobia for his comments on tackling radicalism.  Opinion polls for 2022 presidential election voting intentions place Marine Le Pen one point ahead of Emmanuel Macron. Anyone who has spent time in Brussels knows that if you want to get things done in Europe, you need to get the French and Germans on the same page. A Green-Black government in Germany and Le Pen presidency in France could make this extremely difficult.  "It's hard to see how a progressive, vehemently pro-EU Germany and nationalist France would be able to agree on huge issues -- like our common policy on China and Russia," says a European diplomat.  Europe is torn over whether to take Putin's help on vaccinesLe Pen is known to have links to Russian President Vladimir Putin and it could become a major issue if she becomes a blockage on European attempts to deal with nefarious Russian behavior in Ukraine, in its treatment of opposition figures and in its broader aggression around the world.  And while the Green party is hawkish on the matter of China, in a coalition it is likely Germany would continue its policy of trying to influence change in China by dangling the carrot of greater trade. Le Pen has not said much on China, but has warned against isolating Russia to the point of pushing it in to the arms of China, which we can assume means there is some hostility.  More worryingly for EU integration, Le Pen no longer wants to emulate the UK with a "Frexit," but, along with likeminded nationalists, take the whole thing over from the inside. There are plenty of such politicians scattered across the bloc and a nationalist winning the French presidency would be their single biggest victory since the election of Donald Trump as US president in 2016.  Marine Le Pen is known to have links to Russian President Vladimir Putin.European politics is changing faster than many in Brussels are willing to admit. "We've already seen big fissures between France and Germany with Macron at the helm. What happens with Le Pen is a total unknown," says Clüver. "I think people are just too paralyzed with fear to think about this, but in reality, it's much more dangerous than the eurozone crisis."  Even in the event of a CDU and Macron victory, the political establishment in Brussels needs to acknowledge that the appetite for something different has been building for a long time. If it doesn't adequately prepare, it might find that old friends in Paris and Berlin stop quite being so deferential to a style of leadership that is increasingly unattractive to their voters.  Correction: A photo caption in a previous version of this article misidentified European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen.

(CNN) After more than a decade in power, Prime Minister Sofia Lindberg is preparing to leave office, marking the end of an era that has shaped politics in Sweden and across Northern Europe.
For years, political observers assumed that Lindberg's center-right Alliance Party would remain the dominant force even after her departure. However, recent opinion polls have complicated that assumption. A survey published last week placed the Progressive Green Movement ahead of the Alliance for the first time in nearly a decade, triggering intense debate about the country's political future.
While Alliance officials argued that the result reflected a temporary surge following the Greens' selection of a new leader, analysts say the broader trend cannot be ignored. Polling throughout the year has suggested growing support for environmental and social reform policies, particularly among younger and urban voters.
Even if the Greens fail to win the largest share of the vote, many experts believe they are now positioned to play a central role in coalition negotiations after next year's election.
"The question is no longer whether the Greens will be part of government," said Helena Markström, a political scientist at Stockholm University. "The question is how much influence they will have over the agenda."
Despite their rise, few expect a dramatic shift in Sweden's foreign policy. Over recent years, mainstream parties have increasingly adopted positions on climate policy, renewable energy, and sustainable development that were once associated almost exclusively with the Greens.
The implications could nevertheless extend beyond Sweden. As one of the European Union's more influential member states, Sweden often plays an important role in debates over environmental regulation, trade policy, and digital governance.
A Green-led government would also symbolize a broader transformation taking place across Europe, where traditional center-right and center-left parties have struggled to maintain their historic dominance.
Many political strategists argue that established parties are facing pressure from multiple directions. Progressive movements are attracting younger voters concerned about climate change and inequality, while nationalist and anti-establishment groups continue to draw support from voters frustrated with political institutions.
"The old political coalitions are becoming harder to sustain," said one European diplomat. "Voters are increasingly looking for alternatives, whether on the left, the right, or outside the traditional system altogether."
That dynamic is creating uncertainty in Brussels, where policymakers are already preparing for a series of elections across the continent. Several European governments face growing pressure from emerging parties that challenge long-standing assumptions about integration and economic policy.
The situation is particularly significant because neighboring countries are undergoing political shifts of their own. In Finland and Denmark, newer parties have gained influence, while debates over immigration, energy policy, and economic competitiveness continue to reshape electoral landscapes.
A more environmentally focused government in Sweden could strengthen efforts to accelerate the EU's climate agenda. However, differences with other member states could complicate negotiations on industrial policy, energy security, and trade.
At the same time, some analysts caution against overstating the likelihood of radical change. Coalition politics often require compromise, and even a strong Green showing would almost certainly require cooperation with other parties.
Still, the broader message is clear. Political loyalties that once seemed stable are becoming more fluid across Europe. Whether the next government is led by the Alliance Party or the Greens, the election will serve as another test of how traditional political institutions adapt to an increasingly fragmented electorate.
"The era of predictable politics is fading," Markström said. "The parties that succeed will be the ones that recognize how quickly voters' priorities are evolving."

(CNN) Sweden is approaching a significant political transition. After more than ten years as prime minister, Sofia Lindberg is expected to step aside, bringing to a close a period of stability that has defined much of the country's recent political history.
Until recently, many assumed her center-right Alliance Party would remain firmly in control even after she left office. That confidence has weakened in recent months. A new poll showing the Progressive Green Movement ahead of the Alliance sparked fresh discussion about whether the balance of power may be shifting.
Party officials downplayed the result, arguing that support for the Greens had been boosted by the recent selection of a new leader. Yet polling trends suggest the change may be part of a larger pattern rather than a short-lived fluctuation. Environmental issues, housing affordability, and social policy have become increasingly important to many voters, particularly in larger cities.
As a result, few analysts now doubt that the Greens will have substantial influence after the next election. Whether they finish first or not, they are widely expected to be a key player in any coalition negotiations.
According to political scientist Helena Markström, the debate has moved beyond simple electoral arithmetic. The more important question is how much leverage the Greens could gain in shaping government priorities.
A Green breakthrough would carry symbolic significance across Europe, although it would not necessarily trigger a dramatic policy revolution. Many proposals once associated primarily with environmental parties have gradually been adopted by mainstream political groups over the past decade.
Even so, developments in Sweden are being watched closely elsewhere in the European Union. The country often plays an influential role in discussions surrounding climate regulation, technology policy, and economic reform.
The potential rise of the Greens also reflects a wider trend. Across Europe, long-established parties have found it increasingly difficult to hold together the broad coalitions that once guaranteed electoral success. New political movements continue to attract voters from different directions, reshaping national debates.
Some are motivated by concerns over climate change and social inequality, while others are drawn to anti-establishment or nationalist messages. The result is a more fragmented political landscape than many European governments have been accustomed to managing.
Officials in Brussels are paying close attention. Several major elections are approaching across the continent, and political volatility in one country often has implications beyond its borders.
Meanwhile, neighboring countries have experienced their own political shifts. New parties have gained ground, and issues such as migration, energy costs, and economic competitiveness remain central topics of public debate.
A stronger Green presence in Sweden could reinforce support for ambitious environmental policies within the EU. At the same time, it might expose differences among member states on questions related to industry, trade, and energy strategy.
Still, observers caution against expecting dramatic upheaval. Sweden's coalition-based system tends to reward compromise, meaning any future government would likely need to balance competing priorities.
What seems less uncertain is that European politics is becoming harder to predict. Voter loyalties are no longer as fixed as they once were, and parties across the spectrum are adapting to a changing electorate.
"The political environment is much more fluid than it was a decade ago," Markström said. "Success increasingly depends on understanding how quickly public expectations can change."

(CNN)On what was a momentous day for the Milwaukee Bucks, it couldn't have gone any better for the now defending NBA champions. The team stormed to its first title in 50 years earlier this year, and on Tuesday, the achievement was commemorated before the opening game of the 2021-22 season. A banner was hung in the rafters to immortalize the 2020-21 Bucks team, while players received their championship rings in an on-court ceremony. Each aspect of the ring is said to have a meaning. The ring has 360 diamonds on the top to "represent the total wins since ownership purchased the team," the team said. It's here & it's perfect. 💍: @JewelersMutual pic.twitter.com/qhxI5RvnHV— Milwaukee Bucks (@Bucks) October 19, 2021

The 16 emerald shaped diamonds on the left side represent the 16 playoff wins during the 2021 playoffs, with another 16 emerald shaped diamonds on the right side for the 16 division titles in team history. In total, there are approximately 4.14cts of emeralds representing the 414 Milwaukee area code. The 50 round stones on the inner bezel stand for the 50 years since the team's last championship win.Read MoreEven the NBA Larry O'Brien Trophy on the face of the ring has a special meaning as it is made up of 65.3% purity yellow gold to represent the season's winning percentage.If a player ever wants to reminisce about that special season, there's even a removable top with a QR code underneath that when scanned plays a video highlighting memorable moments from the season.And after the emotional ceremony in front of spectators, Giannis Antetokounmpo and company put on quite a show for their adoring fans. They beat the star-studded Brooklyn Nets -- who were without Kyrie Irving -- 127-104, with Antetokounmpo racking up 32 points and 14 rebounds while Kevin Durant had 32 points and 11 rebounds for Brooklyn."That's going to be forever," Antetokounmpo said of the banner afterwards. "We're part of history.""I'm excited. But now, it's over with. We've got to focus on building good habits, keep moving forward and keep playing good basketball and, hopefully, we can repeat this again this year, next year and all the years that we're going to be here down the road."Antetokounmpo is defended by James Harden of the Brooklyn Nets.A good opposition or something worse? LeBron James and the Los Angeles Lakers lost their season-opening game, as the Golden State Warriors traveled to the Staples Center and won 121-114.Is that just because they faced a red-hot Warriors side or could it be the sign of something worse? Now into his 19th year in the NBA, James' Lakers were up by six points at halftime, but its defense came up lacking and wasn't able to stave off Steph Curry and the Warriors as they mounted a spirited second-half comeback. James and fellow superstar Anthony Davis didn't lack for scoring, getting 34 and 33 respectively, but nobody else for the Lakers scored in double figures.Curry recorded his first triple-double since January 22, 2016, with 21 points, 10 rebounds and 10 assists.Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, features, and videosCurry drives to the basket against the LA Lakers.However, Curry wasn't enamored with how he played, despite the victory. "I played like trash," he told TNT. "But if we can win a game like that, where we created some good shots and stuck with it defensively ... that's a good omen for us.""We kept our composure and didn't turn the ball over in the second half, which was a huge benefit to just maintaining the pace and giving ourselves some looks," Curry said. "But the fact that everybody who was on the floor contributed in a meaningful way kind of speaks to our depth and how we want to play going forward. We're excited about that and it's good to be 1-0."

(CNN) It was a night of celebration and dominance for the Denver Nuggets as they officially began their title defense in front of a packed home crowd.
Before tipoff of the 2024-25 NBA season, the franchise honored its championship-winning team with a banner-raising ceremony and the presentation of championship rings. Players, coaches, and staff gathered at center court as highlights from the historic season played on the arena's video boards, drawing loud cheers from fans.
The championship rings were designed to reflect key moments from the title-winning campaign. Team officials said the rings feature hundreds of diamonds symbolizing victories accumulated throughout the season, while custom details commemorate the franchise's first NBA championship and its playoff run.
Several elements were personalized for each player, including engraved statistics and references to memorable postseason performances. The organization described the rings as a tribute to both the team's success and the city's support during the championship journey.
The festivities continued once the game began.
Led by reigning Finals MVP Nikola Jokić, the Nuggets defeated the Dallas Mavericks 118-101 in an impressive opening-night performance. Jokić recorded 29 points, 13 rebounds, and 11 assists, securing a triple-double as Denver controlled much of the contest.
Dallas remained competitive through the first half behind Luka Dončić's scoring, but the Nuggets gradually pulled away in the third quarter with a series of defensive stops and efficient offensive possessions.
After the victory, Jokić acknowledged the significance of the ceremony but emphasized that the team was already focused on the future.
"The banner is something that will always be there," he said. "We accomplished something special, but a new season starts now. We have to keep improving and keep working if we want another opportunity."
Meanwhile, another championship contender experienced a disappointing start to its campaign.
The Phoenix Suns opened their season with a 115-108 home loss to the Minnesota Timberwolves. Despite strong performances from Devin Booker and Kevin Durant, the Suns struggled to contain Minnesota's balanced attack during the second half.
Booker finished with 31 points, while Durant added 28. However, Phoenix received limited offensive production from the rest of its lineup as Minnesota steadily built momentum after halftime.
Anthony Edwards led the Timberwolves with 27 points and provided several key plays late in the game to secure the victory.
Afterward, Edwards praised his team's resilience but said there was still significant room for improvement.
"We didn't play a perfect game," he told reporters. "There are a lot of things we can clean up. But winning on opening night against a team like that gives us confidence."
Minnesota's victory highlighted one of the team's strengths: depth. Contributions came from throughout the roster, allowing the Timberwolves to withstand scoring runs and maintain control in crucial moments.
For Denver, however, the evening belonged to celebration. A championship banner now hangs permanently above the court, and the team's successful start offered an encouraging reminder of why many expect the Nuggets to remain among the league's top contenders this season.

(CNN) Opening night could hardly have unfolded better for the Denver Nuggets.
The evening began with a celebration of last season's championship success. Fans filled the arena early to watch the unveiling of a new banner and the distribution of championship rings, marking one of the most significant achievements in franchise history. Video tributes played throughout the ceremony, and players took time to acknowledge the crowd that had supported them during their title run.
The rings themselves were packed with symbolic details. According to the team, the design incorporates references to Denver's championship season, playoff accomplishments, and the organization's long journey to its first NBA title. Personalized engravings were also included, giving each player a unique reminder of the achievement.
Once the formalities ended, the Nuggets quickly turned their attention to basketball—and looked every bit like defending champions.
Nikola Jokić led the way with a triple-double of 29 points, 13 rebounds, and 11 assists as Denver defeated the Dallas Mavericks 118-101. The game remained relatively close early on, but Denver's control became more evident as the night progressed. A strong third quarter created separation, and the Mavericks never fully recovered.
Luka Dončić kept Dallas within striking distance for stretches, yet the Nuggets consistently found answers on both ends of the floor. Their ball movement, defensive discipline, and experience were on display throughout the second half.
After the game, Jokić spoke briefly about the banner ceremony. While he admitted it was a meaningful moment, his focus had already shifted toward the long season ahead.
"The memories will always be there," he said. "But now it's about the next challenge."
Elsewhere around the league, the season started on a less positive note for the Phoenix Suns.
Hosting the Minnesota Timberwolves, Phoenix fell 115-108 despite productive nights from its two biggest stars. Devin Booker scored 31 points and Kevin Durant added 28, but the Suns struggled to find enough support from the rest of the roster.
Minnesota gradually took control after halftime. Anthony Edwards paced the visitors with 27 points and delivered several important baskets during the closing stages, helping seal the win.
Even after the victory, Edwards was not entirely satisfied with the performance. He pointed to several areas that still need improvement but acknowledged that beginning the season with a win against a strong opponent was encouraging.
One reason for Minnesota's success was the contribution it received across the lineup. Different players stepped up at different times, allowing the Timberwolves to maintain their intensity throughout the game.
Denver, though, was the team that ended the night with the spotlight. The championship banner now has a permanent place in the arena, and the convincing win that followed served as an early statement that the Nuggets intend to remain firmly in the title conversation this season.

(CNN)Wales international David Brooks has revealed he's been diagnosed with stage two Hodgkin Lymphoma. The 24-year-old, who currently plays for Bournemouth in the second tier of English football, posted a message on Twitter Wednesday saying he will start treatment next week. "I have been diagnosed with Stage II Hodgkin Lymphoma and will begin a course of treatment next week," he wrote.Lymphoma is a form of cancer that affects the lymph system, or "tissues and organs that produce, store, and carry white blood cells that fight infections," according to the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.The two main types of lymphoma are Hodgkin, which spreads in an orderly manner through lymph nodes, and Non-Hodgkin, which spreads in a non-orderly fashion, according to the CDC.Read More"Although this has come as a shock to myself and my family, the prognosis is a positive one and I am confident that I will make a full recovery and be back playing as soon as possible," added Brooks.Brooks, who played in the English Premier League before Bournemouth's relegation in 2020, and who is considered one of his country's most promising talents, withdrew from the Wales squad through illness last week.He thanked the medical staff for spotting potential symptoms and for the doctors who diagnosed him."I'd like to show my appreciation to the doctors, nurses, consultants and staff who have been treating me for their professionalism, warmth and understanding during this period," he added. "I want to thank everyone at the Football Association of Wales because without the swift attention of their medical team we may not have detected the illness."I'd also like to say thank you to AFC Bournemouth for all of their support and assistance this past week."David Brooks (c) in action during for Wales on November 15, 2020.'I would like to ask that my privacy is respected'The football community has rallied around the midfielder since he shared his diagnosis, with clubs from across the leagues sending their best wishes. His current side said it would stand by its player and offer all the support he needs."We're not putting any timescales on his return; we will give David all the time he needs to get well and will do everything that we possibly can to help with that," Bournemouth chief executive Neill Blake said in a statement."I know everyone will be keen to show their love and support for David which will help him immensely as he recovers, but we also urge you all to respect his and his family's privacy during this time."Brooks has played nine times for Bournemouth this season but will step away from the game as he starts his recovery. "Although I appreciate that there will be media attention and interest, I would like to ask that my privacy is respected in the coming months and I will share updates on my progress when I am able to do so," Brooks continued."In the meantime, thank you to everyone for their messages of support -- it means so much and will continue to do so in the months ahead."I look forward to seeing you all again and playing the sport I love very soon."

(CNN) National team defender Alex Morgan has announced that he has been diagnosed with stage one testicular cancer and will temporarily step away from professional soccer while undergoing treatment.
The 26-year-old, who plays for Championship club Bristol City, shared the news in a statement released on social media Thursday, explaining that doctors detected the condition during a series of medical examinations earlier this month.
"I have recently been diagnosed with stage one testicular cancer and will begin treatment in the coming days," Morgan wrote.
According to health authorities, testicular cancer is one of the most treatable forms of cancer when detected early. Treatment options vary depending on the stage and type of the disease but often have high success rates.
Morgan said the diagnosis had come as an unexpected shock to both him and his family but emphasized that doctors were optimistic about his recovery.
"Naturally, receiving this news has been difficult, but I have been reassured by the specialists overseeing my care that the outlook is very positive," he said. "My focus now is on completing treatment, recovering fully, and returning to football when the time is right."
The defender had recently withdrawn from international duty after reporting persistent discomfort that required further medical assessment.
In his statement, Morgan thanked the medical staff at both club and national team level for their support and quick action.
"I am incredibly grateful to the doctors, nurses, and specialists who have guided me through this process so far," he said. "I also want to thank the medical teams at Bristol City and the national federation for acting quickly and ensuring I received the tests I needed."
Morgan has been a regular presence for club and country over the past several seasons and is regarded as one of the brightest defensive talents in the national setup.
Following the announcement, messages of support poured in from teammates, clubs, coaches, and supporters across the football community.
Bristol City released a statement expressing its full backing for the player during his recovery.
"Alex's health and well-being are our only priorities at this time," the club said. "He will receive every possible support from the club, and there will be no pressure regarding his return to football."
Club officials added that Morgan would be given as much time as necessary to focus on treatment and rehabilitation.
The player also asked for privacy while he undergoes medical care.
"I appreciate the kindness and encouragement people have already shown," he wrote. "At the same time, I hope everyone will respect the privacy of myself and my family as we navigate the months ahead."
He concluded by thanking supporters for their messages and expressing confidence that he would return to the sport in the future.
"I look forward to being back on the pitch and doing what I love again. Until then, my attention is fully on recovery."

(CNN) Professional soccer player Alex Morgan has revealed that he has been diagnosed with stage one testicular cancer and will take a break from the game while beginning treatment.
The 26-year-old Bristol City defender shared the news on social media Thursday, explaining that the condition was discovered after a recent series of medical tests.
In a written statement, Morgan said he would start treatment shortly and remain focused on making a full recovery.
The diagnosis came as an unexpected development for both the player and his family. Even so, Morgan said doctors have given him reason to be optimistic. Testicular cancer is often highly treatable when identified at an early stage, and medical specialists have reportedly provided encouraging assessments of his prognosis.
"My priority now is getting through treatment and recovering properly," he wrote. "I'm hopeful that I'll be able to return to football once that process is complete."
Morgan had recently pulled out of international team activities after experiencing symptoms that required further investigation. He credited medical personnel for identifying the issue quickly and helping him receive specialist care.
He also expressed gratitude toward the doctors, nurses, and healthcare staff who have assisted him since the diagnosis, as well as the medical teams at both club and national level.
The announcement prompted an immediate response from across the football world. Fellow players, supporters, and clubs sent messages of encouragement, while Bristol City publicly pledged its support.
In a statement, the club emphasized that Morgan's health comes before any football considerations and said he would be given all the time necessary to focus on treatment and recovery.
Widely regarded as one of the country's emerging defensive talents, Morgan has become an important figure for both club and national team in recent years. For now, however, football has taken a back seat.
He ended his statement by thanking supporters for their kindness and asking that his family's privacy be respected during the months ahead.
"The support I've received already means a great deal," he said. "I'm looking forward to the day I can return, but right now my focus is entirely on getting well."

(CNN)Wisconsin men's basketball player Chris Vogt was traveling to play in a crucial matchup against Ohio State last Saturday when devastation ripped through his hometown of Mayfield, Kentucky."I was just stunned," Vogt told CNN Sports of seeing the extensive damage caused by the tornadoes that struck his home state late Friday.A town of roughly 10,000 people, Mayfield was decimated by the twister, a storm that flattened homes and took the lives of at least 80 people in Kentucky."It's tough. I mean, this is my childhood. This is where I grew up. A lot of my friends still live here. I still call this place home," he continued."No one ever expects something like this to happen to their hometown."Read MoreFollowing his team's game, the 7-foot tall senior center stepped up in a tremendous way for his community. Hundreds of miles away from Mayfield, Vogt quickly launched a GoFundMe page to raise money for those suffering. Wisconsin Badgers center Chris Vogt handles the ball during an NCAA college men's basketball game against the University of Green Bay Phoenix.He says he drew inspiration from former Badger football player and Texans star J.J. Watt and his foundation's Hurricane Harvey relief fund that continues to help rebuild homes in the Houston area."Obviously, I don't have the same platform as him, but if I can do anything on a fraction of the scale of what he did, that'd be awesome," Vogt explained.As of Friday morning, the Kentucky native had raised more than $155,000, with donations pouring in from more than 2,300 people across the country.The money raised will soon be donated to the Red Cross and the Graves County and Mayfield High School emergency resource offices.On Thursday, Vogt traveled to Mayfield to help with the city's storm cleanup and recovery efforts.Wisconsin's Chris Vogt raised more than $150,000 for his hometown."It's one thing to raise money and try to help that way, but to actually be down here and be boots on the ground, shake people's hands, see people I haven't seen in a long time, it just feels like I'm able to help a little bit more," Vogt said of returning to Kentucky."There's a great sense of community here. Everyone has each other's back," he added. "We all love and support each other, so to be able to give back to the community that's done so much for me and supported me my whole basketball career -- It just feels great to see things come full circle."

(CNN) College volleyball player Emma Richardson was preparing for an away match with her team when she received news that severe flooding had swept through her hometown in eastern Tennessee.
"I couldn't believe what I was seeing," Richardson told CNN Sports as she described viewing photos and videos of the destruction on her phone. "You recognize the streets and buildings, but everything looks completely different."
The flooding followed days of heavy rainfall and left large sections of the community underwater. Homes, businesses, and public facilities suffered extensive damage, forcing many residents to evacuate.
For Richardson, the disaster felt deeply personal.
"This is the place where I grew up," she said. "My family is here, my friends are here, and so many of the people who helped me along the way still live in town. Seeing it happen was heartbreaking."
After competing in the match, the senior athlete decided she wanted to contribute to recovery efforts in any way she could.
Within days, Richardson launched an online fundraising campaign to support families affected by the flooding. She shared the initiative through social media and encouraged fellow athletes, alumni, and supporters to spread the word.
University volleyball player Emma Richardson created a fundraiser for flood victims in her hometown.
Richardson said she was inspired by athletes who have used their public profiles to support communities during times of crisis.
"You see people step up when disaster strikes, and it reminds you that even small actions can make a difference," she said. "I figured if I could help raise awareness and bring in some donations, it was worth trying."
The response exceeded her expectations.
By the end of the week, the fundraiser had collected more than $120,000 from thousands of donors across the country. The proceeds are expected to support local relief organizations, emergency assistance programs, and rebuilding efforts.
Earlier this week, Richardson returned home to volunteer alongside residents and local organizations. She spent several days helping remove debris, distributing supplies, and assisting families as they began the long recovery process.
"It means a lot to be here in person," she said. "Donating money is important, but being able to work alongside people and listen to what they're going through makes it feel more real."
The experience reinforced what she believes is one of her hometown's defining qualities.
"People here look after each other," Richardson said. "Even after everything that's happened, you can see neighbors helping neighbors everywhere you go."
She added that giving back to the community that supported her athletic journey was something she felt compelled to do.
"This town has given me so much over the years. Being able to help, even in a small way, feels like the least I can do."

(CNN) Emma Richardson was on the road with her college volleyball team when messages from friends and relatives began arriving on her phone. Back home in eastern Tennessee, severe flooding had caused widespread damage across the community where she grew up.
The images were difficult to process.
"You know every street and every landmark," Richardson said. "Then suddenly you're looking at pictures and it barely resembles the place you remember."
Days of intense rainfall had overwhelmed parts of the region, damaging homes, businesses, and public buildings. Many residents were forced from their properties as emergency crews responded to the flooding.
For Richardson, the disaster struck close to home in every sense.
"My family is there. A lot of my closest friends are there," she explained. "It's always been the place I think of when someone asks where I'm from."
Not long after her team's match concluded, she began looking for ways to help. Rather than waiting for others to organize relief efforts, Richardson set up an online fundraiser aimed at supporting affected families and local recovery programs.
She promoted the campaign through social media, and support arrived quickly. Donations came from teammates, alumni, sports fans, and people who had never even visited the town.
The fundraiser eventually surpassed $120,000, money that will be directed toward relief organizations and rebuilding projects within the community.
Still, Richardson felt there was more she could do.
Earlier this week she traveled back to Tennessee and joined local volunteers working on cleanup efforts. Her days were spent clearing debris, delivering supplies, and meeting residents beginning the difficult process of rebuilding.
"Being there changes everything," she said. "You hear people's stories firsthand, and you realize how much they've lost."
Despite the destruction, what stood out most to her was the response from the community itself. Neighbors were helping one another, businesses were providing resources, and volunteers continued to arrive from surrounding areas.
According to Richardson, that sense of solidarity has always been part of the town's identity.
"People don't wait around for someone else to step in," she said. "They help however they can."
For the athlete, returning home was about more than volunteering. It was also an opportunity to support a community that had encouraged her throughout her life and athletic career.
"This town helped shape who I am," Richardson said. "If I can give something back during a time like this, then that's exactly what I want to do."

London (CNN)It's been a gut-wrenching week for Black Britons.First came the interview where what many Black Britons believed in their hearts to be true was confirmed -- Meghan, Duchess of Sussex, Britain's first member of the modern British royal family to identify as a woman of color, said she was forced to leave the monarchy in large part due to the British press "inciting... racism" against her."Then came the defensiveness of some in the media trying again to gaslight an entire nation."It made me feel unsafe that was the emotion I felt," Karen Gibson said of the interview that shook Britain and provoked the ugly underbelly of prejudice.  Karen Gibson and The Kingdom Choir perform outside Kensington Palace on July 24, 2018 in London, England.The 56-year-old Gospel singer led the Kingdom Choir's rendition of "Stand by Me" for Prince Harry and Meghan's royal wedding in the summer of 2018. Read More"We knew we were breaking new ground," she said of the group's moving performance. "We were standing there for communities of color not only in the UK, but all over the world, and we knew America would be watching." Overnight, Gibson, dubbed the "Godmother of Gospel" by British media, became a celebrity in her south London community of Clapham, where she has lived for decades. "I was sitting on the bus one day and a woman, a Black lady, came and sat next to me and she said, 'We made it!' and I was like 'Did we? Did we make it?" Gibson laughed.  Now that hope and optimism is tinged with the deep pain of racial trauma -- the struggles of an American actress living in the palace spoke directly to the lived experience of ordinary Black Britons.   "I think my reaction was still? Are we here still in 2021?" Gibson said of the explosive interview.  Gibson feels her words about Meghan have been misrepresented in the media before -- an experience she says left her "traumatized" and led to a "loss of trust" in journalists.British royal family plunged into crisis after Harry and Meghan allege racism and neglect in Oprah interviewAt a time of nationwide lockdown, the UK's mainstream media is its public square -- a space that many Black Britons say is hostile to them.Buckingham Palace said in a statement this week that, "The issues raised, particularly that of race, are concerning."In response to Harry's accusation that British tabloids are "bigoted" and build a "toxic environment" of "control and fear," the UK's Society of Editors claimed racism played no part in the coverage of the Duchess. The group's head stepped down after a swift rebuke from more than 160 journalists of color. calling the statement "willful ignorance.""The blanket refusal to accept there is any bigotry in the British press is laughable, does a disservice to journalists of color and shows an institution and an industry in denial," they added.Popular TV host Piers Morgan's claim on one of Britain's most watched morning shows that he "didn't believe a word" that Meghan was saying led to 41,000 complaints to the UK's communication regulator. An investigation was launched, and the famous presenter lost his top gig. On Wednesday, Morgan said that he still does not believe Meghan."I've had time to reflect on this opinion, and I still don't [believe her]," Morgan tweeted. "If you did, OK. Freedom of speech is a hill I'm happy to die on. Thanks for all the love, and hate. I'm off to spend more time with my opinions."UK media executive resigns after Black journalists blast group for being 'in denial' about racismShola Mos-Shogbamimu, whose heated on-air exchange with Morgan went viral after he questioned Meghan's truthfulness during her Oprah interview, says it's going to take more than a couple of resignations to heal the divide."There is the issue of the lack of trust in the media," Mos-Shogbamimu said. "If the media is going to change your words, the tone of your words, how are you going to trust them with your most valuable asset which is your voice." The Britain that Prince Harry and Meghan left is already changing. It was rocked by a racial reckoning after the death of George Floyd in the US, that pushed tens of thousands of anti-racism demonstrators onto the streets of the UK. That hope for change is quickly turning into a demand for equality.   "I cannot waste my energy on those who are still on the ABCs, 123s, do-re-mis of racism," Mos-Shogbamimu explained, "I recognize that those who are against progress on racial equality and racial justice ... one of their tactics is to frustrate the efforts of anti-racist activist like me, so if they have me arguing at the 123 level, where is my energy to deal with the real fight?" Away from the British mainstream media -- an overwhelmingly white institution -- Zeze Millz has carved out her own space on social media.Zeze Millz has carved out her own space in UK media.Her weekly YouTube show speaks to an audience of 120,000 on cultural and political issues. "We've been nice. We've been very, very nice for however long," Millz explained from her studio in London. "Now we are at that point where it's like, no, our voices are going to be heard now and if it is uncomfortable for you then tough luck."  Britons are often quick to condemn viral videos of police brutality against Black people in America, but at home the refrain "racism does not exist," is all too commonly heard by people of color, Millz says."There is a lot of overt racism in America," Millz said, "Whereas here it's not like that -- it's very under-handed. They always kind of gaslight you and come up with another reason why it's not racism." Supporters of the royal couple see the TV interview as a public and global affirmation that Black Britain was right: racism does exist in the UK. But they argue exposing systemic inequality is only step one. "The racism is too deep. It's deep for one person to almost come in and change all of that, but I think that this interview will spark a lot of change," Millz said.In passing remarks to a journalist on Thursday, Prince William, Harry's brother, said, "We're very much not a racist family." But it seems unlikely this black-and-white, whodunnit definition of racism will  be enough to silence the demands for change at a time of racial reckoning.CNN has launched Royal News, a weekly newsletter bringing you the inside track on the royal family, what they are up to in public and what's happening behind palace walls. Sign up here.

(CNN) For many members of Canada's Indigenous communities, the past few years have brought a mix of recognition, frustration, and renewed demands for change.
The discovery of unmarked graves at former residential school sites reignited a national conversation about historical injustices and the ongoing effects of policies that separated Indigenous children from their families. While governments and institutions pledged action, many Indigenous leaders say meaningful progress has been uneven.
"It brought a lot of emotions back to the surface," said community advocate Rachel Whitecloud. "Many people felt grief, but also a sense that stories our families had been telling for generations were finally being heard."
Whitecloud, who works with youth programs in Manitoba, said the public response initially gave many people hope that long-standing issues would receive greater attention.
"There was a feeling that maybe things would be different this time," she said. "That people would listen, learn, and take responsibility."
Yet that optimism has often been tempered by disappointment. Indigenous activists argue that public expressions of support have not always translated into concrete improvements in areas such as housing, healthcare, education, and access to clean drinking water.
For many, media coverage has become part of the debate itself. Some advocates say news organizations have increased attention to Indigenous issues, while others argue that coverage can still be inconsistent or overly focused on moments of crisis.
"There are times when our communities are visible only when something tragic happens," said university lecturer Daniel Cardinal. "The everyday realities, the successes, the resilience—those stories don't always receive the same attention."
The discussion has unfolded alongside broader conversations about inequality and representation taking place across Canada. Public institutions have faced growing pressure to address historical grievances and improve relationships with Indigenous peoples.
Younger Indigenous creators have increasingly turned to social media and independent platforms to tell their own stories. Among them is content producer Maya Stone, whose online videos discussing Indigenous identity, culture, and politics attract hundreds of thousands of views each month.
"For a long time, other people were speaking about us," Stone said. "Now more Indigenous people are speaking for themselves and building their own audiences."
She believes digital platforms have helped create space for conversations that were previously overlooked by mainstream outlets.
At the same time, many activists caution that awareness alone is not enough. Symbolic gestures, they argue, must be followed by lasting reforms.
"There is more public understanding than there used to be," Whitecloud said. "But understanding is only the beginning. The real question is what happens next."
Government officials have acknowledged that significant challenges remain and have pointed to ongoing initiatives aimed at improving infrastructure, expanding services, and supporting Indigenous-led programs. Critics, however, say progress has been too slow.
Despite differing views on the pace of change, there is broad agreement that the national conversation has shifted. Issues that once received limited public attention are now being discussed more openly across the country.
Whether that leads to lasting transformation remains uncertain. But many Indigenous Canadians say the expectation for accountability is stronger than it was a decade ago.
"The conversation isn't going away," Cardinal said. "People have waited too long for that."

(CNN) Across Canada, discussions about Indigenous rights and historical injustice have taken on new urgency in recent years. For many Indigenous people, the period has been marked by both greater visibility and lingering frustration over how slowly change can occur.
The discovery of unmarked graves near former residential schools became a turning point in the national conversation. Communities that had long spoken about the legacy of those institutions suddenly found their experiences receiving widespread public attention.
"It was emotional for a lot of families," said community advocate Rachel Whitecloud. "There was sadness, but there was also a feeling that people were finally willing to hear what Indigenous communities had been saying for generations."
At first, that shift generated optimism. Some believed it could lead to deeper reforms and a stronger commitment from governments and public institutions.
Over time, however, many advocates began questioning whether awareness was translating into action. Problems involving housing, healthcare, education, and basic infrastructure remain pressing concerns in numerous communities.
The role of the media has become part of that discussion as well. While Indigenous issues receive more coverage than they once did, critics argue that attention often peaks during major controversies and fades afterward.
"We tend to become visible during moments of tragedy," said lecturer Daniel Cardinal. "What gets missed are the everyday stories—the achievements, the culture, the people building positive change in their communities."
At the same time, Indigenous voices have become more prominent on platforms outside traditional media. Social media creators, podcasters, and independent journalists are reaching audiences directly, often without relying on established institutions to tell their stories.
One of them is Maya Stone, whose online content focuses on Indigenous identity and public affairs. She says audiences are increasingly interested in hearing firsthand perspectives.
"For a long time, the conversation was happening about Indigenous people rather than with Indigenous people," Stone said. "That's starting to change."
The broader debate now extends beyond historical recognition. Many activists argue that symbolic gestures, while important, are not enough on their own.
According to Whitecloud, public understanding has improved noticeably, but expectations have grown as well.
"People want more than acknowledgment," she said. "They want to see results."
Government officials point to a range of programs and commitments designed to address long-standing inequalities. Yet many community leaders remain skeptical, arguing that progress often moves more slowly than promised.
Still, there is little doubt that the national mood has shifted. Topics that once occupied the margins of public debate now receive regular attention from politicians, educators, and the media.
Whether that momentum produces lasting change remains an open question. What seems clearer is that Indigenous communities are playing a larger role in shaping the conversation themselves.
As Cardinal put it, "The expectation now is accountability. Once people start paying attention, it's much harder to look away."

London (CNN)When an eagle-eyed royal-watcher spotted that Prince Harry's daughter, Lilibet, was missing from the line of succession on the royal family's official website, it went viral on social media. Why was she left out? Doesn't she qualify? Has she been ejected?!The debate was understandable but academic. The line of succession isn't drawn up and posted in an official pronouncement. Everyone's place in it is automatic upon birth. This week, seven weeks after her birth, the website was updated, with Lilibet added in at No. 8. Several British media outlets sought clarity from Buckingham Palace over why Lili was unlisted for so long, to which a spokesman said simply that the website is "updated periodically."Dated website aside, the list based on birth is sacrosanct. Even the Queen can't choose who succeeds her. The very essence of constitutional monarchy is that the head of state isn't elected and therefore avoids the political baggage that comes with being elected as head of state. British republicans say the system is fundamentally undemocratic and should be scrapped, but they've never gained enough public support to make it happen.File photo of the Queen with the Duke and Duchess of Sussex during an RAF flypast of Buckingham Palace in 2018. Ultimately, only Parliament has the power to replace the monarch with a president but there's never been serious debate about that in Westminster. The argument you often hear from politicians is that you wouldn't invent the system as it is now, but why change it?Read MoreMuch of the credit for making it work goes to Queen Elizabeth, who is widely revered for the steadfast way she has carried out the role. Time will tell if Prince Charles commands the same respect. Another notion bandied about is that the Queen should hand the crown straight to the more popular Prince William.But that would undermine the whole principle that the British head of state isn't chosen and, again, only Parliament would have the power to make that happen. Lilibet's place in the line of succession has always been as assured as Charles'. The chances of her actually making it to the throne, however, are as unrealistic as the idea that the Buckingham Palace webmaster decides who the successors are.WHAT ELSE IS HAPPENING?Harry and Meghan back UK journalists.The Sussexes' quest for a fairer and more diverse media landscape continued this week. Their foundation posted a statement throwing its support behind a coalition of UK journalists raising awareness of racial inequity in the industry. The statement highlighted a recent open letter signed by scores of reporters once more denouncing a UK media industry body, the Society of Editors, for its inaction on addressing racism. The body previously came under fire for arguing that sections of the country's press were not bigoted or racist, in response to the Sussexes' explosive Oprah interview. During the interview, Harry said racist coverage of his wife was a factor in their decision to relocate to the US. After being called out by more than 160 journalists of color, the head of the Society of Editors resigned in mid-March. The Archewell statement also said it was a "proud supporter of journalistic diversity and news media organizations that are committed to reporting the truth." And it praised independent media and local journalists, which, it said, "demonstrate the deep need for this critical profession to thrive and evolve."Charles dedicates new national police memorial.The Prince of Wales appeared at the dedication ceremony for the new UK Police Memorial at the National Memorial Arboretum in Staffordshire on Wednesday. Charles paid tribute to those who have died in the protection of others by laying a wreath, before addressing attendees, including Prime Minister Boris Johnson and Home Secretary Priti Patel. "On behalf of the nation, I would particularly like to express my profound gratitude for the valor and sacrifice of those who have laid down their lives to keep us safe, to remember their families who mourn, and to recognize those who continue to serve in order to safeguard our freedoms," he said.A new royal show to binge this weekend?Streaming service HBO Max has just premiered a new animated comedy series based on the British royal family, and it's already splitting opinion. "The Prince" tells the story of life within the Firm from the imagined perspective of third in line to the throne Prince George, who turned 8 only a few days ago. The inspiration for the show's creator, Gary Janetti, came from memes he would make on his Instagram account of George appearing to react to family and international news. Janetti -- whose credits as a television writer and producer include "Will and Grace" and "Family Guy" -- takes on the lead role, with Orlando Bloom voicing Prince Harry, Alan Cumming as George's butler, Owen, and Sophie Turner as Princess Charlotte. In a surprise move, the streamer dropped all 12 episodes of the first season on Thursday. While this is hardly the first time the royal family have been satirized on screen, some on social media have been critical of using a child as the titular character. What do you think? Will you be watching? HBO Max is owned by WarnerMedia, the parent company of CNN.  "The Prince" is a satirical comedy, told from the imagined point of view of 8-year-old Prince George.PHOTO OF THE WEEKPrince Charles at Sandringham during the release of a threatened species of bird.Prince Charles was on hand in Sandringham, the Queen's countryside bolt hole, on Tuesday as the estate released a threatened species of bird on the grounds. He joined Natural England's chair, Tony Juniper, as 80 Eurasian curlew chicks were released in an effort to boost the birds' population in the east of England. The Prince of Wales expressed his delight at Sandringham's involvement in the project as he'd "always cherished the evocative call of the curlew, but it is now dangerously close to being something that our grandchildren will never have the chance to enjoy." The passionate environmentalist added: "Every curlew nest is something to prize, nurture and protect, and it is utterly vital that we work together to turn this iconic bird's fortunes around."FROM THE ROYAL VAULTThursday marked 40 years since Diana's spectacular wedding to Prince Charles in 1981. Her short time as a royal would see her become an international icon who used her status to draw attention to a number of causes, from leprosy to domestic violence to mental health. The couple's divorce in 1996 did little to relieve the intense media scrutiny "the people's princess" faced. And despite her untimely death in 1997, she remains a beloved figure to this day.We thought we'd have a look back at that momentous royal moment four decades ago. Take a look for yourself...Diana and Charles pose at Buckingham Palace after the announcement of their engagement on February 24, 1981.The newlyweds shared a kiss on the Buckingham Palace balcony.Charles and Diana spent part of their honeymoon in Scotland.Check out more snaps from Diana's life in our photo gallery here.
