Shanghai (CNN) — Five weeks into a lockdown of one of the world's largest cities, Chinese authorities are showing no sign of backing down — and the 25 million people confined inside Shanghai are running out of patience.
Since late March, residents have been restricted to their homes or residential compounds, allowed outside only for mandatory PCR testing or medical emergencies. Food distribution, managed through neighborhood committees, has been inconsistent enough that some people have gone days without adequate supplies. Videos of residents shouting from apartment windows, banging pots in protest, and confronting hazmat-suited officials have spread rapidly on Chinese social media — before being taken down. The volume of public dissent is unusual in a country where such expressions are tightly controlled.
When the lockdown began, officials described it as a targeted, rolling measure covering two districts in sequence. It now covers the entire city, with no clear end date.
Tens of thousands of cases are being recorded daily. The overwhelming majority are asymptomatic or mild. That distinction doesn't matter under China's zero-Covid policy, reaffirmed publicly by President Xi Jinping as recently as last month: every positive case must be transferred to centralized quarantine, regardless of how sick — or not sick — the person actually is. Exhibition halls, stadiums and schools have been converted into quarantine centers. Reports of overcrowding, poor sanitation and insufficient food have filtered out, despite efforts to control the narrative.
The economic ripple effects extend well beyond city limits. Shanghai's port is the largest in China and among the busiest in the world. Weeks of disruption have deepened backlogs and added strain to global supply chains that were already fragile after two years of pandemic turbulence.
A diverging world
Elsewhere, the direction of travel is clear. Across Europe, North America and much of Southeast Asia, governments have moved on — dropping mask mandates, reopening borders, scaling back testing. Britain lifted all legal restrictions in February. The US ended its public health emergency in May. In Asia, South Korea dropped its outdoor mask mandate in April. Japan has cautiously reopened to tourists. Thailand and Vietnam, once among the region's more aggressive suppressors, have pivoted toward living with the virus as vaccination rates climbed.
China stands alone among major economies still committed to elimination. That position has become harder to sustain as Omicron — far more transmissible than earlier variants — has led many epidemiologists outside China to conclude that zero-Covid is no longer a realistic long-term goal. The question, in most of the world, has shifted from whether to transition away from the policy to how and when.
Beijing, for now, isn't engaging with that question publicly. State media frames the lockdown as necessary sacrifice. Officials point to China's comparatively low death toll as proof the approach has worked. But with growth forecasts being cut, factories sitting idle, and citizens more openly frustrated than at almost any point in recent memory, the pressure is building — quietly, and not yet visibly, but building all the same.
CNN's Beijing bureau contributed to this report.

(CNN)The 2021 NCAA college football season has looked a lot different than 2020: stadiums are packed with fans, the tailgates, the marching bands and the cheerleaders are back, too.All those things that the sport has sorely missed during the height of the pandemic will be on full display when the two best teams from the nation's best conference meet in what's become the sport's capital city in the last decade or so.It's top-ranked Georgia and third-ranked Alabama facing off in the SEC Championship game at Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta and it doesn't get a whole lot better than this!Weathering stormy TidesAlabama has been the dominant force in college football for the last dozen years. The Crimson Tide have won six national championships and seven SEC titles under legendary coach Nick Saban.Read MoreA tremendous 106 of their players have been drafted into the NFL in that span -- 38 of them in the first round. Six of those first round picks came from last year's squad, which steamrolled its way through a 13-0 season on its way to another national title.Losing all that talent to the NFL might cause some teams to rebuild, but not Saban and the Crimson Tide. They just reload.Nick Saban has won six national titles at Alabama since he took the helm in 2007.Alabama was the nation's top-ranked team and the national title favorite again as 2021 began, with a new quarterback under center in Bryce Young and a new offensive coordinator in Bill O'Brien.After a 2-0 start, they got all they could ask for in their first true road game at Florida. The Gators rallied from an 18-point deficit and had a chance to tie it with three minutes left but failed on a two-point conversion. 'Bama hung on for the 31-29 win.The Tide were 5-0 when they went to Texas A&M, and that's when their unbeaten run came crashing down -- Seth Small's last-second field goal giving the Aggies a shocking 41-38 win.Crimson Tide players were left wondering if their dreams of a national title were now over as they waded through the sea of A&M fans that had stormed the field celebrating the upset. But you can never underestimate the heart of a champion and that's what Alabama showed the rest of the way.They won five straight games, including a pair of narrow wins over LSU and Arkansas, to secure another SEC title game berth. But they wouldn't have stayed in the national title hunt without a comeback for the ages against in-state rivals Auburn.After struggling the whole game, Bryce Young led the Tide on a 97-yard touchdown drive in the final minute and 43 seconds to tie the game at 10. Young wasn't going to be denied in overtime. His two-point conversion pass broke the deadlock in the 4th extra period, giving 'Bama a win that seemed unlikely the first 58 minutes of the game.Bryce Young sparked Heisman talk after leading the Crimson Tide to a gritty victory over Auburn.Young's performance led many to consider him a top contender now for the Heisman Trophy and helped his team weather yet another storm."Over the course of the season, I think sometimes when things have gone poorly, players have responded very well," Saban told reporters after the Crimson Tide's win over Auburn. "It's great to have the resiliency to overcome it, but you'd like to be able to sustain (it) with consistency, so you don't get in those situations."They'll need to find that consistency as they face their toughest challenge of the year against the Georgia Bulldogs.Dawg days are over?After winning a fourth national title in seven years as Alabama's defensive coordinator, Kirby Smart decided to leave his mentor and friend Nick Saban and take the head coaching job at conference rival Georgia in early 2016. The Bulldogs had been a good team (19 straight bowl appearances) but now they wanted to be great.Georgia's last national title was in 1980 and they've only won the SEC twice since 1982, but they believed Smart was the man that could take them to the promised land.Kirby brought his mentor's blueprint for success with him to Athens: a powerful defense, a solid running game and an efficient passing attack -- and it paid dividends right away.Georgia head coach Kirby Smart has brought his mentor's approach to Athens -- with much success -- but he still lacks a national title.Georgia got to the national title game, but fell, in Smart's second season. And that loss to end the 2017 season was a painful one, as it was against -- you guessed it -- Alabama.The game was in Atlanta and the Bulldogs led 13-0 at halftime. 'Bama's star QB Jalen Hurts was struggling, so Saban decided to start freshman Tua Tagovaiola in the second half.Tua led an epic comeback, capping it off with a 41-yard touchdown pass to DeVonta Smith, giving the Tide a 26-23 overtime win. It was their fifth national title under Saban and his first without his top assistant, who had to find a way to overcome the sting of that defeat and move forward. Georgia made some big-time bowl games the next three years but none of them were in the college football playoffs, and fans were starting to get a bit restless.But 2021 has been a different story. The Dawgs have forged a perfect 12-0 record, outscoring their opponents by almost 34 points a game.They lead the nation in fewest points allowed and fewest yards allowed per game and their closest win was a 10-3 victory over Clemson in the season opener. That's the kind of season Alabama's used to having, not Georgia.The Bulldogs have been No. 1 in the country ever since Alabama's loss at Texas A&M and roll into the SEC title game with a ton of confidence and even a bit of swagger."This team has responded to all the things we've challenged them with," said Smart after beating Georgia Tech on Saturday. "We hit them with different things each week, things we want to really focus on -- accountability from their standpoint of holding other players accountable from week to week for different things. And they've done it each and every time."What's at stake?So for the first time since that national title game meeting almost four years ago, Alabama and Georgia meet again. And it's in Atlanta again, this time for the SEC crown.Both teams feel as though they need this win but for very different reasons.For Alabama, which enters No. 3 in the college football playoff rankings, the chances of a seventh national title in the Nick Saban era are on the line here: no team with two losses has made the college football playoff since the four-team format began in the 2014 season.A lot could happen in other conference title games on Saturday which could allow the Crimson Tide to sneak in with a second loss, but they can't count on that happening.Georgia's No. 1 in the playoff rankings and with a loss, they'd still secure a top-four spot, even if it isn't as the number one seed.But for this team, it's about beating Alabama. Kirby Smart's 0-3 against his former boss and the Bulldogs have lost six straight in the series overall -- their last win coming in 2007, which was Saban's first season with the Crimson Tide.The Bulldogs head into the SEC Championship guaranteed a spot in the College Football Playoffs, but a win over 'Bama would be huge for the team."Every year is independent of the previous," Smart said. "Our job is to go play the best possible game we can. That's what we've been trying to build towards this year. We haven't played our best yet, and we think our best is still out there. That's the goal."Another Georgia team, the Atlanta Braves, had to get past a Los Angeles Dodgers team which had haunted them for years on their road to a first World Series title in 26 years. In order for the Bulldogs to win the national title which has eluded them for the last 41 years, they're going to have to climb over their own boogeyman: Alabama.

(CNN) The 2023 college basketball season has delivered everything fans could have hoped for: packed arenas, deafening student sections, and the kind of rivalry matchups that remind you why March became its own cultural institution.
All of that will be on full display when two of the sport's historic powerhouses collide in the Big East Championship at Madison Square Garden in New York City — the self-proclaimed mecca of basketball. It's top-ranked Connecticut and third-ranked Marquette, and the stakes could hardly be higher.
The Husky Machine
UConn has long been one of college basketball's most decorated programs. The Huskies have won five national championships and have become synonymous with tournament excellence under coach Dan Hurley, who has steadily rebuilt the program into a genuine superpower since arriving in Storrs in 2018.
This season, UConn entered the year as the clear favorite after dismantling the field en route to a national title the previous spring. Their roster turned over significantly, losing four players to the NBA Draft, but Hurley simply reloaded. That has become something of a trademark.
The Huskies opened the year 7-0 before a tense road trip to Villanova nearly derailed things. Trailing by nine with under four minutes to play, freshman guard Solo Ball — who had struggled to find his footing early in the season — took over, scoring eight straight points and setting up the game-winning layup with eleven seconds left. UConn survived, 74-73.
They have not lost since. A 17-1 conference record heading into Championship weekend is as dominant a run as the Big East has seen in years, and Ball has gone from question mark to the frontrunner for Big East Player of the Year. His emergence has given UConn something even their previous title team lacked: a genuine go-to scorer in the closing minutes of tight games.
"He's grown up before our eyes," Hurley told reporters after UConn's regular season finale. "Early on, he was trying to do too much. Now he trusts the system, trusts his teammates, and when the moment gets big, he rises."
The Golden Eagles Circle
Marquette's path to this moment has been equally compelling, if a little more turbulent. Head coach Shaka Smart, now in his third season in Milwaukee, finally has the pieces to make a genuine title run — and his team knows it.
The Golden Eagles opened the year unranked but won eleven of their first thirteen games, climbing steadily into the top ten. Their calling card has been suffocating perimeter defense and a balanced offense that features five players averaging double figures — the first Marquette team to accomplish that in over two decades.
Their only real stumble came in late January, a two-game losing skid against Providence and Georgetown that briefly threatened to derail their conference title hopes. Smart responded by simplifying the offense and shifting to a smaller, quicker lineup — a gamble that paid off immediately. Marquette won eight straight to close the regular season, including an emphatic 81-64 road win over then-No. 6 Creighton.
Senior guard Kam Jones has been the engine of it all, averaging 22 points per game since the lineup change and drawing comparisons to previous Marquette greats. "We went through a tough stretch and it made us better," Jones said after the regular season ended. "We know who we are now. We stopped trying to be perfect and started playing together."
What's at stake?
For UConn, a Big East title would be the program's sixth in the Dan Hurley era and would all but cement their status as the No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament. More than that, a win would validate their claim to being the best team in the country — a claim a few voters still dispute despite their record.
For Marquette, the calculus is different. Smart is 0-4 against UConn since arriving at the program, and the Golden Eagles have not won the Big East Tournament since 2003. A win on Saturday would shatter both of those storylines at once, and with Selection Sunday one day away, it would send the program into the tournament on the kind of momentum that has historically translated to deep runs.
Two years ago, a different Marquette team reached the Elite Eight before bowing out to eventual champion Kansas. Smart and his players believe this group is better. Whether they're right depends on 40 minutes in the Garden.
"We've put ourselves in a position to compete for something real," Smart said this week. "Now we have to go do it. Nobody's going to hand us anything — especially not them."

(CNN) College basketball's 2023 season has given fans just about everything they could ask for — packed arenas, raucous student sections, and the kind of rivalry matchups that turn March into its own national holiday.
Saturday brings the best of it. Top-ranked Connecticut and third-ranked Marquette meet in the Big East Championship at Madison Square Garden, the self-declared mecca of the sport, and both programs have something serious to play for.
The Husky Machine
UConn has been here before. Five national championships, a tradition of tournament excellence, and Dan Hurley, now in his sixth season in Storrs, who has quietly turned the Huskies back into one of the sport's most feared programs.
This season followed a familiar script. Four players left for the NBA Draft after last spring's title run. Hurley reloaded. The Huskies started 7-0 before a late scare at Villanova — trailing by nine with four minutes left — exposed some early-season fragility. That's when freshman guard Solo Ball took over. Eight straight points, a game-winning layup with eleven seconds remaining. UConn won 74-73 and has not lost since.
Ball has become the season's defining story. Hesitant and turnover-prone in October, he's now the Big East's leading Player of the Year candidate, giving UConn something even their title team lacked: someone who thrives when the game is on the line.
"He's grown up before our eyes," Hurley said after the regular season finale. "When the moment gets big, he rises."
A 17-1 conference record is the result. It is the most dominant Big East regular season in years.
The Golden Eagles Circle
Marquette started the year unranked and nobody outside Milwaukee expected much. Shaka Smart, in his third season with the program, had been building toward something — and it became clear early on that this group was different.
Five players averaging double figures. The program's first to do that in over two decades. Defense that suffocates from the perimeter. The Golden Eagles climbed steadily into the top ten before a two-game stumble in late January — losses to Providence and Georgetown — threatened to unravel the whole thing.
Smart's response was to simplify. A smaller, quicker lineup, a stripped-down offense. Marquette won eight straight to close out the regular season, capped by an 81-64 demolition of then-No. 6 Creighton on the road. Senior guard Kam Jones averaged 22 points per game over that stretch and started drawing comparisons to the program's celebrated alumni.
"We went through a tough stretch and it made us better," Jones said. "We stopped trying to be perfect and started playing together."
What's at stake?
UConn wants a Big East title, a No. 1 seed, and the right to call themselves undisputed national frontrunners. Most of the country already believes it. A few voters still don't. Winning Saturday would settle the argument.
Marquette's situation is more pointed. Smart is 0-4 against UConn since his arrival, and the Golden Eagles haven't won the Big East Tournament since 2003. A win would erase both of those at once — and send the program into Selection Sunday on the kind of momentum that translates to NCAA Tournament runs.
Two years ago a Marquette team reached the Elite Eight before falling to eventual champion Kansas. Smart believes this group is better. Saturday will go a long way toward finding out.
"We've put ourselves in a position to compete for something real," he said this week. "Nobody's going to hand us anything — especially not them."

(CNN)Should this NFL game be shown as a live broadcast or on the History Channel?That's the question that's been put to Tom Brady and Drew Brees ahead of their meeting in the NFL playoffs this Sunday, the first postseason game in NFL history to feature two opposing starting quadragenarian quarterbacks.The 43-year-old Brady tweeted a "History Awaits" picture ahead of the clash, while 42-year-old Brees gave it a thumbs up, saying: "This is good!"https://t.co/kvv2dNTmHS pic.twitter.com/ND8nmGmZG7— Tom Brady (@TomBrady) January 12, 2021

The combined age of these legends of the sport -- they occupy the top two spots in nearly all quarterback records -- will be 85 years and 169 days old on Sunday when the New Orleans Saints host the Tampa Bay Buccaneers in the NFC Divisional playoffs.That's the oldest combined age of opposing starting quarterbacks in a single game -- regular-season or postseason -- in league annals.Read MoreBrady joked that he will have to overcome an age difference with Brees if he wants to win on Sunday."He's a lot younger than me," Brady told the media. "He's 18 months younger than me. Eighteen months ago, I felt a lot better."READ: 'If I would have known then what I know now, I would have never stepped foot in the NFL'Two of the greatest quarterbacks in NFL history Brees and Brady  share a few words after the regular season game between the Saints and the Buccaneers.Old, but not outThey may well not have the years ahead of them like their superstar quarterback rivals Patrick Mahomes, Lamar Jackson and Josh Allen might do, but Brady and Brees are certainly no slouches.Having moved to Tampa Bay after 20 years with the New England Patriots, Brady has guided the Buccaneers to the postseason at the first time of asking, throwing for 4633 yards and 40 touchdowns in his 21st season in the league.However, although he's formed a productive connection with the host of superstar wide receivers at his disposal, games against the Saints this season have proved a problem for Brady and co.The two sides have faced off twice this season, with the Saints winning on both occasions. And if they are to advance to the next round of the playoffs, the Buccaneers will have to defeat the Saints in Louisiana as well.The last game between the NFL teams ended in a resounding 38-3 win for the Saints, but that was over two months ago and, in that time, Brady believes the Buccaneers have "certainly come a long way."Brady warms up before playing against the Washington Football Team in the playoffs."I think we're just going to keep improving," the six-time Super Bowl champion said."The more we're together, the more we're talking about football, the more we're trying to be on the same page, the better it is. It's a complex game -- there's a lot of moving parts (and) there's a lot of coordination involved between a lot of different positions."We're just going to keep trying to make improvements. We've got a big test this weekend (against) one of the great teams in the NFL [who has] consistently been one of the great teams for a long time. We're going to have to go play a great football game."While Brady is attempting to make a playoff run with a new team, coach and players for the first time in his storied career, Brees and Saints head coach Sean Payton are in complete synchronicity after 15 years together.Having led New Orleans to the Super Bowl XLIV title in 2009, the pair appear to be making a final push to claim that elusive second championship ring together with rumors circulating that Brees could step away after this season.Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, features, and videosBrees looks to throw a pass against the Chicago Bears during the fourth quarter in the NFC Wild Card Playoff game.And when Brady moved to the same division as him in the summer, a clash between the two older statesmen was something Brees "envisioned.""When Tom Brady signed with the Bucs and I knew that he was coming to our division, I envisioned this game," he told the media."So what was that nine months ago, eight months ago, I envisioned this game happening because I knew our aspirations as a team, to be in the playoffs and beyond."And I certainly knew what he was bringing to the Bucs and that talented roster. And so I think this is probably where we all envisioned being at this point in season."

(CNN) Should Saturday's NBA playoff game be broadcast on ESPN or framed and hung in the Smithsonian?
That's the question swirling around LeBron James and Kevin Durant ahead of their second-round clash this weekend — the first postseason series in NBA history to feature two opposing players each with more than 27,000 career regular-season points still actively competing at an elite level.
James, 40, posted a characteristically understated message on Instagram ahead of the series: a black-and-white photo of the two men shaking hands at midcourt, captioned simply "Legacy." Durant, 36, responded within the hour: "Let's give them something to remember."
The combined career statistics these two men have accumulated defy easy summary. Between them: eight NBA championships, six scoring titles, two Olympic gold medals apiece, and more All-NBA selections than any two players in the league's modern era. Both rank in the all-time top five in points scored. Historians of the sport have spent the better part of a decade arguing over which of them deserves to be called the most skilled offensive player the game has ever produced.
Older, not slower
They may not have the runway that younger stars like Jayson Tatum, Anthony Edwards, or Shai Gilgeous-Alexander still enjoy, but dismissing either man as a spent force would be a serious mistake.
James, completing his 22nd season after joining the Golden State Warriors on a one-year deal that raised eyebrows across the league last summer, has averaged 24 points, nine assists, and seven rebounds per game in the regular season — numbers that would be remarkable for a player a decade younger. Critics questioned whether he could adapt to a new system under head coach Steve Kerr. The answer has been emphatic.
The Warriors and Durant's Phoenix Suns met twice during the regular season. Phoenix won both, including a dominant 118-97 victory in February in which Durant scored 38 points and held James to 19 on nine-of-twenty-two shooting. James has not forgotten.
"That one stung," he admitted to reporters on Thursday. "But we're a different team now than we were in February. I'm a different player. We've had time to build something here and I think people are starting to see what that looks like."
Durant, meanwhile, is fresh off dismantling a young Oklahoma City Thunder team in the first round, averaging 31 points per game in a five-game series that was closer than the outcome suggested. Head coach Frank Vogel has had five seasons to build an offense around Durant's singular abilities, and the results have been quietly remarkable: three Western Conference Finals appearances and one championship in 2022.
"This is where you want to be," Durant told reporters after closing out the Thunder. "This matchup, this stage. I've been thinking about it since the bracket came out."
What's at stake?
For James, the calculus is straightforward. A deep playoff run — ideally a championship — with a third different franchise would cement an argument many already believe is settled: that he is the greatest to ever play the game. A title with Golden State, following rings with Cleveland and the Los Angeles Lakers, would be unprecedented.
For Durant, the stakes are no less significant. At 36, he is widely expected to announce his retirement at season's end regardless of outcome. A second championship, won against the player who has defined his era, would be the crowning note to a career that needs no further justification — but would certainly benefit from one.
The two men have faced each other in the playoffs three times previously, with Durant's teams holding a 2-1 series advantage. Each meeting has produced at least one moment that replayed for years afterward on highlight reels and in arguments between fans.
"I've known for a long time that this is what the end of my career would look like," Durant said on Thursday. "Competing at the highest level, against the best. That's all I've ever wanted. Sunday gives us that."

(CNN)It was supposed to be the underworld's impenetrable communication tool, a digital safe space to plot crimes ranging from drug trafficking to murder away from the prying eyes of the law.But for nearly three years, an encrypted app used by criminals was covertly monitored by the FBI and Australia's Federal Police (AFP) -- leading to hundreds of arrests and tens of millions of dollars in asset seizures, authorities revealed on Tuesday.  In a statement, the AFP said they and the FBI had been reading the clandestine communications of criminals since 2018 on the ANoM app -- a black-market product only accessible on specially prepared mobile phones.According to the AFP, intelligence gathered from the decrypted messages led to the arrest of 224 suspects on more than 500 charges and the seizure of 3.7 metric tons of drugs and nearly $35 million in cash over the past three years in Australia.A number of those charged are allegedly linked to Australian-based Italian mafia, outlaw motorcycle gangs, Asian crime syndicates and Albanian organized crime, the AFP added.Read More"Operation Ironside," as the investigation was named, began three years ago as a collaboration between several global law enforcement agencies, including the FBI, the AFP, New Zealand police and Europol.Handout images from the Australian Federal Police show arrests and seizures made under Operation Ironside.Speaking at a news conference on Tuesday, AFP Commissioner Reece Kershaw said multiple criminal plots were also foiled thanks to law enforcement access to the app, including a planned mass shooting at an Australian suburban cafe and the assassination of a family of five.ANoM could only be found on phones bought through the black market, which had been stripped of the capability to make calls or send emails, according to the AFP. The phones could only send messages to another device that had the app and criminals needed to know another criminal to get a device."The devices organically circulated and grew in popularity among criminals, who were confident of the legitimacy of the app because high-profile organized crime figures vouched for its integrity," the AFP statement said.Unknown to the app's users, the FBI had access to the app and its communications, which the organization then used to collect information on criminal operations. According to court documents released by the US government on Tuesday, the ANoM was given to San Diego FBI agents by a developer who had created it to compete on the market for encrypted communications platforms. The developer, whose identity is not revealed, offered it to help the FBI with its ongoing investigations, in return for a reduced sentence on charges he was facing.In May 2021, the top five countries where ANoM devices were used were Germany, the Netherlands, Spain, Australia, and Serbia, according to the documents.Kershaw said the criminals didn't use codes or pseudonyms on ANoM, brazenly discussing crimes in the mistaken confidence their communications were safe.The Australian Federal Police and other international law enforcement agencies seized tonnes of drugs and millions of dollars in cash as part of Operation Ironside."Essentially, we have been in the back pockets of organized crime and operationalized a criminal takedown like we have never seen," Kershaw said."The use of encrypted communication apps presents significant challenges to law enforcement and ANoM has given law enforcement a window into the level of criminality that we have never seen before on this scale." Hundreds of arrests and tons of drugs were also seized overseas as a result of the operation, the AFP said, but didn't specify where. Police also seized assets in Australia which authorities estimated would be worth millions of dollars once they were sold.In total over the course of the three years, more than 9,000 police officers across 18 countries were involved in maintaining the operation.The AFP said more arrests are expected in Australia and overseas as a result of the operation.Kershaw added there were other, larger encrypted communication apps which police were working to access.CNN's Angus Watson and Sarah Faidell contributed to this article.

(CNN) It was marketed as an unbreakable fortress for sensitive corporate communications — a premium encrypted messaging platform promising total privacy from competitors, hackers, and governments alike. Instead, it became one of the most productive surveillance tools European law enforcement had ever deployed against organized crime.
Authorities in the Netherlands, France, and Belgium revealed on Monday that they had secretly infiltrated EncroChat, an encrypted communications network widely used by criminal networks across Europe, for nearly two years — harvesting tens of millions of messages and triggering one of the continent's largest coordinated law enforcement operations in recent memory.
The joint operation, codenamed "Operation Emma," was led by Europol and Eurojust in coordination with national police forces across more than a dozen countries. Officials said the infiltration of the network had produced over 115 million intercepted messages, leading to more than 1,500 arrests across Europe, the seizure of over 30 metric tons of narcotics, and the confiscation of more than 180 million euros in cash and assets.
Speaking at a press conference in The Hague on Monday, Europol Executive Director Catherine De Bolle described the operation as a landmark moment for cross-border law enforcement. "For the first time, we were able to read the real-time communications of criminal organizations at scale," she said. "These were not low-level offenders. These were the highest levels of organized crime — the planners, the financiers, the decision-makers."
EncroChat devices, which sold for several thousand euros each on the black market, were modified smartphones with the camera, microphone and GPS functions disabled. They could only communicate with other EncroChat devices and were marketed explicitly to users who required, in the words of the platform's own promotional material, "total operational security." The platform had an estimated 60,000 users worldwide at its peak, the majority of whom, authorities allege, were engaged in criminal activity.
The breakthrough came when French cyber-investigators managed to implant malware onto the EncroChat network's servers, located in France, allowing them to intercept messages before they were encrypted. The operation ran covertly for several months before EncroChat administrators detected the intrusion and shut the platform down in June 2020, sending a warning to users telling them to destroy their handsets immediately.
By then, authorities say, the damage — from the criminals' perspective — was done. The intercepted communications contained detailed discussions of drug shipments, weapons deals, contract killings, and money laundering operations. In several cases, law enforcement agencies were able to intervene before planned attacks took place, including the disruption of at least three alleged contract murders in the Netherlands alone.
Not all of the operation's legal foundations have gone uncontested. Defense lawyers in multiple countries have challenged the admissibility of EncroChat evidence in court, arguing that the mass interception of private communications violated the rights of defendants. Several cases in Germany and the United Kingdom are still working through the courts, with judges reaching differing conclusions on the legality of how the data was obtained and shared between jurisdictions.
Europol said Monday that further arrests were anticipated as investigators continued to work through the volume of material collected, and that related operations targeting successor networks were already underway.
"Criminal networks will always look for new tools," De Bolle said. "But this operation has shown that no platform is beyond reach."

(CNN) It was sold as an unbreakable private communications tool — premium encrypted messaging, marketed to anyone who needed their conversations completely off the grid. What it actually became was one of the most productive surveillance assets European law enforcement has ever run against organized crime.
Authorities in the Netherlands, France, and Belgium announced Monday that they had secretly infiltrated EncroChat, an encrypted network with tens of thousands of users across Europe, for nearly two years. The result: over 115 million intercepted messages, more than 1,500 arrests across the continent, upwards of 30 metric tons of narcotics seized, and more than 180 million euros in cash and assets confiscated.
The operation, codenamed "Operation Emma," was coordinated through Europol and Eurojust, with national police forces from more than a dozen countries involved. Europol Executive Director Catherine De Bolle, speaking in The Hague on Monday, called it a turning point. "For the first time, we were able to read the real-time communications of criminal organizations at scale," she said. "These were not low-level offenders. These were the planners, the financiers, the decision-makers."
EncroChat handsets changed hands on the black market for several thousand euros each. They were modified smartphones — camera disabled, microphone disabled, GPS stripped out — that could only communicate with other EncroChat devices. The platform's own promotional material promised, in so many words, total operational security. At its peak, it had an estimated 60,000 users worldwide. Authorities allege the overwhelming majority were criminals.
The breakthrough came when French cyber-investigators managed to implant malware on EncroChat's servers, which were physically located in France, allowing them to intercept messages before encryption kicked in. The covert phase ran for several months until EncroChat administrators detected the intrusion in June 2020 and shut everything down, sending users a blunt instruction: destroy your handsets immediately.
By that point, it was too late. The intercepted messages contained detailed discussions of drug shipments, weapons deals, contract killings, and money laundering arrangements. In several cases, law enforcement stepped in before planned attacks occurred — including, Dutch officials said, the disruption of at least three alleged contract murders in the Netherlands.
The operation has not been without controversy. Defense lawyers across multiple jurisdictions have challenged whether EncroChat evidence is admissible in court, arguing the mass interception of private messages violated defendants' rights. Cases in Germany and the United Kingdom are still moving through the courts, with judges reaching different conclusions about how the data was gathered and shared across borders.
More arrests are expected, Europol said Monday, as investigators continue working through the material collected. Related operations targeting networks that emerged in EncroChat's wake are already underway.
"Criminal networks will always look for new tools," De Bolle said. "But this operation has shown that no platform is beyond reach."

(CNN)A leading English referee and his family have been subjected to online death threats following two erroneous decisions in successive games he took charge of.Mike Dean had received criticism for sending off West Ham's Tomas Soucek on Saturday and Southampton's Jan Bednarek on February 2, despite reviewing both incidents on the pitch-side monitor.Both red cards were subsequently overturned on appeal by the respective clubs."It is inexcusable that Mike Dean and his family have received online abuse, including death threats, as a result of doing his job officiating at a Premier League match," said Premier League chief executive Richard Masters in a statement sent to CNN."It is completely unacceptable that we are seeing abusive behaviour aimed at players, managers and match officials regularly on social media platforms. Read More"Mike and his family have our full support in reporting this to the police. This once again highlights the need for greater proactive intervention from social media companies to stop online abuse and identify offenders."The 53-year-old Dean, arguably the Premier League's most high-profile referee, has reportedly notified the police of the abusive messages. "We can confirm officers are investigating allegations of malicious communications reported today, Monday 8 February," Merseyside Police told CNN in a statement. "The reports relate to social media accounts and enquiries are ongoing."We monitor the internet, and if we identify any offenses we will take action. Those who use the internet to target others, and who commit a criminal offense such as hate crime or malicious communications in doing so, are not beyond the law -- and can and will be investigated and brought to justice."Dean will referee Leicester City's FA Cup fifth-round tie against Brighton on Wednesday, but he asked not be involved in any of Premier League's fixtures this weekend.READ: Chelsea left 'disgusted' by online racism directed at Reece James and calls for more actionMike Dean has received death threats following two erroneous decisions.'Abhorrent messages'Mike Riley, the head of refereeing in England, said the abuse directed at Dean is "totally unacceptable.""We fully support Mike's decision to report these messages his family received to the police," he said. "Nobody should be victim of abhorrent messages like this. Online abuse is unacceptable in any walk of life and more needs to be done to tackle the problem."West Ham midfielder Soucek also tweeted his support for Dean and his family."Whatever decisions are made on the pitch should stay on the pitch," he wrote. "I don't like hearing about it interfering with personal life and I send Mike Dean and his family my support. There is no place for abuse of any kind. It is in the past and I'm now focused on the rest of the season."During the 2020/2021 season, a number of Premier League players have received abuse -- in particular racist abuse -- on their social media accounts.Last week Oliver Dowden, who is Britain's Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport, told Parliament that the government's "online safety Bill, we will require social media companies to take swift and effective action against criminal abuse online, and as part of that we will put in place effective user reporting and redress mechanisms."

(CNN) A prominent Premier League manager and members of his coaching staff have been targeted with threatening messages on social media following a contentious tactical decision that contributed to his club's defeat in a high-profile match over the weekend.
Brentford manager Thomas Frank confirmed on Monday that he and several of his backroom staff had received abusive messages, including threats of physical harm, after his decision to substitute a key forward in the second half was widely blamed by supporters for costing the team a crucial victory. Frank said he had reported the messages to the police and had been in contact with the Premier League.
"It is deeply upsetting, not just for me but for my family and for members of my staff who have nothing to do with decisions made on the pitch," Frank said in a statement released through the club. "I have been in football long enough to know that fans are passionate and that results matter. But there is a line, and threats of violence are far beyond it."
The Premier League condemned the abuse in firm terms. "Threats of violence and intimidatory behavior directed at any manager, player, official, or member of staff are completely unacceptable," said a spokesperson. "We fully support Thomas and his staff in reporting this matter to police and will cooperate fully with any investigation."
The Professional Footballers' Association and the League Managers Association issued a joint statement expressing solidarity with Frank and calling for urgent action from social media companies. "This is not an isolated incident. Managers, players and officials are routinely subjected to abuse that in any other professional context would be treated as a serious criminal matter," the statement read. "Social media platforms must do more — proactively and immediately — to identify and remove threatening content and share information with law enforcement."
The Metropolitan Police confirmed they had received a report and that enquiries were ongoing. "We take all reports of malicious communications seriously," a spokesperson said. "Threatening behavior online is not consequence-free, and those found to have committed offenses will be pursued."
Frank is expected to take charge of Brentford's next fixture on Thursday, though the club said it was in ongoing discussions with the Premier League about security arrangements. He briefly addressed reporters ahead of the match, striking a composed but clearly frustrated tone.
"I love this game. I love managing in this league," he said. "But it has to be safe for everyone involved — not just the players on the pitch, but the people on the touchline, in the offices, and at home."
Several current Premier League players took to social media to express support for Frank, with a number calling on platforms to take greater responsibility for content posted on their sites. Britain's Sports Minister also weighed in, saying the government's forthcoming Online Safety legislation would place new legal obligations on platforms to act swiftly against criminal abuse.

(CNN) Brentford manager Thomas Frank says he and members of his coaching staff have received threatening messages on social media after a controversial substitution during the weekend's defeat was blamed by some supporters for costing the club the match.
Frank confirmed Monday that he had reported the abuse to police and contacted the Premier League. The messages, which included threats of physical harm, followed widespread criticism of his decision to withdraw a key forward in the second half.
"It is deeply upsetting — not just for me but for my family and for staff members who have nothing to do with decisions made on the pitch," Frank said in a statement released by the club. "I have been in football long enough to know that fans are passionate. But threats of violence are far beyond any line."
The Premier League's response was unequivocal. "Threats of violence directed at any manager, player, official, or member of staff are completely unacceptable," a spokesperson said. "We fully support Thomas and his staff in reporting this to police."
The Professional Footballers' Association and the League Managers Association followed with a joint statement, tying Frank's experience to a broader pattern. Managers, players, and officials, they argued, are routinely subjected to abuse that would be treated as a serious criminal matter in any other profession. Social media companies, they said, needed to act — proactively, not reactively.
Metropolitan Police confirmed they had received a report. "Threatening behavior online is not consequence-free," a spokesperson said. "Those found to have committed offenses will be pursued."
Frank is expected to manage Thursday's fixture. Ahead of it, he spoke briefly to reporters — composed, but not hiding his frustration. "I love this game. I love managing in this league," he said. "But it has to be safe for everyone — not just the players on the pitch, but the people on the touchline, in the offices, and at home."
Several players posted messages of support, and Britain's Sports Minister used the incident to reiterate the government's position: the forthcoming Online Safety legislation would require platforms to act swiftly against criminal abuse, with legal consequences for those that don't.

Barcelona, SpainSeparatist parties won enough seats on Sunday in Catalonia's regional parliament to strengthen their majority, although a strong showing for the local branch of Spain's ruling Socialists pointed to a dialogue, rather than breakup, with Madrid.With over 99% of ballots counted, separatists won 50.9% of the vote, surpassing the 50% threshold for the first time. The most likely scenario was for the two main separatist parties to extend their coalition government.The final outcome is unlikely, however, to lead to any repeat of the chaotic, short-lived declaration of independence from Spain that took place in 2017. Tensions have ebbed and most voters were more concerned about the Covid-19 pandemic than independence.Low turnout of 53% amid the pandemic, down from 79% in the previous election in 2017, may have favored separatist parties, whose supporters were more mobilized.Election monitors swapped face masks for full-body protective suits during the final hour of voting, "the zombie hour", which was reserved for people with confirmed or suspected Covid-19. Other precautions during the day included temperatures taken on arrival, hand gel and separate entries and exits.Read MoreLeftist separatist party Esquerra Republicana de Catalunya (ERC) said it would lead the regional government and seek the support of other parties for a referendum on independence."The country starts a new era with (separatists) surpassing 50% of the vote for the first time. ... We have an immense strength to achieve a referendum and the Catalan republic," said acting regional chief Pere Aragones, who led his party's slate of candidates.He urged Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez to enter into talks to agree on a referendum.But the fragmented vote, which saw the Socialists win the highest percentage of votes, 23%, and the same number of seats as ERC -- 33 in the in the 135-seat assembly -- means they will also try to form a government.Socialist candidate Salvador Illa, who until recently led Spain's coronavirus response as health minister, argued there was a broad call in Catalonia for reconciliation after years of separatism and said he would try to seek a majority in parliament.That would require an unlikely alliance, however, with other parties.The centre-right pro-independence Junts won an estimated 32 seats, while far-left separatist party CUP got nine. Both those parties are considered key to achieving another separatist coalition government.Spanish nationalist far-right party Vox won 11 seats in Catalonia's parliament for the first time, ahead of the People's Party, the main Spanish conservative party, and the centre-right Ciudadanos. Vox is already the third-largest party in Spain's national parliament.But with ERC seen getting more lawmakers than Junts this time, that could boost the stability of Spain's central government.The result could be seen as good news for Sanchez as his Socialist party won almost double the 17 seats it got in 2017.ERC has provided key votes to the Socialists in the Spanish parliament in exchange for talks on the Catalan political conflict.

Brussels, Belgium
Flemish nationalist parties secured enough seats on Sunday in Belgium's regional parliament to extend their majority, though a resilient performance by Flemish social democrats suggested that coalition negotiations in Brussels could prove long and complicated.
With over 98% of ballots counted, the combined nationalist bloc took 52.3% of the vote, their strongest showing in a decade. The most probable outcome was a continuation of the existing center-right Flemish coalition, though smaller parties held significant leverage over the final shape of any government.
The result is unlikely to trigger any immediate push for formal secession from Belgium. While tensions between Dutch-speaking Flanders and French-speaking Wallonia remain a persistent feature of Belgian political life, most voters on Sunday cited economic anxieties and the rising cost of living as their primary concerns — not the constitutional question of the country's future.
Turnout was 68%, down from 74% at the previous regional election, a dip that analysts said may have modestly benefited nationalist parties whose core supporters tend to vote at higher rates in lower-participation elections.
The centre-right New Flemish Alliance, known by its Flemish initials N-VA, remained the dominant force in the region, winning an estimated 29 seats in the 124-seat Flemish Parliament. Party leader Bart De Wever declared the result a mandate for greater autonomy and renewed fiscal transfers between the regions.
"Flanders has spoken clearly," De Wever told supporters at the party's election night gathering in Ghent. "We want control over our own future — our own budget, our own social policy, our own choices. That conversation with Brussels cannot be delayed any longer."
The far-right Vlaams Belang, which has increasingly competed with N-VA for the Flemish nationalist vote in recent cycles, won an estimated 26 seats, a slight decline from its previous high-water mark. The party's leader, Tom Van Grieken, nonetheless framed the result as a continued rejection of what he called Belgium's "dysfunctional federal model."
The Flemish social democrats, Vooruit, delivered the evening's most notable surprise, winning 21 seats — a gain of four — and positioning themselves as a potentially decisive partner in any coalition talks. Party leader Melissa Depraeter said her strong showing showed that Flemish voters wanted economic reform, not constitutional upheaval.
"People are worried about their energy bills, their wages, their children's futures," Depraeter told reporters as results came in. "They did not vote tonight for an abstract debate about borders. They voted for change."
For Belgian Prime Minister Georges-Louis Bouchez, whose MR party governs at the federal level in an awkward coalition that spans the linguistic divide, the result offered mixed signals. N-VA's continued dominance deepens pressure on the federal government to accelerate talks on a new state reform, a process that has stalled repeatedly in the current legislative term.
Constitutional experts noted that the path to any formal partition of Belgium — a scenario that resurfaces in political discussion every few years — remains extraordinarily complicated, requiring approval across multiple parliaments and a level of cross-community consensus that does not currently exist.
For now, the most immediate question is a simpler one: who will govern Flanders, and on what terms.

Brussels, Belgium
Flemish nationalist parties won enough seats on Sunday to extend their majority in Belgium's regional parliament, though a stronger-than-expected performance from Flemish social democrats signaled that the road to a new coalition government in Brussels would be anything but smooth.
With nearly 99% of ballots counted, the combined nationalist bloc claimed 52.3% of the vote — their strongest result in a decade. A continuation of the existing center-right Flemish coalition appeared the most likely outcome, though smaller parties retained enough leverage to complicate negotiations significantly.
None of this is likely to produce any immediate push toward formal secession. The question of Belgium's constitutional future — the long-running tension between Dutch-speaking Flanders and French-speaking Wallonia — remains live, but voters on Sunday were more preoccupied with energy prices and the cost of living than with the country's survival as a single state.
Turnout came in at 68%, down from 74% in the previous regional election. Analysts suggested the lower figure may have modestly favored nationalist parties, whose supporters tend to show up in greater numbers when broader enthusiasm fades.
The centre-right New Flemish Alliance, or N-VA, held its position as the region's dominant political force, winning an estimated 29 seats in the 124-seat Flemish Parliament. Party leader Bart De Wever framed the result as a mandate for deeper autonomy and a renegotiation of fiscal arrangements between the regions.
"Flanders has spoken clearly," De Wever told supporters in Ghent on Sunday night. "We want control over our own future — our own budget, our own social policy. That conversation cannot be delayed any longer."
The far-right Vlaams Belang, which has spent several election cycles eating into N-VA's nationalist base, won around 26 seats — a modest decline from its previous peak. Leader Tom Van Grieken nonetheless insisted the result was a further rebuke of what he called Belgium's "dysfunctional federal model."
The evening's real story, though, may have been Vooruit. The Flemish social democrats picked up four seats to reach 21, instantly positioning themselves as a potentially pivotal player in coalition talks. Leader Melissa Depraeter was blunt about what she thought Sunday's vote actually meant.
"People are worried about their energy bills, their wages, their children's futures," she told reporters as results came in. "They did not vote tonight for an abstract debate about borders."
For federal Prime Minister Georges-Louis Bouchez, the results were mixed at best. N-VA's dominance deepens pressure on his already-strained coalition to accelerate stalled talks on a new state reform — a process that has collapsed repeatedly over the past several years.
Constitutional experts were quick to note, as they tend to after every Flemish election, that the formal partition of Belgium would require approval across multiple parliaments and a level of cross-community consensus that remains nowhere in sight.
For now, the question is more prosaic: who governs Flanders, and what they will have to give away to do it.

(CNN)After a delay of more than a year due to the pandemic, the Tokyo 2020 Summer Olympics are finally underway.Competition began Wednesday morning with Japan's softball team facing off against Australia at the Fukushima Azuma Baseball Stadium. The opening ceremony will be held Friday, but Olympic events typically begin before the formal start for scheduling purposes. These Olympics are expected to be a competition unlike the world has ever seen. Organizers decided to push ahead despite Japan's struggle to rein in the spread of Covid-19, and with just two days until the formal opening, it is still not clear whether the public health measures in place are enough to prevent the Games from becoming a global superspreader event. Athletes have already begun arriving, and more than 70 cases tied to the Olympics have been identified. American Cat Osterman celebrates with teammates during the softball game between Italy and the US on Wednesday.Organizers had spent months preparing for 11,000 athletes from 200 countries and thousands of journalists to descend on a single city, a potential logistical nightmare in the middle of a pandemic and without the support of the public. Opinion polls in Japan show most people oppose holding a major sporting event during a public health crisis.Read MoreWorld Health Organization Director General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, who is in Tokyo for the Games, expressed confidence Japan could safely host the event in a speech to the International Olympic Committee (IOC), though its plans would be "put to the test." "There is no zero risk in life; there is only more risk, or less risk. And you have done your best," he said. The Olympic village is prepped with coronavirus testing and health centers, with signs reminding residents to wear face masks and keep at least 1 meter (about 3.3 feet) away from each other. Athletes will be contact-traced and tested for Covid-19 daily; if they test positive, they will be taken to an isolation facility outside the village, and will not be able to compete.When Tokyo extended its coronavirus state of emergency until August 22, the IOC took the unprecedented step of barring fans from attending the competition.Japan's chief Olympics official, Toshiro Muto, did not rule out canceling the quadrennial sporting event last minute on Tuesday.JUST WATCHED'This was my final curtain bow': Athlete's Olympic dreams crushed by CovidReplayMore Videos ...MUST WATCH'This was my final curtain bow': Athlete's Olympic dreams crushed by Covid 02:36The plan was that those participating in the Games would live isolated from the Japanese public, with athletes living in 21 residential buildings. However, the efficacy of that system is already in doubt. "It's obvious that the bubble system is kind of broken," Tokyo public health expert Dr. Kenji Shibuya said in an interview with Reuters. "There seems to be some sort of interaction between guests and visitors and also local people."The IOC and Tokyo 2020 organizers did not immediately respond to CNN's request for comment on Shibuya's remarks.Friday's opening ceremony is expected to draw a far smaller crowd than previous editions. The CEOs of several major sponsors, including Toyota and Procter & Gamble, will not attend. However, United States First Lady Jill Biden and French President Emmanuel Macron are both expected to be there. Brisbane set to get the nod The IOC is expected to approve on Wednesday its newest Olympic host city: Brisbane, Australia. Brisbane is expected to host the 2032 Olympics.Brisbane will likely be awarded the 2032 Summer Games, as it was the only candidate in the running after being formally approved to host the Games in June. The IOC expressed confidence in Brisbane's bid after the city on Australia's Gold Coast successfully hosted the 2018 Commonwealth Games.2032 will be the third time Australia hosts the Summer Games. The 1956 Games were held in Melbourne and the 2000 Summer Olympics were held in Sydney. The 2024 Summer Olympics will be held in Paris and the 2028 Games in Los Angeles.

(CNN) After years of anticipation and months of mounting uncertainty, the Paris 2024 Summer Olympics are set to open in spectacular fashion along the banks of the Seine — but the road to Friday's ceremony has been anything but smooth.
Competitive events began Tuesday with football and rugby sevens matches held across multiple cities, as is customary before the formal opening. But overshadowing the early action has been a wave of security concerns, logistical strain, and persistent questions about whether one of the world's most densely populated capitals can absorb the demands of hosting the planet's largest sporting event.
French authorities have deployed approximately 45,000 police officers and 10,000 military personnel across Paris in what officials are calling the most extensive security operation in the country's history. The unprecedented scale of Friday's opening ceremony — which will take place outdoors along a six-kilometer stretch of the Seine River rather than inside a stadium — has drawn particular scrutiny, with an estimated 300,000 ticket holders and up to 222 delegations of athletes expected to participate in the floating parade.
"The scale of what we are attempting has no precedent in Olympic history," Paris 2024 chief organizer Tony Estanguet acknowledged at a press conference on Monday. "There are risks. We are managing them seriously and with every resource available to us."
Those reassurances have not fully calmed nerves. Just days before the ceremony, French authorities arrested several individuals in connection with an alleged plot to disrupt the Games, prompting an emergency security review. The French interior ministry confirmed the arrests but declined to provide further details, citing the ongoing nature of the investigation.
Transport networks have also come under pressure. Paris's metro system, already operating near capacity during peak summer tourism season, has struggled to absorb the additional load of arriving athletes, journalists, and spectators. Long queues at Charles de Gaulle airport and widespread delays on regional train lines prompted complaints from delegations arriving over the weekend.
The athletes' village, located in Saint-Denis just north of the city, has itself become a talking point. Several national Olympic committees flagged concerns about the absence of air conditioning in residential units — an unusual omission for a summer Games — with organizers defending the decision on environmental grounds and pointing to distributed cooling units as an alternative. The United States, Britain, Canada, and several other delegations announced they had installed their own cooling systems at their own expense.
American swimmer Caeleb Dressel, who is bidding to add to his five gold medals from Tokyo, was diplomatic when asked. "It's warm," he said with a smile. "We've dealt with it."
Los Angeles looks ahead
With Paris finally underway, attention in Olympic circles has already begun to shift toward 2028, when the Games move to Los Angeles for the third time. The LA28 organizing committee is expected to present a progress report to the International Olympic Committee later this week, with officials describing the city's preparations as on schedule.
Los Angeles previously hosted the Games in 1932 and 1984. The 1984 edition, held during a Soviet-led boycott, is widely remembered as a financial and organizational success that helped rescue the Olympic movement from a period of crisis.
LA28 chairman Casey Wasserman sought to frame the 2028 Games as an opportunity to build on Paris's ambitions. "Every Games teaches the next host something new," he told reporters on the sidelines of a pre-opening reception. "We are watching, learning, and preparing."
The 2032 Summer Olympics are scheduled for Brisbane, Australia, which was confirmed as host city in 2021. It will be the third time Australia has hosted the Summer Games, following Melbourne in 1956 and Sydney in 2000.

(CNN)New York Mets first baseman Pete Alonso expressed his thankfulness to be alive after he said he survived a rollover vehicle accident in Tampa, Florida, on Sunday. Alonso told reporters Monday he was driving his pickup to Mets camp in Port St. Lucie when he was hit by another driver who ran a red light. Alonso, who set the major league home run record for rookies in 2019, said he was uninjured after his Ford pickup truck flipped over "probably three times.""This is a really special spring training because yesterday was a really close experience to death," Alonso said. "I'm just really thankful to be alive."I was coming here to work, coming to spring training, and the next thing I know I'm kicking my windshield in trying to get out of a flipped-over car," he said.Alonso said his wife, Haley, was following him in another vehicle when the crash happened. On Monday, Haley posted several videos and photos on her Instagram account of Alonso's mangled truck at the reported crash site.Pete Alonso's wife wife, Haley, shared this photo of the car crash on InstagramRead MoreThe Tampa Police Department told CNN the crash happened just before 6:30 p.m. and confirmed that another driver hit Alonso's truck after running a red light.There were only minor injuries reported and the other driver was issued a citation, Public Information Officer Jamel Lanee' said."Just really blessed to be here. Thankful nothing is wrong," Pete Alonso said. "Also thank you, Ford, for having great engineering."CNN's Jamiel Lynch and Kevin Dotson contributed to this report

(CNN) Los Angeles Dodgers outfielder Cody Bellinger said he was grateful to have walked away unharmed after a serious car accident on a highway outside Phoenix, Arizona, on Saturday, just days before the start of the team's spring training camp in Glendale.
Bellinger told reporters Sunday that he was driving to the Dodgers' training facility when a vehicle travelling at high speed crossed into his lane on Interstate 10 and sideswiped his SUV, sending it into the guardrail. Bellinger said his vehicle sustained significant damage to the driver's side and that he was briefly shaken before realizing he had escaped serious injury.
"It happened so fast," Bellinger said. "One second everything is normal, and then you're just trying to figure out what just happened. I'm really lucky."
The 2019 National League MVP said the incident had given him a fresh sense of perspective heading into the new season. "You get so caught up in the grind, in the preparation, that you forget to step back and appreciate that you're healthy and you're here," he said. "Yesterday reminded me of that pretty quickly."
Bellinger's agent confirmed that the outfielder had been examined by team medical staff on Sunday morning and was cleared of any injuries. He was present and took part in light workouts at the Dodgers' complex on Sunday afternoon, according to a team spokesperson.
The Arizona Department of Public Safety confirmed the incident and said the other driver had been cited for unsafe lane change. No other vehicles were involved and no injuries were reported.
Bellinger, who is entering his third season with the Dodgers after signing a multi-year contract extension last winter, is expected to be in the starting lineup when the team opens their Cactus League schedule next week.
"I called my family right after," Bellinger said. "That was the first thing I did. Just wanted them to know I was OK. Honestly, I'm just happy to be here talking to you guys."

(CNN)A six-year corruption investigation into bribery schemes in multiple world football organizations ended Tuesday. The US Department of Justice awarded $201 million to FIFA, international football's governing body; CONCACAF, the confederation overseeing football in North and Central America along with the Caribbean countries; CONMEBOL, the governing confederation in South America, and other affected entities. The DOJ determined the organizations had been victims of decades-long bribery schemes that have corrupted football and stunted the game's development."I am delighted to see that money which was illegally siphoned out of football is now coming back to be used for its proper purposes, as it should have been in the first place," said FIFA President Gianni Infantino. "The truth is that, thanks to (US Justice Department) intervention back in 2015, we have been able to fundamentally change FIFA from a toxic organisation at the time, to a highly esteemed and trusted global sports governing body."FIFA corruption probe targets 'World Cup of fraud,' IRS chief saysThe probe resulted in charges levied against more than 50 people and corporations from more than 20 countries, the DOJ said. The charges are "primarily in connection with the offer and receipt of bribes and kickbacks paid by sports marketing companies to soccer officials in exchange for the media and marketing rights to various soccer tournaments and events," the DOJ statement read. Read MoreAn indictment was initially unsealed in May 2015, the DOJ statement read, which charged 14 FIFA officials and sports marketing executives with racketeering, honest services wire fraud and money laundering offenses among others. An additional 16 FIFA officials were charged with similar crimes after an indictment was unsealed in December 2015, DOJ said. "Four corporate entities have pleaded guilty and others, including banking institutions, have acknowledged their roles in criminal conduct through deferred prosecution or non-prosecution agreements," the DOJ statement read, adding that the investigation is still ongoing. FIFA, CONCACAF, and CONMEBOL will distribute funds from the $201 million reward into a newly created World Football Remission Fund, which will be established under the FIFA Foundation "that is focused on youth programs, community outreach and humanitarian needs," DOJ said.CNN's Laura Smitherman contributed to this report.

(CNN) A sweeping seven-year investigation into corruption and financial misconduct across international athletics concluded Wednesday, as the US Department of Justice announced it would return $165 million to the World Athletics governing body, the International Association of Athletics Federations, and several national athletics federations identified as victims of a systemic doping-cover-up bribery scheme.
The DOJ determined that officials within multiple organizations had accepted payments from athletes and their coaches in exchange for suppressing positive doping test results, allowing competitors to continue competing at the highest levels of the sport — including at the Olympic Games — while concealing banned substance use from anti-doping authorities.
"This was a profound betrayal of clean athletes everywhere," said World Athletics President Sebastian Coe. "Money that was stolen from the integrity of our sport will now be returned to programs that protect it. This is a significant day, but it is also a reminder of how much damage was done and how much work remains."
The investigation, which began in 2016 following a series of whistleblower disclosures and an explosive report by an independent commission established by the World Anti-Doping Agency, resulted in charges against 38 individuals and four corporate entities across 15 countries. Among those charged were former senior officials of the Russian Athletics Federation, intermediaries operating in multiple jurisdictions, and representatives of several sports management agencies.
Charges included wire fraud, money laundering, obstruction of justice, and conspiracy to defraud anti-doping bodies. Several individuals have already pleaded guilty, while others are still awaiting trial. The DOJ said the investigation remained ongoing.
The recovered funds will be channeled into a newly established Clean Sport Development Fund, to be jointly administered by World Athletics and an independent oversight board. According to the DOJ, the fund will support anti-doping education initiatives, athlete welfare programs, and the development of athletics infrastructure in lower-income countries where governance oversight has historically been weakest.
"We are committed to making sure this money reaches the athletes and communities it was always meant to serve," Coe said.
The case drew comparisons to the 2015 FIFA corruption scandal, in which US federal prosecutors similarly used wire fraud and money laundering statutes to pursue misconduct in an international sports organization, given that much of the relevant financial activity passed through American banks.
CNN's Washington bureau contributed to this report.

(CNN) A seven-year federal investigation into corruption at the heart of international athletics ended Wednesday, with the US Department of Justice announcing it would return $165 million to World Athletics, the International Association of Athletics Federations, and several national federations identified as victims of a wide-ranging bribery scheme tied to doping cover-ups.
The DOJ concluded that officials across multiple organizations had accepted payments from athletes and coaches in exchange for burying positive drug test results — allowing competitors to perform at the Olympic level while concealing banned substance use from anti-doping authorities.
"This was a profound betrayal of clean athletes everywhere," said World Athletics President Sebastian Coe. "Money stolen from the integrity of our sport will now be returned to programs that protect it." He called it a significant day, but added that it was equally a reminder of how much damage had been done.
The investigation opened in 2016, spurred by whistleblower disclosures and a damning independent report commissioned by the World Anti-Doping Agency. By its conclusion, 38 individuals and four corporate entities across 15 countries had been charged. Former senior officials of the Russian Athletics Federation were among those named, alongside intermediaries operating across multiple jurisdictions and representatives of several sports management firms. Charges included wire fraud, money laundering, obstruction of justice, and conspiracy to defraud anti-doping bodies. Some defendants have already pleaded guilty. Others are still awaiting trial. The DOJ noted the investigation is continuing.
The recovered funds will go into a newly created Clean Sport Development Fund, jointly administered by World Athletics and an independent oversight board. The DOJ said the money would support anti-doping education, athlete welfare, and athletics infrastructure development in lower-income countries — places where governance has historically been thinnest.
The case drew natural comparisons to the 2015 FIFA scandal. In both instances, US prosecutors were able to assert jurisdiction because the financial transactions at the center of the schemes passed through American banks — a legal foothold that proved decisive.
CNN's Washington bureau contributed to this report.

London (CNN)Queen Elizabeth II will skip next week's key climate conference in Glasgow, according to a royal source. The news comes after the 95-year-old monarch was admitted to a hospital last week for "preliminary investigations." Buckingham Palace had announced earlier on Tuesday that the Queen would "regretfully" cancel a planned appearance at an evening reception at the COP26 summit and instead make a video address."Her Majesty is disappointed not to attend the Reception but will deliver an address to the assembled delegates via a recorded video message," the statement said. Earlier this month, the Queen was overheard saying the lack of action on tackling the climate crisis was "irritating."        View this post on Instagram            A post shared by The Royal Family (@theroyalfamily)
A source close to the palace told CNN at the time that her overnight stay in the hospital last week was "not Covid related." She returned to Windsor Castle "in good spirits" after one night, the palace said.Read MoreShe had canceled a trip to Northern Ireland last week following medical advice to rest more, but resumed "light duties" on Tuesday with two virtual audiences with Korean ambassador Gunn Kim, and Swiss ambassador Markus Leitner.Last week, she declined an honor of being named "Oldie of the Year" by a British magazine, saying she didn't meet "the relevant criteria." She "politely but firmly" declined the award through her assistant private secretary with "warmest best wishes."What we know about the Queen's health "Her Majesty believes you are as old as you feel, as such The Queen does not believe she meets the relevant criteria to be able to accept, and hopes you will find a more worthy recipient," Tom Laing-Baker said in a letter.Earlier this month, the Queen made the decision to use a walking stick in public for the first time "for comfort."While the Queen doesn't appear to be slowing down when it comes to her ceremonial responsibilities, she has since handed off long-haul tours to other family members. Still, she's still traveling extensively across the UK, with CNN's conservative estimates showing that the Queen traveled more than 1,000 kilometers (621 miles) from October 1 through to October 19.
