Rome (CNN) Pope Francis will not travel to Lisbon next week for the closing ceremony of World Youth Day, according to a Vatican spokesperson. The announcement follows a period of reduced public engagements for the 86-year-old pontiff, who has been managing ongoing knee and hip problems that have increasingly limited his mobility in recent months.
The Vatican had confirmed earlier on Monday that the Pope would "reluctantly" withdraw from the planned in-person appearance at the Parque Eduardo VII gathering, which is expected to draw hundreds of thousands of young Catholics from around the world. He would instead deliver a blessing and address via video link from the Apostolic Palace.
"The Holy Father deeply regrets that he is unable to be present in Lisbon for this historic occasion but looks forward to being with the faithful in spirit and through his message," the statement read.
Earlier this month, Francis had used particularly blunt language about the church's responsibility to young people during a general audience in St. Peter's Square, drawing applause from pilgrims when he said institutional "timidity" in the face of social injustice was "simply not acceptable."
A senior Vatican official confirmed to CNN that the decision to withdraw from Lisbon was made purely on medical advice and was unrelated to any acute health event. The Pope had been seen using a wheelchair at several recent public engagements, a visible sign of the mobility difficulties he has experienced since undergoing colon surgery in 2021.
Despite the travel cancellation, Francis has continued to carry out a full schedule of meetings and audiences at the Vatican. CNN's review of his public calendar shows he conducted more than fourteen formal engagements in the first three weeks of July alone, including audiences with several heads of state and a lengthy address to a symposium on environmental theology.
He declined to reduce that pace last week, presiding over his regular Wednesday general audience and meeting privately with a delegation of Ukrainian Catholic bishops, despite calls from some within the Curia for a more cautious approach to his schedule.
Last month, the Pope dismissed suggestions he was considering retirement, saying he had "no intention" of following the path taken by his predecessor Benedict XVI. "When the Lord wants me to stop, he will let me know," he told a group of Jesuit journalists in a wide-ranging interview.
Still, the decision not to make the journey to Lisbon — a trip he had publicly expressed enthusiasm for as recently as last month — is the latest in a series of adjustments to his public role. Long-haul foreign travel has become increasingly rare, with shorter regional trips and domestic engagements now making up the bulk of his schedule outside of the Vatican.

Rome (CNN) Pope Francis will not travel to Lisbon for the closing ceremony of World Youth Day, the Vatican confirmed Monday — the latest in a series of adjustments to the 86-year-old pontiff's public schedule as ongoing knee and hip problems continue to limit his mobility.
The announcement came after weeks of reduced appearances. Rather than attending in person at the Parque Eduardo VII gathering, which is expected to draw hundreds of thousands of young Catholics, Francis will deliver his address and blessing via video link from the Apostolic Palace.
"The Holy Father deeply regrets that he is unable to be present in Lisbon for this historic occasion but looks forward to being with the faithful in spirit and through his message," a Vatican statement said.
The decision was made on medical advice, a senior official told CNN, and was not connected to any new or acute health event. Francis has been seen in a wheelchair at several recent public engagements — a visible sign of the difficulties he has managed since undergoing colon surgery in 2021.
None of that has slowed his pace at the Vatican itself. CNN's review of his public calendar shows more than fourteen formal engagements in the first three weeks of July alone, including audiences with multiple heads of state and a lengthy address to a symposium on environmental theology. He presided over his regular Wednesday general audience last week and met privately with a delegation of Ukrainian Catholic bishops, even as some within the Curia have quietly urged a more cautious schedule.
Earlier this month he drew extended applause from pilgrims in St. Peter's Square after saying that institutional "timidity" in the face of social injustice was "simply not acceptable" — language noticeably sharper than his usual register on the subject.
Last month, Francis dismissed retirement talk outright. "When the Lord wants me to stop, he will let me know," he told a group of Jesuit journalists in a wide-ranging interview — a pointed reference to his predecessor Benedict XVI, who stepped down in 2013.
Still, not making the trip to Lisbon is a notable absence for a pope who had spoken openly about wanting to be there. Long-haul foreign travel has grown increasingly rare. What was once a signature feature of his papacy — stadium masses on multiple continents, exhausting multi-country tours — has gradually given way to shorter domestic engagements and meetings that do not require him to leave Rome.

(CNN)"Man, it's a saying that time heals all," LeBron James said following the Los Angeles Lakers' win over Chicago on Saturday.Still, one year on from Kobe Bryant's sudden death, those inside the Lakers organization still acutely feel his absence. While enough time still hasn't passed to allow James to move on completely, remembering Bryant and honoring the competitive spirit that made him such a fierce competitor has helped his grieving process.Several times during last season's victorious playoff and NBA Finals run, the Lakers wore their 'Black Mamba' jerseys to commemorate Bryant and his daughter Gianna, who died in a helicopter crash alongside seven others on January 26 last year.The jerseys were designed by Bryant -- who gave himself the 'Black Mamba' nickname during his career -- and worn for the first time in the 2018 season. The Lakers would go on to win their first Larry O'Brien Trophy since Bryant's last title in 2010.After the conclusion to last season was played in the Disney World bubble, the Lakers are back playing their home games at Staples Center where the two jersey numbers -- eight and 24 -- that Bryant wore during his 20-year Laker career are hanging in the rafters.Read More"As devastating and as tragic as it was -- and still is -- to all of us involved with it, only time. And it takes time. Everyone has their own grieving process," James said."Us being able to remember him, wearing [Mamba] jerseys during the postseason and have the postseason we had ... we have a lot of guys wearing his shoes, I'm able to wear the two-four on my finger every night and then when we play at Staples Center you see the eight, two-four in the rafters to live his legacy on."It's a lot of things that die in this world, but legends never die, and he's exactly that. So it's all about representing that." READ: He was born to play basketball, but for Kobe Bryant that was never enoughREAD: Kobe Bryant -- The 24 moments that made a global superstarJUST WATCHEDItalian town mourns death of 'son' Kobe BryantReplayMore Videos ...MUST WATCHItalian town mourns death of 'son' Kobe Bryant 02:47Bryant was James' boyhood idol and the pair went on to become close friends. James naturally struggled following Bryant's death and admitted it was a mental challenge being the leader of the Lakers organization during that time."I try not to put myself back in that head space because it's just too dark, for not only myself but for our organization and for everyone that's involved in it," he recalled."As a leader of the ballclub, it was my job and my responsibility to take it all on and represent our team with the most strength that I could. I wanted to let everyone inside this organization know that I was okay doing that."'I still have trouble'Anthony Davis, the Lakers co-captain alongside James, admitted that he still struggles to come to terms with Bryant's death.The 6-foot-10 big man has helped James shoulder much of the responsibility that comes with being an organization's leading figure -- both on and off the court -- including leading the team to win last season's title in Bryant's memory."As we approach his one-year anniversary, it saddens our hearts to actually come to the realization that he's gone," Davis said. "I know I still have trouble with it, you still just can't believe it, especially when you were really close to him."Davis' dominant performances in that victorious postseason elevated him to another plane and established his place among the league's best players. JUST WATCHEDVanessa Bryant gives tearful tribute to late Kobe and GigiReplayMore Videos ...MUST WATCHVanessa Bryant gives tearful tribute to late Kobe and Gigi 01:54Part of what drove him to find another gear was the determination to win the title as a dedication to Bryant. Davis said the entire team was immediately aware that a championship would take on a greater significance in the wake of Bryant's death."It was January 26 of last year when it happened. From that point on, we were like: 'We have a purpose,'" he recalled. "To this day, 'Mamba on three!' anytime we bring it in because we still want to recognize that he's a part of our organization. And ever since the tragedy happened, we had a mindset that this is bigger than, you know, ourselves.""When the tragedy happened, it was more so, you know, 'Let's do it for him' and that's what we ended up doing all last year. Even when we play now, it's in the back of our mind that we are still playing for Kobe, his family and to help the city of LA, and I think we brought a ton of joy to our city when we won."We know we fought to the end for a purpose, and it wasn't just for ourselves. It was for the Bryant family and we were able to get that accomplished."On the court, Bryant and Pau Gasol formed a devastating partnership and would win two NBA championships. Off it, they became brothers. Gasol and his family have spent a lot of time with Bryant's wife Vanessa and their three girls since his death.JUST WATCHEDMichael Jordan: When Kobe died, a piece of me diedReplayMore Videos ...MUST WATCHMichael Jordan: When Kobe died, a piece of me died 00:47Pau's younger brother, Marc, joined the Lakers during the offseason and hopes to emulate his sibling's achievements with the franchise. He still struggles to open up on the incident that took Bryant's life, but says his presence is keenly felt within the organization and is still used as inspiration to achieve the team's goals this season."I'm not comfortable talking about it. I'm sorry. Still to this day, I have never really talked about it," Marc said."He will be a part of not only the team, the franchise, the city ... it's very emotional ... he's obviously someone that we looked up to."

(CNN) "There are days when it still doesn't feel real," Dwyane Wade said in a quiet moment after a charity event in Miami last weekend, marking the one-year anniversary of the death of his closest friend in basketball.
One year after the passing of David Stern — the NBA commissioner who transformed the league into a global phenomenon — those whose careers he shaped most directly are still processing a loss that cut deeper than professional admiration. For Wade, and for many others across the league, honoring Stern's legacy has become part of how they carry him forward.
Stern died on January 1, 2020, following a sudden brain hemorrhage. He was 77. Over 30 years as commissioner, he oversaw the NBA's expansion from a domestically struggling league into one of the most recognized sports properties in the world, building platforms for the very players who now mourn him.
"He gave so many of us a stage," Wade said. "Not just to play, but to be who we are. To have opinions, to have a voice. That wasn't always the case and he changed it."
LeBron James, who came of age in the league Stern built and clashed with the commissioner on more than one occasion, spoke about him with unmistakable warmth. "We didn't always see eye to eye and I think he respected that, and I respected him for it," James said. "He was tough, he was honest, and he cared about this league more than anybody."
'He saw the whole board'
Current NBA Commissioner Adam Silver, who served as Stern's deputy for years before succeeding him in 2014, said the past year had only deepened his appreciation for what his predecessor built — and for how much of what the league takes for granted today traces back to decisions Stern made decades ago.
"David had this ability to see around corners," Silver said at a memorial event held in New York last week. "He saw the global game before anyone else did. He saw the business of basketball becoming something that could rival any entertainment property in the world. And then he went and built it."
Silver said he still finds himself reaching for the phone to call Stern when a major decision confronts the league — a reflex that hasn't faded. "I'll probably never fully stop doing that," he admitted.
Among the players who knew Stern personally, the tributes have been consistent in one theme: he treated them as partners, even when the relationship was combative. Chris Paul, president of the National Basketball Players Association, said Stern's willingness to engage directly with players — sometimes fiercely — ultimately made the league stronger.
"He pushed back. Hard, sometimes. But he also listened," Paul said. "That's rarer than people think in this business."
The NBA held a moment of silence before games on New Year's Day to mark the anniversary. Several teams wore commemorative patches on their jerseys, and a tribute video produced by the league's entertainment division aired during halftime of the nationally televised game.
For Silver, the anniversary was a moment to reflect not just on what Stern achieved, but on the standard he set. "Every decision I make, I ask myself whether it moves the game forward," Silver said. "That's what he taught me. That's what he was."

(CNN)The Texas Rangers could become the first major US sports team to play before a full house since the coronavirus pandemic began shutting stadiums and arenas a year ago this week.The Major League Baseball club announced Wednesday that Globe Life Field will be "fully open" to its 40,518 capacity for the Rangers' April 5 home opener against the Toronto Blue Jays, according to Rangers spokesman John Blake. "The Rangers are encouraged that the Governor's Office has given clearance for us to fully open Globe Life Field at the start of the 2021 Major League Baseball season," Neil Leibman, the team's president of business operations and Chief Operating Officer, said in a statement. 'It's too soon for it': Small business owners react to Texas dropping mask mandateThe announcement came on the day that Texas Gov. Greg Abbott allowed businesses in the state to reopen 100% and lifted the mask wearing mandate -- even as health officials warned against the loosening of pandemic restrictions. After April 5, the team will host games at slightly reduced capacity with socially distanced seating sections. Blake said the total number of tickets sold "will depend on demand" and the team is "currently working through this" prior to single tickets going on sale. Read MoreStill, fans at the games will be required to wear masks except when they are "actively eating or drinking at their ticketed seats," according to the team. The team hosted its first regular season game at the newly constructed, $1.2 billion ballpark, on July 24, 2020, with no fans in attendance. The pandemic forced MLB to hold 2020 postseason games at four neutral site stadiums in California and Texas -- including Globe Life Field.A limited number of fans were allowed at games at Globe Life Field. The home of the Rangers hosted the World Series in October -- the first neutral site World Series since the 1940s. On October 27, 11,437 spectators witnessed the Los Angeles Dodgers clinch the title over the Tampa Bay Rays.More schools are preparing to open as coronavirus cases drop and vaccinations riseThe Rangers have yet to host a full crowd since the ballpark's opening.Abbott announced 100% reopening last week since active Covid-19 cases and hospitalizations were down to levels not seen in months."Too many Texans have been sidelined from employment opportunities. Too many small business owners have struggled to pay their bills. This must end. It is now time to open Texas 100%," he said.Critics sounded the alarm, saying only a small percentage of the state's population had been vaccinated -- less than 7% at the time -- and aggressively spreading variants of the virus could lead to another explosion of cases.And several of the states' largest cities -- including the capital, Austin -- as well as counties and businesses have said they will still require masks when Abbott's mandate is lifted. On Wednesday, 8.85% of the state population has been fully vaccinated against Covid-19, according to the Johns Hopkins University's Coronavirus Resource Center.Harris County Judge Lina Hidalgo on Wednesday urged residents to continue to follow safety guidelines despite Abbott's order, saying "now is not the time to give up."

(CNN) The Las Vegas Raiders could become the first NFL team to welcome a full stadium crowd since the pandemic emptied arenas across the country more than a year ago, after Nevada officials signaled they would allow Allegiant Stadium to operate at full capacity beginning with the team's preseason home opener.
The franchise announced Thursday that Allegiant Stadium would be open to its 65,000-seat capacity for the Raiders' first preseason game against the San Francisco 49ers on August 14, pending final confirmation from the Nevada Department of Health. Raiders President Marc Badain said the organization was "thrilled" at the prospect.
"We built one of the most spectacular stadiums in professional sports in large part to give our fans an experience unlike any other," Badain said in a statement. "To finally be able to welcome all of them through the doors is something we have been working toward since the day this building opened."
The announcement coincided with Nevada Governor Steve Sisolak's decision to lift most remaining capacity restrictions on large indoor venues across the state, citing a sustained decline in Covid-19 hospitalizations and an accelerating vaccination rollout. As of Thursday, approximately 39% of Nevada's adult population had been fully vaccinated, according to state health department figures.
Some health officials expressed caution. Dr. Fermin Leguen of the Southern Nevada Health District acknowledged the improving picture but noted that full indoor stadiums presented a different challenge than outdoor venues. "We are encouraged by the trends," he said at a briefing Thursday. "We also ask that residents continue to exercise personal responsibility."
Fans attending the game will still be encouraged, though not required, to wear masks inside the stadium once Nevada's indoor mask mandate expires on June 1. The team said it would maintain enhanced cleaning protocols and increase ventilation throughout the building.
The Raiders' situation is complicated by the franchise's unusual circumstances. Allegiant Stadium, a $1.9 billion domed facility, opened in July 2020 — just as the pandemic was forcing sports leagues to play without spectators. The Raiders played their entire inaugural season in Las Vegas without a single fan in the building, a deeply frustrating experience for a franchise that had relocated from Oakland specifically to build a new identity and fanbase in the Nevada desert.
A limited number of fans were permitted to attend selected home games during the 2020 season at other NFL venues, but the Raiders were among the teams that played before empty seats for the entire year.
The NFL is expected to confirm full stadium attendance for all 32 teams when the 2021 regular season begins in September, though individual state and local regulations may affect some franchises.
Las Vegas Mayor Carolyn Goodman welcomed the announcement, calling it a significant step in the city's economic recovery. "This city runs on hospitality, on spectacle, on bringing people together," she said. "Having a full house at Allegiant Stadium is exactly the signal Las Vegas needs to send to the world right now."
Not all voices were in agreement. Several epidemiologists cautioned that the Delta variant, which had begun emerging in other parts of the world, posed a potential threat to the reopening timeline. "We are in a better place than we were six months ago," said Dr. Brian Labus of the University of Nevada Las Vegas School of Public Health. "That does not mean the pandemic is over."
As of Thursday, 42,000 tickets for the preseason opener had already been sold. Team officials said the remaining inventory would go on sale by the end of the week.

(CNN) The Las Vegas Raiders may be about to do something no NFL team has managed in over a year: fill a stadium.
Nevada officials signaled Thursday they would allow Allegiant Stadium to operate at full capacity starting with the Raiders' preseason home opener against the San Francisco 49ers on August 14, pending final sign-off from the state health department. Raiders President Marc Badain didn't hide his enthusiasm.
"We built one of the most spectacular stadiums in professional sports to give our fans an experience unlike any other," he said in a statement. "To finally be able to welcome all of them through the doors is something we have been working toward since the day this building opened."
The announcement came alongside Nevada Governor Steve Sisolak's decision to lift most remaining capacity restrictions on large indoor venues, citing falling Covid-19 hospitalizations and a faster-than-expected vaccine rollout. About 39% of the state's adult population had been fully vaccinated as of Thursday, according to state health figures.
Not everyone is ready to declare victory. Dr. Fermin Leguen of the Southern Nevada Health District called the trend encouraging while urging caution about fully indoor venues. "We also ask that residents continue to exercise personal responsibility," he said at a Thursday briefing.
Masks will be encouraged but not required once Nevada's indoor mandate expires on June 1. The team said it would maintain enhanced cleaning protocols and boost ventilation throughout the building.
The full-crowd moment carries a particular weight for this franchise. Allegiant Stadium — a $1.9 billion domed facility the Raiders moved from Oakland specifically to inhabit — opened in July 2020, just as sports leagues were shutting fans out entirely. Las Vegas played its entire first season in Nevada in an empty building. No fans, no atmosphere, no payoff for the relocation that was supposed to give the franchise a fresh start.
The NFL is widely expected to allow all 32 teams to play before full crowds when the regular season opens in September, though local regulations could complicate things for some franchises.
Las Vegas Mayor Carolyn Goodman was unambiguous about what a full Allegiant Stadium means. "This city runs on hospitality, on spectacle, on bringing people together," she said. "Having a full house is exactly the signal Las Vegas needs to send to the world right now."
A few voices pushed back. Dr. Brian Labus of the UNLV School of Public Health pointed to the emerging Delta variant as a reason for humility. "We are in a better place than we were six months ago," he said. "That does not mean the pandemic is over."
By Thursday evening, 42,000 of the stadium's 65,000 tickets had already been sold. The remaining inventory, the team said, would go on sale before the end of the week.

(CNN)Before Proud Boys member Nick Ochs was arrested and charged in connection with the insurrection at the US Capitol, he raised $300 on the Christian-oriented website GiveSendGo.com  to help get him to DC.Following Ochs' arrest, a supporter turned to GiveSendGo to raise money for his legal defense, a campaign that has garnered nearly $20,000 for the member of the right-wing extremist group. The campaigns for Ochs weren't the only controversial causes on the site. A CNN review found more than two dozen fundraisers related to protesting the outcome of the presidential election, raising travel funds to attend the January 6 protest in Washington and other right-wing causes.Among the campaigns and their beneficiaries: Ali Alexander, a Stop the Steal organizer who raised money for a "security and administrative team." As of today, he's reached 75% of his $40,000 goal on the platform. Friends and family of Enrique Tarrio, the leader of the Proud Boys, have raised more than $113,000 for his legal defense on GiveSendGo.com. Tarrio was arrested two days before the insurrection at the Capitol and charged with destruction of property for burning a Black Lives Matter banner after a protest in December and with possessing high-capacity firearm magazines. Jim Hoft, founder of the conservative news outlet Gateway Pundit, is currently the beneficiary of two campaigns on GiveSendGo.com, totaling more than $135,000, intended to fund an investigation into alleged voter fraud in Michigan and to "take on the tech giant censorship of conservative voices."At least five other campaigns that collectively raised nearly $200,000 are tied to self-described Proud Boys members looking for funds for "protective gear," travel expenses to the January protest in Washington, DC, and medical costs after a December rally in the capital turned violent.   Requests for comment from Alexander, Tarrio and Hoft went unanswered. Read MoreFormer Trump campaign staffers worked on National Mall rally the day of the Capitol riotThough the money raised through GiveSendGo for extremists and other controversial causes "get a lot of limelight," the company's co-founder said the site hosts "thousands" of other campaigns that are in no way contentious.Still, the site has emerged as an alternative fundraiser for those who have been kicked off -- or shunned by -- larger crowdfunding platforms, such as GoFundMe. Jacob Wells, the co-founder of GiveSendGo, told CNN just because a cause may be unpopular it doesn't mean a person shouldn't have an opportunity to raise money from like-minded supporters. He said the decision at GiveSendGo about whether to allow someone to raise funds on the site is focused on the two-pronged question of whether it's legal and, if so, whether there's anything in its stated goal that's "derogatory to anybody."GiveSendGo's terms and conditions prohibit any "abusive or hateful language" on its platform, as well as campaigns for "items that promote hate, violence and racial intolerance." How Trump allies stoked the flames ahead of Capitol riot "We're not here to take sides," Wells said in an interview with CNN. "We don't necessarily condone on our platform a campaign any more than when you tweet on Twitter that Twitter somehow agrees with you," Wells said.Wells, who said he is a "Jesus guy" as opposed to someone motivated by politics, likened the decision to host campaigns for groups or people that some may find unsavory to a missionary looking to save souls in a brothel."As best we can, we're going to represent the hope of Jesus in every situation to people who use our platform," Wells said. "We're going to cover it all with grace."Critics, however, say GiveSendGo and others in the tech industry enable extremists by refusing to take a firm stand against them.The Proud Boys and other groups like them need "an architecture through which they can meet, recruit and finance themselves," said Imran Ahmed, CEO of the Center for Countering Digital Hate."Big tech has not just made it easy," Ahmed added. "It has turned a blind eye to extremist activities." Michael Hayden, a spokesman for the Southern Poverty Law Center, which tracks alleged hate groups, offered this blunt take:"We want to limit the capacity of these groups being able to get money in any way," he said. "Repeatedly, we've seen if the money is not there, the presence will not be there either."














We offer several ways to reach our journalists securely.



Hayden brushed aside extremist groups' claims that their fundraising is protected free speech as a "slanted and corrupted" interpretation used to "vilify those who seek to limit their power."Not every company's view on who can raise money is as charitable as GiveSendGo's.PayPal, for example, said it no longer provides services to GiveSendGo, citing unspecified violations of PayPal's Acceptable Use Policy. But Wells told CNN it was GiveSendGo that cut ties with PayPal rather than adhere to its demand that the company not cater to certain clients."This is really coming down ideological lines," Wells said. "We're not just going to cut off one half of the population's ability to espouse their ideas or desires because the other side yells at us to do it."CNN did not independently authenticate all the campaigns or verify whether the funds reached their designated recipients. In many of the controversial cases, the campaigns remained on the GiveSendGo website but the ability to donate had been suspended. Wells said that action was not taken by GiveSendGo; rather, he said, it was the result of third-party vendors in charge of processing payments or the individual administrating the account. Not all the contributions on GiveSendGo are monetary. For folks inclined to give spiritual support, rather than financial, there is "pray now" button. The campaign for Help Storm the Captial (sic) Patriots Legal Defense, for example, got $10 from one anonymous donor, but received 12 prayers. Many of the fundraising campaigns GiveSendGo supports are of the sort Wells envisioned when he co-founded the giving platform with his sister seven years ago: A scholarship in the name of a beloved coach. A child in need of surgery. Five hundred dollars for "cat supplies" for a disabled couple caring for 14 rescued felines. The site exploded in popularity among the right over the summer when it began hosting a fundraiser for the legal defense of Kyle Rittenhouse, the Illinois teen charged with killing two people amid the unrest following the police shooting of Jacob Blake in Kenosha, Wisconsin. As first reported by the Daily Beast, GiveSendGo allowed the Rittenhouse fundraiser after it was banned by GoFundMe. Rittenhouse claimed he was there to protect people and property from rioters and opened fire in self-defense. Prosecutors charged the 17-year-old with two felony charges of homicide.While the case had a polarizing effect, Wells told CNN, "In my opinion, the media tried to paint a very one-sided story about what happened."Since launching the GiveSendGo fundraising effort, Rittenhouse's supporters have exceeded their stated goal of half a million dollars, raising $585,940 from more than 13,000 donors, according to the website. GiveSendGo has also provided a platform for a number of police officers involved in controversial uses of force, Wells said, including the police officer charged with killing George Floyd, an officer who pulled the trigger in the raid on Breonna Taylor's apartment, and the Wisconsin officer who shot Jacob Blake. Authorities have since declined to file criminal charges against any officers for Taylor's death or for the shooting of Blake. The officer charged with Floyd's killing is awaiting trial.Wells said GiveSendGo's growing popularity has coincided with improved financial performance. Once "a labor of love" that made little money, he said he expects campaigns on the site to bring in $20 to $30 million in 2021, from which the company takes a cut of at least 3%.Along with its increased popularity, the site has drawn additional scrutiny from the media, including in a recent article by The Washington Post about fundraising related to the Proud Boys and other controversial causes.Eugene Volokh, a UCLA law professor who specializes in First Amendment issues, said there was nothing illegal -- or, in his view, immoral -- about GiveSendGo's business."Whatever you might think about Kyle Rittenhouse, I hope we can agree that he is entitled to a criminal defense," Volokh said. "If people want to help hire lawyers, in part because of their ideological views, that's something that is constitutionally protected."Ochs, the Proud Boys member, charged in connection with the siege on the Capitol, did not return a phone call seeking comment for this article. He previously told CNN that he was working as a professional journalist when he entered the building, and that he didn't go into any congressional offices or the chambers."We didn't have to break in, I just walked in and filmed," he said hours after the siege. CJ Grisham, a Proud Boys member from Texas, who raised funds on GiveSendGo for tactical gear so the group can be "ready at a moment's notice to protect our communities," said the Proud Boys should be entitled to raise funds just like anyone else. He said his fundraising campaign brought in nearly $5,000 for a cause he explicitly stated was nonviolent, but that he has so far been denied any money because a third-party vendor that processes payments for GiveSendGo decided his campaign was "too risky.""Who gets to decide what a good cause is?" Grisham asked. "Are we gonna let big tech decide what a good cause is?"

A crowdfunding platform that promotes itself as a champion of free expression has become a fundraising hub for a range of controversial political figures and activist groups, drawing criticism from watchdog organizations and renewed scrutiny from payment providers.
An examination of campaigns hosted on the platform found dozens of fundraisers connected to politically charged causes, including legal defense efforts, travel expenses for demonstrations, and investigations into disputed election claims. Several campaigns have collectively raised hundreds of thousands of dollars from supporters across the country.
Among the beneficiaries are activists facing criminal charges, organizers of large-scale political rallies, and media personalities who claim they have been unfairly targeted by mainstream institutions. Supporters argue that these individuals deserve access to financial assistance regardless of their political views, while critics contend that such fundraising can strengthen extremist networks and enable harmful activities.
The platform's leadership maintains that its role is not to judge the popularity of a cause but to determine whether it complies with the law and the company's policies. According to company executives, campaigns are generally permitted as long as they do not explicitly advocate violence, discrimination, or illegal conduct.
"We believe people should have the opportunity to support causes they care about," one executive said in a recent interview. "Our responsibility is to provide a service, not to act as an ideological gatekeeper."
That position has sparked debate among advocacy groups that monitor online extremism. Some researchers argue that fundraising infrastructure is a critical component of political movements, particularly those operating on the fringes of public discourse. Restricting access to financial resources, they say, can reduce the reach and influence of organizations associated with violence or hate.
Others view the issue through the lens of free expression. Legal scholars note that raising money for legal representation, public advocacy, or political organizing is often protected under constitutional principles, even when the underlying cause is controversial.
The company has also faced pressure from financial partners. Several payment processors have reportedly suspended services for certain campaigns or declined to handle transactions associated with higher-risk activities. In some cases, fundraising pages remain visible online even though donations can no longer be accepted.
Despite the controversy, the platform continues to grow. Originally established to support charitable and community-based projects, it now hosts campaigns ranging from medical expenses and disaster relief to political activism and legal defense funds. Company leaders say increasing traffic and donations have significantly expanded the platform's reach in recent years.
The debate surrounding the site reflects a broader question facing the technology industry: should digital platforms remain neutral hosts for lawful activity, or should they take a more active role in determining which causes deserve access to their services? As political polarization continues to shape public life, that question is unlikely to disappear anytime soon.

A crowdfunding website that presents itself as a defender of open participation has found itself at the center of another debate over online fundraising and political activism.
A review of campaigns on the platform shows that a number of politically contentious figures and groups have used it to collect money for legal bills, travel costs, public events, and other projects. Some of those campaigns have attracted substantial support, bringing in tens or even hundreds of thousands of dollars.
Supporters of the platform argue that access to fundraising tools should not depend on whether a person's views are widely accepted. Many of the campaigns involve activists, commentators, or organizers who say they have been marginalized by larger institutions or removed from other online services.
Critics see the situation differently. Organizations that track extremist activity argue that money is often what allows controversial movements to expand, organize events, and maintain a public presence. In their view, platforms that provide fundraising opportunities cannot completely separate themselves from the causes they host.
Company representatives reject that criticism. They say their decisions are based primarily on legality and compliance with site policies, not on political ideology. If a campaign does not promote unlawful behavior or explicitly target people with hateful content, it is generally allowed to remain online.
The company's approach has not insulated it from outside pressure. Payment providers and financial partners have occasionally restricted transactions linked to certain campaigns, leaving some fundraising pages active but unable to receive donations.
The controversy highlights a larger disagreement that extends well beyond a single website. Technology companies increasingly face demands to decide where the line should be drawn between open access and social responsibility. Some believe platforms should remove controversial causes before they gain momentum. Others worry that giving private companies that power could create a different set of problems.
Meanwhile, the platform continues to expand beyond politics. Alongside the headline-grabbing campaigns are far more ordinary fundraising efforts: families covering medical expenses, local communities supporting disaster victims, and individuals seeking help during difficult times.
As debates over speech, accountability, and online platforms continue, services like this one are likely to remain under close public scrutiny. Whether they are viewed as neutral tools or active participants in political life may depend largely on who is looking.

(CNN)There's a new voice coming to the daytime talk show scene.Warner Bros. (which is owned by CNN's parent company) announced Wednesday that Academy Award and Grammy-winning artist Jennifer Hudson will helm a talk show that will air on Fox television stations."I have experienced so much in my life; I've seen the highest of the highs, the lowest of the lows, and just about everything in between but as my mother always told me, 'Once you think you've seen it all, just keep on living,'" Hudson said in a statement. "People from around the world have been a part of my journey from the beginning -- twenty years ago -- and I'm so ready to join their journey as we sit down and talk about the things that inspire and move us all," Hudson added. "I have always loved people and I cannot wait to connect on a deeper level and let audiences see the different sides of who I am, the human being, in return. And I couldn't be more thrilled to do it alongside this incredible team. We're about to have a lot of fun and shake things up a little bit!"Hudson first came to fame as a contestant on "American Idol" before releasing multiple albums and starring as Effie White in the 2006 film "Dreamgirls," a big-screen adaptation of the hit Tony-winning Broadway musical.Read MoreHudson won a best supporting actress Oscar for the role.She suffered a great tragedy after her mother, brother and young nephew were murdered by her sister's estranged husband in 2008. Hudson most recently starred as Aretha Franklin in the film "Respect." The one-hour talk show will debut in the fall.

(CNN) Another familiar face is making the move into daytime television.
NBCUniversal announced Tuesday that Emmy-winning actress and producer Viola Davis will host a new daytime talk show set to air across several major network affiliates next year.
"I've been fortunate to experience so many different chapters in my life and career," Davis said in a statement. "Each one has taught me something valuable about resilience, purpose, and the importance of human connection. I look forward to creating a space where people can share their stories and where we can learn from one another."
Davis added that she hopes the program will highlight meaningful conversations and celebrate people from all walks of life. "I've always been fascinated by what drives people, what challenges them, and what inspires them to keep moving forward. This show is an opportunity to explore those stories and connect with audiences in a new way."
Davis first gained widespread recognition for her acclaimed stage performances before establishing herself as one of Hollywood's most respected actors. She earned an Academy Award for her role in the 2016 film "Fences" and has also received multiple Emmy and Tony Awards during her career.
Read More
Throughout her professional journey, Davis has often spoken publicly about overcoming poverty and adversity during her childhood. More recently, she appeared in the historical action film "The Woman King," which received praise from both critics and audiences.
The hour-long talk show is expected to premiere in the spring.

(CNN) Daytime television is getting another high-profile addition.
NBCUniversal said Tuesday that actress and producer Viola Davis will be launching her own daytime talk show, which is scheduled to air on a group of network-affiliated stations next year.
In a statement announcing the project, Davis reflected on the experiences that have shaped her career and personal life.
"I've been fortunate to live through many different chapters," she said. "Those experiences have taught me about perseverance, purpose, and the value of connecting with other people. I'm excited to bring those conversations to a wider audience."
She said the show will focus on stories that reveal what motivates people and how they navigate life's challenges. Davis also noted that she is eager to hear from guests with a wide range of backgrounds and perspectives.
Davis first drew attention for her work in theater before becoming one of the most celebrated performers in film and television. Her portrayal in the 2016 movie "Fences" earned her an Academy Award, adding to a collection of honors that already included Emmy and Tony recognition.
Read More
The actress has frequently discussed the hardships she faced growing up, including periods of financial struggle. In recent years, she starred in "The Woman King," a historical drama that was well received by critics and moviegoers alike.
The new talk show will run for one hour and is expected to debut in the spring.

(CNN)The Alabama Crimson Tide, Michigan Wolverines, Georgia Bulldogs and the Cincinnati Bearcats will compete in the College Football Playoff, the selection committee announced on Sunday.In the semifinal games scheduled for December 31, No. 1 Alabama (12-1) will face off against No. 4 Cincinnati (13-0) at the Cotton Bowl Classic at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas, while No. 2 Michigan (12-1) will play No. 3 Georgia (12-1) at the Orange Bowl at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens, Florida. The winners will square off in the national championship game at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis, Indiana, on January 10 (8 p.m. ET).Cincinnati, a member of the American Athletic Conference, is the first school from a non-Power Five conference to play in the CFP since its inception in 2014. The Power Five conferences consist of the Atlantic Coast Conference, the Big 12 Conference, the Big Ten Conference, the Pac-12 Conference and the Southeastern Conference.Alabama is making a CFP-best seventh appearance after winning the SEC Championship Game 41-24 over Georgia on Saturday. Under coach Nick Saban, the top-ranked Crimson Tide rolled past Ohio State last year in the national title game, earning Alabama its sixth title in 12 seasons.Read MoreMichigan dominated Iowa 42-3 to win the Big 10 championship on Saturday, the first for coach Jim Harbaugh. It will be playing in its first playoff.Georgia lost its final game but had been the top team for most of the season and will be playing in its second playoffs.The fifth-ranked team heading into the bowl season is the Notre Dame Fighting Irish (11-1). Notre Dame head coach Marcus Freemen will make his coaching debut at the team's upcoming bowl game. Freeman replaced Brian Kelly, who left the school to become head coach at Louisiana State University earlier this week.Ohio State Buckeyes (10-2) finished at No. 6.

(CNN) The college football postseason field is officially set, with the selection committee announcing the four teams that will compete for the national championship on Sunday.
The No. 1-ranked Texas Longhorns (12-1), No. 2 Oregon Ducks (12-1), No. 3 Florida State Seminoles (13-0) and No. 4 Washington Huskies (12-1) secured spots in this year's College Football Playoff.
The semifinal matchups, scheduled for New Year's Day, will feature top-seeded Texas facing fourth-seeded Washington in the Sugar Bowl at Caesars Superdome in New Orleans, Louisiana. Meanwhile, Oregon will take on Florida State in the Rose Bowl at the Rose Bowl Stadium in Pasadena, California.
The winners of those contests will advance to the national championship game, which is set to be played later in January at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas.
Florida State's inclusion marks a significant achievement for the program, which completed an undefeated regular season and captured its conference championship. The Seminoles relied on a balanced roster and strong defensive performances throughout the year to remain among the nation's elite teams.
Texas earned the top seed after winning its conference title game and recording several victories over ranked opponents. Under head coach Steve Sarkisian, the Longhorns have returned to national prominence following several seasons outside the championship conversation.
Oregon enters the playoff after a dominant campaign that featured one of the country's highest-scoring offenses. The Ducks secured key victories during conference play and finished the season with momentum following a convincing championship-game performance.
Washington claimed the final playoff position after compiling a strong résumé highlighted by multiple wins against ranked competition. The Huskies' passing attack played a central role in their success throughout the season.
Just outside the playoff field, the fifth-ranked Penn State Nittany Lions (11-2) narrowly missed selection despite another successful year. The team is expected to receive an invitation to a major bowl game and finish the season against a highly ranked opponent.
The sixth-ranked LSU Tigers (10-3) also remained in contention until the final rankings were released. LSU's explosive offense helped keep the Tigers in the national discussion throughout much of the season.
With the playoff bracket now finalized, attention turns to the semifinal games, where four of the country's top programs will compete for the opportunity to play for a national championship.

(CNN) The race for college football's national title entered its final stage Sunday as the selection committee revealed the four teams that will compete in the College Football Playoff.
Claiming the playoff spots were the Texas Longhorns, Oregon Ducks, Florida State Seminoles and Washington Huskies, each of whom put together standout seasons to earn a chance at the championship.
The semifinals will be played on New Year's Day. Top-seeded Texas is scheduled to meet Washington in the Sugar Bowl in New Orleans, while Oregon and Florida State will face each other in the Rose Bowl in Pasadena. The two winners will move on to the national championship game later in January in Houston.
Florida State arrives in the playoff unbeaten after navigating its schedule without a loss and securing a conference title along the way. The Seminoles consistently found ways to win, combining a disciplined defense with timely offensive performances.
Texas enters as the No. 1 seed after an impressive season that included a conference championship and several notable victories. Head coach Steve Sarkisian has guided the program back into the national spotlight, putting the Longhorns in position to compete for their first title in years.
Oregon earned the second seed behind a powerful offense that ranked among the nation's most productive. The Ducks finished the season strongly and strengthened their case with a commanding performance in their conference championship game.
Washington rounded out the playoff field thanks to a résumé built on quality wins and a dynamic passing attack. The Huskies spent much of the season near the top of the rankings and ultimately did enough to secure the committee's confidence.
The first team left out of the playoff was Penn State, which finished fifth after posting an 11-2 record. Despite missing the top four, the Nittany Lions are expected to be featured in one of the postseason's premier bowl games.
LSU followed closely behind at No. 6. The Tigers remained in the playoff conversation for much of the year, fueled by one of the most explosive offenses in college football.
Now that the bracket has been finalized, the focus shifts from rankings and debate to the games themselves. Four teams remain in contention, and two semifinal matchups will determine who gets the opportunity to compete for the national championship.

(CNN)Sergio Garcia made Ryder Cup history on Saturday, becoming the player with the most matches won in the famous competition. The 41-year-old claimed his 24th Ryder Cup match victory, winning 3&1 alongside fellow Spaniard Jon Rahm against US pair Brooks Koepka and Daniel Berger in the morning foursomes. The European duo pulled off a remarkable comeback to claim the vital victory for the visitors, coming back from three shots down. Garcia reacts to a putt on the 8th hole during a foursomes match.It is their second win of the weekend together, having also won their foursome match on Friday morning at Whistling Straits. Garcia made his debut at the 1999 Ryder Cup, appearing at nine further tournaments. Read MoreIn 2006, he was unbeaten over the first two days, becoming only the second player after Ian Woosnam to win all four points from his foursomes and fourball matches.Entering this year's competition, Garcia had scored just as many points as the entire US team combined.During the previous Ryder Cup at Le Golf National, France, in 2018, Garcia overtook Nick Faldo's record to become Europe's all-time leading points scorer with 25.5 points.However, although he passed Faldo for the number of points, it took him until Saturday to pass him for the most matches won at the Ryder Cup. Faldo had 23 matches won, but now Garcia is the record holder, having earned his 23rd and 24th match victories in Wisconsin. Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, features, and videosRahm and Garcia line up a putt on the 15th green.When asked about breaking the record after his round, Garcia said it was "great," although Europe's predicament in the Ryder Cup was his sole focus. When he was told about it on the 17th hole, he said he "didn't know the record and I didn't care.""It's great but it's not. We need more wins and unfortunately we are not getting them at the moment," he told the media. "Obviously Jon (Rahm) and I, we did what we had to and what we could, and unfortunately it just looks like the American guys are just playing a little bit better than us, and it's a shame. But you know, we are not giving up. We're going to fight until the end as hard as we can. Hopefully we have a good afternoon this afternoon and see what happens."

At the Presidents Cup on Saturday, veteran golfer Justin Thomas added another milestone to his already impressive international career, becoming one of the most successful American players in team competition history.
The 32-year-old secured his 18th match victory in the event after teaming up with Patrick Cantlay to defeat the International Team pairing of Sungjae Im and Corey Conners in the morning foursomes session. The American duo rallied after a difficult start, overturning a two-hole deficit midway through the round to earn a 2&1 victory.
It marked their second consecutive win together during the tournament, having also claimed a point in Friday's team matches. Thomas has become known for his strong performances in team events and has consistently delivered points for the United States throughout his appearances.
Since making his debut several years ago, Thomas has established himself as one of the team's most reliable competitors. Entering this year's competition, he was already among the leading active American players in total points earned.
The latest victory moved him further up the all-time standings and reinforced his reputation as a player who thrives under pressure. His partnership with Cantlay has been particularly effective, with the pair demonstrating strong chemistry throughout the week.
Despite reaching another personal milestone, Thomas focused on the overall contest rather than his individual achievement. Speaking after the round, he emphasized that the team's position in the competition remained the priority.
"It's nice, of course, but we're here to win as a team," Thomas told reporters. "Personal records are something you can appreciate later. Right now, we're trying to keep momentum on our side and continue putting points on the board."
He added that the International Team had been highly competitive and that the United States could not afford to become complacent.
"There are still a lot of matches left," he said. "We've played some good golf, but every session matters. We need to stay focused and keep fighting for every point."

Justin Thomas reached another notable landmark in his team-match career on Saturday, but it wasn't something he seemed eager to dwell on.
Playing alongside Patrick Cantlay in the morning foursomes session at the Presidents Cup, Thomas helped secure a 2&1 win over Sungjae Im and Corey Conners. The Americans had trailed earlier in the match before gradually working their way back and taking control over the closing holes.
The result gave Thomas his 18th career victory in Presidents Cup competition and continued a productive week for the partnership. He and Cantlay had already earned a point together the previous day, and their combination once again proved difficult to beat.
Over the years, Thomas has built a reputation as a player who often raises his level in team events. His record in international competition reflects that consistency, and Saturday's win pushed him further up the list of successful American performers.
Still, the achievement was not at the forefront of his mind after the round. When reporters mentioned the milestone, Thomas quickly redirected attention to the larger picture.
"It's something I'll probably appreciate more later," he said. "Right now, the team result is what matters."
He also praised the opposition, noting that the matches had been closer than the scoreboard sometimes suggested. With plenty of golf still remaining, Thomas stressed that there was no room for overconfidence.
"We've put ourselves in a good position, but nothing's finished yet," he said. "Every point is important, and we have to keep showing up and doing our job."
For Thomas, the record books can wait. The immediate goal remains the same: helping his team finish the week on top.

(CNN)The death of a pregnant Polish woman has reignited debate over abortion in one of Europe's most devoutly Catholic countries, with activists saying she could still be alive if it were not for a near total ban on terminating pregnancies.Tens of thousands of Poles took to the streets to protest in January this year when a Constitutional Tribunal ruling from October 2020 that terminating pregnancies with fetal defects was unconstitutional came into effect, eliminating the most frequently used case for legal abortion.Activists say Izabela, a 30-year-old woman in the 22nd week of pregnancy who her family said died of septic shock after doctors waited for her unborn baby's heart to stop beating, is the first woman to die as a result of the ruling.The government says the ruling was not to blame for her death, rather an error by doctors.Izabela went to hospital in September after her waters broke, her family said. Scans had previously shown numerous defects in the fetus.Read More"The baby weighs 485 grams. For now, thanks to the abortion law, I have to lie down. And there is nothing they can do. They'll wait until it dies or something begins, and if not, I can expect sepsis," Izabela said in a text message to her mother, private broadcaster TVN24 reported.When a scan showed the fetus was dead, doctors at the hospital in Pszczyna, southern Poland, decided to perform a Caesarean. The family's lawyer, Jolanta Budzowska, said Izabela's heart stopped on the way to the operating theater and she died despite efforts to resuscitate her.Protesters hold up lights, a Polish flag and a photograph of Iza as they take part in a demonstration on November 6, 2021 in Warsaw."I couldn't believe it, I thought it wasn't true," Izabela's mother Barbara told TVN24. "How could such a thing happen to her in the hospital? After all, she went there for help."Budzowska has started legal action over the treatment Izabela received, accusing doctors of malpractice, but she also called the death "a consequence of the verdict".In a statement on its website, the Pszczyna County Hospital said it shared the pain of all those affected by Izabela's death, especially her family."It should ... be emphasized that all medical decisions were made taking into account the legal provisions and standards of conduct in force in Poland," the hospital said.On Friday, the hospital said it had suspended two doctors who were on duty at the time of the death.The Supreme Medical Chamber, which represents Polish doctors, said it was not immediately able to comment.Not one moreWhen the case came to public attention as a result of a tweet from Budzowska, the hashtag #anijednejwiecej or "not one more" spread across social media and was taken up by protesters demanding a change to the law.However, Poland's ruling Law and Justice (PiS) party rejects claims that the Constitutional Tribunal ruling was to blame for Izabela's death, attributing it to a mistake by doctors."When it comes to the life and health of the mother ... if it is in danger, then terminating the pregnancy is possible and the ruling does not change anything," Prime Minister Mateusz Morawiecki said on Friday.PiS lawmaker Bartlomiej Wroblewski told Reuters that the case should not be "instrumentalized and used to limit the right to life, to kill all sick or disabled children."But activists say the ruling has made doctors scared to terminate pregnancies even when the mother's life is at risk."Izabela's case clearly shows that the ruling of the Constitutional Tribunal has had a chilling effect on doctors," Urszula Grycuk of the Federation for Women and Family Planning told Reuters."Even a condition that should not be questioned -- the life and health of the mother -- is not always recognized by doctors because they are afraid."Overturning Roe could mean women seeking abortions have to travel hundreds of miles In Ireland, the death of 31-year-old Savita Halappanavar in 2012 after she was refused a termination provoked a national outpouring of grief credited by many as a catalyst for the liberalization of abortion laws.Budzowska told Reuters that a debate similar to the one that took place in Ireland was underway in Poland."Both Izabela's family and I personally hope that this case ... will lead to a change in the law in Poland," she said.Poland's president proposed changing the law last year to make abortions possible in cases where the fetus was not viable. The Law and Justice dominated parliament has yet to debate the bill.

(CNN) The death of a woman whose pregnancy was complicated by severe fetal abnormalities has intensified debate over abortion laws in a European country where access to the procedure has become increasingly restricted.
Thousands of demonstrators gathered in major cities over the weekend after activists linked the woman's death to legislation that sharply limits the circumstances under which pregnancies can be terminated. Protesters argued that legal uncertainty surrounding abortion has made some physicians reluctant to intervene even when a patient's health is deteriorating.
The woman, who was in the second trimester of pregnancy, was admitted to a regional hospital after experiencing serious medical complications, according to statements from her family and legal representatives. Prenatal examinations had reportedly identified significant abnormalities in the fetus.
Family members said doctors chose to continue monitoring the pregnancy rather than perform an immediate termination. As the woman's condition worsened, she developed a severe infection and later died despite medical efforts to save her.
Her death has become a focal point for abortion-rights advocates, who say restrictive laws have created confusion among healthcare providers about when intervention is legally permitted. Demonstrators carried signs and candles during rallies held in several cities, calling for reforms that would provide doctors with greater discretion in emergency situations.
Lawyers representing the family have requested a formal investigation into the medical decisions made during the woman's treatment. They argue that both individual clinical judgments and the broader legal environment should be examined.
Hospital officials expressed condolences to the family and said all actions taken by medical staff were consistent with existing regulations and professional standards. Authorities have launched a review to determine whether any procedural errors occurred.
The case quickly gained national attention after details were shared on social media. A slogan translating to "Never Again" spread widely online and became a rallying cry for demonstrators demanding changes to the country's abortion policies.
Government leaders have rejected suggestions that abortion restrictions directly caused the woman's death. Officials maintain that current laws allow physicians to act when a patient's life or health is in danger and argue that any failures were related to medical decision-making rather than legislation itself.
Supporters of the restrictions contend that the laws are designed to protect unborn children and should not be blamed before investigations are complete. Several lawmakers urged the public to wait for official findings before drawing conclusions about responsibility.
Medical and legal experts remain divided. Some argue that physicians may become overly cautious when legal penalties are unclear, while others insist that existing exceptions provide sufficient flexibility for doctors to respond to emergencies.
The case has drawn comparisons to incidents in other countries where the deaths of pregnant women sparked national conversations about reproductive rights and ultimately influenced legislative change.
For activists seeking reform, the woman's death represents what they describe as the human cost of restrictive policies. They hope the public attention generated by the case will lead lawmakers to revisit the country's abortion laws and clarify the circumstances under which medical intervention can occur.

(CNN) A woman's death following complications during pregnancy has reignited a contentious national debate over abortion policy, with critics arguing that restrictive laws may be influencing medical decisions in hospitals.
Public anger grew after details of the case emerged, prompting demonstrations in several cities. Protesters say the legal framework surrounding abortion has created an atmosphere in which doctors may hesitate to act quickly, even when a patient's condition becomes dangerous.
According to relatives and legal representatives, the woman was hospitalized during her second trimester after experiencing serious complications. Earlier examinations had identified major fetal abnormalities, raising concerns about the viability of the pregnancy.
Family members contend that doctors continued to monitor the situation instead of ending the pregnancy immediately. Over time, the woman's health reportedly deteriorated, and she developed a severe infection. Despite treatment efforts, she later died.
The tragedy has become a powerful symbol for abortion-rights campaigners. Many argue that the law's restrictions have had a chilling effect on medical professionals, leaving them uncertain about when intervention is legally protected.
Attorneys representing the family have called for a full inquiry into the circumstances surrounding the woman's care. They want investigators to examine not only the actions of the medical team but also whether the legal environment contributed to the outcome.
Hospital officials expressed sympathy to the family while defending the conduct of staff. They said treatment decisions were made in accordance with applicable regulations and medical guidelines. Health authorities have since opened a review into the case.
As news of the death spread online, a slogan meaning "Never Again" gained traction across social media platforms and appeared on banners at public demonstrations. The phrase quickly became associated with demands for legal reform.
Government officials have disputed claims that abortion laws were responsible. They argue that doctors retain the authority to intervene when a pregnant woman's life or health is threatened and say any mistakes should be assessed through the ongoing investigation.
Backers of the current legislation have echoed that view, warning against drawing political conclusions before all the facts are established. They maintain that the purpose of the law is to protect fetal life while still allowing necessary medical treatment.
The case has nevertheless intensified discussion among doctors, legal scholars and advocacy groups. Some experts believe uncertainty about legal consequences can influence clinical decisions, while others argue the existing rules already provide sufficient exceptions.
Observers have also pointed to similar cases abroad where the deaths of pregnant women became catalysts for broader debates over reproductive rights and healthcare policy.
For many demonstrators, the issue extends beyond a single case. They see it as evidence of deeper problems within the legal system governing reproductive healthcare and are calling on lawmakers to revisit the rules to prevent similar tragedies in the future.

(CNN)Sixteen years after winning his PGA Championship title, Phil Mickelson is roaring back -- with a chance at history. The five-time major winner carved out a two-shot lead for himself on Friday at the 103rd edition of the competition.And, annoyingly for Mickelson, it could have been even better. The 50-year-old birdied six of his first 11 holes but bogeyed three of the last six to finish the day 5-under par and two shots ahead of the chasing pack at the Ocean Course on Kiawah Island, South Carolina. READ: How Hideki Matsuyama became Japan's new national heroRead MoreMickelson plays his shot from the 15th tee during the second round of the 2021 PGA Championship.But despite the tough ending to his round -- the wind caused havoc as it picked up later on -- Mickelson described having "fun" out on the course. He got informed midway through his post-round press conference that he was the leader thanks to Branden Grace's double bogey, and he hopes he can hear a similar thing on Sunday. "So, if you were to tell me that like Sunday night, I'd really enjoy that, but right now there's a lot of work to do," Mickelson said. "I'm not sure it's going to stand today. We'll see. But the fact is I'm heading into the weekend with an opportunity and I'm playing really well and I'm having a lot of fun doing it."If he is able to hold off his younger competitors, Mickelson would become the oldest winner of a major. Julius Boros holds that record, winning the 1968 PGA at age 48. Jack Nicklaus won his last major at 46. It's been eight years since Mickelson won his last major, the Open Championship in 2013. He has claimed two PGA Tour victories since then, as well as two wins on the PGA Tour Champions in 2020. Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, features, and videosMickelson chips to the 17th green.And after lamenting some focus issues in previous weeks, Mickelson says it's something he's been "working" on. "I'm just making more and more progress just by trying to elongate my focus," he said. "I might try to play 36, 45 holes in a day and try to focus on each shot so that when I go out and play 18, it doesn't feel like it's that much."He added: "I might try to elongate the time that I end up meditating, but I'm trying to use my mind like a muscle and just expand it because as I've gotten older, it's been more difficult for me to maintain a sharp focus, a good visualization and see the shot."

Six years after his most recent major triumph, Rory McIlroy has put himself back in contention for another memorable victory. The four-time major champion surged into the lead on Friday during the second round of the US Open, finishing with a three-shot advantage over the rest of the field.
The Northern Irishman looked in complete control for much of the day. He collected five birdies across his first 13 holes and appeared on course for an even lower score before a pair of late bogeys reduced his advantage. Nevertheless, he ended the round at 6-under par, putting himself in a strong position heading into the weekend.
Despite the disappointing finish, McIlroy remained upbeat afterward and said he was pleased with the overall quality of his play. During his post-round interview, he was informed that several rivals had dropped shots on the closing holes, leaving him alone at the top of the leaderboard.
"That's nice to hear, but there are still two long days ahead," McIlroy said. "I've put myself in a good position, but nothing has been won yet. The course is demanding, and there are a lot of great players chasing."
If McIlroy can maintain his lead through Sunday, it would mark his first major championship victory in more than a decade. Since lifting the PGA Championship trophy in 2014, he has come close on several occasions but has been unable to add to his major tally.
After recent tournaments in which inconsistency affected his results, McIlroy credited his improved performance to better mental preparation. He explained that he has spent considerable time working on concentration and staying committed to every shot.
"I've been focusing on keeping my attention in the present moment," he said. "It's easy to think ahead in a tournament, but I've tried to stay disciplined and approach each shot with the same level of commitment."
He added that mental training has become increasingly important as his career has progressed.
"You have to keep developing every part of your game," McIlroy said. "For me, that includes focus, patience, and trusting what I see. Those things can make a huge difference over four rounds."

Rory McIlroy finds himself in a familiar position once again: near the top of the leaderboard at a major championship and carrying plenty of momentum into the weekend.
The four-time major winner produced one of the strongest rounds of the day on Friday at the US Open, building a three-shot lead over the field. For much of the afternoon, it looked as though his advantage might be even larger. McIlroy picked up several birdies early and was moving steadily away from his competitors before a couple of mistakes late in the round slowed him down.
Even so, he walked off the course with plenty to be encouraged about. By the time he finished speaking with reporters, he had learned that other contenders had stumbled on the closing stretch, leaving him alone in first place.
"It's good news, obviously," McIlroy said. "But we're only halfway through the tournament. There are still a lot of holes left, and this course can change things pretty quickly."
A victory on Sunday would carry particular significance. Although McIlroy has remained one of golf's biggest names, another major title has eluded him for years despite several close calls.
He suggested that recent improvements have less to do with swing changes and more to do with what is happening between his ears. In recent weeks, he has devoted extra attention to maintaining concentration for long periods and avoiding the lapses that can prove costly in major championships.
"I've worked hard on staying locked in for the whole round," he explained. "When your mind starts drifting, that's usually when mistakes happen."
McIlroy believes that aspect of the game becomes even more important with experience. Physical skills still matter, but so does the ability to remain patient and focused when the pressure starts to build.
As he heads into the weekend with the lead, that's the challenge he faces now: not getting ahead of himself and continuing to do the small things well, one shot at a time.

(CNN)Stuart Baxter has been sacked from his position as manager by Indian Super League club Odisha after making comments about rape during a post-match interview.The British former professional footballer made the comments about refereeing decisions during his side's defeat to Jamshedpur on Monday."You need decisions to go your way and they didn't," Baxter told Indian channel Star Sports. "I don't know when we're going to get a penalty. I think one of my players would have to rape someone or get raped himself if he was going to get a penalty."Odisha tweeted on Monday to say it was "appalled" by the comments."It is completely unacceptable whatever the context and does not reflect the values of the club," the club wrote on Twitter. "We, at Odisha FC, unreservedly apologize and the club management will handle this matter internally."Read MoreThe club then released a statement on Tuesday announcing Baxter had been sacked."Odisha FC has decided to terminate Head Coach, Stuart Baxter's contract with immediate effect,"it said. "The interim coach for the remainder of the season will be announced soon."Visit CNN.com/sport for more news, videos and featuresBaxter began playing professional football in England with Preston North End and has gone on to have a nomadic managerial career. The 67-year-old enjoyed successful spells with AIK in Sweden and Kaizer Chiefs in South Africa, before being hired by Odisha in June 2020 following a second spell with the South African national team.The club currently sits bottom of the Indian Premier League with just eight points from 14 matches.

(CNN) A professional basketball coach has been dismissed by his club after making controversial remarks during a post-game interview that drew widespread criticism from fans, advocacy groups and league officials.
The comments were made following his team's narrow loss in a domestic league match on Saturday. Frustrated by what he believed were unfavorable officiating decisions, the coach used an analogy involving domestic violence while discussing the number of fouls awarded to his team.
"We seem to have to go through something extreme before we get a call," the coach said during a television interview. "At this point, it feels like one of our players would have to be assaulted before the referees notice anything."
The remarks quickly sparked backlash on social media, with critics accusing the coach of trivializing a serious issue. Several supporters called on the club to take disciplinary action, while advocacy organizations condemned the language as inappropriate and insensitive.
Later that evening, the club released a statement distancing itself from the comments.
"The views expressed by our head coach do not reflect the values of this organization," the statement said. "We consider the remarks unacceptable and sincerely apologize to anyone offended. The matter is being reviewed internally."
Less than 24 hours later, the team announced that the coach's contract had been terminated with immediate effect.
"Following a review of recent events, the club has decided to part ways with the head coach," the organization said in a statement. "An interim coaching arrangement will be announced in the coming days."
The coach, who enjoyed a lengthy playing career before moving into management, has worked for clubs in several countries and previously led teams to domestic titles. He joined the club before the start of the current season with the aim of rebuilding a struggling roster.
Despite expectations of improvement, results have been disappointing. The team currently sits near the bottom of the league standings after winning only a handful of games through the first half of the campaign.
League officials declined to comment on the dismissal but reiterated their commitment to maintaining professional standards among players, coaches and club personnel.
The incident has renewed discussion about accountability in sports and the consequences public figures may face for comments made during emotionally charged post-match interviews.

(CNN) A basketball club has fired its head coach after comments he made in a televised post-game interview triggered criticism and calls for disciplinary action.
The controversy began after the team's defeat on Saturday. Speaking about what he viewed as poor officiating, the coach used an analogy involving domestic violence to express frustration over refereeing decisions.
During the interview, he suggested that his players would need to experience something extreme before receiving favorable calls from officials. The remark was immediately criticized online, with many arguing that it made light of a serious social issue.
Reaction was swift. Fans, commentators and advocacy groups condemned the statement, saying such language had no place in professional sports regardless of the context in which it was used.
The club responded later the same day, making clear that it did not support the coach's comments.
