The charts illustrate the gender distribution across different age categories in Japan and Brazil in the year 1995.
Overall, the male and female populations in Japan were relatively equal, especially in the 15-60 age brackets, showing a balanced demographic structure. The 45-50 age category constituted the highest percentage of people in Japan, while the greatest proportion of the Brazilian population fell into the 0-5 age group, displaying a noticeable gender imbalance.
Looking closely at the age demographics, Brazil featured a massive proportion of its population in the younger brackets, making up over 45% of the total in the 0-20 age range. Specifically, the largest segment of Brazilians was found in the 0-5 age group, with a male-to-female ratio of about 8% to 7.5%. The population of Brazilians aged 65 and above experienced a sharp drop, representing under 3% of the total population with roughly equal numbers of men and women.
Conversely, Japanese citizens between the ages of 20 and 50 formed the most substantial portion of the demographic makeup, representing nearly 40% of the total. The 45-50 age bracket was recorded as the most populous group in Japan, containing roughly 5% of the population for each sex. The gender ratio in the 80 years and older category was shown to be the smallest fraction of Japan's population, standing at 0.5% for males and 1.5% for females.

The provided charts break down the populations of Japan and Brazil by age and gender in 1995. Right away, Japan’s demographic profile stands out as highly balanced between men and women, especially throughout the working-age years of 15 to 60. Most of Japan's citizens were concentrated in the 45-50 age bracket. Brazil, on the other hand, leaned heavily toward the youngest age groups; the 0-5 range made up its largest demographic and showed a slight imbalance between boys and girls.
Breaking down Brazil's numbers reveals a distinctly young country. Individuals under the age of 20 made up over 45% of the entire population. The absolute peak was that 0-5 age group, where boys accounted for roughly 8% and girls 7.5%. As age increased, the population numbers plummeted. By the time the chart reaches the 65-and-older category, less than 3% of the population remains, though the gender split in this older group was fairly even.
Japan’s data tells a completely different story. The bulk of its population—nearly 40%—fell between 20 and 50 years old. The single largest cohort was the 45-50 age group, holding steady at about 5% for both men and women. At the very top of the age spectrum, the 80-plus demographic was the smallest. Interestingly, this oldest group showed a noticeable gender gap, with women making up 1.5% of the total population compared to just 0.5% for men.

Nowadays, written language is used in comparatively more informal and relaxed way. I assume that it happens because of the need of making communication easier and faster. Without features of formal written language it is simplier to share your idea in a shorter period of time. This option is essential in a modern temp of life. This essay will cover both advantages and disadvantages of this development.
The less formal and tense written language provides some pros. First of all, without formalization the process of communication requires less amount of time. Then letters consists of less number of symbols and can help to solve the discussed problem much faster. Moreover, this way of communication does not support formal borders between members of a dialogue. Then it is easier to come closer with the interlocutor and feel no pressure during the using of written language.
On the other hand, development of making written language more informal and relaxed has some cons. Firstly, communication goes less respectful. Then interlocutors have almost no opportunity to pay a respect to each other. Formalized written language is significant in issue of social borders that can hihlight a paid respect. Secondly, sometimes the main gist of the letter goes unclear, because informal language is more relaxed. In these conditions, the quidity of the letter can be hidden with less formal features. If participants of the communication are not close, they will not understand each other correctly.
In conclusion I would like to say that the process of using written language in a more informal way has both advantages and disadvantages. It can be revealed in different situations. That is why everyone should have their own opinion based on the information discussed in this essay.

Nowadays, voice messages are used in a comparatively more frequent and regular way instead of traditional phone calls. I assume that it happens because of the need of making daily communication more convenient and multitasking. Without the necessity of typing long texts or arranging a live call, it is easier to share your thoughts on the go. This option is highly demanded in a modern temp of life. This essay will cover both advantages and disadvantages of this development.
The increasing use of audio messages provides some pros. First of all, without typing, the process of sending a message requires less amount of effort. Then the sender can just speak naturally and explain the discussed issue much faster. Moreover, this way of communication supports emotional expression between members of a dialogue. Then it is easier to understand the mood of the interlocutor and feel a deeper connection during the conversation.
On the other hand, the development of relying on voice messages has some cons. Firstly, communication becomes less convenient for the receiver. Then the listener has almost no opportunity to hear the message in public places without headphones. Text messages are significant in situations where silence is required. Secondly, sometimes the main gist of the audio goes lost, because spoken language is less structured. In these conditions, the main point of the recording can be hidden among unnecessary pauses or filler words. If participants of the communication are in a hurry, they will not get the information quickly.
In conclusion I would like to say that the process of using voice messages in a more frequent way has both advantages and disadvantages. It can be beneficial or annoying in different situations. That is why everyone should have their own opinion based on the information discussed in this essay.

Voice notes have quickly become a go-to alternative to traditional phone calls and long text messages. This shift makes perfect sense given how much we value multitasking today; leaving a quick audio clip lets people share their thoughts on the fly without having to stop and type or schedule a live conversation. While this trend certainly fits our fast-paced lifestyles, relying heavily on voice messages comes with both distinct benefits and a few noticeable drawbacks.
On the plus side, recording a memo requires practically zero effort compared to typing out a lengthy paragraph. You can just talk naturally, which usually means you can explain a complex situation much faster. Beyond just saving time, audio captures tone of voice and emotion in a way that plain text simply cannot. Hearing someone's actual voice helps convey their mood, making the exchange feel much more personal and connected than a standard chat thread.
However, the convenience for the sender often becomes a burden for the receiver. Unlike a text that can be glanced at anywhere, a voice message demands a quiet environment or a pair of headphones. In meetings or noisy public spaces, listening to an audio clip is basically impossible. Furthermore, people tend to ramble when they speak. A point that could have been a concise sentence often gets buried under pauses and tangents. If the listener is in a rush, digging through a two-minute recording to find one specific detail is incredibly frustrating.
Ultimately, swapping texts for voice messages is a double-edged sword. Whether it is a helpful tool or a minor annoyance really depends on the context of the conversation and the environment of the people involved.

Information regarding the millions of tons of coffee exported from five different continents every five years from 2000 to 2015 is provided in the table.
Overall, it can be seen that the total amount of coffee exports grew substantially over the 15-year period. Of those millions of tons, South America represented the highest and Oceania represented the lowest.
One of the most notable characteristics of the table was the increase in the total of exports from 550.5 million in 2000 to 810.2 million in 2015. South America exported the most during the 15-year period, accounting for 450.4 million in 2015, accounting for around three-fifths of the total. Those in Oceania exported more too, but they remained with the lowest numbers from 12.5 in 2000 to 18.2 million in 2015.
In Africa and Asia the coffee exports experienced growth during the 15 years. The former grew by 15 million and the latter by more than double in 2015. Similarly, those in Central America exported more during the first 10 years, however, they experienced a slight drop of 8 million at the end of the period.

The provided table tracks coffee export volumes—measured in millions of tons—across five continents between 2000 and 2015, recorded at five-year intervals. Looking at the big picture, the global coffee trade expanded significantly during this timeframe. South America consistently dominated the market, while Oceania exported the smallest share of the group.
The sheer volume of global exports saw a massive jump, rising from 550.5 million tons in 2000 to an impressive 810.2 million by 2015. South America was the primary driver of this growth. By the end of the 15-year span, the continent was shipping out 450.4 million tons, effectively claiming about three-fifths of the worldwide total. Oceania also experienced an upward trend, though its figures remained comparatively tiny—inching from 12.5 million up to just 18.2 million tons over the same period.
Elsewhere, Africa and Asia both enjoyed steady increases in their export capacity. African shipments grew by 15 million tons overall, while Asian output actually more than doubled by the end of 2015. Central America followed a slightly different pattern; although local producers successfully ramped up exports for the first decade, they faced a minor setback at the very end of the period, with volumes dropping by roughly 8 million tons.

The pie chart and table gives us an information about the various places that are visited by the tourists in Britain in the year of 1999. It can be be observed that tourists showed interested in four major attractions.
Overall, the tourists are visited the theme parks the most, and their favourite place is Blackpool Pleasure Beach with a significant margin.
Taking a closer look at the pie chart, it can be seen that theme parks and museums & galleries are the two major attractions for the visitors. 38% of the tourists prefered to visit the theme parks which makes it the highest attraction in Britain. The second on the chart is museums and galleries with 37% and it is followed by historic houses and monuments with 16%. The last place on the chart is wildlife parks and zoos which is only 9% of the visitors are traveled in 1999.
Meanwhile, the theme parks are the favourite place for the tourists, one place is attracted nearly half of these visitors. 47% of the theme park visitors went to Blackpool Pleasure Beach which is the highest percentage. Next is Alton Towers with 17% and Pleasureland in Southport comes third with 16%. The same percentage of theme parks visitors traveled to Chessington World of Adventures and Legoland in Windsor which is 10%.

The pie chart and table provide information about the various transportation methods used by commuters in Australia in the year 2005. It can be observed that commuters utilized four major modes of transport.
Overall, the commuters used public transport the most, and their favourite method is trains with a significant margin.
Taking a closer look at the pie chart, it can be seen that public transport and personal vehicles are the two major choices for the commuters. 42% of the commuters preferred to use public transport which makes it the highest mode in Australia. The second on the chart is personal vehicles with 35% and it is followed by walking and cycling with 15%. The last place on the chart is carpooling which only 8% of the commuters used in 2005.
Meanwhile, since public transport is the favourite choice for the commuters, one method attracted nearly half of these users. 45% of the public transport users went on trains which is the highest percentage. Next is buses with 20% and trams come third with 15%. The same percentage of public transport users travelled by ferries and subways which is 10%.

The provided charts illustrate how Australian commuters traveled to work in 2005, breaking down the broad transportation categories and offering a closer look at specific public transit preferences. Right away, public transport stands out as the most popular option overall, with the train network taking the clear lead among transit users.
When looking at general commuting habits, public transit and private cars heavily dominated the landscape. A solid 42% of people relied on public transport, making it the top choice nationwide. Driving a personal vehicle followed closely behind at 35%. Active commuting, such as walking or cycling, accounted for a respectable 15% of the total, leaving carpooling as the least popular method at just 8%.
Because public transport was the primary way to get around, the data further breaks down exactly which services these commuters used. Nearly half of all public transit riders—45%, to be exact—opted for trains. Buses made up the next largest share at 20%, while trams carried about 15% of the daily traffic. The remaining commuters were split evenly, with ferries and subways each claiming 10% of the public transit ridership.

Group or team activities are great ways of teaching more important life skills, however, there are activities which can be done alone that will also teach some important skills. Both ones are important in teaching life skills. Below, we will see which one of them is a great way of teaching more important life skills.
First of all, we need to understand that doing activities in group, or as a team can help us deepen our knowlodge, because they provide us with the ability to share ideas and learn from each other which is something crucial in our daily lives as human being as we were all created to live together as communities. In addition, group or team activities will not only allow us to know the other person living next us but to figure out what their feelings are and to be patient to them when they need us. However, doing activities alone have also its own benefits.
In fact, personal activities or some of them we do alone can give us some understanding about how the world is spinning around us as well. Firsthand, sometimes we need to do a few activities alone as this can help us to develop some personal important life skills to know exacly who we really are and what is our mission on the earth. Second, doing personal activities such as walking out, or going for a walk will not only strengthen our physical being but also to be mentally healthy.
In conclusion, both of them have positive and negative aspects to consider. However, in my opinion, doing activities in group or as a team can teach more important life skills than those activities which are done alone because group activities let us explore the world around us which will later teach us important skills which are necessary for our survival.

Studying with a group or as a team is a great way of gaining more academic knowledge, however, there are study methods which can be done alone that will also build some important study habits. Both ones are important in achieving academic success. Below, we will see which one of them is a great way of gaining more important academic skills.
First of all, we need to understand that studying in a group, or as a team can help us deepen our understanding, because they provide us with the ability to share notes and learn from each other which is something crucial in our school lives as students as we were all enrolled to learn together in classrooms. In addition, group or team studying will not only allow us to know the other classmates sitting next to us but to figure out what their perspectives are and to be helpful to them when they need us. However, studying alone has also its own benefits.
In fact, personal study sessions or some of the assignments we do alone can give us some understanding about how the academic material is structured as well. Firsthand, sometimes we need to do a few readings alone as this can help us to develop some personal important study skills to know exactly what we really struggle with and what is our academic goal for the semester. Second, doing personal study habits such as reading quietly, or reviewing flashcards will not only strengthen our memory but also to be mentally focused.
In conclusion, both of them have positive and negative aspects to consider. However, in my opinion, studying in a group or as a team can teach more important academic skills than those study sessions which are done alone because group studying lets us explore the concepts around us which will later teach us important knowledge which is necessary for our graduation.

While group study sessions are fantastic for expanding your academic knowledge, hitting the books on your own is just as crucial for developing essential study habits. Both approaches play a major role in a student's success. It is worth exploring, however, which method actually provides the most long-term value for a student's educational growth.
Collaborating with peers is incredibly helpful for deepening our understanding of a complex subject. When we study in teams, we get the chance to share notes, compare viewpoints, and fill in each other's knowledge gaps. Since schools are naturally communal environments, learning to work alongside classmates is a vital part of the experience. Beyond just passing tests, group work teaches us how to listen to different perspectives and support our peers when they struggle with a difficult concept.
That being said, solo studying brings its own unique advantages. Working through assignments independently forces us to grapple with the material directly. Sometimes, we just need to sit alone with a textbook to figure out exactly which topics we find challenging and how to tackle them without relying on someone else's input. Furthermore, solitary habits like quiet reading or reviewing flashcards are fantastic for building memory retention and improving our mental focus over long stretches of time.
Ultimately, both study methods have clear benefits depending on the situation. I tend to believe, though, that collaborative studying is the superior way to build essential skills. By exploring concepts together, students develop a much richer, multifaceted understanding of the material—something that is absolutely critical not just for graduation, but for thriving in the real world.

Most vehicles release a toxic amount of fumes into the atmosphere. This, in turn, has grown into an environmental issue of global concern that governments across the world believe can be mitigated by adding on to the price of fuels. I chiefly disagree with the effectiveness of this method as next to nothing had been altered since the policy enforcement due to convenience issues.
The number of cars utilizing fuel has not dwindled. Even though many alternatives had been suggested, there was no mass production of transportation units for the use of average salary workers. The middle crust of society does not possess sufficient income to shift to environmentally friendly solutions for the fear of maintenance hardships. For instance, an electrical car would necessitate a spacious charging spot in front of a garage that not many possess. As a consequence, the more convenient and trustworthy solution to buy fuel is resorted to.
The population of the world as a whole does not stay in one place. As more and more people are born, there is a growing need for transport to be manufactured in a short span of time. Although a part of the population may be able to abandon their cars in exchange for bikes or buses, a significant portion of the growing society still opts for refueling cars in gas stations to get to places faster.
In conclusion, while the idea of the price policy introduction bore few fruits, they had not been enough to make a contribution to solving the carbon footprint. Ironically, people would rather overspend for their comfort than think about nature.

Most factories release a massive amount of toxic waste into local water sources. This, in turn, has grown into an environmental issue of global concern that governments across the world believe can be mitigated by adding heavy taxes on industrial corporations. I chiefly disagree with the effectiveness of this method as next to nothing has been altered since the policy enforcement due to economic issues.
The number of companies utilizing cheap disposal methods has not dwindled. Even though many eco-friendly alternatives had been suggested, there was no affordable infrastructure for the use of average manufacturing plants. Mid-sized businesses do not possess sufficient income to shift to environmentally friendly solutions for the fear of financial hardships. For instance, a green filtration system would necessitate a massive installation space inside a factory that not many possess. As a consequence, the more convenient and profitable solution to pay the tax and continue polluting is resorted to.
The population of the world as a whole demands more products. As more and more people are born, there is a growing need for goods to be manufactured in a short span of time. Although a few corporations may be able to abandon their old methods in exchange for sustainable practices, a significant portion of the growing industry still opts for paying environmental fines to get products out faster.
In conclusion, while the idea of the tax policy introduction bore few fruits, they had not been enough to make a contribution to solving industrial pollution. Ironically, corporations would rather overspend on taxes for their convenience than think about nature.

Industrial pollution is one of the most pressing environmental crises we face today, and many governments have tried to fix it by slapping heavy taxes on factories that dump waste. While the intention is good, I honestly don't think this approach works. In reality, very little has actually changed since these financial penalties were introduced, mostly because it remains easier for companies to simply pay the fees and carry on.
Despite the new taxes, factories haven't stopped relying on cheap, harmful disposal methods. We constantly hear about green alternatives, but the infrastructure just isn't there for the average manufacturing plant to make the switch affordably. Smaller and mid-sized businesses simply don't have the profit margins to overhaul their entire operation without risking bankruptcy. Upgrading to a high-tech water filtration system, for example, requires an enormous amount of physical space and operational downtime that most facilities can't spare. Because of this, managers usually decide that taking the financial hit from a pollution tax is the safer, more practical business choice.
Furthermore, global demand for cheap consumer goods is higher than ever. With a rapidly growing population, factories are under immense pressure to churn out products at breakneck speeds. While a handful of high-profile brands might be able to pivot to completely sustainable manufacturing, the vast majority of the industrial sector doesn't have that luxury. They prefer to pay the fines and keep their assembly lines moving without interruption.
Ultimately, while taxing industrial waste might have seemed like a strong deterrent on paper, it hasn't actually put a dent in global pollution levels. It turns out that when pushed, most corporations are more than willing to eat the cost of a fine if it means protecting their bottom line and maintaining their convenience.

However, in my opinion, there are still some essential advantages to know foreign languages.

Some people claim that spatial navigation skills are still beneficial in spite of the appearance of applications providing GPS navigation in real time. Modern software is able to give quite accurate directions to different places, and it seems that it will soon replace physical maps and will get rid of the necessity to learn geography. However, in my opinion, there are still some essential advantages to knowing how to navigate independently.
First of all, the physical environment is such a complicated and constantly changing system that it is impossible to automate the navigation process to keep all details. For instance, if a road is closed due to sudden construction, it will unlikely be recognized by GPS immediately. Thus, it is too hard to teach a device to understand all the local changes that you might encounter.
Another important aspect of learning navigation is knowing your surroundings. Spatial awareness is a great source for understanding the layout of a city and its neighborhoods. If you use online maps, you will never know the actual geography or landmarks that contain historical value.
Moreover, learning spatial navigation is a tool of training your brain. It helps to develop memory and spatial intelligence. For instance, people didn't refuse studying math after the calculation device had been invented, because it is still important to understand how it works and it is necessary for developing other sciences.
Finally, there is a lot of places and occasions when you do not have a battery charge or internet access. Then digital maps are not available. But if you need to find your way being in a remote area, it is better to know at least some basic orientation skills. Otherwise, you can occur in an unpleasant situation.
To sum up, I definitely agree with the statement that learning navigation skills is essential in spite of technology progress. Although GPS is able to direct you anywhere you want, it is crucial to continue learning to navigate in order to understand your surroundings, to know your city and understand its peculiarities and, of course, to make your brain work.

Even with GPS and real-time navigation apps on every smartphone, many argue that basic map-reading and spatial skills are still incredibly valuable. It is easy to assume that digital maps will completely erase our need to understand geography, given how accurately they provide turn-by-turn directions. Still, I believe relying entirely on a screen is a mistake, and knowing how to navigate on your own offers some major advantages. 
For one thing, the physical world is messy and unpredictable. A GPS app might not immediately register a sudden road closure, a flooded street, or a newly built detour. If you lack a basic mental map of an area, you are completely helpless the moment the software fails to adapt to real-world conditions. Beyond just avoiding traffic, navigating by yourself actually connects you to your environment. Constantly staring at a blue dot on a screen means you miss out on the actual layout of a city. Learning to orient yourself helps you understand how neighborhoods connect and allows you to discover landmarks you would otherwise just drive past.
Building spatial awareness is also a fantastic way to keep your mind sharp. Forcing yourself to remember routes and visualize directions actively develops your memory. Think about it like basic math: we didn't stop teaching addition and subtraction just because calculators were invented. Understanding the underlying logic of where you are is still crucial.
There is also the obvious issue of technology simply failing. Batteries die, and remote areas frequently lack cell service. If you are hiking or driving through the mountains and lose your signal, knowing how to read a physical map or orient yourself isn't just an outdated hobby—it can keep you out of a genuinely dangerous situation.
Ultimately, while navigation apps are undeniably convenient, they shouldn't entirely replace our natural sense of direction. Developing spatial awareness keeps our brains active, helps us truly experience the places we travel, and ensures we are never left stranded just because a phone died.

In this essay, I will explore the advantages and disadvantages of this trend.On the one hand, both students and their country can benefit from this trend. First, students can choose more suitable methods for themselves because there are more options. The and teaching methods are quite different . According to foreign experts, Secondly, people can improve their foreign language, they can contact with when they are in a foreign country, and they quickly master the foreign language. Thirdly, it helps to promote the mutual understanding between their mother land and their destination countries. These students exchange information with the locals. The past twenty years have seen a dramatic change in the number of employees who work from home. In this essay, I will explore the advantages and disadvantages of this trend.
On the one hand, both employees and their companies can benefit from this trend. First, workers can choose more comfortable environments for themselves because there are more flexible options. The office settings and daily routines are quite different. Secondly, people can save their commuting time, they can stay with their family when they are in their own house, and they quickly improve their work-life balance. Thirdly, it helps to promote the productivity and efficiency of the business. These employees manage their schedules independently. Workers can get better focus on their tasks. Managers in the company will get more completed projects without office distractions. Similarly, the businesses also can get reduced costs regarding office space.
On the other hand, there are disadvantages too. Firstly, it costs people a lot of money to set up a home office. Many individuals have to spend their savings to buy computers and equipment to support their work at home. Secondly, some workers cannot stand the big isolation from their colleagues, they lose their motivation and quit their jobs.
To conclude, given the fact that working from home helps employees with their daily routines and promotes the productivity of companies, even if there are some drawbacks, I think the benefits outweigh the problems.

Over the last couple of decades, the shift toward remote work has completely transformed the traditional nine-to-five routine. While ditching the daily commute sounds like a dream to many, working from home comes with its own unique set of pros and cons that are worth exploring.
There are definitely clear benefits for both staff and employers. For one thing, people finally have the freedom to design a workspace that actually suits them, rather than being stuck in a rigid corporate cubicle. Without the stress of rush hour traffic, employees gain hours back every single week. This extra time at home naturally leads to a much healthier work-life balance. Beyond personal well-being, businesses often see a massive boost in overall productivity. When people can manage their own schedules and block out office distractions, they tend to get a lot more done. Plus, companies can save a fortune on commercial real estate when half their workforce is logging in from their living rooms.
That being said, the remote lifestyle isn't perfect. Setting up a proper home office isn't cheap, and many workers find themselves spending a lot of money out of pocket just to buy reliable Wi-Fi routers, ergonomic chairs, and extra monitors. Furthermore, not everyone handles isolation well. Being cut off from the casual, day-to-day banter with coworkers can leave some people feeling completely disconnected, which sometimes leads to burnout or even quitting altogether.
Ultimately, while working remotely requires a solid initial setup and can occasionally get lonely, I believe the advantages far outweigh the downsides. The flexibility it provides makes for happier employees and more efficient businesses.

In the diagram, the steps needed to obtain a dough for a pizza are shown, figures are given for grams and time required for the process.
In the initial stage, the staple ingredients, namely, 4 coup of 00 flour, 325 gr of water ( roughly one and a half cups) and a half teaspoon of dry active yeast respectively, are blended together in a cup.
After being placed in a stand mixer, the primordial dough undergo through 3 mixing stages, with the first and the third at slow speed for 2 minutes each, and the second at medium speed for 5 minutes.
Subsequently, the dough is going to be left to rest as long as its thickness has increased twofold, which it would takes at least 2 Hours.
Having gotten rid of the bubbles by squeezing them manually, the resulting portion of dough is either divided thoroughly into 3 slices equally sized or making each of the three parts weigh 275 grams.

In the diagram, the steps needed to obtain fresh pasta dough are illustrated, and figures are provided for the measurements and time required for the procedure.
In the initial stage, the main ingredients, namely, 2 cups of semolina flour, 3 large eggs, and a half teaspoon of olive oil respectively, are combined together in a bowl.
After being transferred to a wooden board, the initial mixture undergoes through 2 kneading stages, with the first at a gentle pace for 5 minutes, and the second at a vigorous pace for 10 minutes.
Subsequently, the dough is going to be left to rest wrapped in plastic film to allow the gluten to relax, which it would take at least 30 minutes.
Having rolled out the dough by pressing it through a machine, the resulting sheet of pasta is either cut thoroughly into thin strands equally sized or making the entire piece into wide ribbons for pappardelle.

The provided diagram breaks down the process of making fresh pasta dough, including the required ingredient measurements and estimated preparation times.
To start, you combine the base ingredients in a large bowl: two cups of semolina flour, three whole eggs, and about half a teaspoon of olive oil. Once the ingredients are fully mixed, the dough is moved to a wooden board for kneading. This hands-on process happens in two distinct phases. First, you knead the mixture gently for five minutes to bring everything together. After that, you work the dough much more vigorously for another ten minutes until it becomes smooth and elastic.
Next, the dough needs some time to rest. It is wrapped tightly in plastic film and left to sit for at least half an hour, which gives the gluten enough time to relax. Finally, the rested dough is flattened into a thin sheet using a pasta machine. From there, you can either slice the sheet into thin, evenly sized strands for spaghetti, or cut the whole piece into much wider ribbons to make pappardelle.

The diagram shows the process of coffee from production to preparing for sale in supermarkets and shops.
Overall, it is certain that coffee goes through 11 different stages from picking the beans to packing in jars.
Firstly, coffee beans are picked and then dried by humans. The dried beans are later roasted in a machine then in the blast freezer machine, it's cooled down rapidly. Furthermore, after cooling out they go through a grinding process where they turned into powder, then mixed with high-temperature water after a while, it goes from straining process.
We are now arriving at the later process where coffee is now frozen in the refrigerator. In the third last stage, again it goes from the grinding process but this time it is frozen and after this, they put them in a vacuum machine where it evaporates the water from coffee to make them dry.
Lastly, the coffee is ready to pack in the jar and ready to sell on the market.

The diagram shows the process of tea from production to preparing for sale in supermarkets and shops.
Overall, it is certain that tea goes through 10 different stages from picking the leaves to packing in boxes.
Firstly, tea leaves are picked and then withered by humans. The withered leaves are later rolled in a machine then in the oxidation room, it's fermented slowly. Furthermore, after fermenting they go through a firing process where they turned into dry flakes, then sorted with high-tech equipment after a while, it goes from grading process.
We are now arriving at the later process where tea is now blended in the factory. In the third last stage, again it goes from the sorting process but this time it is flavored and after this, they put them in a packaging machine where it weighs the portions of tea to make them precise.
Lastly, the tea is ready to pack in the box and ready to sell on the market.

The provided diagram illustrates the step-by-step process of producing tea, from the initial harvesting of the leaves to the final retail packaging. Overall, the production involves ten distinct stages, beginning with hand-picking and ending with the finished product boxed up for consumers.
The process begins when fresh tea leaves are manually harvested and laid out to wither. Once the leaves have lost a sufficient amount of moisture, they are fed into a rolling machine. This prepares them for the oxidation room, where they are left to slowly ferment. Following fermentation, the leaves undergo a high-heat firing process that completely dries them out, turning them into crisp flakes. Afterward, specialized equipment is used to sort and grade the tea based on size and quality.
In the final stages of production, the graded leaves are blended. Depending on the specific product line, they may also be flavored at this point. Next, the tea is routed into an automated packaging machine that precisely weighs out the required portions. Finally, the processed tea is sealed into boxes and distributed to supermarkets for sale.

The line graph and chart below show data on the average daily maximum and minimum temperatures in degrees Celsius and the average number of days with rainfall each month, from January to December, for Brisbane and Canberra in Australia.
Overall, both cities have similar tendencies throughout the entire period. Likewise, Canberra has lower temperatures than Brisbane during the whole year. In addition, Brisbane has a higher average number of days with rainfall than Canberra during most of the year.
To start with, Canberra's maximum average daily temperatures have a significant decrease in tendency, from 27 degrees in January to only 11 degrees in July. In contrast, Brisbane's daily maximum temperatures present lower fluctuations from 28 degrees in January to just 21 degrees in July. Additionally, Brisbane reaches its highest maximum average daily temperature in February at 30 degrees, whilst Canberra reaches its lowest minimum average daily temperature in June and July at 2 degrees. Furthermore, the average number of days with rainfall decreases moderately during the winter season in Brisbane, from 14 days in February to 7 days in June, July and August. However, this is the opposite tendency in the average number of days with rainfall in Canberra which slightly increases from 7 days in February to 10 days in June, July and August.
To sum up, Canberra has rainy and cold winters, whilst Brisbane has rainy and warm summers.

The line graph and chart below show data on the average daily maximum and minimum temperatures in degrees Celsius and the average number of days with rainfall each month, from January to December, for Sydney and Melbourne in Australia.
Overall, both cities have similar tendencies throughout the entire period. Likewise, Melbourne has lower temperatures than Sydney during the whole year. In addition, Sydney has a higher average number of days with rainfall than Melbourne during most of the year.
To start with, Melbourne's maximum average daily temperatures have a significant decrease in tendency, from 26 degrees in January to only 13 degrees in July. In contrast, Sydney's daily maximum temperatures present lower fluctuations from 27 degrees in January to just 18 degrees in July. Additionally, Sydney reaches its highest maximum average daily temperature in February at 28 degrees, whilst Melbourne reaches its lowest minimum average daily temperature in July and August at 6 degrees. Furthermore, the average number of days with rainfall decreases moderately during the spring season in Sydney, from 15 days in March to 8 days in September, October and November. However, this is the opposite tendency in the average number of days with rainfall in Melbourne which slightly increases from 8 days in February to 14 days in July, August and September.
To sum up, Melbourne has rainy and cool winters, whilst Sydney has rainy and warm autumns.

The provided charts compare the average monthly temperatures and rainfall frequency for Sydney and Melbourne over the course of a year. Overall, while both cities follow the same general seasonal cycle, Melbourne stays noticeably cooler than Sydney year-round. Sydney also tends to experience more rainy days, though the two cities see their wettest weather at completely different times of the year.
Looking at the temperature data, Melbourne's climate is much more variable. Its average daily maximum drops sharply from a summer peak of 26 degrees in January down to just 13 degrees by July. Sydney, on the other hand, enjoys much milder seasonal shifts. Its warmest days average around 28 degrees in February, and the city only cools down to about 18 degrees mid-year. During the dead of winter—specifically July and August—Melbourne's overnight lows routinely dip to a chilly 6 degrees.
Rainfall trends also move in opposite directions. In Sydney, rainy days are most frequent in early autumn, peaking at about 15 days in March before drying out to around 8 days per month throughout the spring. Melbourne sees the reverse pattern. Its late summers are relatively dry, with just 8 rainy days in February, but the city gets steadily wetter as the year progresses, averaging 14 rainy days a month from July through September.
Ultimately, Melbourne is characterized by its cold, damp winters, whereas Sydney sees its heaviest rainfall while the weather is still warm.

It is not uncommon for teenagers to worship celebrities. From singers to actors, many young people around the world look up to these stars and attempt to emulate their lifestyles. In my opinion, allowing children to idolize celebrities can often be detrimental, as they frequently set a poor example.Many of today’s celebrities tend to lack moral values, and they promote behaviour inappropriate for young people. For instance, some music videos feature explicit lyrics glorifying drug use and violence. In addition, the media often reports on celebrities who indulge in excessive partying or drinking alcohol despite being underage. These types of headlines can have a negative influence on impressionable teens who might falsely believe that they should follow suit to appear cool or successful.Poor morals aren’t the only issue, as many popular stars are also well-known for their irresponsible spending habits. They routinely splurge on luxury items such as designer clothing or expensive cars without any consideration for budgeting or saving money responsibly. This kind of behaviour is dangerous because it is normalizing materialism and teaching teens that costly possessions will bring them happiness instead of focusing on what really matters in life: meaningful relationships, knowledge, and hard work.To conclude, young adults should keep in mind that society’s perception of fame and fortune is based on temporary, insubstantial things rather than what actually matters. In the grand scheme of things, it would be far more beneficial for teenagers to spend time developing their own set of values rooted in quality education and strong family relationships.

It is not uncommon for teenagers to follow social media influencers. From daily vloggers to fashion models, many young people around the world look up to these internet personalities and attempt to emulate their lifestyles. In my opinion, allowing children to idolize influencers can often be detrimental, as they frequently set unrealistic expectations.
Many of today’s social media stars tend to present heavily edited versions of reality, and they promote standards that are unattainable for young people. For instance, some popular creators use digital filters to showcase flawless appearances and luxurious vacations. In addition, the internet often rewards influencers who engage in dramatic controversies or dangerous pranks to gain views. These types of content can have a negative influence on impressionable teens who might falsely believe that they should act similarly to gain attention or social validation.
Unrealistic lifestyles aren’t the only issue, as many internet personalities are also well-known for their relentless promotion of consumerism. They routinely advertise expensive beauty products or trendy fashion items without any consideration for the financial burden it places on their audience. This kind of behaviour is dangerous because it is normalizing hyper-consumerism and teaching teens that purchasing advertised goods will bring them happiness instead of focusing on what really matters in life: genuine experiences, personal growth, and hard work.
To conclude, young adults should keep in mind that the internet's perception of success and beauty is based on curated, superficial things rather than what actually matters. In the grand scheme of things, it would be far more beneficial for teenagers to spend time developing their own self-esteem rooted in real-world achievements and authentic friendships.

It seems like almost every teenager today follows at least a few social media influencers. Whether they are watching daily vloggers or fashion icons, kids everywhere are glued to these online personalities, often trying to copy how they dress and live. But honestly, I think treating influencers like role models does significantly more harm than good, primarily because they are selling a reality that simply doesn't exist.
The main issue is that these internet stars curate heavily edited versions of their lives, pushing lifestyle standards that are entirely out of reach for the average kid. A quick scroll through a feed usually turns up endless photos tweaked with digital filters, showing off flawless skin and non-stop, expensive vacations. On top of that, social media platforms tend to reward creators who pull dangerous stunts or stir up petty drama just to get clicks. For an impressionable teenager, consuming this content daily can be incredibly damaging. They can easily fall into the trap of thinking they need to act out or look physically perfect just to be liked or noticed by their peers.
Beyond the fake lifestyles, there is also the relentless pressure to buy things. Influencers are practically walking billboards, constantly pushing their followers to purchase the latest skincare routine or fast-fashion haul without caring if these items are actually affordable for their young audience. This endless cycle of sponsored posts is dangerous. It normalizes hyper-consumerism and convinces kids that buying the right products is the fast track to happiness, pulling their attention away from things that actually matter like personal growth and real-world experiences.
Ultimately, teenagers need to understand that the glossy success they see online is highly curated and entirely superficial. Rather than trying to emulate a stranger's digital highlight reel, kids would be much better off focusing on their actual lives—building authentic friendships, working hard, and developing genuine self-confidence.

The bar chart depicts the proportion of the total world population in 4 countries in 1950 and 2002, and predictions for 2050. Overall, it can be seen India and China occupy much higher percentage in all periods while the USA, and Japan tend to be much smaller and drop down in 2002.
It is clear that India steadily increases the percentage of its population since 1950. Specifically, the percentage in each period is 15 percent, around 17 percent, and will be approximately 18 percent, in 1950, 2002, and 2050 respectively. In contrast, China used to be the highest percentage of population in 1950 and 2002. The each percentage indicates more than 23 percent in 1950 and dropped to 20 percent in 2002. Furthermore, the prediction about population will plummet to 15 percent.
Interestingly, firstly Japan was the smallest percentage in 4 countries in 1950 and slightly goes down in 2002. The percentage seems to be about 4 percent in 1950 and its percentage will plummet to approximately 1 percent in 2050. The bar chart depicts the proportion of total global energy consumption in 4 countries in 1980 and 2010, and predictions for 2040. Overall, it can be seen China and the USA occupy a much higher percentage in all periods while Russia, and Germany tend to be much smaller and drop down in 2010.
It is clear that China steadily increases the percentage of its consumption since 1980. Specifically, the percentage in each period is 10 percent, around 20 percent, and will be approximately 25 percent, in 1980, 2010, and 2040 respectively. In contrast, the USA used to have the highest percentage of consumption in 1980 and 2010. The percentage indicates more than 25 percent in 1980 and dropped to 22 percent in 2010. Furthermore, the prediction about consumption will plummet to 18 percent.
Interestingly, firstly Germany was the smallest percentage in 4 countries in 1980 and slightly goes down in 2010. The percentage seems to be about 6 percent in 1980 and its percentage will plummet to approximately 2 percent in 2040. Russia also goes down to 10 percent in 2010, but the percentage will stay the same in 2040.

This bar chart illustrates how global energy consumption is distributed among four major nations across three distinct years: 1980, 2010, and a projected outlook for 2040. Right away, it is obvious that the United States and China dominate global energy use, taking up the vast majority of the share across all timeframes. Meanwhile, Russia and Germany account for significantly smaller fractions, with both showing a downward trend following 1980.
When looking closely at the data, China's energy footprint shows rapid, uninterrupted growth. The country accounted for just 10% of global consumption in 1980, doubled that figure to roughly 20% by 2010, and is projected to hit nearly 25% by 2040. The United States tells a completely different story. While it was easily the biggest energy consumer historically—claiming over a quarter of the global total in 1980—its share shrank to 22% by 2010. Forecasters expect this decline to continue, projecting a further drop to roughly 18% by 2040.
As for the smaller consumers, Germany consistently held the lowest share of the group. Starting at about 6% in 1980, its slice of the pie dipped slightly by 2010 and is expected to shrink to a mere 2% by 2040. Russia experienced a similar, albeit less drastic, reduction. After dropping to a 10% share in 2010, its proportion is expected to plateau and remain at that exact same level through the 2040 projections.

The provided chart breaks down the percentage of Australian men and women who met the national guidelines for daily physical exercise in 2010, categorized by age. Overall, younger Australians were far more likely to work out every day, with men generally maintaining more active lifestyles than women in most age brackets.
Looking closely at the data, it is clear that daily physical activity peaks during youth and steadily declines with age. The 15-24 demographic was the most active overall, with roughly 45% of young men and 42% of young women hitting the daily recommendation. Interestingly, as people reach retirement age, this gender divide completely disappears. In fact, the 65 and older cohort was the only category on the entire chart where men and women reported the exact same rate of daily exercise.
Middle-aged residents, on the other hand, struggled the most to stay active. Those between 35 and 44 years old recorded the lowest exercise rates across the board, hovering right around 25% for both sexes, though men still held a slight edge. The most noticeable gender gap, however, appeared just slightly later in life. Among 45- to 54-year-olds, male participation jumped back up to about 30%, leaving women trailing significantly behind at just 22%.

This chart breaks down how much coffee, tea, and milk people drink annually across four different nations: the UK, Brazil, Japan, and the United States. All figures are measured in liters per person.
Looking first at tea, the UK easily takes the top spot. Japan trails distantly in second place, leaving both the US and Brazil at the very bottom of the chart. Coffee, on the other hand, tells a completely different story. Brazil drinks the most by a significant margin, though the United States follows closely behind. When it comes to milk, Americans consume the highest volume, easily doubling the per capita intake of the British.
Overall, the cultural divides are striking. British tea consumption is roughly three times higher than Japan's, completely dwarfing the other two nations. Meanwhile, Brazil's coffee intake exceeds American numbers by more than 30%, and US milk consumption heavily outpaces the rest of the group.

The bar charts below illustrate the different distributions of people who bought pop, rock, and classical music based on gender and age.

However, the percentages for people of different ages fond of classical music showed a huge disparity from the other two types.

Among the four age groups, teenagers (16-24) and young people (25-35) were main pop and rock music supporters, with each group contributing roughly 30%. On the other hand, regardless of their gender, classical music lovers only made half proportion as much as those who love pop and rock music. Interestingly, slightly over 20% of people older than 45 paid for classical music albums, while teenagers (16-24) and middle-aged people (25-35) accounted for no more than 3% combined in this category. Nevertheless, young adults who love classical music reached a surprising record at 16%.

However, the percentages for people of different ages fond of documentary films showed a huge disparity from the other two genres.
In detail, nearly 30% of males watched action or comedy films, compared to approximately 19% of female counterparts.
