On the other hand, regardless of their gender, documentary film lovers only made half proportion as much as those who love action and comedy films. Interestingly, slightly over 20% of people older than 45 paid for documentary films, while teenagers (16-24) and middle-aged people (25-35) accounted for no more than 3% combined in this category. Nevertheless, young adults who love documentary films reached a surprising record at 16%.

These bar charts break down the demographics of moviegoers who prefer action, comedy, and documentary films, sorted by both age and gender. Right away, you can see that men made up a larger share of the audience across all three categories. Action and comedy drew very similar crowds, but the viewer profile for documentaries looked completely different.
For action and comedy, male viewership sat at around 30%, noticeably higher than the 19% of women who chose these genres. Unsurprisingly, younger audiences drove most of these numbers. Teenagers and young adults between 16 and 35 made up the bulk of the fanbase, each bracket accounting for roughly 30% of the total. That interest naturally tapers off with age; about a quarter of 35- to 45-year-olds watched these fast-paced films, but that figure plummeted to just 10% for the over-45 crowd.
Documentaries, meanwhile, attracted a much smaller audience overall—roughly half the size of the action and comedy viewership, regardless of gender. The age breakdown also flips the script entirely. While teenagers and the youngest adults barely registered—making up less than 3% of the documentary audience combined—older viewers showed up in high numbers. In fact, more than 20% of moviegoers over 45 opted for documentaries. Interestingly, there was also a surprising spike among one specific younger demographic, with 16% of older twenty-somethings regularly choosing non-fiction films.

Sport play an essential role in shaping the personality of individual. Some people believe that sports which are played in teams are more beneficial like football, while others think that solo sports like tennis or swimming is better. The following essay will discuss both arguments and give my opinion that sports which are played in teams have more advantages over solo sport activites.
First of all, there is no doubt that the individual sport activities enahnce people confidence. It helps building the personality and strentghen the mentality of the individuals. For examole, one role model in my home country was playing tennis table, he mentioned once in an interview, that sport helped him not only to win trophies but it raised his mental strength and shaped his pesonality.
However, some people believe that, sports which are played in teams
have more advantages and all community will get the benefits. Soccer, for example, will help the players specifically the young children to understand working together and sharing the responsibility. In addition people will grow with better understanding to the concept of sharing achievements which can improve all the community. For instance, i was a member of soccer team in high school, me and my friends were taught, in order to win games we should put plans together and work as one unit. Furthermore, it helps me later in my career to understand the meaning of leadership and how the sharing responsibility and working together could bring the success to our endeavour.
To conclude, solo sport can bring proud and honour to individuals but
sports which played in teams teach people the meaning of working together which will improve the community in the future.

Traveling plays an essential role in broadening the perspective of an individual. Some people believe that trips which are taken in groups are more beneficial like guided tours, while others think that solo travel like backpacking alone is better. The following essay will discuss both arguments and give my opinion that trips which are taken in groups have more advantages over solo travel activities.
First of all, there is no doubt that individual travel activities enhance people's independence. It helps building the problem-solving skills and strengthen the adaptability of the individuals. For example, one famous travel blogger in my home country was exploring Europe alone, he mentioned once in an interview, that traveling helped him not only to see landmarks but it raised his self-reliance and shaped his personality.
However, some people believe that, trips which are taken in groups have more advantages and all members will get the benefits. Group tours, for example, will help the travelers specifically the young adults to understand compromising together and sharing the experiences. In addition people will grow with better understanding to the concept of sharing memories which can improve all the relationships. For instance, I was a member of a travel club in university, me and my friends were taught, in order to enjoy trips we should put itineraries together and work as one unit. Furthermore, it helps me later in my life to understand the meaning of collaboration and how the sharing planning and traveling together could bring the success to our journey.
To conclude, solo travel can bring independence and self-discovery to individuals but trips which taken in groups teach people the meaning of experiencing the world together which will improve the interpersonal skills in the future.

Traveling is one of the best ways to broaden your perspective. When it comes to planning a trip, people often debate whether it is better to join a group tour or head out backpacking entirely alone. While flying solo definitely has its perks, I firmly believe that exploring with others offers a much richer and more rewarding experience.
There is no denying that traveling alone forces you to become more independent. Navigating a foreign city by yourself builds incredible problem-solving skills and makes you highly adaptable. I remember reading an interview with a well-known travel blogger who spent a year exploring Europe on his own; he credited that solo journey with completely transforming his self-reliance and shaping who he became as an adult.
Despite those benefits, sharing a journey with a group brings unique advantages that you simply cannot get on your own. For younger travelers in particular, group trips teach the invaluable art of compromise. You learn how to navigate different personalities and share responsibilities, whether that means splitting the cost of accommodation or taking turns picking out restaurants. I actually joined a university travel club a few years ago, and organizing our itineraries taught us how to operate as a cohesive team. Looking back, that experience really improved my ability to collaborate. It showed me that working together to overcome inevitable travel hiccups usually leads to a much more successful and memorable trip.
Ultimately, while exploring the world by yourself is a great way to discover your own independence, traveling alongside a group fosters deep connections and teaches vital interpersonal skills that last a lifetime.

The bar graphs shed light on impetuses driving people to choose the major of MBA and job applicants' qualities considered by recruiters.
Overall, there are mainly two features manifested by the diagrams. First, satisfactory employment opportunities are the major reason for choosing this degree. Second, personality and relevant academic experience are regarded as the most critical qualities by a majority of employers.
According to the first chart, the percentage of interviewees deeming job opportunities to be the major reason for choosing this major reaches 65%, while any other reason is reported by less than 20%. Notably, serving other people is the most uncommon driving force, reported by only 8%.
As shown in the second chart, the percentage of recruiters valuing personalities most is the same as that of employers attaching the greatest importance to academic experience, each reaching 25%. Other relevant experience and presentation/appearance are reported by 20% of employers respectively. Qualifications are deemed to be the essential quality by the smallest percentage of recruiters, 10%.

The bar graphs shed light on impetuses driving people to choose the major of Data Science and job applicants' qualities considered by tech recruiters.
Overall, there are mainly two features manifested by the diagrams. First, high salary expectations are the major reason for choosing this degree. Second, coding proficiency and relevant internship experience are regarded as the most critical qualities by a majority of employers.
According to the first chart, the percentage of interviewees deeming high salaries to be the major reason for choosing this major reaches 60%, while any other reason is reported by less than 25%. Notably, contributing to academic research is the most uncommon driving force, reported by only 5%.
As shown in the second chart, the percentage of recruiters valuing coding proficiency most is the same as that of employers attaching the greatest importance to internship experience, each reaching 30%. Communication skills and problem-solving abilities are reported by 15% of employers respectively. Formal certifications are deemed to be the essential quality by the smallest percentage of recruiters, 10%.

These two bar charts break down why students decide to pursue a degree in Data Science, alongside what tech recruiters actually prioritize when making hiring decisions. Right away, two main trends stand out: students are heavily motivated by the prospect of a high salary, whereas employers are looking for hard coding skills and real-world experience.
When it comes to choosing the major, the promise of a lucrative paycheck clearly dominates. A full 60% of the surveyed students pointed to high salary expectations as their primary motivation, leaving all other reasons trailing behind at under 25%. Interestingly, a desire to contribute to academic research barely registered as a driving force, motivating just 5% of the group.
The employer data reveals exactly what it takes to actually land a job in the field. Hiring managers placed the highest premium on practical capability, with coding proficiency and previous internship experience tying for the top spot at 30% each. Soft skills, such as communication and general problem-solving, were less of a priority, capturing 15% of the vote respectively. Perhaps surprisingly, formal certifications ranked at the very bottom of the list, considered an essential quality by only 10% of the recruiters surveyed.

Nowadays, the Internet has become without doubt the most powerful, convenient and free source of information and communication. It has dramatically changed how we communicate with each other and how we obtain news. With only one push of button on our devices like a smart phone or a laptop we can speak our family members who are thousand miles away from us or read current news without a need for watching TV or buying a newspaper. However, this convenience of the Internet carries some potential threats to the users such as violation of their privacy rights and overflow of unreliable information. Therefore, the users should demand some forms of regulations of the Internet to reduce these risks.
Certainly, the use of the Internet needs to be regulated if we want to preserve our privacy when using it. Unfortunately, there is an enormous flow of our personal data on the Internet. For example, we eagerly share a lot of information about ourselves simply from our name, address, phone number to more serious one like our accounts number or social security. However, the big sales companies very often buy an access to this data to target us and influence our choices. This practice should be illegal and we as the users need to have a say how our personal data should be handled on the Internet. For this reason, we need to demand privacy laws to have better control how our personal information is managed on the Internet.
Another risk of having the Internet free from regulation is that we have no means of protection from unreliable information. I find this risk very dangerous since many people have used the Internet as their primary source of news and rely on it to stay updated with daily news. Now, anybody can become a reporter as long as he or she has access to the Internet. Simply, people can write and post their comments that often don’t have any factual or scientific base. We have witnessed this misinformation during the debate regarding vaccinations for the COVID-19. Many people who objected to be vaccinated made up their mind reading some posts on the Facebook or the Twitter that stated harmfulness of vaccines. These posts are allowed to be posted, because the social media don’t have any obligation to check the content of posts. Clearly, this wouldn’t happen if there were some forms of regulations that can verify better factual content of information on the Internet, so the potential readers can avoid the risk of misinformation.
Surely, the Internet can no longer stay without any regulatory laws. These policies are necessary to protect our privacy and keep us informed with more reliable information. Simply, the regulation polices of the Internet give us more power to be in control when using the Internet to obtain information or share personal data.

Nowadays, social media has become without doubt the most popular, engaging and accessible form of entertainment and communication. It has dramatically changed how teenagers interact with each other and how they form their identities. With only one push of a button on their devices like a smartphone or a tablet, they can share their daily activities with friends who are thousands of miles away or watch entertaining videos without a need for going outside. However, this convenience of social media carries some potential threats to young users such as negative impacts on their mental health and exposure to inappropriate content. Therefore, the public should demand some forms of age regulations on social media to reduce these risks.
Certainly, the use of social media needs to be regulated if we want to preserve the mental well-being of young people. Unfortunately, there is an enormous pressure to maintain a perfect image on the Internet. For example, teenagers eagerly consume a lot of content about unrealistic beauty standards, luxurious lifestyles, and filtered photos. However, the algorithms of these applications very often promote this content to target them and influence their self-esteem. This practice should be controlled and we as a society need to have a say how digital content should be recommended to minors on the Internet. For this reason, we need to demand stricter algorithms to have better control how media affects young minds on social platforms.
Another risk of having social media free from regulation is that we have no means of protection from harmful trends. I find this risk very dangerous since many children have used these platforms as their primary source of entertainment and rely on them to spend their free time. Now, anybody can upload a video as long as he or she has access to the Internet. Simply, people can create and post their media that often don’t have any age-appropriate filters. We have witnessed this danger during the viral spread of dangerous online challenges. Many kids who participated in these challenges made up their mind watching some videos on TikTok or Instagram that depicted risky behavior. These videos are allowed to be posted, because the tech companies don’t have any strict obligation to verify the age of their users. Clearly, this wouldn’t happen if there were some forms of regulations that can verify better the age of users on the Internet, so the young viewers can avoid the risk of harmful exposure.
Surely, social media can no longer stay without any regulatory laws. These policies are necessary to protect youth mental health and keep them safe from dangerous trends. Simply, the regulation policies of social networks give parents more power to be in control when their children use these platforms to interact or consume media.

There is no denying that social media completely dominates how we connect and entertain ourselves today. It has fundamentally rewired how teenagers build relationships and figure out who they are. With just a quick swipe on a phone, kids can chat with distant friends or scroll through endless streams of videos instead of hanging out in person. But giving children unrestricted access to these platforms comes with massive downsides—namely, serious mental health struggles and exposure to wildly inappropriate content. Because of this, it is becoming clear that we need much stricter age regulations across these apps.
If we actually want to protect the mental well-being of the younger generation, we have to start policing how these platforms operate. Right now, the pressure to look and act perfect online is suffocating. Teenagers are constantly bombarded with heavily edited photos, impossible beauty standards, and influencers flaunting luxury lifestyles. Worse still, the algorithms are designed to push this exact type of content directly into their feeds to keep them hooked, often destroying their self-esteem in the process. We shouldn't just accept this as normal. We desperately need oversight to dictate how these recommendation algorithms target minors, giving us a fighting chance to protect their mental health.
Beyond the psychological toll, a lack of age verification leaves kids completely exposed to harmful trends. Many children now spend the bulk of their free time scrolling, treating social media as their main source of entertainment. The problem is that anyone with an internet connection can upload anything, usually without dealing with any meaningful content filters. Just look at the wave of dangerous viral challenges we've seen in recent years. Kids end up attempting incredibly risky stunts simply because they watched a few trending videos on their feeds. Tech companies currently get away with hosting this material because they aren't legally forced to verify how old their users actually are. If strict age gates were mandated by law, we could finally stop young, impressionable users from stumbling into this kind of danger.
Ultimately, the "Wild West" era of social media has to end. Implementing firm age restrictions isn't about censorship; it's a necessary step to safeguard youth mental health and block access to dangerous trends. Putting these laws in place would finally give parents the leverage they need to keep their kids safe online.

The two pie chart depict the outcome of a survey about the guest service of the Parkway Hotel in 2005 and 2010 on 100 customers.
Overall, 5 scale of the customer service are illustrated on each pie chart. In both year, the bulk of customer feedback are positive and profitable to the hotel. However, in 2005, the two smallest rated opininon will become the first highest in 2010.
In 2005, guests deserved mostly a negative review, namely between satisfactory and very poor. Nevertheless, this trend update to a positive one, namely between excellent and satisfactory in the year 2010. In five years, even if the worst feedback led almost to a fourfold decrease, the second last negative review doubled in 2010.
Regarding to the feeling of a positive customer service, there was an approximate 25% growth from both outstanding and good guest service. This is that the medium satisfaction has a downward trend over the five years from 45% to 17%

The two pie charts depict the outcome of a survey about the customer service of the Riverside Restaurant in 2010 and 2015 based on 100 diners.
Overall, 5 scales of customer satisfaction are illustrated on each pie chart. In both years, the bulk of customer feedback was generally positive and beneficial to the restaurant. However, in 2010, the two highest rated opinions were relatively small, but they became the most dominant in 2015.
In 2010, guests provided a notable amount of negative reviews, ranging between satisfactory and very poor. Nevertheless, this trend updated to a highly positive one, ranging between excellent and good in the year 2015. In five years, even though the worst feedback experienced almost a fourfold decrease, the poor review category dropped significantly as well in 2015.
Regarding the feeling of positive customer service, there was an approximate 30% growth for both outstanding and good restaurant service combined. It is also notable that the medium satisfaction level experienced a downward trend over the five years, decreasing from 40% to 15%.

These two pie charts break down the results of customer satisfaction surveys conducted at the Riverside Restaurant in 2010 and 2015, capturing the feedback of 100 diners each year. Right away, you can see a massive improvement in how people viewed their dining experience over that five-year span.
Back in 2010, the restaurant struggled with a noticeable chunk of negative feedback. A significant portion of the respondents rated their visits anywhere from just "satisfactory" down to "very poor." By 2015, however, the situation had completely turned around. The absolute worst reviews plummeted to a quarter of what they used to be, and the standard "poor" ratings shrank dramatically alongside them.
As those negative scores faded, the higher ratings naturally surged. The combined share of diners who felt the service was "excellent" or "good" jumped by roughly 30%. Interestingly, as more people moved into these top-tier categories, the middle-of-the-road "satisfactory" rating took a steep dive, dropping from 40% of the total responses in 2010 down to just 15% in 2015. Ultimately, the data shows that the restaurant clearly managed to elevate its average, everyday service into a genuinely outstanding experience.

Throughout modern history there have always been professional and gifted individuals from developed countries who aspire to immigrate into more developed societies. Recently this trend has been more emphatic resulting in a disputable discussion which has been widely talked about. Should rich governments permit local workplaces to recruit foreign employees? I personally agree with this point of view and I will elaborate on the reasons in this essay.
There are several possible advantages of hosting employers from poor countries. First, foreign employees can bring with them new innovations and methods of research which in turn will enrich the local markets and possibly contribute to enhancing the knowledge. In addition, foreign employers are generally paid lower income in comparison to local workers, this could save up money for other investments. Moreover, a swarm of skilled immigrants will probably increase the competition among local employers and encourage them to improve in different aspect in order to secure employment.
There are other aspects which merit consideration. Most of the foreign employers would utilize their occupation in the developed countries in improving their skills and knowledge and might consider going back to their homelands. This knowledge transfer would probably benefit both sides. As developed nations will employ the new comers and harness their experience in developing the education, health and other forms. Interestingly, this prosperity in developed countries will presumably lower the influx of unemployed and illiterate immigrants into the rich countries.
To conclude, I agree with the title statement regarding the several advantages of such an action. Historically, if rich countries had adopted skilled people from poor nations, there would have probably been fewer immigrants into their developed countries with all the know repercussions of this phenomenon.

Throughout recent decades, a growing number of talented professionals from developing nations have chosen to pursue careers in wealthier countries, leaving behind their places of origin. This phenomenon, commonly referred to as brain drain, has sparked considerable debate among policymakers and academics alike. Should developing countries take active measures to retain their skilled workforce, or should they encourage free movement? I personally believe that allowing skilled workers to seek opportunities abroad carries significant long-term benefits for all parties involved.
There are several notable advantages associated with permitting skilled professionals to work overseas. To begin with, workers who gain experience in advanced economies often return with valuable knowledge, updated practices, and broader professional networks. These individuals can subsequently contribute to improving local industries, educational institutions, and healthcare systems. Furthermore, many emigrants send remittances back to their home countries, providing financial support to families and stimulating local economies. This influx of foreign currency can be substantial in smaller or lower-income nations, often exceeding official development aid.
Additional considerations reinforce this perspective. Exposure to competitive international environments pushes skilled workers to refine their abilities and adopt global standards of excellence. Upon returning, these professionals tend to introduce improved methodologies and foster innovation within local institutions. Moreover, countries that maintain open pathways for their citizens to work abroad often develop stronger diplomatic and economic ties with host nations, which can facilitate future trade and cooperation agreements.
In conclusion, while concerns about losing talented individuals are understandable, the broader picture suggests that encouraging skilled emigration can yield positive outcomes for developing nations in the long run. Historically, countries that have embraced this openness have often seen greater economic development and stronger international partnerships compared to those that imposed restrictions on their workforce.

Over the past few decades, talented professionals from poorer nations have increasingly left to build careers in wealthier countries. The debate around this trend — often called brain drain — has grown louder among policymakers and academics. Should developing countries try harder to keep their best workers at home, or does free movement actually serve everyone better? My view is that allowing skilled workers to seek opportunities abroad tends to benefit all sides, even if the advantages are not always immediate.
The most obvious benefit is the knowledge workers bring back. Professionals who spend years in advanced economies do not just earn higher wages — they return with updated practices, broader networks, and exposure to systems that simply do not yet exist in their home countries. That kind of knowledge transfer can do more for a local hospital or university than years of government funding alone. On top of that, emigrants routinely send money home. Remittances from overseas workers frequently outstrip official development aid in smaller economies, giving families financial stability and injecting real currency into local markets.
There is more to consider beyond the economic angle. Working in competitive international settings pushes people to sharpen skills they might never have developed otherwise. When they return — and many do — they tend to raise standards around them, sometimes quite dramatically. Countries that keep these pathways open also tend to build stronger diplomatic and trade ties with the nations hosting their citizens, opening doors in other areas entirely.
The fear of permanently losing talented people is real and worth taking seriously. But looking at the longer arc, nations that have remained open to skilled emigration have generally fared better economically and diplomatically than those that tried to lock talent in. The goal should not be to stop people from leaving — it should be to create conditions worth coming back to.

Some people think that outdoor activities are more beneficial for children's development than playing computer games. This essay will elaborate a point of agreement to the above statement.
To commence with the first reason, outdoor activities are good for children's health. As children run and play with ones' friends, outdoor activities will apparently relieve the stress. Furthermore, it is scientifically proven that an exercise is beneficial for all mankind as it is recommended by the doctors and scientists. For instance, doctors encourage the patients who have non-communicable diseases, known as lifestyle illnesses, to exercise more. Thus, it will prevent children from getting illnesses.
Additionally, spending time with one's friends will definitely help a child to build a healthy relationship with others. As a child learns universal values and realizes important aspects of life, it will greatly influence the way a child will live one's life. For example, a child can learn sportsmanship while playing sports with his or her friend.
On the other hand, playing computer games can lead a child to be addicted with electronic gadgets. Addiction is one of the most risky things which may put one's life into a pit. If a computer game consists of violent or aggressive contents within it, a child may get influenced. There are a great number of reports and thesis which focus on how computer games affect the children's behaviour in a negative way.
Moreover, electrical gadgets affect one's eyesight. Nowdays, as most of people are exposed to the lights from the devices, it can be easily proven that there are more people with glasses comparing to the past. Using a computer for a short period of time is not risky, however, using a computer for a long time for certain purposes, even if it is for research work, it will affect one's eyesight badly.
To conclude, it is proven that outdoor activities are more beneficial than playing computer games. Therefore, communities, parents, and teachers ought to encourage children to do outdoor activities rather than ordering a child be sitted for a long time, the act which causes unhealthiness.

Some people argue that participating in outdoor activities provides greater developmental benefits for children than engaging with video games. This essay agrees with that position and will present several reasons to support it.
To begin with, outdoor activities contribute significantly to children's physical health. When children run, jump, and engage in active play, their bodies develop strength and endurance while stress is naturally reduced. Medical professionals and researchers have long supported the view that regular physical activity is essential for human health. Doctors, for instance, routinely advise patients suffering from lifestyle-related conditions such as obesity or type 2 diabetes to incorporate more movement into their daily routines. Encouraging children to be active outdoors therefore serves as a preventative measure against such illnesses later in life.
In addition, outdoor activities create opportunities for children to develop meaningful social skills. Interacting with peers in a shared physical environment teaches children how to communicate, cooperate, and resolve conflicts. These experiences help shape a child's character and inform how they relate to others throughout life. Playing team sports, for example, instills values such as fairness, perseverance, and respect for others, qualities that are difficult to develop through a screen.
By contrast, excessive engagement with video games carries notable risks. Children who spend large amounts of time gaming may develop unhealthy dependencies on electronic devices. Furthermore, exposure to violent or aggressive content in certain games has been linked in numerous studies to behavioral changes in young players. The cumulative effect of such exposure can be harmful to a child's emotional and psychological development.
Prolonged screen time also poses a direct threat to children's eyesight. Increased rates of vision problems in younger generations have been widely observed and are frequently associated with extended device use. Even when screen time is used for educational purposes, the physical strain on the eyes remains a genuine concern.
In conclusion, outdoor activity is clearly more beneficial to children's overall development than playing video games. It is therefore the responsibility of parents, educators, and communities to actively promote and encourage children's participation in outdoor play.

There is a widely held view that outdoor activities do more for a child's development than video games do. This essay takes the same position, and the following paragraphs explain why.
Physical health is perhaps the most obvious benefit. Running around outside, whether in organized sport or simple free play, builds strength, burns energy, and helps keep stress in check. This is not just common sense — doctors have backed it for decades. Patients with conditions like obesity or type 2 diabetes are regularly told to move more, and the logic applies just as well to children. Habits formed early tend to stick, so an active childhood is one of the better defenses against lifestyle diseases down the road.
There is also a social dimension that outdoor play handles particularly well. When children share a physical space — a playground, a football pitch, a park — they have to deal with each other in real time. They negotiate, argue, make up, and eventually figure out how to get along. Team sports are a useful example: a child learning to lose gracefully or to support a struggling teammate is picking up something that no amount of screen time easily replicates.
Video games, on the other hand, come with real downsides when use becomes excessive. The risk of dependency on devices is well documented, and for younger children especially, it can creep up quickly. Games with violent or aggressive content add another layer of concern — a body of research has consistently found connections between such content and shifts in children's behavior and emotional responses.
Vision is another issue worth raising. It has become almost unremarkable how many young people now wear glasses, and prolonged exposure to screens is a significant factor. Even when a child is using a computer for something productive, the physical strain on the eyes is still present.
All things considered, outdoor activity comes out well ahead of gaming when it comes to children's development. Parents, teachers, and communities share a responsibility here — not to ban screens outright, but to make sure children are spending enough time outside to grow up healthy, socially capable, and physically well.

The Internet is employed extensively across the world, serving as an essential tool of communication. Nevertheless, the widespread use of internet triggers an intense debate. Some people hold that the use of internet should fall under control, while others disagree and argue that such control is groundless. This essay will analyse both positions and illuminate my standpoint.
As for those who support the control of the internet use, they may have their position grounded upon the fact that criminal activities run rampant online, threatening individual and public security, as exemplified by hacking technology aimed at stealing personal data illegally, fraudulent advertisements aimed at cheating money and notices post by terrorist groups brainwashing the public. Such activities, if unchecked, may exert undesirable effects on individuals and society. Given such omnipresent dangers online, the control of online activities may be justified.
On the contrary, in terms of those objecting to the control of the internet use, they may justify their position by highlighting importance of unlimited online activities, as demonstrated by the improvement of work efficiency. A case in point is that posting advertisements online is apparently more efficient than issuing leaflets on a street, from the perspective of a company intending to publicising its services and products. However, the notices post online may present fake information cheating the public. To address this issue, it is necessary to control online activities.
As far as I am concerned, in view of omnipresent hazards online, intervention is needed to screen dangerous information and ensure the safety of the internet use.
To conclude, the control of online activities helps to protect us from various cybercrimes, although it may slightly affect the efficiency of work. Personally, I opine that the importance of online control should not be underestimated.

The internet has become one of the most widely used tools in modern life, fundamentally changing how people communicate, work, and access information. However, its rapid expansion has given rise to a significant debate: should internet use be regulated by authorities, or should individuals be free to use it without restriction? This essay will examine both sides of the argument before presenting a personal viewpoint.
Those who advocate for internet regulation often base their position on concerns about safety and security. The online environment has become a space where criminal activity is increasingly common. Cybercriminals exploit personal data through hacking, scammers deceive users through fraudulent schemes, and extremist groups use online platforms to spread harmful ideologies. Without some form of oversight, these threats are likely to grow unchecked, putting both individuals and society at risk. Proponents of regulation therefore argue that a controlled internet is a safer one.
On the other side of the debate, opponents of internet regulation emphasize the value of free and open access. The internet has transformed the way businesses operate, enabling companies to reach global audiences at a fraction of the cost of traditional methods. Online platforms also allow individuals to express opinions, access education, and participate in democratic processes. Imposing restrictions, critics argue, risks undermining these freedoms and could be misused by governments to suppress legitimate speech or limit access to information.
Nevertheless, the existence of misinformation, cybercrime, and harmful content online makes a compelling case that some degree of regulation is necessary. The key lies in striking the right balance — implementing controls that target genuinely dangerous content without infringing on the rights of ordinary users.
In conclusion, while unrestricted internet access offers clear benefits in terms of freedom and efficiency, the dangers posed by criminal and harmful online activity cannot be ignored. In my view, carefully managed regulation is not only justifiable but essential to ensuring that the internet remains a safe and productive space for all.

Few technologies have reshaped daily life as thoroughly as the internet. It connects people across continents, drives commerce, and gives individuals access to more information than any library could hold. Yet precisely because it is so powerful and so pervasive, it has become the subject of a persistent and often heated debate: should governments regulate what happens online, or is that kind of control an overreach? This essay looks at both positions before offering a personal conclusion.
The case for regulation tends to start with safety. Spend enough time online and the risks become apparent — hackers targeting personal and financial data, elaborate scams designed to separate people from their money, and extremist content engineered to radicalize vulnerable users. These are not minor inconveniences. They cause real harm, and without some form of oversight there is little to stop them from spreading further. For those who support regulation, the argument is straightforward: an unmonitored internet is a dangerous one.
The opposing view is equally understandable. Open internet access has been transformative in ways that are hard to overstate. Businesses can now reach customers around the world without the costs of traditional advertising. Individuals can learn, organize, and speak freely in ways that were simply not possible before. Critics of regulation worry that once governments have the tools to control online content, those tools are easily turned toward silencing dissent or restricting access to inconvenient truths. It is a concern with historical backing.
That said, the sheer volume of harmful material that circulates online — misinformation, criminal activity, content designed to exploit or endanger — makes it difficult to argue that no intervention is warranted. The real challenge is not whether to regulate, but how to do so without doing more damage than good.
On balance, some level of internet regulation seems not just reasonable but necessary. The goal should be targeted controls that address genuine threats while leaving the open, connected nature of the internet broadly intact. Freedom and safety are not mutually exclusive — but finding that balance requires careful thought, not a blanket approach in either direction.

Many countries around the world are fortunate to possess abundant natural resources such as oil, natural gas, and valuable minerals. While some argue that such wealth breeds complacency among citizens, this essay disagrees with that view. Instead, it will argue that economic wealth derived from natural resources has the potential to improve living standards and motivate citizens to contribute to their country's continued growth and development.
To begin with, resource-based economic wealth creates opportunities that directly benefit ordinary citizens. In economies where employment can be difficult to secure, thriving industries built around natural resources generate a wide range of jobs, from technical and engineering roles to administrative and service positions. Rather than making people careless, these opportunities tend to instill a sense of purpose and contribution. Citizens who are employed and financially stable are more likely to invest in their communities and take pride in their country's progress.
Furthermore, economic wealth has the power to transform how a country is perceived on the global stage. Nations that have developed their natural resources effectively have, in many cases, built modern infrastructure, expanded access to education and healthcare, and raised the overall quality of life for their populations. Human ambition plays a significant role in this process. People naturally desire improved living conditions and greater financial security, and this drive encourages both individuals and governments to make productive use of available resources rather than squandering them.
In conclusion, economic wealth rooted in natural resources is far from a cause of national carelessness. On the contrary, it serves as a foundation for development and a better quality of life. When managed well, such wealth motivates citizens and governments alike to strive for continued progress, raising the country's standing both domestically and internationally.

A significant number of countries sit on considerable reserves of oil, gas, and minerals — resources that, when developed, can generate enormous national wealth. Some people take the view that this kind of prosperity makes citizens complacent, with little incentive to work hard or care about their country's future. This essay disagrees. Natural resource wealth, when put to good use, tends to improve lives and give people more reason to invest in where they live, not less.
One of the clearest benefits is employment. Finding stable work is a challenge in many parts of the world, and industries built around natural resources create jobs across a wide range of skill levels — engineering, logistics, administration, and beyond. It is difficult to argue that this makes people careless. If anything, the opposite is true: when people have work and a reliable income, they tend to feel a stronger connection to their country and a greater stake in how it develops. The workforce that powers these industries is also what sustains them, which gives citizens both purpose and influence.
There is also a broader effect on how a country presents itself to the world. Nations that have channeled resource revenues into infrastructure, education, and public services have, in a number of cases, undergone remarkable transformations within a generation or two. Part of what drives this is simply human nature — people want more comfort, more security, more opportunity for themselves and their families. That ambition, rather than being a flaw, is often what pushes countries forward.
To sum up, resource wealth does not make citizens idle. It opens doors, raises expectations, and provides governments with the means to build something lasting. The desire for a better life is a powerful motivator, and in countries rich with natural resources, that desire has frequently translated into genuine national development.

The graph illustrates the unemployment rate of the graduates by genders, who are between 20 and 24 years old and who are living in a European country, between 2008 and 2009.
Overall, it is clear that the graduate unemployment rate showed a fluctuated feature in the time period, both genders unemployment rate was slightly decrease till the end of 2008 and at the end of 2008 the percentage was increased and reached to highest point, and same characteristic was shown in the year 2009 as well.
In January 2008, the percentage of the men and women unemployment rate was 10% and around 7% respectively. And in April and July 2008, their percentage was decreased sharply till October 2008, and in October 2008 the percentage of the men increased around 17% and women increased around 12 %.
In year 2009, between January and October, the percentage of the men was slightly decreased and in October it increased sharply around 23% and reached to the top point of unemployment rate in 2009. The rate of the unemployment women was not so stable and it reached to the lowest rate in April with a percentage of about 3%. And it started to increase in July and reached to the highest point in October 2009, which is around 14%.

The graph presents data on the unemployment rate among graduates aged 20 to 24 in a European country, broken down by gender, covering the period from 2008 to 2009.
Overall, unemployment among both male and female graduates followed a broadly similar pattern across the two years, with rates generally declining in the middle months before rising sharply toward the end of each year. Male unemployment consistently exceeded female unemployment throughout the entire period.
In January 2008, the unemployment rate for male graduates stood at approximately 10%, while the rate for female graduates was lower, at around 7%. Both figures declined through April and July of that year, suggesting a period of relative improvement in graduate employment. However, this trend reversed by October 2008, when male unemployment rose sharply to approximately 17% and female unemployment climbed to around 12%, representing notable increases for both groups.
A similar pattern emerged in 2009. Between January and around mid-year, male graduate unemployment declined gradually, though it surged again by October 2009, reaching its peak value for the entire period at approximately 23%. Female graduate unemployment proved more volatile during this year. It fell to its lowest recorded point in April 2009, dropping to roughly 3%, before recovering and then rising steeply. By October 2009, the female unemployment rate had climbed to approximately 14%, its highest point across the full two-year period.
In summary, both genders experienced recurring cycles of decline and sharp increase, with male graduates bearing a consistently higher unemployment rate and both groups reaching their highest levels of unemployment in October 2009.

The graph shows unemployment rates among university graduates aged 20 to 24 in a European country, separated by gender, across 2008 and 2009.
The headline pattern for both groups is fairly consistent: rates tended to fall during the middle of each year before climbing sharply toward October. Male unemployment sat above female unemployment for virtually the entire period.
At the start of 2008, around 10% of male graduates were unemployed, compared to roughly 7% of their female counterparts. Both figures eased through the spring and summer months, pointing to some improvement in the job market for young graduates. That improvement did not last. By October 2008, male unemployment had jumped to about 17% and female unemployment to around 12% — a substantial reversal for both.
2009 followed a broadly similar arc, though with some differences in the detail. Male unemployment drifted downward in the first half of the year, only to spike again in October, this time reaching roughly 23% — the highest point recorded across the full two years. The female trend was less predictable. Unemployment among women fell sharply to about 3% in April, its lowest level in the entire dataset, before turning upward again. By October 2009 it had reached approximately 14%, the highest female rate seen across both years.
Taken together, the data suggests a recurring seasonal or cyclical pattern, with conditions for young graduates worsening notably each October. The gap between male and female unemployment narrowed at certain points but never closed, and by the end of the period both groups were significantly worse off than they had been at the start.

In regard to global wealth,the North America is highest at about 34 percent followed by Europe and Rich Asia-Pacific with about 30 % and 24% respectively.The contribution of the remaining countries to the global wealth is relatively low being below 5 percent where the global wealth of India and Africa is of same value.
However,when it comes to the population,China stands first with about 24 % followed by India,Europe,and other Asia Pacefic with a similar percentage of about 15 percent.Despite having a large global wealth,North America and Rich Asia-Pacefic has lowest population of about 6 percent and 5 percent respectively.

The graph displays the proportion of global wealth and population share across several regions of the world, allowing for a comparison between economic output and demographic size.
Overall, there is a striking contrast between the regions that hold the most wealth and those that are home to the largest populations. North America, Europe, and Rich Asia-Pacific dominate in terms of global wealth, while China and India account for the largest shares of the world's population.
Regarding global wealth distribution, North America holds the largest share at approximately 34%, making it the wealthiest region by a considerable margin. Europe follows in second place with around 30%, and Rich Asia-Pacific contributes approximately 24% of global wealth. Together, these three regions account for the vast majority of the world's wealth. The remaining regions contribute relatively little, with India, Africa, China, and Other Asia-Pacific each holding shares of below 5%, with India and Africa recording identical figures.
When it comes to population, the picture is almost entirely reversed. China has the highest population share of any single region at around 24%, followed closely by India and Europe, both of which account for approximately 15% of the global population. Other Asia-Pacific records a similar figure. In sharp contrast, North America and Rich Asia-Pacific, despite their considerable economic dominance, are home to only around 6% and 5% of the global population respectively.
In summary, the data highlights a significant global imbalance, whereby the regions with the smallest populations tend to control the greatest proportions of global wealth, while the most populous regions hold comparatively little.

The graph compares regional shares of global wealth and population, placing economic weight alongside demographic size for several regions around the world.
The most striking takeaway is the disconnect between where wealth is concentrated and where people actually live. The regions with the biggest economies are not the ones with the most people — and the reverse holds just as firmly.
On the wealth side, North America leads with roughly 34% of the global total. Europe is close behind at around 30%, and Rich Asia-Pacific accounts for about 24%. Between them, these three regions hold the overwhelming majority of the world's wealth. Everything else is marginal — India, Africa, China, and Other Asia-Pacific each come in below 5%, with India and Africa sitting at the same figure.
Population tells a very different story. China accounts for about 24% of the global population, more than any other region. India and Europe both sit at around 15%, with Other Asia-Pacific in a similar range. North America and Rich Asia-Pacific, for all their economic weight, are home to just 6% and 5% of the world's people respectively.
The contrast is hard to miss. The three wealthiest regions together hold well over 80% of global wealth but represent a comparatively small slice of the global population. Meanwhile, regions like China, India, and Africa, which together account for a large majority of the world's people, hold only a fraction of its wealth. The data makes the scale of global economic inequality difficult to ignore.

There is a common belief that imposing longer prison sentences is the most effective way to reduce crime in society. However, others contend that alternative approaches may prove more successful. This essay fully agrees with the latter view, as lengthy incarceration alone does not address the root causes of criminal behaviour.
It is worth acknowledging that longer prison sentences do carry certain advantages. The prospect of spending many years behind bars may deter some individuals from committing crimes in the first place. Additionally, an extended period of imprisonment gives offenders time to reflect on their actions, which could, in some cases, contribute to genuine rehabilitation.
Despite these benefits, there are significant drawbacks to relying on long sentences as a primary solution. Individuals who spend prolonged periods in prison often struggle to reintegrate into society upon release. Having been isolated from everyday life for years, they may find it difficult to secure employment or rebuild social connections, making them more vulnerable to reoffending. In this way, long sentences may inadvertently contribute to the very problem they are meant to solve.
More fundamentally, crime is frequently driven by social and economic pressures that imprisonment cannot address. Poverty and unemployment are among the strongest predictors of criminal activity. When people are unable to meet their basic needs, some may turn to crime out of desperation. Governments would therefore be better served by implementing policies that stimulate economic growth, reduce unemployment, and provide adequate social support.
Mental and emotional strain is another contributing factor. The pressures of work, financial instability, and family responsibilities can accumulate to a point where individuals feel overwhelmed. Without accessible mental health support or adequate social safety nets, some may resort to criminal behaviour as a means of coping.
In conclusion, while longer prison sentences may offer a limited deterrent effect, they fall short as a comprehensive solution to crime. Addressing the economic and psychological conditions that lead people toward criminal behaviour is likely to be far more effective in the long term.

A widely held view is that handing down longer prison sentences is the best tool societies have for reducing crime. Others push back on this, arguing that different approaches would work better. This essay sides firmly with the latter position — longer sentences may have a role to play, but they cannot do the job alone.
There is something to be said for the deterrence argument. Knowing that a conviction could mean years in prison might give some people pause before breaking the law. Time behind bars also creates space for reflection, and for some offenders, that period of forced removal from ordinary life genuinely prompts change.
But the limitations become clear when you consider what happens at the end of a long sentence. Someone who has spent years cut off from society often emerges without the skills, contacts, or confidence needed to find work and build a stable life. That kind of vulnerability makes reoffending more likely, not less — which undermines the whole purpose of the punishment.
The deeper issue is that crime rarely appears out of nowhere. Economic hardship is one of the most consistent drivers. When unemployment rises and people cannot afford to meet basic needs, some turn to crime not out of moral failing but out of desperation. Policy responses that target poverty and create economic opportunity are likely to do far more to reduce crime than extending sentences ever could.
Stress and mental burden matter too. Juggling work, debt, childcare, and family care takes a real toll, and without adequate support systems, that pressure can push people toward harmful choices. Addressing these pressures through accessible mental health services and stronger social safety nets deserves far more attention in the crime reduction conversation.
Longer sentences are not worthless, but treating them as the primary solution misses the point. Crime is a symptom. Treating the underlying conditions — poverty, unemployment, mental strain — is where the real gains are to be found.

The graphs compare the participation of full-time and part-time workers in Australia as differenciated by gender and age group in the period betweeen 1973 and 1993.
Overall, it is clearly seen that the number of full-time workers for both male and female experienced a slight decline as part-time work become more favourable among these employees. Moreover, despite their gender, there are more people in the age group of 15-19 years old employed for part-time jobs.
Based on the age group, it is illustrated that the number of full-time and part-time male workers between 25-49 years old has no significant difference. However, the lines indicated that for female employees the number of people working full-time and part-time were more fluctuated, meaning that age group influenced their employment status. For example, the female employees at the age group of 15-19 years old working full-time was accounted for almost reaching to 50 per cent in 1973, but stepping down to 20 per cent in 1993; while part-time workers at the same age group has a higher number 1993 compared to those who worked in 1973 by around 32 per cent and 8 per cent, respectively.

The graphs illustrate how full-time and part-time employment participation in Australia changed between 1973 and 1993, with data broken down by both gender and age group.
Overall, full-time employment declined for both male and female workers over the two decades, while part-time work became increasingly common. Across both genders, the 15 to 19 age group showed the highest levels of part-time employment participation.
Looking at male workers, those in the 25 to 49 age group showed relatively stable patterns, with full-time and part-time participation rates remaining broadly similar throughout the period and showing no dramatic divergence. This suggests that age had a limited influence on the employment status of male workers within this bracket.
For female workers, however, the picture was considerably more dynamic, with age playing a much more significant role in shaping employment patterns. Among female employees in the 15 to 19 age group, full-time participation was high in 1973, approaching 50%, but had fallen sharply to around 20% by 1993. Part-time work among the same group moved in the opposite direction, rising from approximately 8% in 1973 to around 32% in 1993, representing a substantial shift in how younger women engaged with the workforce over the period.
In summary, the data reveals a clear trend away from full-time and toward part-time employment across the two decades, with this shift being especially pronounced among younger female workers. Male employment patterns, by comparison, remained more stable across age groups throughout the same period.

The graphs track participation in full-time and part-time work in Australia from 1973 to 1993, with figures separated by gender and age group.
The broad trend across both genders is a gradual move away from full-time employment and toward part-time work over the two decades. Among younger workers in particular — those aged 15 to 19 — part-time employment was the dominant mode by the end of the period, regardless of gender.
Male workers in the 25 to 49 bracket tell a fairly uneventful story. Full-time and part-time participation rates in this group stayed relatively close together and did not shift dramatically in either direction over the twenty years. Age, within this range at least, does not appear to have been a strong determining factor for male employment status.
The female data is where the more interesting patterns emerge. Age had a much more visible influence on women's employment choices, and the changes over time were sharper. Young women aged 15 to 19 were heavily concentrated in full-time work in 1973, with participation close to 50%. By 1993 that figure had dropped to around 20%. Part-time work among the same group went the other way — up from roughly 8% in 1973 to about 32% in 1993. That is a substantial reversal within a single age cohort over just two decades.
Taken together, the graphs point to a workforce in transition. Part-time employment steadily gained ground across the board, but the scale of that shift varied considerably depending on gender and age. Younger women experienced the most pronounced changes, while male workers in their prime working years remained comparatively consistent in their employment patterns throughout the period.

This presented chart depicts how many workers and factories are there in England and Wales for 50 years, starting from 1851.
In brief, the number of male and female workers decreased during this period of time, whereas the number of factories increased.
Turning to the details, the number of male worker and female worker showed similar trend. The number of male employees started at 287100, which is almost 2 times higher than the number of female, in 1851 and then, it gradually decreased. Finally, it ended at 31000 in 1901. Second, 190000 female employees recorded in 1851 however, it also constantly decreased. This number finished at 30000 in 1901.
In contrary to this, while there were only 225 factories in 1851 in England and Wales, in 1901, 600 factories were recorded. This number generally inclined however, after 1891, it slightly declined almost 40 every decades.
Interestingly, In 1881 this number reached at 721, which is the highest record during the period of time.

The chart presents data on the number of male and female workers alongside the number of factories recorded in England and Wales over a fifty-year period, from 1851 to 1901.
Overall, the most notable contrast in the data is between the workforce figures and the factory count. While the number of both male and female workers declined consistently over the period, the number of factories followed an opposing trend, increasing substantially across the same timeframe.
In 1851, male workers numbered 287,100, a figure roughly twice that of female workers, which stood at 190,000. Both categories then declined steadily throughout the period. By 1901, male worker numbers had fallen to 31,000, while female workers had dropped to approximately 30,000. By the end of the period, the gap between male and female worker numbers, which had been considerable at the outset, had narrowed to almost nothing.
The trajectory of factory numbers was markedly different. Starting at just 225 factories in 1851, this figure rose considerably over the following decades, reaching its peak of 721 in 1881 — the highest point recorded across the entire period. Following this peak, the number of factories experienced a modest decline, falling by approximately 40 every decade. Despite this late decrease, the total still stood at 600 by 1901, more than double the figure recorded fifty years earlier.
In summary, the data reflects a significant restructuring of industrial activity in England and Wales during the second half of the nineteenth century, with fewer but larger operations seemingly replacing the more labour-intensive model of earlier decades.

The chart covers a fifty-year snapshot of industrial England and Wales, tracking the number of male and female workers and the number of factories from 1851 to 1901.
The headline finding is a clear divergence between two trends running in opposite directions. Workers — both male and female — became steadily fewer over the period, while the number of factories grew. That combination points toward a shift in how industry was organized rather than a simple decline in activity.
At the start of the period, male workers numbered 287,100, nearly double the female figure of 190,000. From there, both declined without interruption. By 1901, male workers had fallen to 31,000 and female workers to around 30,000 — a dramatic drop for both, and one that effectively erased the gap between them. Two groups that began the period at very different sizes ended it at almost identical levels.
Factories moved in the opposite direction. There were just 225 in 1851, a figure that climbed steadily before peaking at 721 in 1881 — the highest point in the entire dataset. After that, the count dipped slightly, losing roughly 40 factories per decade, and settled at 600 by 1901. So even accounting for the late decline, the number of factories had more than doubled over the fifty years.
The overall picture suggests a period of industrial consolidation. Fewer workers were employed, yet more factories existed — implying that individual operations were becoming larger and more mechanized, absorbing output that had previously required a much greater human workforce.

The bar chart demonstrates the unemployed graduates who are between 20 and 24 years old by gender in percentage. The survey is conducted in one European country from 2008 to 2009.
In general, the unemployment rates in males over a two-year period were higher than the females. The highest rate of unemployment in males was approximately 22% in Oct 2009, reaching nearly 40% more than females. Whereas the unemployment for female graduates in percentage was 6% reported in Apr 08, July 08 and Apr 09 which were the lowest rate of unemployment for graduates over the period.
First of all, there was a slight decline in the unemployment rates for all graduates in the beginning of year, from 10% in Jan 08 to 7% in Apr 08 in males whilst female graduates decreasing from 7.5% to 6% . The unemployment for both genders in July 2008 remained same rates as previous. An increase in percentage of unemployed graduates in Oct 08 took nearly twofold of last month with males by 16% and females by 11%. Subsequently, a fall in the unemployment rates for all graduates were recorded in Jan 09, by 12.5% in men and 8% in women. A further reduction in the rate of unemployed female graduates in Apr 09 was 6% whilst there was no change in the male graduates. Afterwards, the unemployment rates in women returned to 10% in July 09 whereas there was a brief decrease in males by 11%. This was followed by a surge in the unemployment rates for graduates in Oct 09 by 22% for males and 14% for females.

The bar chart illustrates the unemployment rates of graduates aged between 20 and 24 years, categorised by gender, in a European country. The data spans a two-year period from 2008 to 2009 and is recorded at four intervals each year: January, April, July, and October.
Overall, male graduate unemployment was consistently higher than female graduate unemployment throughout the entire period. The peak male unemployment rate of approximately 22% in October 2009 was nearly 40% higher in relative terms than the corresponding female figure. The lowest unemployment rates for female graduates, at 6%, were recorded on three separate occasions: April 2008, July 2008, and April 2009.
At the beginning of 2008, male unemployment stood at 10% and female unemployment at 7.5%. Both figures declined modestly by April, falling to 7% and 6% respectively, and remained unchanged through July 2008. October 2008 brought a sharp reversal, with male unemployment rising to approximately 16% and female unemployment climbing to around 11%, representing roughly a twofold increase for both groups compared to the preceding months.
The start of 2009 saw unemployment ease for both genders, dropping to 12.5% for males and 8% for females in January. By April, female unemployment had fallen further to 6%, while male unemployment held steady. July 2009 then saw a divergence: female unemployment rose sharply to 10%, while male unemployment dipped slightly to 11%. The period concluded with a pronounced surge in October 2009, when male unemployment peaked at 22% and female unemployment reached its highest point of 14%.
In summary, both groups followed a broadly cyclical pattern of decline and increase, with the most significant rises occurring in October of each year and male graduates consistently experiencing higher rates of unemployment throughout.

The bar chart tracks unemployment among graduates aged 20 to 24 in a European country, broken down by gender across eight data points from early 2008 to late 2009 — measured each January, April, July, and October.
Male unemployment ran higher than female unemployment at every point in the dataset. The gap was most pronounced in October 2009, when the male rate hit approximately 22% — nearly 40% above the female figure in relative terms. At the other end of the scale, female unemployment touched its floor of 6% on three separate occasions: April 2008, July 2008, and April 2009.
The story of 2008 begins with a modest dip. Male unemployment eased from 10% in January to 7% by April, while the female rate slipped from 7.5% to 6%. Both held at those levels through July. Then October brought a sharp jolt upward — male unemployment jumped to around 16% and female to roughly 11%, close to double the figures from the previous quarter.
2009 opened with some recovery. January brought the rates back down to 12.5% for men and 8% for women. April continued the downward move for women, reaching 6% again, while the male figure stayed flat. Things diverged in July: women's unemployment climbed back to 10% while men's edged slightly lower to 11%. The year then closed with the steepest rise of the entire period — male unemployment surged to 22% and female unemployment to 14% in October 2009, the highest readings recorded for both groups.
The pattern across both years is fairly consistent: rates tend to soften in the first half of each year before spiking in the final quarter. The October peaks grew larger each year, and while the gender gap fluctuated, it never closed.

As the global population continues to rise, ensuring sufficient and sustainable food sources has become one of the most pressing challenges of our time. Among the various solutions being explored, the use of insects — both as a food source and as a tool in agriculture — has attracted growing attention. This essay examines both the agricultural benefits of insects and the potential health risks associated with consuming them.
From an agricultural perspective, insects offer a range of valuable benefits. Many species play a meaningful role in improving soil quality and supporting plant growth. When used as organic fertilizer, insects and their byproducts can provide plants with essential nutrients, helping them grow more quickly and remain healthier over time. This natural approach to cultivation is particularly appealing because it is cost-effective and readily accessible, requiring minimal effort to obtain since insects occur naturally in the environment. Farmers in many regions of the world have long incorporated insects into their agricultural practices, recognizing their efficiency as a sustainable and low-cost alternative to chemical fertilizers.
However, the consumption of insects as food is a more complex matter. While insects are indeed eaten regularly in many cultures and are considered a reliable source of protein and nutrients, they also carry potential health risks. Improper preparation or handling of insects can lead to gastrointestinal problems, bacterial infections, and other adverse reactions in the human body. Such issues tend to arise most frequently when people lack the knowledge or means to process insects safely before consumption.
In conclusion, insects hold genuine promise both as an agricultural resource and as a food source, but their benefits must be weighed carefully against the health risks they may pose when not handled correctly.

Feeding a growing global population is one of the defining challenges of this century, and it has pushed people to look beyond conventional food systems for answers. Insects have emerged as one of the more unconventional proposals — not just as something to eat, but as a resource with real agricultural value. Both angles are worth examining.
On the farming side, the case for insects is fairly strong. Certain species contribute meaningfully to soil health, helping plants absorb nutrients more effectively and grow with greater vigor. Used as a natural fertilizer, insect matter can reduce plant die-off and improve yields without the cost or environmental footprint of synthetic alternatives. For smallholder farmers in particular, this is an attractive option — insects are free, abundant, and require no complex supply chain to obtain. It is no coincidence that communities in many parts of the world have been using insects in their agricultural practices for generations.
Eating insects is a different matter, and the picture is more complicated. In many cultures, insects are a normal and accepted part of the diet, valued for their protein content and nutritional density. But consumption is not without risk. Poorly prepared or improperly stored insects can cause digestive problems, bacterial infections, and other health complications. These issues tend to surface most often in contexts where people lack clear guidance on how to safely process insects for food.
The common thread running through both the benefits and the risks is knowledge. When insects are used well — whether in the field or in the kitchen — the results can be genuinely positive. When they are used carelessly, the consequences can be harmful. Broader education around safe practices seems to be the missing piece in making insect use, in either context, more reliably beneficial.

In the flowchart given, 8 stages in the life cycle of salmon are illustrated. It is seen that cycle starts when salmon lay eggs in the river and finishes when they return to river from the ocean to spawn.
The life cycle of salmon is initiated when a female lays eggs in the river and incubate them. Offsprings emerge from the eggs in a form of small fish form which are then reared in the freshwater before they enter the estuary. After a certain period of time, the fish change their habitation to estuary which is the entrance from river to ocean. Then, they completely inhabit the ocean environment, followed by a migration to rearing areas. These salmon, in this way, keep growing and eventually reach the point of maturation where sexual reproduction can take place between two opposite sex. Once mating occurs, they migrate back to the river area where the life cycle can be repeated for the next generation.

The flowchart depicts the eight key stages in the life cycle of the salmon, beginning with the laying of eggs in a freshwater river and ending with the return of mature salmon to the same environment to reproduce.
The cycle begins when a female salmon deposits her eggs in a river, where they are incubated until they hatch. The offspring emerge as small fish and spend an initial stage of their lives developing in the freshwater environment of the river. Once they have grown sufficiently, the young salmon move into the estuary, the transitional zone where the river meets the ocean. This stage represents a critical shift as the fish begin to adjust to changing water conditions.
From the estuary, the salmon proceed to enter the open ocean, where they undergo further development. They then migrate to designated rearing areas within the ocean, where conditions support continued growth. Over time, the salmon mature fully, eventually reaching the stage at which they are capable of sexual reproduction. At this point, males and females come together to mate.
Following reproduction, the adult salmon undertake a return migration, travelling back from the ocean toward the freshwater river where the cycle originally began. Upon arriving in the river, the salmon spawn, and the entire process is set in motion once again for the next generation.
In summary, the life cycle of the salmon is a continuous and self-renewing process, characterised by movement between freshwater and oceanic environments, and driven ultimately by the biological imperative to reproduce.

The flowchart outlines the eight stages of the salmon life cycle, tracing the fish's journey from freshwater river to open ocean and back again.
It all begins in the river. A female salmon lays her eggs in freshwater, where they are incubated until hatching. The young fish that emerge spend their earliest weeks and months developing in this same freshwater environment, feeding and growing before they are ready to move on.
The next transition takes the juvenile salmon into the estuary — the point where the river gradually gives way to the sea. This is an important intermediate stage, allowing the fish to acclimatize to the shift in salinity before they make the full move into the ocean.
Once in the open ocean, the salmon are far from finished growing. They migrate to specific rearing areas where conditions favour continued development, and it is here that the bulk of their growth takes place. Gradually, over what may be several years, they reach full maturity — the stage at which they become capable of reproduction.
At that point, something remarkable happens. The mature salmon turn around and begin the journey back, navigating from the ocean, through the estuary, and into the freshwater river. Once there, they spawn — and in doing so, set the entire cycle in motion for the next generation.
What the flowchart captures, in eight neat stages, is a journey defined by movement and return. The salmon's life is essentially a long loop, shaped by the need to grow in one environment and reproduce in another.
