Globalization refers to the growing interconnectedness of countries and peoples across the world, encompassing the movement of people, goods, ideas, and culture across national borders. It has attracted significant attention from international organizations, which have sought to use it as a vehicle for promoting human rights, economic development, and social progress. However, globalization is not without its drawbacks, and its benefits are not shared equally among all nations.
One of the most significant concerns surrounding globalization is the exclusion of poorer countries from its advantages. Nations that lack the infrastructure, technological capacity, or skilled workforce to participate in the global economy often find themselves left behind. Unable to compete with wealthier and more established trading partners, these countries struggle to access international markets and miss out on the economic opportunities that globalization can provide. This widening gap between developed and developing nations remains one of the central criticisms of the globalization process.
On a more troubling note, globalization has also facilitated the growth of transnational crime. The increased movement of people and goods across borders has made it easier for criminal networks to traffic illegal drugs by air, sea, and land. Human smuggling has similarly become a more organized and widespread problem. These criminal activities carry serious consequences, not only for governments but also for families and communities affected by addiction and violence.
Environmental concerns represent another negative dimension of globalization. The dumping of hazardous waste into oceans and other natural environments has caused significant ecological damage, polluting water sources and posing serious health risks to both wildlife and human populations.
In conclusion, while globalization has undeniably brought progress and opportunity to many parts of the world, it has also created new challenges. International organizations and national governments must work together to ensure that its benefits are more equitably distributed and that its harmful consequences are effectively addressed.

Globalization, at its core, is about connection — the accelerating flow of people, goods, money, and ideas across national borders. International bodies have long championed it as a path toward greater human rights, economic opportunity, and cultural exchange. And for many countries, that promise has been at least partially fulfilled. But the picture is far from uniformly positive.
The most persistent criticism is one of uneven distribution. The countries best positioned to benefit from globalization tend to be those that were already relatively wealthy, well-connected, and technologically capable. Poorer nations, lacking skilled workforces and modern infrastructure, often find themselves unable to compete in global markets or attract meaningful investment. Rather than closing the gap between rich and poor countries, globalization has in many cases widened it — leaving the most vulnerable nations further behind while the most powerful pull further ahead.
Crime is another area where globalization's darker side becomes visible. More open borders and greater volumes of international trade have provided cover for criminal networks to expand their operations. Drug trafficking by air, sea, and land has grown into a global industry, and human smuggling has followed a similar trajectory. The knock-on effects within affected communities — addiction, violence, family breakdown — are real and well-documented.
Environmental damage is a further consequence that deserves attention. The disposal of hazardous industrial waste into oceans and coastal waters is a problem that has grown alongside global trade, with serious implications for ecosystems and the communities that depend on them.
Globalization is neither simply good nor simply bad — it is a force that magnifies existing inequalities and creates new risks even as it opens new possibilities. The challenge for international organizations and governments is not to slow it down, but to manage it more deliberately, ensuring that its costs do not fall disproportionately on those least equipped to bear them.

The psychological impact of color is a subject that deserves more attention than it typically receives. Some experts and designers advocate for careful consideration of color schemes when decorating environments such as workplaces and hospitals. This essay agrees with the view that color significantly affects psychological wellbeing, and argues that the deliberate use of color in professional and medical environments can either support or hinder the people who occupy them.
Colors carry deep emotional associations that influence how people feel and behave. This connection is so well established that emotion charts and psychological models frequently use different colors to represent different emotional states. Blue, for instance, is widely associated with sadness and low mood, while red is linked to passion and heightened emotional arousal. Grey tones, meanwhile, are commonly associated with anxiety and low energy. Given these associations, decorating hospitals or offices in grey or blue would seem counterproductive if the goal is to foster positivity and productivity.
A compelling historical anecdote illustrates the influence color can have on behavior. Princess Elisabeth of Austria, famously preoccupied with her weight and physical appearance, reportedly decorated the walls of her apartments in purple. Purple is said to suppress appetite, and the princess reportedly found it easier to maintain her strict dietary habits within those surroundings. While anecdotal, this example suggests that color can shape behavior in subtle but meaningful ways.
On the more positive side, certain colors are known to encourage creativity, calm, and confidence. Green, with its associations with nature and tranquility, could be a particularly effective choice for hospital environments, helping patients feel more at ease during stressful periods. Yellow, associated with confidence, awareness, and optimism, may be well suited to office spaces, encouraging workers to perform at their best and approach their tasks with energy and self-assurance.
In conclusion, color is far more than a decorative choice. Its effects on mood and psychological state are real and should be taken seriously when designing spaces intended for specific purposes. Just as physical conditions matter to our health, so too does the psychological environment we inhabit, and color plays a meaningful role in shaping that environment.

Color does more to a room than fill it with visual interest. It shapes how people feel, how they concentrate, and how they behave — and yet decisions about color in workplaces and hospitals are often made with little thought given to these effects. This essay takes the position that color genuinely influences psychological wellbeing and that the people responsible for designing professional and medical spaces ought to take that seriously.
The emotional associations tied to specific colors are not arbitrary. They are consistent enough across cultures that psychologists have mapped them onto models of human emotion. Blue tends toward sadness and low energy; red stirs passion and intensity; grey has a well-earned reputation for producing anxiety and mental heaviness. Decorating a hospital ward or an open-plan office in these tones seems almost perverse when the goal is to help people heal or think clearly.
History offers the occasional vivid illustration. Princess Elisabeth of Austria — Sissi, as she was known — was famously fixated on her figure and is said to have decorated her private apartments entirely in purple, a color long associated with suppressing appetite. Whether or not the science fully supports it, she apparently found the environment helpful in maintaining her strict self-imposed routines. It is a curious example, but it points toward something real: our surroundings do not just reflect our intentions, they quietly shape them.
The more encouraging side of this is that certain colors actively support positive states. Green, with its associations with nature, balance, and calm, seems well suited to hospital settings, where patients are already dealing with stress and uncertainty. Yellow carries connotations of confidence, alertness, and optimism — qualities that translate well to a workspace where people need to bring their best. Thoughtful use of these colors is not a minor detail; it is a meaningful contribution to the environment people work and recover in.
Psychological health and physical health are not as separate as we sometimes treat them. Color touches both. Designing a space without considering its emotional effect is like designing it without considering the lighting — technically possible, but a missed opportunity to do something genuinely useful for the people inside it.

The chart presented illustrates how parents in a particular country allocated their evening time across four childcare activities in 2022, namely bathing, reading, outdoor play, and preparing meals. The data is further broken down to show the proportion of time each activity was performed jointly by both parents, as well as separately by mothers and fathers respectively.
The activity most frequently carried out by both parents together is reading, which accounts for approximately 65% of the total time dedicated to this task. Conversely, meal preparation represents the activity least likely to be shared, with parents engaging in it together only around 15% of the time, suggesting a more divided approach to domestic responsibilities in this area.
Regarding activities performed jointly, bathing children falls somewhere in the middle, with both parents participating together roughly 45% of the time, while outdoor play sees shared involvement in approximately 55% of cases. These figures indicate a moderate level of cooperation between parents in physical and recreational caregiving tasks.
When comparing mothers and fathers individually, notable differences emerge. Mothers consistently dedicate more time than fathers to meal preparation and bathing, with the gap being particularly pronounced in meal preparation, where mothers account for nearly 75% of the individual time spent compared to fathers at just under 10%. Fathers, on the other hand, appear more involved than mothers in outdoor play when acting alone. Overall, the data suggests that while joint participation is highest in recreational activities, domestic and hygiene-related tasks remain disproportionately carried out by mothers.

The chart shows how parents in one country divided their evening childcare time across four activities in 2022 — bathing, reading, outdoor play, and meal preparation — with the data split between time spent together and time spent separately by each parent.
Of the four activities, reading stands out as the one parents are most likely to do as a pair, accounting for around 65% of total time on that task. Meal preparation sits at the other extreme, with both parents present for only about 15% of the time — a striking contrast that hints at how unevenly domestic duties can still be distributed. Bathing and outdoor play land in between, at roughly 45% and 55% shared time respectively.
The individual breakdowns are where things get more telling. Mothers dominate meal preparation and bathing when parents act alone, and the gap in meal preparation is hard to ignore: mothers account for close to 75% of solo time on that task, while fathers contribute barely 10%. Outdoor play is one of the few areas where fathers edge ahead, at least when each parent is acting independently.
Taken together, the pattern is fairly consistent. Parents are most likely to share the lighter, more enjoyable tasks like reading and outdoor play, while the more routine or labour-intensive jobs — cooking, bathing — fall largely to mothers. It is a familiar picture, and the numbers here put some clear proportions on it.

The table provided presents data on the caloric intake from four staple foods — rice, wheat, maize, and potatoes — consumed per person per day across four selected countries: Japan, Germany, Nigeria, and Brazil. Values are expressed in calories per person per day.
Overall, rice constitutes the dominant caloric source in Japan, while Germany records the highest consumption of wheat among the four nations. Nigeria leads in calorie intake from maize, whereas Brazil shows a more evenly distributed consumption pattern across the four food categories.
Japan records the highest rice consumption at 1380 calories per person per day, far exceeding Brazil at 210 and Nigeria at 45. Germany, in contrast, consumes only 12 calories from rice daily. In terms of wheat, Germany leads significantly with 920 calories, followed by Brazil at 340 and Japan at 105. Nigeria records the lowest wheat intake among the four countries, contributing a mere 28 calories per day.
Regarding maize, Nigeria dominates with 1150 calories per person per day, compared to Brazil at 620 and Germany at 90. Japan records no measurable maize consumption. As for potatoes, Germany again ranks highest with 490 calories, while Japan contributes the least at 35. Nigeria and Brazil record 180 and 260 calories from potatoes respectively. When total caloric intake is considered across all four food categories, Germany and Nigeria emerge as the highest consumers overall, whereas Japan and Brazil fall comparatively lower in aggregate figures.

The table presents daily caloric intake from four staple foods — rice, wheat, maize, and potatoes — across Japan, Germany, Nigeria, and Brazil, with all figures given in calories per person per day.
At a glance, rice is by far the most significant source of calories in Japan, while Germany relies most heavily on wheat. Nigeria stands out for its exceptionally high maize consumption, and Brazil spreads its intake more evenly across the four categories than any of the other countries.
Japan's rice consumption is the highest recorded in the table at 1380 calories per day, dwarfing Brazil's 210 and Nigeria's 45. Germany barely registers here, at just 12 calories from rice. Wheat tells a different story — Germany leads at 920, with Brazil at 340 and Japan at 105. Nigeria consumes very little wheat at only 28 calories daily.
Maize is where Nigeria dominates, reaching 1150 calories per person per day against Brazil's 620 and Germany's 90. Japan consumes no maize at all. Potato intake follows a somewhat similar pattern to wheat, with Germany again topping the list at 490 calories, while Japan sits at the bottom with just 35. Nigeria and Brazil come in at 180 and 260 respectively.
Looking at total intake across all four foods, Germany and Nigeria consume the most overall, though for very different reasons — Germany through wheat and potatoes, Nigeria through maize. Japan and Brazil both fall lower in aggregate, despite Japan's commanding lead in rice alone.

Nowadays, the younger generation tends to avoid recreational activities such as hiking or alpinism. It seems that young people prefer to spend their weekends indoors, gaming or engaging in other online activities. If that is the case, by promoting sports more actively and organising sports competitions, the schools could counteract this trend.Living in the era of technology, the wide availability and variety of online entertainment are compelling young people to spend a lot of their free time indoors, using their gadgets. Numerous relatively cheap forms of amusement, such as online games, videos, audiobooks and plenty of other activities, tempt teenagers to stay at home and lead more sedentary lifestyles. A recent survey revealed that the vast majority of students would prefer spending their weekends playing computer games, rather than in the nature. That is one clear reason why our modern youth is less interested in recreational sports.To address this issue, the school management could intervene and offer a better range of outdoor sports activities and competitions, both within the school curriculum and as extra-curricular activities. For example, high schools could increase the number of weekly football, running and climbing classes, and organise competitions at the end of each term. As a result, pushed by their desire to win, students would do more walking, jogging, running and mountaineering during the school term, in preparation for the competition. This would reduce the time spent online, lessen the students’ dependence on the Internet for entertainment, and eventually boost their desire to spend more time enjoying the natural environment.To sum up, screen addiction plays a major role in young people’s avoidance of various outdoor activities. Opening up more recreational sports and offering frequent competitions on school grounds is a great way to motivate more students to spend their leisure time in the nature.

In recent years, there has been a noticeable decline in the number of young people participating in outdoor and physical leisure activities such as cycling, swimming, or team sports. Instead, the majority of teenagers appear to favour spending their free time on social media platforms or streaming services. This shift in behaviour is concerning, and community centres along with local authorities could play a meaningful role in reversing it by investing in accessible recreational programmes and public sporting events.
The rapid expansion of digital entertainment has fundamentally altered how young people choose to spend their spare time. Streaming platforms, social media applications, and online multiplayer games offer constant stimulation at little to no cost, making it increasingly difficult for outdoor activities to compete for attention. Studies have consistently shown that adolescents today spend an average of several hours per day in front of screens, often at the expense of physical activity. This sedentary behaviour not only reduces engagement with the natural world but also contributes to declining physical fitness among younger age groups.
One effective response to this problem would be for community centres and local governments to expand their offerings of free or low-cost outdoor recreational programmes. Organising regular events such as cycling races, open swimming competitions, or community hiking challenges would give young people structured and socially engaging reasons to step outside. When activities are framed as social events rather than exercise obligations, teenagers are far more likely to participate willingly and consistently. Over time, repeated exposure to these experiences could help reshape leisure habits and cultivate a genuine appreciation for outdoor pursuits.
In conclusion, the dominance of digital entertainment is a primary driver behind the retreat of young people from outdoor recreational activities. By creating more accessible and engaging sporting opportunities at the community level, local institutions can offer a compelling alternative and gradually restore the balance between screen time and active outdoor living.

Over the past decade or so, fewer and fewer young people seem to be spending their free time doing anything particularly physical — cycling, swimming, joining a local sports team. Most teenagers, given the choice, will gravitate toward a screen. Whether that means scrolling through social media, watching shows, or playing online games, the pull of digital entertainment is strong and getting stronger. This is a real problem, and community centres along with local authorities are arguably better placed than anyone to do something about it.
It is not hard to see why outdoor activities are losing ground. Streaming platforms and multiplayer games offer endless stimulation, available instantly and for next to nothing. They are also social in their own way, which matters a great deal to teenagers. Research regularly shows that adolescents spend several hours a day on screens, often at the direct expense of physical activity. The knock-on effects — declining fitness, less time outdoors, weakening ties to the natural environment — are well documented at this point.
The most practical response is probably the most straightforward one: give young people better reasons to go outside. Community centres and local councils could invest in free or heavily subsidised outdoor events — cycling races, open swimming galas, group hiking days. The key is framing. When something feels like a social occasion rather than an exercise class, participation tends to follow naturally. Young people are not opposed to being active; they just need the right context for it.
None of this will happen overnight. But if local institutions make a sustained effort to build these kinds of programmes, the habits that form during adolescence could look quite different a generation from now. The goal is not to demonise screens — it is to make the outdoors genuinely competitive with them.

Nowadays, with the development of education, more and more students go to colleges for academic learning. However, some people consider that we should provide more vocational courses because a lack of practical workers such as electricians and plumbers. Personally, I think academic study is obviously important bacause it can improve the technology and the misunderstanding of job-not the lack of vocational training- is the main reason for a lack of labored workers.
It is no doubt that the academic learning in university can induct the practical training and provoke the development of technology in different areas such as agricuture. If we deeply understand the theory of academic knowledge and reasonablely apply them in different fields, we could efficiently improve the technology and help human beings to increase their living standard. For instance, the development of drones turly improve the quantity of crops, which is benifit for famers. However, this technology need a lot of basic theories to invent the drones and teach worker to handle this machine. At this time, the academic study becomes much more important for practical workers.
Some people claim that it is because a lack of vocational training, thus leading to a lack of qualified worker. However, in my opinion, I think there are mainly two reasons come from the misunderstanding of jod cause this bad situation. For one aspect, some people think that practical workers usually cannot earn much more money compared to empolyees working in office. Thus, they just give up this occupation for their career. For another aspect, some people do not respect those qualified worker, which make them feel their job are secondary. At last, there are no more people would like to choose this type of works.
In conclusion, I think academic study is very much important for students to improve the technology, and the lack of practical workers is not because of less provided vocational training, but the misunderstanding of job between some people.

In contemporary society, the question of whether educational systems should prioritise academic programmes or expand vocational training has become increasingly debated. While some argue that the shortage of skilled tradespeople such as carpenters and mechanics is a direct consequence of insufficient vocational education, I believe that academic study remains fundamentally important, and that the root cause of this shortage lies in deeply held social attitudes toward manual occupations rather than in any deficiency of practical training provision.
Academic education plays an indispensable role in driving technological advancement across virtually every sector of the economy. A thorough understanding of scientific and theoretical principles enables researchers and engineers to develop innovations that ultimately benefit society as a whole. The medical field, for instance, depends heavily on years of academic study in biology and chemistry before any practical application becomes possible. Without this theoretical foundation, the discoveries that underpin modern medicine, renewable energy, and digital communications would simply not exist. Academic learning, therefore, should not be viewed as separate from practical progress but rather as its very foundation.
Regarding the shortage of vocational workers, the problem is more cultural than structural. Many individuals are discouraged from pursuing trades not because training opportunities are unavailable, but because manual work is widely perceived as inferior to office-based professional careers. Parents frequently steer their children toward university degrees in the belief that academic qualifications guarantee higher social status and financial reward. Furthermore, tradespeople often receive insufficient recognition for the complexity and value of their skills, reinforcing the perception that such careers are less desirable. Addressing these attitudes through public awareness campaigns and improved wage structures would likely prove far more effective than simply expanding vocational curricula.
In conclusion, academic education is essential for sustaining technological progress and should not be reduced in favour of vocational training. The real challenge lies in transforming societal perceptions of skilled trades so that more young people feel genuinely encouraged to pursue these equally valuable career paths.

The debate over whether schools and universities should focus more on academic study or expand vocational training has gained a lot of traction recently. Advocates for more vocational courses often point to shortages of carpenters, mechanics, and other tradespeople as evidence that the system is failing. I disagree with that diagnosis. Academic education remains just as important as ever, and the real reason trades are struggling to attract workers has more to do with how society values those jobs than with how many training courses are on offer.
It is worth being clear about what academic study actually does. Theoretical knowledge is not some abstract luxury — it is the foundation that makes practical innovation possible in the first place. Modern medicine, clean energy technology, and advances in agriculture all trace back to years of scientific research built on academic learning. The drone technology now used in farming, for example, required substantial expertise in physics, engineering, and computing before anyone could put it to practical use. Dismissing academic study as disconnected from the real world misunderstands how progress actually works.
The shortage of skilled tradespeople is a genuine problem, but it is not primarily a training problem. Talk to young people about why they avoid trades, and two things come up repeatedly. First, there is a widespread belief — often reinforced by parents — that working with your hands means earning less and being valued less. Second, tradespeople rarely receive the kind of public recognition that goes to doctors or engineers, even when their work is just as technically demanding. These are social attitudes, and no amount of new vocational courses will fix them on their own.
What is actually needed is a cultural shift: better pay, more public recognition for skilled trades, and an education system that stops treating university as the only respectable path. The answer is not less academic learning — it is a broader understanding of what counts as skilled, valuable work.

In recent time, it is observed that the number of students enrolling for Science or Mathematics in higher education is decreasing all over the world. There are plenty of reasons that are causing this decline which will affect the society negatively. In this essay, I will discuss why the number of pupils is deacreasing who prefer science stream and what will be the impacts on a community if it continues.
The main reason of this situation is lack of trained science and mathematics teachers. STEM teachers are not able to kindle the joy of learning among students. If students do not get proper foundation and motivation in their school life to pursue STEM subjects, they will develop Math or Science phobia. Subsequently, they will rather prefer other streams to complete their higher education.
Eventually, this situation will impact the scociety in many ways. Firstly, there would be scarcity of doctors all over the world which will affect health sector dramatically. Eventually, people will suffer with many health problems. Secondly, if students do not take STEM subjects in university, there will not be STEM teachers to teach future generation. According to me, government must take firm steps to avoid these situations in future. There must be some training program to keep STEM teachers up to date to enhance their teaching. This will enable teachers to make science subjects enjoyable for students, for instance, teachers may incorporate technology in the learning process.
To conclude, I believe that government and teachers should take the resposibility to create the conducive environment to learn these subjects in fun way. This will, eventually, increase the number of students enrolling for STEM subjects in recent future. Otherwise, it may have negative impacts on society which will not be a good sign for any country.

In recent years, a worrying trend has emerged in educational institutions around the world, whereby fewer students are choosing to pursue science, technology, engineering, and mathematics at the university level. This decline is driven by a combination of systemic and social factors, and if left unaddressed, it carries serious consequences for economies and societies globally. In this essay, I will explore the underlying causes of this trend and examine the potential impacts it may have on communities in the future.
One of the primary reasons for declining enrolment in STEM fields is the way these subjects are taught at the secondary school level. When students encounter uninspiring or overly theoretical instruction, they frequently develop a negative association with science and mathematics at an early age. This early disengagement is difficult to reverse. Additionally, cultural perceptions play a significant role, as STEM careers are sometimes viewed as excessively demanding or socially isolating compared to careers in business or the arts. The absence of relatable role models, particularly for female students and those from underrepresented backgrounds, further reinforces the sense that these disciplines are not meant for everyone.
The consequences of this trend, if sustained, could be considerable. A reduced pipeline of qualified scientists, engineers, and medical professionals would place significant strain on healthcare systems, infrastructure development, and technological innovation. Nations that fail to produce sufficient STEM graduates may find themselves increasingly dependent on foreign expertise, weakening their economic competitiveness. Governments and educational institutions must therefore act decisively by investing in teacher training, updating curricula to reflect real-world applications, and introducing mentorship schemes that connect students with working professionals in STEM fields.
In conclusion, declining interest in STEM education is a multifaceted problem rooted in poor early instruction and limiting social perceptions. Concerted action from governments, schools, and communities is essential to reverse this trend before its long-term consequences become irreversible.

Something concerning has been happening in classrooms and universities across the world for some time now: fewer students are choosing to study science, mathematics, or engineering. The reasons are not mysterious, but they are layered, and the implications of doing nothing about it are serious enough to warrant real attention. This essay looks at what is driving the decline and what happens to society if the trend continues unchecked.
A lot of the problem starts early. When science and mathematics are taught in dry, abstract ways with little connection to anything students actually care about, those subjects quickly earn a reputation for being hard and joyless. By the time students reach university, many have already written STEM off entirely — a decision shaped years earlier by uninspiring classroom experiences. Cultural attitudes compound this. In many places, STEM careers are still seen as antisocial, excessively demanding, or simply not glamorous. The lack of visible role models, especially for young women and students from minority backgrounds, quietly reinforces the message that these fields are not really for them.
The knock-on effects of sustained low enrolment are not hard to imagine. Fewer STEM graduates means fewer doctors, fewer engineers, fewer researchers — and that shortage does not stay abstract for long. Healthcare systems come under pressure. Infrastructure projects stall. Countries that cannot develop their own technical expertise become reliant on importing it, which is both costly and strategically vulnerable.
The solutions are fairly well understood: better teacher training, curricula that connect theory to real problems, and mentorship programmes that show students what a STEM career actually looks like. The harder part is political will. Without sustained investment and a genuine shift in how these subjects are presented and perceived, the decline will continue — and the consequences will be felt well beyond the classroom.

The table and the graph illustrate information about the number of visitors of Australia in million from foreign countries from 1975 to 2005.
Overall, the number of overseas residents who came to Australia incresed in a straight-line progression from 8.8 to 30.4 during mentioned period of time. The most visitors was from Japan - 3.2 and 12.0, whereas the least number was from China - 0.3 and 0.8 - in 1975 and 2005, respectively.
People from South Korea were in the second place based on the number of visitors - 2.9 in 1975 and 9.1 in 2005. In the third place there was European nations - 1.1 and 4.5 at the same time. The number of Britans was 0.9 and 2.9, whereas the number of americans was 0.4 and 1.1, in 1975 and 2005, correspondingly.
It worth to mentioned that that the whole number of foreign visitors of Australia in 1985 and 1995 was approximately 17 and 25, accordingly.

The table and graph present data regarding the number of international tourists who visited Canada, measured in millions, from 1980 to 2010, broken down by country of origin.
Overall, the total number of foreign visitors to Canada rose steadily throughout the entire period, climbing from 11.3 million in 1980 to 38.7 million in 2010. Among all nationalities, visitors from the United States consistently represented the largest group, recording figures of 4.8 million and 15.6 million in 1980 and 2010 respectively. In contrast, tourists from India accounted for the smallest share, with only 0.4 million arrivals in 1980 and 1.2 million in 2010.
Visitors from the United Kingdom ranked second overall, contributing 3.4 million tourists in 1980 and rising to 10.2 million by 2010. France occupied third position, with figures of 1.6 million and 5.8 million recorded at the beginning and end of the period respectively. German visitors numbered 1.1 million in 1980 and grew to 3.7 million in 2010, while arrivals from Australia stood at 0.6 million and 2.1 million during the same years.
It is also worth noting that the total number of international visitors to Canada in 1990 and 2000 stood at approximately 21 million and 30 million respectively, indicating a consistent and relatively uniform rate of growth across each decade of the observed period.

The table and graph together show how international tourist arrivals to Canada changed between 1980 and 2010, with figures broken down by country of origin and expressed in millions.
The overall trajectory is one of steady growth. Total foreign visitor numbers rose from 11.3 million in 1980 to 38.7 million in 2010, with intermediate totals of roughly 21 million in 1990 and 30 million in 2000 — suggesting a fairly consistent pace of increase across each decade. American tourists dominated throughout the entire period, reaching 4.8 million in 1980 and more than tripling to 15.6 million by 2010. At the opposite end of the scale, visitors from India remained the smallest group, moving only from 0.4 to 1.2 million over the same thirty years.
The United Kingdom held second place for the full period, growing from 3.4 to 10.2 million, while France came third with 1.6 million in 1980 and 5.8 million in 2010. Germany and Australia both recorded more modest figures — 1.1 million rising to 3.7 million, and 0.6 million rising to 2.1 million respectively.
What stands out most is the sheer scale of American dominance relative to every other nationality. Even the second-ranked United Kingdom never came close to matching those figures. Meanwhile, the consistent growth across nearly all groups suggests that Canada's appeal as a destination broadened steadily rather than spiking around any particular event or period.

This line graph indicates oil production and consumption per day in China from 1982 to 2006. Clearly, the consumption doubled its production in 2006.
To begin with, oil consumption started the millions of oil barrels per day for almost two, and remained the same by 1990. But, its consumption suddenly surged to about 3.7 million in 1994. Then, the number of consumption skyrocketed from 1998 and reached a peak of more than 6 million in 2006. Consequently, the consumption tripled, compared with 1982.
Secondly, the production also commenced the millions of oil barrels per day around two, and increased its production to three million in 1986. In contrast, its production leveled off by 1994. Afterward, the production slightly kept going up and resulted in 3.5 million in 2006. As a result, the oil production increased, compared with 1982. But it is clear that the pace of production is quite slower than consumption.

The line graph displays natural gas production and consumption measured in billion cubic metres per day in India from 1990 to 2015. Overall, the most striking feature is that consumption grew at a considerably faster pace than production over the period, resulting in a widening gap between the two by 2015.
Regarding consumption, the figure began at approximately 1.5 billion cubic metres per day in 1990 and remained relatively stable until around 1995. Following this plateau, consumption experienced a moderate rise to roughly 2.8 billion cubic metres by 2000. From that point onwards, the rate of increase accelerated sharply, with consumption climbing steeply to reach a peak of approximately 7 billion cubic metres per day in 2015, representing more than four times the initial figure recorded in 1990.
In terms of production, the starting value in 1990 was similar to that of consumption, at around 1.4 billion cubic metres per day. Production grew gradually and reached approximately 2.5 billion cubic metres by 1998, before levelling off for several years through the early 2000s. After 2005, production resumed a modest upward trend, ultimately reaching around 3.8 billion cubic metres per day in 2015. While this represents a meaningful increase over the full period, the pace of growth was considerably slower than that observed in consumption, meaning that by 2015 consumption had surpassed production by nearly double.

The line graph tracks natural gas production and consumption in India between 1990 and 2015, with figures given in billion cubic metres per day. The dominant story is a growing gap between the two lines — consumption pulling further and further ahead of production as the years progress.
Both indicators started from similar positions in 1990, hovering around 1.4 to 1.5 billion cubic metres per day. Consumption held fairly steady through the mid-1990s before picking up moderately, reaching roughly 2.8 billion cubic metres by 2000. After that, the pace changed dramatically. From the early 2000s onward, consumption climbed steeply and without much interruption, eventually peaking at around 7 billion cubic metres per day in 2015 — more than four times the 1990 figure.
Production followed a much quieter trajectory. It rose gradually through the 1990s to about 2.5 billion cubic metres by 1998, then essentially stalled for several years. A modest recovery after 2005 brought it up to approximately 3.8 billion cubic metres by 2015, which is a real increase, but nowhere near enough to keep pace with demand.
The end result is a gap that barely existed in 1990 and had grown to nearly double by 2015 — consumption roughly twice the level of production. It is a pattern that points clearly to rising dependence on external supply, and the steepening consumption curve in the final years of the period suggests that pressure was only intensifying as 2015 approached.

Hobbies are recreational activities that are pursued away from one's school or work. They are meant to be an escape from the mundane and a way of keeping people sane and happy. Unfortunately, they have become almost a luxury in today's fast-paced world.
Several reasons can account for this massive decrease in the pursuit of hobbies. One such reason is the fact that hobbies require time and money which are two very dear resources in today's world. Social sophistication and technological advancements have brought about changes in hobbies. What used to be called hobbies in the past are now considered obsolete and the new ones, including video games and virtual reality require financial commitment in their pursuit. Even if one has the money to pursue such, he or she would have to set aside time in his or her busy schedule to enjoy those hobbies. It is therefore no wonder that most people found to pursue hobbies today are either wealthy youth or comfortable pensioners.
Secondly, lack of interest may be an important factor why people devote little to no time to hobbies. Most of the educated lot are taught from a tender age only to concentrate on their studies in order to secure a comfortable future for themselves. Such children are discouraged from enjoying anything away from anything extracurricular and therefore grow into hobby-less adults who work all day and do nothing else otherwise. This is seen in a lot of Ghanaian homes where doing anything other than studying after school is deemed a taboo. Consequently, many grow into adults with little humour or mirth.
Moreover, many people get exhausted with school or work, which have become extremely intensive nowadays, as compared to the more laid back past. After a tiring day, most people like to crawl into their beds to catch a night-long rest only to return to their busy life the next day.
This lack of hobby pursuit, though understandable, does the individual and society more harm than good. Many individuals today are being treated for one mental health or another. Hobbies are a good way to relax and destress through leisure and can go a long way to improve mental health. Its absence will only wreak havoc for the society, which will only consist of tired and apathetic individuals who could not care less about each other and their surroundings.
In conclusion, it is important, albeit difficult, to pay attention to hobbies in order to improve the health and psyche of the individual and society as a whole. More effort should be geared at encouraging hobbies at school and at the work place.

Leisure activities, broadly defined as pursuits undertaken voluntarily during one's free time for personal enjoyment, have long been regarded as essential components of a balanced and fulfilling life. From gardening and painting to music and sport, such activities have historically provided individuals with a sense of identity and wellbeing beyond their professional obligations. However, in the contemporary world, meaningful leisure appears to be in steady decline, and this development carries worrying implications for both individuals and communities.
A primary factor contributing to this trend is the increasing demands placed on people's time by modern working culture. In many countries, longer working hours have become normalised, and the expectation of remaining digitally available outside of office hours has further eroded the boundary between professional and personal life. As a result, individuals find themselves with shrinking windows of genuinely free time, making sustained engagement in any leisure activity increasingly difficult to maintain. Those who do manage to carve out time often find themselves too mentally fatigued to invest meaningfully in anything requiring concentration or creative effort.
The nature of entertainment itself has also shifted in ways that discourage deeper leisure engagement. Passive consumption of digital content — scrolling through social media feeds, streaming films, or watching short video clips — has replaced more active and personally enriching pursuits for a large portion of the population. While such activities offer immediate gratification, they rarely provide the lasting satisfaction or sense of accomplishment associated with developing a genuine skill or creative practice. Over time, this substitution may contribute to growing feelings of emptiness and purposelessness among individuals who mistake entertainment for genuine leisure.
The consequences of this shift are not merely personal. Communities in which residents share no common leisure interests tend to exhibit weaker social bonds and lower levels of civic engagement. Hobbies and recreational activities have historically served as natural meeting points for people of different backgrounds, fostering friendships and a sense of belonging that transcends workplace or family circles. Their decline therefore represents not only a personal loss but a gradual erosion of the social fabric that holds communities together.
In conclusion, the retreat from meaningful leisure is a symptom of deeper structural problems in modern society, including overwork, cultural passivity, and the relentless pace of daily life. Addressing this decline requires both individual commitment to protecting personal time and broader societal recognition that leisure is not a luxury but a fundamental human need.

Leisure — the kind that involves actually doing something rather than just consuming it — has long been considered one of the markers of a life well lived. Painting, gardening, playing an instrument, building things: these pursuits give people a sense of self that exists entirely outside of work or school. That kind of leisure, though, seems to be quietly disappearing, and the loss is bigger than it might first appear.
The most obvious culprit is time, or rather the lack of it. Working hours in many countries have crept steadily upward, and the expectation of being reachable after hours has blurred whatever boundary once existed between professional and personal life. By the time most people get home, sit down, and decompress, there is not much of the evening left — and what remains is often claimed by exhaustion rather than enthusiasm. Sustaining a hobby under those conditions takes real effort, and many people simply do not have it to spare.
The entertainment landscape has not helped. Streaming services and social media have made passive consumption so effortless and immediately satisfying that more demanding pursuits struggle to compete. Scrolling through a feed or watching three episodes of something requires nothing from you. Learning to sketch, or improving at chess, or tending a vegetable patch — these ask for patience and tolerance of frustration. They reward you eventually, often deeply, but the delayed gratification is a harder sell than it used to be.
What gets lost when people stop having hobbies is not just personal fulfilment, though that matters. Hobbies are one of the few places where people still meet each other sideways — through shared interest rather than obligation. A running club, a community choir, a weekend pottery class: these create the kind of low-stakes, voluntary connection that holds communities together in ways that are hard to manufacture any other way. When that disappears, something in the social texture of a place quietly thins out.
None of this is inevitable. But reversing it requires taking seriously the idea that leisure is not what is left over after everything else is done — it is something worth protecting in its own right. Schools that actively encourage extracurricular passions, and workplaces that genuinely respect boundaries, would be a reasonable place to start.

There is an ongoing debate about wether a government economic progress is far more important than any other type of progress, for instance: social, technological or health advancements. Although this perspectives are both valid, I believe that authorities do not have to value economy over everything else because it leads to overlooking the basic needs of society.
On the one hand, prioritizing economic progress is important because it is the basis that sustains every countries' needs. Without a strong economy, governments would not be able to afford education or health. For instance many studies show that wealthier countries tend to give their citizens a better life quality.
On the other hand, other types of progress such as educational, technological and health developements can lead to better outcomes in society. For instance it has been proven that countries with the best quality of education tend to be more peaceful and have less crime rates than those where education is not a priority.
In my opinion education, health and technological progress when available to evryone can lead to better developement not only financial but also in the equality of opportunies which is the key to much more content and peaceful societies. Governments that value economy over everything else tend to forget that the true value relies on the people and their needs, not in making money. Economy progress is part of a developed society but should not rely at the centre of issues because well being and good quality of life is a complex goal that requires success in many areas of society.

There is a longstanding debate surrounding whether governments should place economic growth at the centre of their national priorities, or whether equal weight should be given to social development, environmental protection, and public welfare. While a thriving economy undeniably provides the financial foundation necessary for a functioning state, I firmly believe that treating economic progress as the sole measure of national success is both shortsighted and ultimately harmful to the populations governments are meant to serve.
Proponents of economic prioritisation argue, with some justification, that a strong national economy generates the tax revenues required to fund public services, infrastructure, and social programmes. Without sustained growth, governments lack the financial capacity to invest in hospitals, schools, or poverty reduction initiatives. In this sense, economic development can be understood as an enabler of broader social progress rather than its competitor. Countries with consistently high GDP figures do tend to perform better across a range of human development indicators, which lends some credibility to this position.
Nevertheless, the argument has clear limitations. History offers numerous examples of nations that achieved impressive economic growth while simultaneously failing large segments of their populations through inequality, environmental degradation, and inadequate access to healthcare or education. Economic figures alone say nothing about how wealth is distributed or whether citizens feel secure, valued, or heard. A government that measures its success purely through growth statistics risks optimising for a number rather than for the lived experiences of real people.
In my view, genuine national progress is inherently multidimensional. Social cohesion, environmental sustainability, educational attainment, and public health are not secondary concerns to be addressed once economic targets are met — they are ends in themselves. Governments that recognise this tend to produce societies that are not only wealthier but more stable, equitable, and resilient in the long term.

Whether governments should put economic growth above all other priorities is one of those debates that never quite goes away, and for good reason — reasonable people sit on both sides of it. A strong economy clearly matters. But the idea that it should sit at the centre of everything a government does, with social welfare, environmental health, and education arranged around it as afterthoughts, strikes me as fundamentally mistaken.
The case for prioritising the economy is not without merit. Revenue has to come from somewhere, and without a functioning economy, there is nothing to redistribute. Countries that have sustained strong growth over time do tend to score better on measures like life expectancy, literacy, and infrastructure quality. In that sense, economic development and human development are not entirely separate things — one can genuinely enable the other.
But the relationship breaks down quickly when you look at how growth actually plays out in practice. Plenty of countries have posted impressive GDP figures while leaving large portions of their populations behind — underfunded schools, crumbling health systems, widening inequality. A rising aggregate number tells you almost nothing about whether a nurse can afford rent, whether a child in a rural area has access to decent education, or whether a community has clean water. Governing by economic statistics alone is a bit like navigating by a single instrument while ignoring everything else on the dashboard.
Real progress is harder to measure and harder to achieve precisely because it involves so many things at once. A society where people feel secure, educated, healthy, and fairly treated is not just a nicer place to live — it is also more stable and more productive over time. Governments that treat social wellbeing as a byproduct of economic success rather than a goal in its own right tend to find, eventually, that they have been optimising for the wrong thing.

According to what is shown, gradually increased from 19 to 34 ages same that from 35 to 54 ages. Between 55 and 64 ages is siginficant increased about between 19 and 24 ages, After that gradually decreased between 65 and 74. Although, Supposed to 25 and 34 ages, They the more consum but between 55 and 64 ages they is consum the highest. Also, The 75 age is the same percentage, unlike the all under 75 ages.
To sum up, we could say that, the all ages is not equal between male and female in same percentage ages, except 75 age, They slight by 25 per cent between unisex. On other hand, Female and male is equal consume in 75 age.

The bar chart illustrates the percentage of males and females across different age groups in the UK who consumed five or more portions of fruit and vegetables per day. Overall, females consistently recorded higher percentages than males across nearly all age groups represented in the data.
Beginning with the younger age groups, both males and females showed relatively modest consumption figures among those aged 16 to 24, with females recording a somewhat higher percentage than their male counterparts. As age increased, the proportion of both sexes meeting the five-a-day recommendation rose gradually, with the gap between males and females remaining fairly consistent through the 25 to 44 age bracket.
The most notable figures appear in the middle-aged and older categories. Both males and females reached their peak consumption levels in the 55 to 64 age group, where the difference between the two sexes was also among the most pronounced in the entire chart. Following this peak, percentages for both genders declined slightly among those aged 65 to 74 before stabilising in the 75 and over category.
The 75 and over age group stands out as a notable exception to the general pattern. Unlike every other age bracket, males and females in this group recorded virtually identical percentages, with both sitting at approximately 25 percent. This convergence suggests that the gender gap in fruit and vegetable consumption, which is evident throughout younger age groups, largely disappears among the oldest segment of the population.

The bar chart shows what percentage of males and females in different UK age groups ate five or more portions of fruit and vegetables per day. The headline finding is simple: across almost every age group, women came out ahead of men.
Among younger adults, consumption figures for both sexes were relatively low, though women still edged above men in the 16 to 24 bracket. Moving into the 25 to 44 range, percentages climbed for both genders at a fairly gradual pace, and the gap between them stayed more or less stable throughout this stretch.
Things become more interesting in the older age groups. Both males and females hit their highest figures in the 55 to 64 category, and it is here that the gap between the sexes also appears widest. After that peak, numbers dip slightly for the 65 to 74 group before settling in the oldest category.
That oldest group — 75 and over — is the one real exception to the pattern. Here, male and female percentages converge at around 25 percent, making it the only age bracket where the two lines effectively meet. It is a curious reversal of everything that came before it, and worth noting precisely because it breaks so cleanly from the trend visible across every other age group in the chart.

In today's era, most students look forward to enrolling in professional courses at universities. However, some believe that authorities should encourage teenagers to take vocational courses as there is a huge shortage of professionals like electricians and plumbers. In my opinion, while there is a scarcity of such workers and the government should make special arrangements for prospective learners, certain other factors should also be considered.
Due to the insufficient work possibilities after completing vocational courses and extremely low wages, scholars are forced to take loans at high interest from banks to attend university programmes. An average adult has to pay more than 70,000 USD yearly for a typical professional course at a university, and most children are unable to accumulate sufficient funds in time, which forces them to borrow this amount from banks. The job market for plumbing and electrical work is highly unregulated, which leads to the exploitation of such workers by the established brands, further distressing the people. Some regulations should be established for these markets to decrease the risk factor for workers.
On the other hand, due to the continuing increase in our population, the demand for doctors, engineers, and professors is also rising. In recent years, governments from all over the world have reported the widening gap between the number of professionals available per 1000 people. For example, in India, there is only 1 doctor available per 50 people in urban areas, and the number is even more concerning in rural areas, where the figure of patients climbs even further.
In my opinion, authorities and universities should work together towards introducing scholarships and other benefits not only for learners of professional courses but also for peoples of vocational courses. Furthermore, better and more wages for plumbers and electricians could also be established to attract more students.
In conclusion, favourable policies should be implemented for qualified workers to attract more potential learners by the government, as well as lower tution fees for university attendees.

In modern society, the question of whether governments should direct more students toward vocational training or continue supporting the expansion of university education is one that generates considerable discussion among educators and policymakers alike. While academic degree programmes remain highly sought after by young people seeking professional careers, I believe that a balanced approach is necessary, one that addresses the structural and financial barriers facing students in both educational pathways.
A significant obstacle discouraging students from pursuing vocational training is the persistent perception that such careers offer limited financial reward and job security. Trade occupations such as welding, plumbing, and electrical work are frequently undervalued in terms of social status despite being indispensable to the functioning of modern infrastructure. Furthermore, the absence of adequate regulatory frameworks in many countries leaves vocational workers vulnerable to unfair employment practices, inconsistent wages, and limited access to benefits. Without meaningful reforms addressing these conditions, encouraging students to enter these fields will remain an uphill struggle regardless of how many training programmes are made available.
At the same time, the demand for university-educated professionals continues to grow in response to expanding populations and increasingly complex global challenges. Healthcare systems worldwide are reporting critical shortages of qualified doctors and nurses, while engineering and technology sectors struggle to recruit sufficient graduates to meet industry needs. Governments that fail to sustain investment in higher education risk creating bottlenecks in essential services that affect millions of citizens directly. Financial barriers, however, prevent many capable students from accessing university education, reinforcing inequality and limiting the overall pool of qualified graduates entering the workforce.
In conclusion, rather than treating vocational and academic education as competing priorities, governments should pursue policies that strengthen both simultaneously. This includes introducing financial support mechanisms for students in all educational tracks, establishing clearer wage standards for skilled trades, and launching public awareness campaigns that challenge outdated perceptions of vocational careers.

The debate over whether governments should push more students toward vocational training or keep backing university expansion is not going away anytime soon, and rightly so — there are real tensions on both sides. My own view is that framing it as a choice between the two is part of the problem. What is needed is a serious effort to fix what is broken in both pathways at the same time.
One of the biggest obstacles facing vocational education is not a lack of training programmes — it is a lack of respect and fair pay for the people who complete them. Electricians, welders, and plumbers keep cities running, yet these careers are routinely treated as fallback options rather than skilled professions. Making that worse, many countries have poorly regulated labour markets for trades, which leaves workers exposed to low wages, unstable contracts, and few protections. Telling students to consider vocational routes while those conditions remain unchanged is not a convincing pitch.
University education has its own serious problems. The cost has ballooned to a point where a significant number of capable students are effectively priced out, or graduate carrying debt that shapes their financial lives for years. Meanwhile, the shortage of trained doctors, engineers, and researchers is real and growing — particularly in developing countries, where the ratio of qualified professionals to population remains deeply inadequate. Letting that gap widen because of access barriers is an expensive mistake.
The practical answer involves governments and institutions working together on several fronts: scholarships and subsidies for students in both tracks, clearer wage floors for skilled trades, and sustained investment in university places for high-demand professions. More than policy, though, what is needed is a cultural shift — one that stops treating a university degree as the only marker of a worthwhile education, and starts valuing the full range of skills that a functioning society actually depends on.

The bar chart depicts the figure for men and women who joined four different evening classes at an adult education center in 2009 while the pie chart illustrates the proportion of participants’ age, divided into five groups.
Overall, it can be seen that women participated more than men in every course, except sculpture. Additionally, the majority of the classes was elders who were 50 years old or over.
Looking at the detail, painting and language were the most popular subject among men and women respectively. Moreover, females joined drama and painting more than males by 10 people. For language course, 20 men attended the class while the number of women were two times higher than that of men, accounting for 40 people. On the other hand, sculpture was the least popular among women and the figure for women participating in this class was lower than the number of men by 10 people.
Regarding ages, 68% of the participants were people over 40 years old, 26% for the 40-49 age group and 42% for the over-50 age group. The remaining 32% was people under 39 years old, including 5% of children who were under 20 years old which was the minority group.

The bar chart displays the number of male and female participants who enrolled in five different weekend workshops at a community learning centre in 2015, while the pie chart shows the age distribution of all attendees, divided into six categories.
Overall, female participants outnumbered male participants in the majority of workshops, with the exception of photography. Furthermore, the largest share of attendees belonged to older age groups, with those aged 45 and above accounting for the greatest proportion of total enrolment.
In terms of individual workshops, cookery and creative writing attracted the highest levels of female participation, whereas woodwork drew the greatest number of male attendees. Women outnumbered men in both cookery and creative writing by approximately 15 participants in each case. For the pottery workshop, 25 men enrolled compared to 45 women, meaning female participation was nearly double that of males. Photography, however, proved to be the exception, with male attendance exceeding female attendance by around 10 participants, making it the only workshop where men formed the majority.
Regarding the age breakdown, participants aged 45 and over constituted 71% of total attendance combined, with the 45 to 54 group representing 29% and the over-55 group accounting for 42%. The remaining 29% comprised younger attendees, with those under 25 years old forming the smallest segment at just 4% of the total. The 25 to 34 and 35 to 44 age groups contributed 11% and 14% respectively, indicating that middle-aged and older adults were far more inclined to attend weekend workshops than younger members of the community.

The bar chart shows how many men and women signed up for five different weekend workshops at a community learning centre in 2015, while the accompanying pie chart breaks down the age profile of all participants across six groups.
The broad pattern is clear enough: women turned out in greater numbers than men for most workshops, and the overall attendee base skewed noticeably older, with those aged 45 and above making up the bulk of enrolment.
Looking at the individual workshops, cookery and creative writing were the standout choices for women, while woodwork was where male attendance peaked. In both cookery and creative writing, women outnumbered men by around 15 people each. Pottery showed an even wider gap — 25 men against 45 women, close to a two-to-one ratio. Photography was the one exception to the female-majority trend, with men edging ahead by roughly 10 participants and making it the only workshop where male attendance came out on top.
The age data tells its own story. Participants aged 45 and over accounted for 71% of all attendees — split between 29% in the 45 to 54 bracket and 42% in the over-55 group. Younger adults were a much smaller presence: the under-25 group made up just 4% of the total, while the 25 to 34 and 35 to 44 groups contributed 11% and 14% respectively. Taken together, the figures paint a picture of weekend workshops as an activity dominated by older adults, with younger age groups remaining largely on the margins.

Overall, there are 3 main steps of processing from the beginning to the end. In the fourth section and sixth section, adding water and liquid is the most important part of these formed of fuel.
On the first step, the corn will be stored and milling to be reached to cooking. It will need 4 hours times to be cooked and water would be added into the cooking. On the second, Fermenting will be hold on (48 hours) after the cooking levels of speed. Then, the separating time has arrived, within this process the liquid is then added into the solids of separating product.
